Science Writer/Editor 
 
Anne Brown Rodgers 
Falls Church, Virginia 
 
 
 
 
 
 
    
 
 
 
 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
1 
 
Part A. Executive Summary 
1 
The 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee (DGAC) was established jointly by the 
2 
Secretaries of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) and the U.S. 
3 
Department of Agriculture (USDA). The Committee was charged with examining the Dietary 
4 
Guidelines for Americans, 2010 to determine topics for which new scientific evidence was likely 
5 
to be available with the potential to inform the next edition of the Guidelines and to place its 
6 
primary emphasis on the development of food-based recommendations that are of public health 
7 
importance for Americans ages 2 years and older published since the last DGAC deliberations.  
8 
 
9 
The 2015 DGAC’s work was guided by two fundamental realities. First, about half of all 
10 
American adults—117 million individuals—have one or more preventable, chronic diseases, and 
11 
about two-thirds of U.S. adults—nearly 155 million individuals—are overweight or obese. These 
12 
conditions have been highly prevalent for more than two decades. Poor dietary patterns, 
13 
overconsumption of calories, and physical inactivity directly contribute to these disorders. 
14 
Second, individual nutrition and physical activity behaviors and other health-related lifestyle 
15 
behaviors are strongly influenced by personal, social, organizational, and environmental contexts 
16 
and systems. Positive changes in individual diet and physical activity behaviors, and in the 
17 
environmental contexts and systems that affect them, could substantially improve health 
18 
outcomes. 
19 
 
20 
Recognizing these realities, the Committee developed a conceptual model based on socio-
21 
ecological frameworks to guide its work (see Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction) and organized its 
22 
evidence review to examine current status and trends in food and nutrient intakes, dietary 
23 
patterns and health outcomes, individual lifestyle behavior change, food and physical activity 
24 
environments and settings, and food sustainability and safety.  
25 
 
26 
The remainder of this Executive Summary provides brief synopses of the DGAC’s topic-specific 
27 
evidence review chapters. Each of these chapters ends with a list of research recommendations 
28 
(see Appendix E-1: Needs for Future Research for a compilation of these recommendations). 
29 
The Committee integrated its findings and conclusions into several key themes and articulated 
30 
specific recommendations for how the report’s findings can be put into action at the individual, 
31 
community, and population levels. The Executive Summary ends with a brief summary of this 
32 
chapter. 
33 
 
34 
 
35 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
2 
 
TOPIC-SPECIFIC FINDINGS AND CONCLUSIONS 
36 
Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
37 
The DGAC conducted data analyses to address a series of questions related to the current status 
38 
and trends in the Nation’s dietary intake. The questions focused on: intake of specific nutrients 
39 
and food groups; food categories (i.e., foods as consumed) that contribute to intake; eating 
40 
behaviors; and the composition of various dietary patterns shown to have health benefits. These 
41 
topics were addressed using data from the What We Eat in America dietary survey, which is the 
42 
dietary intake component of the ongoing National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey. 
43 
Food pattern modeling using the USDA Food Pattern food groups also was used to address some 
44 
questions. In addition, the DGAC examined the prevalence and trends of health conditions that 
45 
may have a nutritional origin, or where the course of disease may be influenced by diet. 
46 
 
47 
The DGAC found that several nutrients are underconsumed relative to the Estimated Average 
48 
Requirement or Adequate Intake levels set by the Institute of Medicine (IOM) and the 
49 
Committee characterized these as shortfall nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E, vitamin C, 
50 
folate, calcium, magnesium, fiber, and potassium. For adolescent and premenopausal females, 
51 
iron also is a shortfall nutrient. Of the shortfall nutrients, calcium, vitamin D, fiber, and 
52 
potassium also are classified as nutrients of public health concern because their 
53 
underconsumption has been linked in the scientific literature to adverse health outcomes. Iron is 
54 
included as a shortfall nutrient of public health concern for adolescent females and adult females 
55 
who are premenopausal due to the increased risk of iron-deficiency in these groups. The DGAC 
56 
also found that two nutrients—sodium and saturated fat—are overconsumed by the U.S. 
57 
population relative to the Tolerable Upper Intake Level set by the IOM or other maximal 
58 
standard and that the overconsumption poses health risks. 
59 
 
60 
In comparison to recommended amounts in the USDA Food Patterns, the majority of the U.S. 
61 
population has low intakes of key food groups that are important sources of the shortfall 
62 
nutrients, including vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and dairy. Furthermore, population intake is 
63 
too high for refined grains and added sugars. The data suggest cautious optimism about dietary 
64 
intake of the youngest members of the U.S. population because many young children ages 2 to 5 
65 
years consume recommended amounts of fruit and dairy. However, a better understanding is 
66 
needed on how to maintain and encourage good habits that are started early in life. Analysis of 
67 
data on food categories, such as burgers, sandwiches, mixed dishes, desserts, and beverages, 
68 
shows that the composition of many of these items could be improved so as to increase 
69 
population intake of vegetables, whole grains, and other underconsumed food groups and to 
70 
lower population intake of the nutrients sodium and saturated fat, and the food component 
71 
refined grains. Improved beverage selections that limit or remove sugar-sweetened beverages 
72 
and place limits on sweets and desserts would help lower intakes of the food component, added 
73 
sugars. 
74 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
3 
 
 
75 
The U.S. population purchases its food in a variety of locations, including supermarkets, 
76 
convenience stores, schools, and the workplace. The DGAC found that although diet quality 
77 
varies somewhat by the setting where food is obtained, overall, no matter where the food is 
78 
obtained, the diet quality of the U.S. population does not meet recommendations for vegetables, 
79 
fruit, dairy, or whole grains, and exceeds recommendations, leading to overconsumption, for the 
80 
nutrients sodium and saturated fat and the food components refined grains, solid fats, and added 
81 
sugars.  
82 
 
83 
Obesity and many other health conditions with a nutritional origin are highly prevalent. The 
84 
Nation must accelerate progress toward reducing the incidence and prevalence of overweight and 
85 
obesity and chronic disease risk across the U.S. population throughout the lifespan and reduce 
86 
the disparities in obesity and chronic disease rates that exist in the United States for certain 
87 
ethnic and racial groups and for those with lower incomes.  
88 
 
89 
The DGAC had enough descriptive information from existing research and data to model three 
90 
dietary patterns and to examine their nutritional adequacy. These patterns are the Healthy U.S.-
91 
style Pattern, the Healthy Mediterranean-style Pattern, and the Healthy Vegetarian Pattern. These 
92 
patterns include the components of a dietary pattern associated with health benefits.  
93 
 
94 
 
95 
Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
96 
A major goal of the DGAC was to describe the common characteristics of healthy diets, and the 
97 
Committee focused on research examining dietary patterns because the totality of diet—the 
98 
combinations and quantities in which foods and nutrients are consumed—may have synergistic 
99 
and cumulative effects on health and disease. The Committee focused on providing a qualitative 
100 
description of healthy dietary patterns based on scientific evidence for several health outcomes. 
101 
 
102 
The DGAC found remarkable consistency in the findings and implications across its conclusion 
103 
statements for the questions examining dietary patterns and various health outcomes. When 
104 
reviewing the evidence, the Committee attempted to adhere to the language used by the study 
105 
authors in describing food groupings. There was variability across the food groupings, and this 
106 
was particularly apparent in the meat group. For example, “total meat” may have been defined as 
107 
“meat, sausage, fish, and eggs,” “red meat, processed meat, and poultry,” or various other 
108 
combinations of meat. Similarly, “vegetables” seemed to most often exclude potatoes, but some 
109 
studies included potatoes, yet those that mentioned potatoes rarely provided information on how 
110 
the potatoes were consumed (e.g., fried versus baked). When reported in the studies, the 
111 
Committee considered these definitions in their review. However, the Committee provided a 
112 
general label for the food groupings in its conclusion statements. 
113 
 
114 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
4 
 
The overall body of evidence examined by the 2015 DGAC identifies that a healthy dietary 
115 
pattern is higher in vegetables, fruits, whole grains, low- or non-fat dairy, seafood, legumes, and 
116 
nuts; moderate in alcohol (among adults); lower in red and processed meat;1 and low in sugar-
117 
sweetened foods and drinks and refined grains. Vegetables and fruit are the only characteristics 
118 
of the diet that were consistently identified in every conclusion statement across the health 
119 
outcomes. Whole grains were identified slightly less consistently compared to vegetables and 
120 
fruits, but were identified in every conclusion with moderate to strong evidence. For studies with 
121 
limited evidence, grains were not as consistently defined and/or they were not identified as a key 
122 
characteristic. Low- or non-fat dairy, seafood, legumes, nuts, and alcohol were identified as 
123 
beneficial characteristics of the diet for some, but not all, outcomes. For conclusions with 
124 
moderate to strong evidence, higher intake of red and processed meats was identified as 
125 
detrimental compared to lower intake. Higher consumption of sugar-sweetened foods and 
126 
beverages as well as refined grains was identified as detrimental in almost all conclusion 
127 
statements with moderate to strong evidence.  
128 
 
129 
Regarding alcohol, the Committee confirmed several conclusions of the 2010 DGAC, including 
130 
that moderate alcohol intake can be a component of a healthy dietary pattern, and that if alcohol 
131 
is consumed, it should be consumed in moderation and only by adults. However, it is not 
132 
recommended that anyone begin drinking or drink more frequently on the basis of potential 
133 
health benefits, because moderate alcohol intake also is associated with increased risk of 
134 
violence, drowning, and injuries from falls and motor vehicle crashes. Women should be aware 
135 
of a moderately increased risk of breast cancer even with moderate alcohol intake. In addition, 
136 
there are many circumstances in which people should not drink alcohol, including during 
137 
pregnancy. Because of the substantial evidence clearly demonstrating the health benefits of 
138 
breastfeeding, occasionally consuming an alcoholic drink does not warrant stopping 
139 
breastfeeding. However, women who are breastfeeding should be very cautious about drinking 
140 
alcohol, if they choose to drink at all.  
141 
 
142 
Following a dietary pattern associated with reduced risk of CVD, overweight, and obesity also 
143 
will have positive health benefits beyond these categories of health outcomes. Thus, the U.S. 
144 
population should be encouraged and guided to consume dietary patterns that are rich in 
145 
vegetables, fruit, whole grains, seafood, legumes, and nuts; moderate in low- and non-fat dairy 
146 
products and alcohol (among adults); lower in red and processed meat; and low in sugar-
147 
sweetened foods and beverages and refined grains. These dietary patterns can be achieved in 
148 
many ways and should be tailored to the individual’s biological and medical needs as well as 
149 
socio-cultural preferences.  
150 
 
151 
                                                 
1 As lean meats were not consistently defined or handled similarly between studies, they were not identified as a 
common characteristic across the reviews. However, as demonstrated in the food pattern modeling of the Healthy 
U.S.-style and Healthy Mediterranean-style patterns, lean meats can be a part of a healthy dietary pattern. 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
5 
 
The dietary pattern characteristics being recommended by the 2015 DGAC reaffirm the dietary 
152 
pattern characteristics recommended by the 2010 DGAC. Additionally, these characteristics 
153 
align with recommendations from other groups, including the American Institute for Cancer 
154 
Research (AICR) and the American Heart Association (AHA). The majority of evidence 
155 
considered by the Committee focused on dietary patterns consumed in adulthood. Very little 
156 
evidence examined dietary patterns during childhood. However, the healthy dietary pattern 
157 
components described above also apply to children and are reaffirmed with the USDA Food 
158 
Patterns, which are designed to meet nutrient needs across the lifespan. 
159 
 
160 
Individual Diet and Physical Activity Behavior Change 
161 
The individual is at the innermost core of the social-ecological model. In order for policy 
162 
recommendations such as the Dietary Guidelines for Americans to be fully implemented, 
163 
motivating and facilitating behavioral change at the individual level is required. This chapter 
164 
suggests a number of promising behavior change strategies that can be used to favorably affect a 
165 
range of health-related outcomes and to enhance the effectiveness of interventions. These include 
166 
reducing screen time, reducing the frequency of eating out at fast food restaurants, increasing 
167 
frequency of family shared meals, and self-monitoring of diet and body weight as well as 
168 
effective food labeling to target healthy food choices. These strategies complement 
169 
comprehensive lifestyle interventions and nutrition counseling by qualified nutrition 
170 
professionals.  
171 
 
172 
For this approach to work, it will be essential that the food environments in communities 
173 
available to the U.S. population, particularly to low-income individuals, facilitate access to 
174 
healthy and affordable food choices that respect their cultural preferences. Similarly, food and 
175 
calorie label education should be designed to be understood by audiences with low health 
176 
literacy, some of which may have additional English language fluency limitations. Although 
177 
viable approaches are available now, additional research is necessary to improve the scientific 
178 
foundation for more effective guidelines on individual-level behavior change for all individuals 
179 
living in the United States, taking into account the social, economic, and cultural environments 
180 
in which they live. 
181 
 
182 
The evidence reviewed in this chapter also indicates that the social, economic, and cultural 
183 
context in which individuals live may facilitate or hinder their ability to choose and consume 
184 
dietary patterns that are consistent with the Dietary Guidelines. Specifically, household food 
185 
insecurity hinders the access to healthy diets for millions of Americans. In addition, immigrants 
186 
are at high risk of losing the healthier dietary patterns characteristic of their cultural background 
187 
as they acculturate into mainstream America. Furthermore, preventive nutrition services that take 
188 
into account the social determinants of health are largely unavailable in the U.S. health system to 
189 
systematically address nutrition-related health problems, including overweight and obesity, 
190 
cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and other health outcomes.  
191 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
6 
 
 
192 
This chapter calls for: a) stronger Federal policies to help prevent household food insecurity and 
193 
to help families to cope with food insecurity if it develops, b) food and nutrition assistance 
194 
programs to take into account the risk that immigrants have of giving up their healthier dietary 
195 
habits soon after arriving in the United States, and c) efforts to provide all individuals living in 
196 
the United States with the environments, knowledge, and tools needed to implement effective 
197 
individual- or family-level behavioral change strategies to improve the quality of their diets and 
198 
reduce sedentary behaviors. These goals will require changes at all levels of the social-ecological 
199 
model through coordinated efforts among health care and social and food systems from the 
200 
national to the local level. 
201 
 
202 
 
203 
Food Environment and Settings 
204 
Environmental and policy approaches are needed to complement individual-based efforts to 
205 
improve diet and reduce obesity and other diet-related chronic diseases. These approaches have 
206 
the potential for broad and sustained impact at the population level because they can become 
207 
incorporated into organizational structures and systems and lead to alterations in sociocultural 
208 
and societal norms. Both policy and environmental changes also can help reduce disparities by 
209 
improving access to and availability of healthy food in underserved neighborhoods and 
210 
communities. Federal nutrition assistance programs, in particular, play a vital role in achieving 
211 
this objective through access to affordable foods that help millions of Americans meet Dietary 
212 
Guidelines recommendations.  
213 
 
214 
The DGAC focused on physical environments (settings) in which food is available. Its aim was 
215 
to better understand the impact of the food environment to promote or hinder healthy eating in 
216 
these settings and to identify the most effective evidence-based diet-related approaches and 
217 
policies to improve diet and weight status. The DGAC focused on four settings—community 
218 
food access, child care, schools, and worksites—and their relationships to dietary intake and 
219 
quality and weight status.  
220 
 
221 
The DGAC found moderate and promising evidence that multi-component obesity prevention 
222 
approaches implemented in child care settings, schools, and worksites improve weight-related 
223 
outcomes; strong to moderate evidence that school and worksite policies are associated with 
224 
improved dietary intake; and moderate evidence that multi-component school-based and 
225 
worksite approaches increase vegetable and fruit consumption. For the questions on community 
226 
food access addressing the relationship between food retail settings and dietary intake and 
227 
quality and weight status, the evidence was too limited or insufficient to assign grades. To reduce 
228 
the disparity gaps that currently exist in low resource and underserved communities, more 
229 
solution-oriented strategies need to be implemented and evaluated on ways to increase access to 
230 
and procurement of healthy affordable foods and beverages, and also to reduce access to energy-
231 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
7 
 
dense, nutrient-poor foods and beverages. Although several innovative approaches are taking 
232 
place now throughout the country, they generally lack adequate evaluation efforts. 
233 
 
234 
The Committee’s findings revealed the power of multi-component approaches over single 
235 
component interventions. For obesity prevention, effective multi-component interventions 
236 
incorporated both nutrition and physical activity using a variety of strategies, such as 
237 
environmental policies to improve the availability and provision of healthy foods and beverages; 
238 
increasing opportunities for physical activity; increased parent engagement (in child care and 
239 
school settings); and educational approaches, such as a school nutrition curriculum. For multi-
240 
component dietary interventions (e.g., to increase consumption of vegetables and fruit) the most 
241 
effective strategies included nutrition education, parent engagement (in school and child care 
242 
settings), and environmental modifications (e.g., policies for nutrition standards, food service 
243 
changes, point of purchase information). 
244 
 
245 
Collaborative partnerships and strategic efforts are needed to translate this evidence into action. 
246 
Further work on restructuring the environment to facilitate healthy eating and physical activity, 
247 
especially in high risk populations, is needed to advance evidence-based solutions that can be 
248 
scaled up.  
249 
 
250 
 
251 
Food Sustainability and Safety 
252 
Access to sufficient, nutritious, and safe food is an essential element of food security for the U.S. 
253 
population. A sustainable diet ensures this access for both the current population and future 
254 
generations.  
255 
 
256 
The major findings regarding sustainable diets were that a diet higher in plant-based foods, such 
257 
as vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and seeds, and lower in calories and animal-
258 
based foods is more health promoting and is associated with less environmental impact than is 
259 
the current U.S. diet. This pattern of eating can be achieved through a variety of dietary patterns, 
260 
including the Healthy U.S.-style Pattern, the Healthy Mediterranean-style Pattern, and the 
261 
Healthy Vegetarian Pattern. All of these dietary patterns are aligned with lower environmental 
262 
impacts and provide options that can be adopted by the U.S. population. Current evidence shows 
263 
that the average U.S. diet has a larger environmental impact in terms of increased greenhouse gas 
264 
emissions, land use, water use, and energy use, compared to the above dietary patterns. This is 
265 
because the current U.S. population intake of animal-based foods is higher and plant-based foods 
266 
are lower, than proposed in these three dietary patterns. Of note is that no food groups need to be 
267 
eliminated completely to improve sustainability outcomes over the current status. 
268 
 
269 
A moderate amount of seafood is an important component of two of three of these dietary 
270 
patterns, and has demonstrated health benefits. The seafood industry is in the midst of rapid 
271 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
8 
 
expansion to meet worldwide demand. The collapse of some fisheries due to overfishing in the 
272 
past decades has raised concern about the ability to produce a safe and affordable supply. In 
273 
addition, concern has been raised about the safety and nutrient content of farm-raised versus 
274 
wild-caught seafood. To supply enough seafood to support meeting dietary recommendations, 
275 
both farm-raised and wild caught seafood will be needed. The review of the evidence 
276 
demonstrated, in the species evaluated, that farm-raised seafood has as much or more EPA and 
277 
DHA per serving as wild caught. It should be noted that low-trophic seafood, such as catfish and 
278 
crawfish, regardless of whether wild caught or farm-raised seafood, have less EPA and DHA per 
279 
serving than high-trophic seafood, such as salmon and trout. 
280 
 
281 
Regarding contaminants, for the majority of wild caught and farmed species, neither the risks of 
282 
mercury nor organic pollutants outweigh the health benefits of seafood consumption. Consistent 
283 
evidence demonstrated that wild caught fisheries that have been managed sustainably have 
284 
remained stable over the past several decades; however, wild caught fisheries are fully exploited 
285 
and their continuing productivity will require careful management nationally and internationally 
286 
to avoid long-term collapse. Expanded supply of seafood nationally and internationally will 
287 
depend upon the increase of farm-raised seafood worldwide.  
288 
 
289 
The impact of food production, processing, and consumption on environmental sustainability is 
290 
an area of research that is rapidly evolving. As further research is conducted and best practices 
291 
are evaluated, additional evidence will inform both supply-side participants and consumers on 
292 
how best to shift behaviors locally, nationally, and globally to support sustainable diets. Linking 
293 
health, dietary guidance, and the environment will promote human health and the sustainability 
294 
of natural resources and ensure current and long-term food security.  
295 
 
296 
In regard to food safety, updated and previously unexamined areas of food safety were studied. 
297 
Currently, strong evidence shows that consumption of coffee within the moderate range (3 to 5 
298 
cups per day or up to 400 mg/d caffeine) is not associated with increased long-term health risks 
299 
among healthy individuals. In fact, consistent evidence indicates that coffee consumption is 
300 
associated with reduced risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease in adults. Moreover, 
301 
moderate evidence shows a protective association between caffeine intake and risk of 
302 
Parkinson’s disease. Therefore, moderate coffee consumption can be incorporated into a healthy 
303 
dietary pattern, along with other healthful behaviors.  However, it should be noted that coffee as 
304 
it is normally consumed can contain added calories from cream, milk, and added sugars. Care 
305 
should be taken to minimize the amount of calories from added sugars and high-fat dairy or dairy 
306 
substitutes added to coffee. 
307 
 
308 
The marketing and availability of high-caffeine beverages and products is on the rise. 
309 
Unfortunately, only limited evidence is currently available to ascertain the safety of high caffeine 
310 
intake (greater than 400 mg/day for adults and undetermined for children and adolescents) that 
311 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
9 
 
may occur with rapid consumption of large-sized energy drinks. Limited data suggest adverse 
312 
health outcomes, such as caffeine toxicity and cardiovascular events. Concern is heightened 
313 
when caffeine is combined with alcoholic beverages. Limited or no consumption of high caffeine 
314 
drinks, or other products with high amounts of caffeine, is advised for children and adolescents. 
315 
Energy drinks with high levels of caffeine and alcoholic beverages should not be consumed 
316 
together, either mixed together or consumed at the same sitting. 
317 
 
318 
The DGAC also examined the food additive aspartame. At the level that the U.S. population 
319 
consumes aspartame, it appears to be safe. However, some uncertainty continues about increased 
320 
risk of hematopoietic cancer in men, indicating a need for more research.  
321 
 
322 
Individual behaviors along with sound government policies and responsible private sector 
323 
practices are all needed to reduce foodborne illnesses. To that end, the DGAC updated the 
324 
established recommendations for handling foods at home.  
325 
 
326 
 
327 
Cross-cutting Topics of Public Health Importance 
328 
The 2010 Dietary Guidelines included guidance on sodium, saturated fat, and added sugars, and 
329 
the 2015 DGAC determined that a reexamination of the evidence on these topics was necessary 
330 
to determine whether revisions to the guidance were warranted. These topics were considered to 
331 
be of public health importance because each has been associated with negative health outcomes 
332 
when overconsumed. Additionally, the Committee acknowledged that a potential unintended 
333 
consequence of a recommendation on added sugars might be that consumers and manufacturers 
334 
replace added sugars with low-calorie sweeteners. As a result, the Committee also examined 
335 
evidence on low-calorie sweeteners to inform statements on this topic. 
336 
 
337 
The DGAC encourages the consumption of healthy dietary patterns that are low in saturated fat, 
338 
added sugars, and sodium. The goals for the general population are: less than 2,300 mg dietary 
339 
sodium per day (or age-appropriate Dietary Reference Intake amount), less than 10 percent of 
340 
total calories from saturated fat per day, and a maximum of 10 percent of total calories from 
341 
added sugars per day.  
342 
 
343 
Sodium, saturated fat, and added sugars are not intended to be reduced in isolation, but as a part 
344 
of a healthy dietary pattern that is balanced, as appropriate, in calories. Rather than focusing 
345 
purely on reduction, emphasis should also be placed on replacement and shifts in food intake and 
346 
eating patterns. Sources of saturated fat should be replaced with unsaturated fat, particularly 
347 
polyunsaturated fatty acids. Similarly, added sugars should be reduced in the diet and not 
348 
replaced with low-calorie sweeteners, but rather with healthy options, such as water in place of 
349 
sugar-sweetened beverages. For sodium, emphasis should be placed on expanding industry 
350 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
10 
 
efforts to reduce the sodium content of foods and helping consumers understand how to flavor 
351 
unsalted foods with spices and herbs.  
352 
 
353 
Reducing sodium, saturated fat, and added sugars can be accomplished and is more attainable by 
354 
eating a healthy dietary pattern. For all three of these components of the diet, policies and 
355 
programs at local, state, and national levels in both the private and public sector are necessary to 
356 
support reduction efforts. Similarly, the Committee supports efforts in labeling and other 
357 
campaigns to increase consumer awareness and understanding of sodium, saturated fats, and 
358 
added sugars in foods and beverages. The Committee encourages the food industry to continue 
359 
reformulating and making changes to certain foods to improve their nutrition profile. Examples 
360 
of such actions include lowering sodium and added sugars content, achieving better saturated fat 
361 
to polyunsaturated fat ratio, and reducing portion sizes in retail settings (restaurants, food outlets, 
362 
and public venues, such as professional sports stadiums and arenas). The Committee also 
363 
encourages the food industry to market these improved products to consumers. 
364 
 
365 
 
366 
Physical Activity 
367 
This chapter provides strong evidence supporting the importance of regular physical activity for 
368 
health promotion and disease prevention in the U.S. population. Physical activity is important for 
369 
all people—children, adolescents, adults, older adults, women during pregnancy and the 
370 
postpartum period, and individuals with disabilities. The findings further provide guidance on the 
371 
dose of physical activity needed across the lifecycle to realize these significant health benefits.  
372 
 
373 
Future Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committees will be asked to carefully review the 
374 
most recent evidence so that the Federal government can fully update the 2008 Physical Activity 
375 
Guidelines for Americans. Given the exceedingly low physical activity participation rates in this 
376 
country, it will be critically important for the next Committee to identify proven strategies and 
377 
approaches to increase population-level physical activity across the lifespan.  
378 
 
379 
INTEGRATING THE EVIDENCE 
380 
The research base reviewed by the 2015 DGAC provides clear evidence that persistent, 
381 
prevalent, preventable health problems, notably overweight and obesity, cardiovascular disease, 
382 
type 2 diabetes, and certain cancers, have adversely affected the health of the U.S. public for 
383 
decades and raise the urgency for immediate attention and bold action. Evidence points to 
384 
specific areas of current food and nutrient concerns and it pinpoints the characteristics of healthy 
385 
dietary and physical activity patterns that can reduce chronic disease risk, promote healthy 
386 
weight status, and foster good health across the lifespan. In addition, research evidence is 
387 
converging to show that healthy dietary patterns also are more sustainable and associated with 
388 
more favorable health as well as environmental outcomes. 
389 

 
Part A. Executive Summary 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
11 
 
 
390 
Effective models of “what works” to promote lifestyle behavior change exist. While they can be 
391 
improved, especially in terms of our capacity for scaling-up in community and health care 
392 
settings, the evidence to date can be used to guide programs and services for individuals and 
393 
families. They also can be used to assist the public and private sectors and communities in 
394 
facilitating innovative environmental change to promote the population’s health.  
395 
 
396 
It will take concerted, bold actions on the part of individuals, families, communities, industry, 
397 
and government to achieve and maintain the healthy diet patterns and the levels of physical 
398 
activity needed to promote the health of the U.S. population. These actions will require a 
399 
paradigm shift to an environment in which population health is a national priority and where 
400 
individuals and organizations, private business, and communities work together to achieve a 
401 
population-wide “culture of health” in which healthy lifestyle choices are easy, accessible, 
402 
affordable, and normative—both at home and away from home. In such a culture, health care and 
403 
public health professionals also would embrace a new leadership role in prevention, convey the 
404 
importance of lifestyle behavior change to their patients/clients, set standards for prevention in 
405 
their own facilities, and help patients/clients in accessing evidence-based and effective nutrition 
406 
and comprehensive lifestyle services and programs. 
407 
 
408 
 
409 

Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 1 
 
Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction  
1 
The Dietary Guidelines for Americans were first released in 1980, and since that time they have 
2 
provided science-based advice on promoting health and reducing risk of major chronic diseases 
3 
through a healthy* diet and regular physical activity. Early editions of the Dietary Guidelines 
4 
focused specifically on healthy members of the public, but more recent editions also have 
5 
included those who are at increased risk of chronic disease. Future editions will continue to 
6 
evolve to address public health concerns and the nutrition needs of specific populations. For 
7 
example, the Dietary Guidelines have traditionally targeted the general public older than age 2 
8 
years, but as data continue to accumulate regarding the importance of dietary intake during 
9 
gestation and from birth on, a Federal initiative has been established to develop comprehensive 
10 
guidance for infants and toddlers from birth to 24 months and women who are pregnant. By 
11 
2020, the Dietary Guidelines for Americans will include these important populations 
12 
comprehensively.  
13 
 
14 
By law (Public Law 101-445, Title III, 7 U.S.C. 5301 et seq.) the Dietary Guidelines for 
15 
Americans is published by the Federal government every 5 years. To meet this requirement, 
16 
since the 1985 edition, the Departments have jointly appointed a Dietary Guidelines Advisory 
17 
Committee of nationally recognized experts in the field of nutrition and health to review the 
18 
scientific and medical knowledge current at the time. The 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory 
19 
Committee (DGAC) was established for the single, time-limited task of reviewing the 2010 
20 
edition of Dietary Guidelines for Americans and developing nutrition and related health 
21 
recommendations to the Federal government for its subsequent development of the 2015 edition. 
22 
This report presents these recommendations to the Secretaries of Health and Human Services and 
23 
of Agriculture for use in updating the Guidelines.  
24 
 
25 
The 2015 DGAC recognizes the importance and key function of the Guidelines in forming the 
26 
basis of Federal nutrition policy and programs. The Guidelines also provides a critical 
27 
framework for local, state, and national health promotion and disease prevention initiatives. In 
28 
addition, it provides evidence-based nutrition and physical activity strategies for use by 
29 
individuals and those who serve them in public and private settings, including public health and 
30 
social service agencies, health care and educational institutions, and business. The food industry 
31 
and retailers as well, can use the Guidelines to develop healthy food and beverage products and 
32 
offerings for consumers.  
33 
 
34 
                                                 
* Throughout this report, the term "healthy" is used to represent the concept of "health-promoting" as 
well as to refer to foods or dietary patterns that are consistent with the Dietary Guidelines. See the 
Glossary for a definition of "health." 
 

Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 2 
 
The potential for the Guidelines to inform policy and practice is critical, given the significant 
35 
nutrition-related health issues facing the U.S. population: 
36 
 Overweight, obesity, and other diet-related chronic diseases (particularly 
37 
cardiovascular diseases, type 2 diabetes, and certain cancers), as well as less common but 
38 
important health outcomes, such as bone health, for which nutrition plays an important 
39 
role. These conditions are prevalent across the entire U.S. population, but are more 
40 
pronounced in low-income populations, creating critical health disparities that must be 
41 
addressed. 
42 
 Less than optimal dietary patterns in the United States, which contribute directly to 
43 
poor population health and high chronic disease risk. On average, current dietary patterns 
44 
are too low in vegetables, fruit, whole grains, and low-fat dairy, and too high in refined 
45 
grains, saturated fat, added sugars, and sodium.      
46 
 Food insecurity, a condition in which the availability of nutritionally adequate foods, or 
47 
the ability to acquire acceptable foods in socially acceptable ways, is limited or uncertain. 
48 
More than 49 million people in the United States, including nearly 9 million children, live 
49 
in food insecure households. 
50 
The economic and social costs of obesity and other diet- and physical activity-related chronic 
51 
disease conditions are enormous and will continue to escalate if current trends are not reversed. 
52 
Therefore, improving diet and physical activity in the population and addressing food insecurity 
53 
and health disparities have great potential to not only reduce the burden of chronic disease 
54 
morbidity and mortality, but also to reduce health care costs.  
55 
 
56 
The DGAC recognized that a dynamic interplay exists among individuals’ nutrition, physical 
57 
activity, and other health-related lifestyle behaviors and their environmental and social contexts. 
58 
Acknowledging this, the DGAC created a conceptual model based in part on the socio-ecological 
59 
model to serve as an organizing framework for its report (Figure B2.1). The figure shows how 
60 
these personal, social, organizational, and environmental contexts and systems interact 
61 
powerfully to influence individuals’ diet and physical activity behaviors and patterns and how 
62 
diverse health outcomes result from this dynamic interplay. An accompanying table expands on 
63 
the figure by listing specific factors that comprise each of the “Determinants” and “Outcomes” 
64 
circles. The table distinguishes those factors that are addressed in the DGAC report from related 
65 
factors that are important but beyond the scope of the report (see Table B2.1 at the end of this 
66 
chapter). 
67 
 
68 
69 

Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 3 
 
Figure B2.1 
70 
71 

Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 4 
 
REVIEWING THE EVIDENCE 
72 
 
73 
Drawing from this conceptual model, the 2015 DGAC reviewed an extensive and diverse body 
74 
of scientific literature to address many research questions. For each of its questions, the 
75 
Committee used a rigorous, evidence-based process to develop its findings. Some of the resulting 
76 
evidence was strong to moderate, and some was found to be evolving and more limited. This 
77 
graded evidence was used to draw scientific conclusion and implication statements and to make 
78 
recommendations that can be used by HHS and USDA in formulating the Dietary Guidelines for 
79 
Americans policy document.  
80 
 
81 
The DGAC used the findings from its evidence reviews to develop a series of chapters that build 
82 
on and complement each other:    
83 
 
Chapter 1 examines current status and trends in food consumption, nutrient intakes, and 
84 
eating behaviors and rates and patterns of major nutrition-related health problems. It 
85 
identifies the nutrients of public health concern and characterizes several dietary patterns that 
86 
are consistent with those associated with positive health outcomes. 
87 
 
Chapter 2 considers relationships between dietary patterns and health outcomes and 
88 
identifies a number of commonalities across patterns, particularly food groups, associated 
89 
with positive health outcomes. It examines these relationships for major chronic diseases 
90 
(cardiovascular diseases, type 2 diabetes, overweight and obesity, and certain cancers), and 
91 
also evaluates several less common, but important, outcomes (bone health, neurological and 
92 
psychological illnesses, congenital anomalies). Where possible, evidence on the impact of 
93 
dietary or comprehensive lifestyle interventions (including diet, physical activity, and 
94 
behavioral strategies) in reducing chronic disease risk outcomes is summarized and can be 
95 
used to inform health promotion and disease prevention strategies at individual and 
96 
population levels. 
97 
 
Chapter 3 reviews characteristics associated with individual dietary and lifestyle behaviors, 
98 
such as meal patterns at home and away from home, acculturation, household food 
99 
insecurity, and sedentary behaviors. It also assesses methods that are effective in  helping 
100 
individuals improve their diet and physical activity behaviors and in enhancing behavioral 
101 
interventions.  
102 
 
Chapter 4 assesses the roles of food environments and settings in promoting or hindering 
103 
healthy eating behaviors of specific population groups (such as pre-school and school-age 
104 
children and adults in the workplace) and evaluates evidence on effective methods and best 
105 
practices to promote population behavior change in communities as well as public and 
106 
private settings to influence and improve health.  
107 
 
Chapter 5 focuses on secure and sustainable diets by examining how dietary guidance and 
108 
food intake influence our capacity to meet the nutrition needs of the U.S. population now and 
109 

Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 5 
 
in the future. The chapter also examines issues related to food safety behaviors in the home 
110 
environment and evaluates new topics of food safety concern, including the safety of 
111 
coffee/caffeine and aspartame.  
112 
 
Chapter 6 considers topics of continuing public health importance that are relevant for 
113 
topics across Chapters 1 through 5 and, are therefore addressed together in this chapter— 
114 
sodium, saturated fat, added sugars, and low-calorie sweeteners.  
115 
 
Chapter 7 discusses the important role that physical activity plays in promoting health.  
116 
 
117 
 
118 
FROM THE 2015 DGAC ADVISORY REPORT TO THE DIETARY 
119 
GUIDELINES FOR AMERICANS  
120 
A major goal of the 2015 DGAC is to summarize and synthesize the evidence to support USDA 
121 
and HHS in developing nutrition recommendations that reduce the risk of chronic disease while 
122 
meeting nutrient requirements and promoting health of  the U.S. population ages 2 years and 
123 
older.  
124 
 
125 
The U.S. Government uses the Dietary Guidelines as the basis of its food assistance programs, 
126 
nutrition education efforts, and decisions about national health objectives. For example, the 
127 
National School Lunch Program and the Elder Nutrition Program incorporate the Dietary 
128 
Guidelines in menu planning; the Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, 
129 
and Children (WIC) applies the Dietary Guidelines in its educational materials; and the Healthy 
130 
People 2020 objectives for the Nation include objectives based on the Dietary Guidelines.  
131 
 
132 
The evidence described here in the 2015 DGAC Report, which will be used to develop the 2015 
133 
Dietary Guidelines for Americans, will help policymakers, educators, clinicians, and others 
134 
speak with one voice on nutrition and health and reduce the confusion caused by mixed 
135 
messages in the media. The DGAC hopes that the 2015 Dietary Guidelines for Americans will 
136 
encourage the food industry and retailers to grow, manufacture, and sell foods that promote 
137 
health and contribute to appropriate energy balance.  
138 
 
139 
In reviewing the evidence on effective interventions and best practices at individual and 
140 
population levels, the 2015 DGAC hopes that the 2015 Dietary Guidelines for Americans will 
141 
also lead to the bold actions needed to transform our health care and public health systems, 
142 
communities, and businesses. A concerted and collaborative focus on prevention is needed and 
143 
the report provides a foundation of research evidence to help create a national “culture of health” 
144 
where healthy lifestyles are easier to achieve and normative. Finally, the 2015 DGAC desires 
145 
that its evidence on healthy dietary patterns, which have been found to be important in reducing 
146 
disease risk and in promoting food security and sustainability in the near- and long-term, will 
147 

Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 6 
 
lead to changes in individual eating behaviors and to systems-wide changes that can help to 
148 
secure a healthy future for the U.S. population.  
149 
 
150 
A GUIDE TO THE 2015 DGAC REPORT 
151 
This Report contains several major sections. Part A provides an Executive Summary to the 
152 
Report. Part B sets the stage for the Report through this Introduction. A second chapter in this 
153 
section provides an integration of major findings as well as specific recommendations for how 
154 
the Report’s evidence-based dietary recommendations can be put into action at the individual, 
155 
community, and population levels.  
156 
 
157 
Part C describes the methodology the DGAC used to conduct its work and review the evidence 
158 
on diet and health. Part D is the Science Base and contains the chapters described above.  
159 
 
160 
The Report concludes with a number of Appendices, including a compilation of the Committee’s 
161 
research recommendations; several appendices describing sources of evidence the Committee 
162 
used in its reviews; a glossary; a brief history of the Dietary Guidelines for Americans; a 
163 
summary of the process used to collect public comments; biographical sketches of DGAC 
164 
members; a list of DGAC Working Group, Subcommittee, and Working/Writing Group 
165 
members; and Acknowledgments.  
166

The central portion of the Conceptual Model represents the concept that the combination of
