including tobacco status and use, stress and its management, medical treatment and management, medication use, and addiction. 
 
 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Part B. Chapter 2: 2015 DGAC Themes and 
1 
Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
2 
The 2015 DGAC set out to examine a broad set of research questions in its effort to develop sound 
3 
recommendations to guide public policies aimed at promoting individual and population health. 
4 
As these efforts moved forward, it became clear that a number of important, overarching themes 
5 
were emerging and that these areas provided a solid base of evidence for the Committee’s 
6 
recommendations. In this chapter, we summarize these themes and put forth our overall 
7 
recommendations to the Secretaries of Health and Human Services and Agriculture.  
8 
 
9 
DGAC 2015 OVERARCHING THEMES 
10 
 The Problem. About half of all American adults—117 million individuals—have one or 
11 
more preventable, chronic diseases that are related to poor quality dietary patterns and 
12 
physical inactivity, including cardiovascular disease, hypertension, type 2 diabetes and diet-
13 
related cancers.1 More than two-thirds of adults and nearly one-third of children and youth 
14 
are overweight or obese, further exacerbating poor health profiles and increasing risks for 
15 
chronic diseases and their co-morbidities.2,3 High chronic disease rates and elevated 
16 
population disease risk profiles have persisted for more than two decades and 
17 
disproportionately affect low-income and underserved communities. These diseases focus the 
18 
attention of the U.S. health care system on disease treatment rather than prevention; increase 
19 
already strained health care costs; and reduce overall population health, quality of life, and 
20 
national productivity. Other less common, but important, diet- and lifestyle-related health 
21 
problems, including poor bone health and certain neuropsychological disorders and 
22 
congenital anomalies, pose further serious concerns.  
23 
 The Gap. The dietary patterns of the American public are suboptimal and are causally 
24 
related to poor individual and population health and higher chronic disease rates. Few, if any, 
25 
improvements in consumers’ food choices have been seen in recent decades. On average, the 
26 
U.S. diet is low in vegetables, fruit, and whole grains, and high in sodium, calories, saturated 
27 
fat, refined grains, and added sugars. Underconsumption of the essential nutrients vitamin D, 
28 
calcium, potassium, and fiber are public health concerns for the majority of the U.S. 
29 
population, and iron intake is of concern among adolescents and premenopausal females. 
30 
Health disparities exist in population access to affordable healthy foods. Eating behaviors of 
31 
individuals are shaped by complex but modifiable factors, including individual, personal, 
32 
household, social/cultural, community/environmental, systems/sectorial and policy-level 
33 
factors (see the 2015 DGAC conceptual model in Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction). 
34 
However, a dynamic and rapidly evolving food environment epitomized by the abundance of 
35 
highly processed, convenient, lower-cost, energy-dense, nutrient-poor foods makes it 
36 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
particularly challenging to implement health promoting diet-related behavior changes at 
37 
individual and population levels.  
38 
 The Dietary Patterns. Current research provides evidence of moderate to strong links 
39 
between healthy dietary patterns, lower risks of obesity and chronic diseases, particularly 
40 
cardiovascular disease, hypertension, type 2 diabetes and certain cancers. Emerging evidence 
41 
also suggests that relationships may exist between dietary patterns and some neurocognitive 
42 
disorders and congenital anomalies. The overall body of evidence examined by the 2015 
43 
DGAC identifies that a healthy dietary pattern is higher in vegetables, fruits, whole grains, 
44 
low- or non-fat dairy, seafood, legumes, and nuts; moderate in alcohol (among adults); 
45 
lower in red and processed meats;i and low in sugar-sweetened foods and drinks and 
46 
refined grains. Additional strong evidence shows that it is not necessary to eliminate food 
47 
groups or conform to a single dietary pattern to achieve healthy dietary patterns. Rather, 
48 
individuals can combine foods in a variety of flexible ways to achieve healthy dietary 
49 
patterns, and these strategies should be tailored to meet the individual’s health needs, dietary 
50 
preferences and cultural traditions. Current research also strongly demonstrates that regular 
51 
physical activity promotes health and reduces chronic disease risk.  
52 
 The Individual. Sound tools and resources, like the Dietary Guidelines for Americans and 
53 
the Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, can help individuals achieve healthy diet and 
54 
physical activity patterns. Moderate to strong evidence also demonstrates that dietary 
55 
interventions implemented by nutrition professionals and individual or small-group 
56 
comprehensive lifestyle interventions that target diet and physical activity and are led by 
57 
multidisciplinary professional teams provide optimal results in chronic disease risk reduction, 
58 
weight loss, and weight loss maintenance. Additional evidence indicates that individuals can 
59 
be helped in their intentions to implement healthy lifestyles by targeting specific eating and 
60 
physical activity behaviors (e.g., meal patterns, cooking and preparation techniques, 
61 
family/household meal experiences, reducing sedentary behaviors in adults and youth, 
62 
reducing screen time in children). Sound behavioral interventions involve engaging 
63 
individuals actively in the behavior change process, using traditional face-to-face or small 
64 
group strategies and new technological approaches (websites and mobile/telephone 
65 
technology), by providing intensive, long-term professional interventions as appropriate, and 
66 
by monitoring and offering feedback on sustainable behavioral change and maintenance 
67 
strategies over time.  
68 
 The Population. Moderate to strong evidence shows that targeted environmental and policy 
69 
changes and standards are effective in changing diet and physical activity behaviors and 
70 
achieving positive health impact in children, adolescents, and adults. Research from early 
71 
child care settings, schools, and worksites demonstrate that policy changes, particularly when 
72 
                                                 
i As lean meats were not consistently defined or handled similarly between studies, they were not identified as a 
common characteristic across the reviews. However, as demonstrated in the food pattern modeling of the Healthy 
U.S.-style and Healthy Mediterranean-style patterns, lean meats can be a part of a healthy dietary pattern. 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
3 
 
combined with multi-faceted programs (e.g., nutrition educational initiatives, parent 
73 
engagement, food labeling, nutrition standards, nutrition and behavioral intervention 
74 
services) can increase healthy food choices and overall dietary quality, and improve weight 
75 
outcomes. Population approaches that engage parents and families, as appropriate, involve 
76 
collaborations across systems and sectors (e.g., schools, food retail, health care institutions 
77 
and providers, and health insurers), and mobilize public-private partnerships to provide 
78 
effective models for producing synergistic effects on diet, physical activity, and health-
79 
related outcomes.  
80 
 The Long-term View. The 2015 DGAC also examined the near- and long-term 
81 
sustainability of healthy dietary patterns as well as the safety of certain key dietary 
82 
constituents (i.e., caffeine and aspartame). Quantitative modeling research showed how 
83 
healthy dietary patterns relate to positive environmental outcomes that improve population 
84 
food security. Moderate to strong evidence demonstrates that healthy dietary patterns that are 
85 
higher in plant-based foods, such as vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and 
86 
seeds, and lower in calories and animal-based foods are associated with more favorable 
87 
environmental outcomes (lower greenhouse gas emissions and more favorable land, water, 
88 
and energy use) than are current U.S. dietary patterns. Furthermore, sustainable dietary 
89 
patterns can be achieved through a variety of approaches consistent with the Dietary 
90 
Guidelines for Americans and, therefore, offer individuals many options and new 
91 
opportunities to align with personal and population health and environmental values systems. 
92 
Healthy, sustainable dietary patterns also may provide new themes for consumer education 
93 
and communication on lifestyle practices that can promote food security now and for future 
94 
generations and create a “culture of health” at individual and population levels.  
95 
In summary, the research base reviewed by the 2015 DGAC provides clear and consistent 
96 
evidence that persistent, prevalent, preventable health problems, notably overweight and obesity, 
97 
cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, and certain cancers, have severely and adversely affected the 
98 
health of the U.S. population across all stages of the lifespan for decades and raise the urgency 
99 
for immediate attention and bold action. Evidence points to specific areas of food and nutrient 
100 
concern in the current U.S. diet. Moderate to strong evidence pinpoints the characteristics of 
101 
healthy dietary and physical activity patterns established to reduce chronic disease risk, prevent 
102 
and better manage overweight and obesity, and promote health and well-being across the 
103 
lifespan.  
104 
 
105 
Although behavior change is complex, moderate to strong evidence now points to effective 
106 
strategies to promote healthy lifestyle behavior changes at individual and population levels. This 
107 
overall research evidence base can be used to inform policy changes, multi-sectorial 
108 
collaborations, as well as product/service reformulation as needed. It can be used with 
109 
confidence to provide guidelines and standards for nutrition and lifestyle intervention 
110 
services/programs in traditional health care and public health settings. It also provides 
111 
frameworks for public and private sector initiatives and community programming to make 
112 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
4 
 
innovative environmental changes that can change population diet and physical activity 
113 
behaviors to promote population health.  
114 
 
115 
Overall, the evidence base on the links between diet, physical activity, and health has never been 
116 
as strong or more compelling. The strength of evidence on “what works” to improve individual 
117 
and population lifestyle behaviors for health also has never been more robust, with solutions and 
118 
models of “best practices.” Furthermore, the increasing convergence of research evidence 
119 
showing that healthy dietary patterns not only reduce disease risks and improve health outcomes 
120 
but are associated with food security and sustainability provide a further, convincing rationale for 
121 
focused attention on prevention and individual and population health promotion. Additional 
122 
research must be conducted to strengthen this evidence base, and recommendations for such 
123 
research are made in each of the chapters in Part D. Science Base (see Appendix E-1: Needs for 
124 
Future Research for a compilation of the DGAC’s research recommendations). 
125 
 
126 
 
127 
DGAC 2015 RECOMMENDATIONS FOR ACTION 
128 
It will take concerted, bold action on the part of individuals, families, communities, industry, and 
129 
government to achieve and maintain healthy dietary patterns and the levels of physical activity 
130 
needed to promote a healthy U.S. population.  
131 
  
132 
This will entail dramatic paradigm shifts in which population health is a national priority and 
133 
individuals, communities, and the public and private sectors seek together to achieve a 
134 
population-wide “culture of health” through which healthy lifestyle choices are easy, accessible, 
135 
affordable and normative—both at home and away from home. In such a culture, preventing 
136 
diet- and physical activity-related diseases and health problems would be much more highly 
137 
valued, the resources and services needed to achieve and maintain health would become a 
138 
realized human right across all population strata, the needs and preferences of the individual 
139 
would be seriously considered, and individuals and their families/households would be actively 
140 
engaged in promoting their personal health and managing their preventive health services and 
141 
activities. Health care and public health professionals would embrace a new leadership role in 
142 
prevention, convey the importance of lifestyle behavior change to their patients/clients, set model 
143 
standards for prevention-oriented activities and client/employee services in their own facilities, 
144 
and manage patient/client referrals to evidence-based nutrition and comprehensive lifestyle 
145 
services and programs. Communities and relevant sectors of our economy, including food, 
146 
agriculture, private business, health care (as well as insurance), public health and education, 
147 
would seek common ground and collaborations in promoting population health. Initiatives would 
148 
be incentivized to engage communities and health care systems to create integrated and 
149 
comprehensive approaches to preventing chronic diseases and for weight management. 
150 
Environmental changes, including policy changes, improved food and beverage standards, 
151 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
5 
 
reformulation of products and services as needed, and programs that enhance population lifestyle 
152 
behavior changes and support preventive services also would be incentivized.  
153 
 
154 
Although these propositions are extremely challenging, it is imperative to seek novel and 
155 
creative, evidence-based solutions. The costs of failing to do so are the continuation of the very 
156 
high rates of preventable diet- and physical activity-related health problems we confront as a 
157 
Nation and the worsening of their serious adverse effects on our quality of life, population 
158 
productivity, and already highly strained healthcare costs. The evidence base has never been 
159 
stronger to guide solutions. What is needed are strong commitments and leadership, the 
160 
development of targeted public and private policies and partnerships, and the implementation of 
161 
evidence-based, cross-sectorial initiatives to achieve them. In the remainder of this chapter, the 
162 
DGAC summarizes specific recommendations guided by our conceptual model, which is 
163 
grounded in the socio-ecological theory model of individual and population lifestyle behavior 
164 
change for health promotion and disease prevention (see Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction).  
165 
      
166 
 
167 
Actions for Individuals and Families/Households 
168 
 Think prevention, know your lifestyle-related health risk profile, make personal goals and 
169 
commitments, and take action to promote personal and household/family health. Work with 
170 
health professionals to assess and monitor your health risks and to personalize your 
171 
preventive lifestyle behavior plan of action.  
172 
 Know and understand how to modify your diet and physical activity to reduce personal and 
173 
family member health risks. Know your current dietary pattern, including your healthy 
174 
choices that can be maintained as well as areas for potential change. Act on this information. 
175 
Seek to make gradual and sustainable changes in your dietary behaviors to achieve one of 
176 
several sound healthy dietary pattern options (e.g., Healthy U.S.-style Pattern, the Healthy 
177 
Mediterranean-style Pattern, or the Healthy Vegetarian Pattern; see Part D. Chapter 1: Food 
178 
and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends). For most people, this will 
179 
mean: 
180 
o Improving food and menu choices, modifying recipes (including mixed dishes and 
181 
sandwiches), and watching portion sizes.  
182 
o Including more vegetables (without added salt or fat), fruits (without added sugars), 
183 
whole grains, seafood, nuts, legumes, low/non-fat dairy or dairy alternatives (without 
184 
added sugars). 
185 
o Reducing consumption of red and processed meat, refined grains, added sugars, sodium, 
186 
and saturated fat; substituting saturated fats with polyunsaturated alternatives; and 
187 
replacing solid animal fats with non-tropical vegetable oils and nuts.  
188 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
6 
 
 The 2015 DGAC advocates achieving healthy dietary patterns through healthy food and 
189 
beverage choices rather than with nutrient or dietary supplements except as needed.  
190 
 Use available Dietary Guidelines for Americans tools and other sound resources to initiate 
191 
positive personal lifestyle changes to improve dietary and physical activity behaviors, 
192 
including goal setting and self-monitoring. 
193 
o As needed, seek regular advice from qualified health care providers to establish a 
194 
personalized plan for prevention that includes steps to adopt healthy dietary patterns and 
195 
physical activity. As appropriate, engage with nutrition and health professionals to 
196 
address personal health risks that can be lowered with sound diet and physical activity, or 
197 
participate in comprehensive lifestyle interventions conducted by trained interventionists 
198 
(registered dietitians/nutritionists, exercise and behavioral specialists).  
199 
o Achieve and maintain a healthy weight. Know your level of obesity risk. Know your 
200 
energy needs and how they change with varying levels of physical activity. Take personal 
201 
action for obesity prevention or weight loss management, as needed, using sound, 
202 
evidence-based tools and resources. Seek to achieve a dietary pattern consistent with the 
203 
Dietary Guidelines for Americans, recognizing that many evidence-based options can 
204 
facilitate weight loss and weight loss maintenance. As appropriate, work with qualified 
205 
nutrition professionals and health providers to create a personalized plan of action for 
206 
obesity prevention. When needed, engage in intensive, long-term nutrition counseling or 
207 
comprehensive lifestyle intervention strategies to achieve maximal, long-term weight loss 
208 
and weight maintenance results.  
209 
o Ensure at home and in public settings, such as schools and early child care programs, that 
210 
young children achieve a high-quality dietary pattern and level of physical activity. 
211 
Encourage their active participation in food experiences and activity choices so that the 
212 
importance of dietary quality and physical activity are reinforced, and healthy lifestyle 
213 
behaviors become normative, habitual, and easier to maintain through adolescence and 
214 
lifelong. 
215 
o Follow on a regular basis, the Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans. Engage in at 
216 
least 2.5 hours a week of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity, such as brisk 
217 
walking, or 1.25 hours a week of vigorous-intensity aerobic physical activity. For weight 
218 
control, at least 1 hour a day of moderate- to vigorous-intensity physical activity may be 
219 
required. Engage children in at least 1 hour a day of moderate- to vigorous-intensity 
220 
physical activity each day. Limit children’s screen time to no more than two hours per 
221 
day. Adults should limit sedentary activity and replace it with aerobic and strengthening 
222 
exercises. As needed, engage with qualified professionals in comprehensive lifestyle 
223 
interventions to achieve maximal impact on healthy dietary and physical activity patterns 
224 
and health outcomes. Get enough sleep! 
225 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
7 
 
o Seek and demand the creation and maintenance of food and physical activity 
226 
environments and resources in your community and in local public, private and retail 
227 
settings so as to promote a “culture of health.” These are strongly needed to facilitate the 
228 
ease of initiating and meeting the U.S. Dietary Guidelines recommendations at home and 
229 
away from home. 
230 
 
231 
Actions for Communities and Populations 
232 
 Aim to make healthy lifestyles and prevention a national and local priority and reality.  
233 
o Create public and private policy changes at the national level that direct and incentivize 
234 
collaborations by multiple sectors of influence, including health care, public health, 
235 
education, food and agriculture, transportation, food retail, the media, non-governmental 
236 
organizations, and service sectors.  
237 
o Incentivize the development of policies and initiatives at local, state, and Federal levels 
238 
that are carried out using cross-sectorial collaborations to promote individual healthy 
239 
lifestyle behavior changes and create community “cultures of health.” These may include 
240 
improvements in built and physical environments to create safe and accessible resources 
241 
and settings for increased physical activity and more widely available healthy food 
242 
choices. They may entail changes in policies, standards, and practices in retail, and public 
243 
and private settings and programs that promote “cultures of health” and facilitate the 
244 
initiation and maintenance of healthy lifestyle behaviors at individual and community 
245 
levels.  
246 
 Seek a paradigm shift in health care and public health toward a greater focus on prevention 
247 
and integration with food systems.  
248 
o Incentivize and support nutrition professionals, health care providers, and other qualified 
249 
professionals in their unique roles of encouraging and counseling patients and clients to 
250 
adopt healthy dietary and physical activity and in offering evidence-based nutrition 
251 
services and comprehensive lifestyle interventions. Integrate preventive lifestyle 
252 
screening, referral, and interventions and services for weight management and chronic 
253 
disease risk reduction into routine practice guidelines and quality assurance standards.  
254 
o Support health care facilities, such as hospitals and clinics, in seeking to model 
255 
prevention and achieving “cultures of health” by offering healthy food choices for 
256 
patients, visitors, and staff; implementing preventive nutrition services and 
257 
comprehensive lifestyle intervention programs; and making referrals to Federal and local 
258 
food assistance programs as needed by their staff and clients.  
259 
o Require health insurance providers to use financial and other positive incentives to 
260 
encourage and motivate health care settings and businesses to support individuals in 
261 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
8 
 
adopting healthy behaviors and engaging, as appropriate, in nutrition and exercise 
262 
counseling and comprehensive lifestyle behavior interventions. 
263 
o Encourage and incentivize health care innovations and community prevention through 
264 
Affordable Care Act (ACA) policies and programs, including expanding preventive 
265 
lifestyle services in traditional health services environments and new retail health 
266 
services environments that link to Federal and local food assistance programs. These 
267 
should provide resources for individuals to engage and sustain personal lifestyle behavior 
268 
change. In addition, ACA programs and policies should increase access to qualified 
269 
professionals and programs and services that promote healthy diet and physical activity 
270 
behaviors. 
271 
o Incentivize businesses to establish employee health benefits plans that include access to 
272 
resources and services that encourage personal health promotion and healthy lifestyle 
273 
behavior changes. Support employers in using positive motivation strategies to realize 
274 
these changes.  
275 
 
276 
 Establish healthy food environments.  
277 
o Establish local, state, and Federal policies to make healthy foods accessible and 
278 
affordable and to limit access to high-calorie, nutrient-poor foods and sugar-sweetened 
279 
beverages in public buildings and facilities. Set nutrition standards for foods and 
280 
beverages offered in public places. Improve retail food environments and make healthy 
281 
foods accessible and affordable in underserved neighborhoods and communities.  
282 
o Develop and expand programs that encourage healthy eating and physical activity habits 
283 
in young children and adolescents within school and early care and other education 
284 
settings. Establish and implement policies and programs that provide nutritious foods, 
285 
limit sugar-sweetened beverages and other unhealthy foods, incorporate nutrition 
286 
curricula and experiences and physical activity opportunities, and increase provider and 
287 
teacher skills to develop and promote these programs. 
288 
o Implement the comprehensive school meal guidelines (National School Lunch Program) 
289 
from the USDA that increase intakes of vegetables (without added salt), fruits (without 
290 
added sugars), and whole grains; limit sodium, added sugars, saturated fat, and trans fat; 
291 
limit marketing unhealthy foods to children; make drinking water freely available to 
292 
students throughout the day; ensure competitive foods meet the national nutrition 
293 
standards (e.g., Dietary Guidelines for Americans); and eliminate sugar-sweetened 
294 
beverages.  
295 
o Improve, standardize and implement Nutrition Facts labels and Front of Package labels to 
296 
help consumers, including those with low literacy levels, make healthy food choices. The 
297 
Nutrition Facts label should include added sugars (in grams and teaspoons) and include a 
298 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
9 
 
percent daily value, to assist consumers in identifying the amount of added sugars in 
299 
foods and beverages and making informed dietary decisions. Standardize and create easy-
300 
to-understand front-of-package (FOP) label on all food and beverage products to give 
301 
clear guidance about a food’s healthfulness. An example is the FOP label recommended 
302 
by the Institute of Medicine, which included calories, and 0 to 3 “nutritional” points for 
303 
added sugars, saturated fat, and sodium. This would be integrated with the Nutrition Facts 
304 
label, allowing consumers to quickly and easily identify nutrients of concern for 
305 
overconsumption, in order to make healthy choices. 
306 
o Align nutritional and agricultural policies with Dietary Guidelines recommendations and 
307 
make broad policy changes to transform the food system so as to promote population 
308 
health, including the use of economic and taxing policies to encourage the production and 
309 
consumption of healthy foods and to reduce unhealthy foods. For example, earmark tax 
310 
revenues from sugar-sweetened beverages, snack foods and desserts high in calories, 
311 
added sugars, or sodium, and other less healthy foods for nutrition education initiatives 
312 
and obesity prevention programs. 
313 
o Align food assistance programs such as SNAP and WIC with the Dietary Guidelines for 
314 
Americans. Provide standards for purchasing that create new demands for healthy foods, 
315 
such as vegetables and fruits, and discourage the purchase and consumption of foods, 
316 
such as sugar-sweetened beverages. Support research to explore ways to improve overall 
317 
diet quality in Federal and local food assistance programs. 
318 
o Support changes to the food environment that can help individuals make healthy choices 
319 
in the foods they consume away from home and those they purchase away from home to 
320 
consume at home. For example, the Committee encourages the food industry to continue 
321 
to reformulate and make changes to improve the nutrition profile of certain foods. 
322 
Examples of such actions include lowering sodium and added sugars content, achieving 
323 
better saturated fat to polyunsaturated fat ratio, and reducing portion sizes in retail 
324 
settings (restaurants, food outlets, and public venues, such as professional sports stadiums 
325 
and arenas). The Committee also encourages the food industry to market these improved 
326 
products to consumers. 
327 
o Implement policies and programs at local, state and national levels in both the public and 
328 
private sectors to reduce added sugars and sodium in foods, limit availability of sugar-
329 
sweetened beverages, and promote healthy snacks. Approaches might include: 
330 
 Making water a preferred beverage choice. Encourage water as a preferred beverage 
331 
when thirsty. Make water accessible in public settings, child care facilities, schools, 
332 
worksites and other community places where beverages are offered.  
333 
 Reducing added sugars in foods and sugar-sweetened beverages in school meals. 
334 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
10 
 
 Making “smart snacks” consistent with the Dietary Guidelines in schools, child care 
335 
settings, parks, recreation centers, sports leagues, after-school programs, worksites, 
336 
colleges and universities, healthcare, and other community settings. 
337 
 Implementing policies that limit exposure and marketing of foods and beverages high 
338 
in added sugars and sodium to all age groups, particularly children and adolescents. 
339 
 Implementing economic and pricing approaches to promote the purchase of healthy 
340 
foods and beverages. For example, taxation on higher sugar-and sodium-containing 
341 
foods may encourage consumers to reduce consumption and revenues generated 
342 
could support health promotion efforts. Alternatively, price incentives on vegetables 
343 
and fruits could be used to promote consumption and public health benefits. 
344 
 Mounting public education campaigns to increase the public’s awareness of the health 
345 
effects of excess added sugars, sodium, saturated fat, and calories. 
346 
 Support and expand access to healthy built environments and advocate wide community use. 
347 
o Increase opportunities for regular public engagement in physical activity through 
348 
improved urban and community designs, enhanced community built environments, 
349 
business spaces, and transportation networks. Urban and community designs should 
350 
encourage and promote active transportation, such as walking and biking. Green 
351 
corridors can increase public safety and enhance active transportation.  
352 
o Incentivize communities to make physical activity accessible, affordable, and safe. 
353 
Encourage public and private sectors to work together to increase access to gyms, bike 
354 
trails, pedestrian walkways, ball fields, and other recreation areas in the communities. 
355 
Promote physical activity through social media, smart phone, and other technologies. 
356 
o Reach out to and engage groups such as new immigrant communities who may abandon 
357 
their native healthy lifestyle habits and others at highest nutritional and health risk, to 
358 
ensure that they learn about resources and are motivated to access, engage in, and sustain 
359 
healthy dietary patterns and physical activities within their cultural preferences. 
360 
 Maintain strong support for Federal food and nutrition programs.  
361 
o Recognize their importance in creating demand for healthy food products as well as in 
362 
shaping and modeling consumer behaviors relating to healthy dietary and physical 
363 
activity patterns.  
364 
o Align program standards with the Dietary Guidelines for Americans so as to achieve the 
365 
2015 DGAC recommendations and promote a “culture of health.” 
366 
 Recognize and place priority on moving toward a more sustainable diet consistent with the 
367 
healthy dietary pattern options described in this DGAC report. Access to sufficient, 
368 
nutritious, and safe food is an essential element of food security for the U.S. population. A 
369 

 
Part B. Chapter 2: Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
11 
 
sustainable diet helps ensure this access for both the current population and future 
370 
generations.  
371 
o Enhance what is already being done by the private and public sectors to improve 
372 
environmental policies and practices around production, processing, and distribution 
373 
within individual food categories.  
374 
o Align local, state, and national practices and policies across sectors to promote a 
375 
sustainable and safe food supply to ensure long-term food security. Support robust 
376 
private and public sector partnerships, practices, and policies across the supply chain and 
377 
extending from farms to distribution and consumption that can incentivize actions to 
378 
develop a food system that embraces a core set of values that embody healthy, safe, and 
379 
sustainable dietary patterns. Monitor, evaluate, and reward sectors that do this. Establish 
380 
new, well-coordinated policies that include, but are not limited to, agriculture, economics, 
381 
transportation, energy, water use, and dietary guidance. Encourage all participants in the 
382 
food system, as they are central to creating and supporting sustainable and safe diets.  
383 
o Shift toward a greater emphasis on healthy dietary patterns and an improved 
384 
environmental profile across food categories to maximize environmental sustainability, 
385 
including encouraging consumption of a variety of wild caught or farmed seafood.  
386 
o Improve the nutrient profiles of certain farmed seafood species, particularly EPA and 
387

388

389

390 
o Offer consumer-friendly information that facilitates understanding the environmental 
391

392 
o Recognize the importance of foodborne illness prevention and encourage consumer 
393

394 
Guidelines for Americans—Clean, Separate, Cook, and Chill, which are the foundation of 
395 
the Fight BAC!® campaign (www.fightbac.org).
