197 
METHODOLOGY 
198 
To address questions on the current status and trends in food and nutrient intakes, the prevalence of 
199 
diet-related chronic diseases in the U.S. population, and the composition of healthful dietary patterns, 
200 
the DGAC relied on analysis of data from several sources and food pattern modeling analyses. Many 
201 
of the questions relied on analysis of data from What We Eat in America (WWEIA), the dietary 
202 
component of the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES), using either existing 
203 
data tables or new analyses conducted by the Data Analysis Team (DAT) upon request of the DGAC 
204 
(see Part C. Methodology, Data Analyses section, and Appendix E-4: NHANES Data Used in DGAC 
205 
Data Analyses). Existing data tables were used when available to answer questions about nutrient 
206 
intake, food group intake, and meal and snack consumption. In some cases, new analyses were 
207 
conducted by DAT agencies to provide additional information on food or nutrient intake, for example, 
208 
by specific population groups, such as pregnant women, or information on potential overconsumption 
209 
of nutrients when supplement intake is considered. New WWEIA/NHANES data analyses also were 
210 
used to answer questions about food category intakes, the energy content and nutrient density of foods 
211 
by point of purchase and location of consumption, and the food choices of self-identified vegetarians. 
212 
 
213 
Data from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) NHANES data tables and from 
214 
the peer-reviewed literature, also were the source of information on prevalence of health conditions, 
215 
including body weight status, lipid profiles, high blood pressure, and diabetes. In addition, NHANES 
216 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
7 
 
data on biochemical indicators of diet and nutrition in the U.S. population were used to help determine 
217 
nutrients that may be of public health concern. To supplement data from NHANES, additional data 
218 
sources were drawn upon to answer questions on the prevalence of health conditions, including the 
219 
National Health Interview Survey, the National Cancer Institute’s Surveillance Epidemiology and End 
220 
Results (SEER) cancer registry statistics, SEARCH for Diabetes in Youth Study (SEARCH), and heart 
221 
disease and stroke statistics from the 2014 report of the American Heart Association.6 
222 
 
223 
Some of the questions posed by the DGAC were best addressed by Food Pattern Modeling (see Part C. 
224 
Methodology, Special Analyses Using the USDA Food Patterns section). These included questions 
225 
about the nutrient adequacy of the USDA Food Patterns, modifications of the patterns for specific 
226 
population groups or to meet specific nutrient targets, and the nutrients provided by various food 
227 
groups in the Patterns. In some cases, questions could be answered with modeling analyses that had 
228 
been conducted for the 2005 or 2010 DGACs, and so the results of these analyses were brought 
229 
forward. The modeling process also was used to develop new USDA Food Patterns based on different 
230 
types of evidence: Healthy Vegetarian Patterns that take into account food choices of self-identified 
231 
vegetarians, and Healthy Mediterranean-style Patterns that take into account food group intakes from 
232 
studies using a Med-diet index to assess dietary patterns. The latter were compiled and summarized to 
233 
answer the questions addressed on dietary patterns composition. The food group content of dietary 
234 
patterns reviewed by the DGAC and found to have health benefits formed the basis for answering these 
235 
questions. WWEIA food group intakes and USDA Food Pattern recommendations were compared 
236 
with the food group intake data from the healthy dietary patterns as part of the answer for these 
237 
questions. 
238 
 
239 
The DGAC took the strengths and limitations of data analyses into account in formulating conclusion 
240 
statements. The grading rubric used for questions answered using NEL systematic reviews do not 
241 
apply to questions answered using data analyses. Therefore, these conclusions were not graded. 
242 
 
243 
 
244 
NUTRIENT INTAKE AND NUTRIENTS OF CONCERN 
245 
An overarching premise of the DGAC is that that the Dietary Guidelines for Americans should provide 
246 
food-based guidance for obtaining the nutrients needed for optimal reproductive health, growth and 
247 
development, healthy aging, and well-being across the lifespan (ages 2 years and older). Specific 
248 
nutrient intake requirements are established for each sex and life-stage group by the Food and 
249 
Nutrition Board of the Institute of Medicine7 and as such, this DGAC report did not reevaluate IOM 
250 
recommendations or make independent specific nutrient recommendations. Rather, the DGAC 
251 
reviewed nutrient intake and biochemical measures of nutritional status and potential nutrient-related 
252 
health outcomes to identify “shortfall nutrients” and “overconsumed nutrients”, and then determined 
253 
whether these nutrients should be designated as “nutrients of public health concern.”  
254 
 
255 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
8 
 
“Shortfall nutrients” are those that may be underconsumed either across the population or in specific 
256 
groups relative to IOM-based standards, such as the Estimated Average Requirement (EAR) or the 
257 
Adequate Intake (AI). The EAR is the best measure of population adequacy of nutrient intake as is it is 
258 
“the average daily intake level estimated to meet the requirement of half of the healthy individuals in a 
259 
particular life stage and gender group.”7 p.3 The EAR is used to estimate the prevalence of inadequate 
260 
intakes within a group. The AI is “a recommended average daily nutrient intake level based on 
261 
observed or experimentally determined approximations or estimates of nutrient intake by a group (or 
262 
groups) of apparently healthy people that are assumed to be adequate—used when an RDA cannot be 
263 
determined.”7 p.3 A high prevalence of inadequate intake either across the U.S. population or in specific 
264 
groups constitutes a shortfall nutrient. 
265 
 
266 
Overconsumed nutrients are those that may be overconsumed either across the population or in specific 
267 
groups related to IOM-based standards such as the Tolerable Upper Limit of Intake (UL) or other 
268 
expert group standards. A high prevalence of excess intake either across the U.S. population or in 
269 
specific group constitutes an overconsumed nutrient. 
270 
 
271 
“Nutrients of concern” are those nutrients that may pose a substantial public health concern and the 
272 
DGAC divided them into two categories—those of concern due to overconsumption and those of 
273 
concern due to underconsumption. To be identified as a nutrient of concern, the DGAC used the 
274 
totality of evidence, evaluating data on nutrient intake and corroborating it with biochemical markers 
275 
of nutritional status, where available, and evidence for associations with health outcomes to establish 
276 
nutrients of concern.  
277 
 
278 
Designation as a nutrient of concern for either under- or overconsumption is intended to communicate 
279 
some level of risk for which the U.S. population may need to modify eating habits. Dietary guidance 
280 
can then be formulated to assist individuals in increasing or decreasing nutrients that are under- or 
281 
overconsumed. 
282 
 
283 
Question1:   What are current consumption patterns of nutrients from foods and 
284 
beverages by the U.S. population?  
285 
Source of evidence: Data analysis 
286 
 
287 
Conclusion  
288 
Nutrient intake data from a representative sample of the U.S. population ages 2 years and older 
289 
indicate that: vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E, folate, vitamin C, calcium, and magnesium are 
290 
underconsumed relative to the EAR. Iron is under-consumed by adolescent and premenopausal 
291 
females, including women who are pregnant. Potassium and fiber are underconsumed relative to the 
292 
AI. Sodium and saturated fat are overconsumed relative to the UL or other standards for maximal 
293 
intake. 
294 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
9 
 
 
295 
Implications 
296 
A dietary pattern emphasizing a variety of nutrient-dense foods will help shift individual and 
297 
population consumption toward recommended intake levels for nutrients of public health concern. 
298 
 
299 
The U.S. population should increase consumption of foods rich in vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E, 
300 
folate, vitamin C, calcium, and magnesium. Adolescent and premenopausal females should increase 
301 
consumption of foods rich in iron. Heme iron from lean meats is highly bioavailable, hence, an 
302 
excellent source.8 A diet emphasizing a variety of nutrient-dense foods will help shift consumption 
303 
toward the recommended intake levels of these shortfall nutrients. The U.S. population should increase 
304 
consumption of foods rich in potassium and fiber. A diet emphasizing a variety of nutrient-dense foods 
305 
will help ensure optimal intake of these shortfall nutrients. In particular, fruit, vegetables and whole 
306 
grains are excellent sources of vitamin A, C, folate, fiber, magnesium and potassium. The U.S. 
307 
population should make concerted and focused efforts to decrease consumption of sodium and 
308 
saturated fat.  
309 
 
310 
The USDA Food Patterns provide guidance for consumption of a nutrient-dense, energy-balanced diet. 
311 
Implementation of eating a healthy diet that is energy balanced while providing sufficient intake of 
312 
shortfall nutrients without exceeding intake of overconsumed nutrients can be achieved through a 
313 
variety of successful behavioral approaches as described in Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and 
314 
Physical Activity Behavior Change. Environmental and policy approaches are also important in 
315 
helping the U.S. population achieve a healthy diet (see also Part D. Chapter 4: Food Environment 
316 
and Settings). Federal nutrition assistance programs are a key aspect of providing critical nutrients for 
317 
growth, development and long-term health for children, those with limited income and older 
318 
Americans.  
319 
 
320 
Review of the Evidence  
321 
To determine nutritional adequacy, the DGAC used 2007-2010 NHANES/WWEIA data to examine 
322 
the intake distributions for 11 vitamins (vitamin A, vitamin B6, vitamin B12, vitamin C, vitamin D, 
323 
vitamin E, vitamin K, folate, thiamin, niacin, and riboflavin), nine minerals (calcium, copper, iron, 
324 
magnesium, phosphorous, potassium, selenium, sodium, and zinc), energy, macronutrients (total fat, 
325 
saturated fat, polyunsaturated fat [including 18:2 and 18:3], protein, carbohydrate), and other 
326 
compounds or components (fiber, carotenoids [alpha-carotene, beta-carotene, lycopene, lutein + 
327 
zeaxanthin], caffeine, cholesterol, and choline) (see Appendix E-2.1: Usual intake distributions, 2007-
328 
2010, by age/sex groups). The DGAC compared the intake estimates across the population age 
329 
distribution to the Dietary Reference Intakes. The committee used data from foods and beverages as 
330 
well as foods and beverages plus dietary supplements when supplement data were available. For 
331 
nutrients with an EAR, the DGAC considered shortfall nutrients to be those where a substantial 
332 
proportion of either the total population or specific age and sex subgroups had intake estimates below 
333 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
10 
 
the EAR. Although multiple approaches can be used to estimate the prevalence of nutrient inadequacy 
334 
in a population, the DGAC used the EAR cut point method.7 Figure D1.1 shows the percent of the U.S. 
335 
population with usual intakes below the EAR. From Figure D1.1, the DGAC determined that vitamin 
336 
D, vitamin E, magnesium, calcium, vitamin A and vitamin C were shortfall nutrients and that there 
337 
may be a high prevalence of inadequate dietary intake of these nutrients.  
338 
 
339 
Of the nutrients with an AI (vitamin K, choline, dietary fiber, and potassium), the DGAC determined 
340 
that a low proportion of the population had fiber and potassium intakes above the AI and so potassium 
341 
and fiber were therefore considered to be underconsumed (Figure D1.2).  
342 
 
343 
Sodium and saturated fat were examined as potentially overconsumed nutrients in relation to the UL 
344 
(for sodium), and the maximum level from the 2010 Dietary Guidelines of less than 10 percent of 
345 
calories from saturated fat (for saturated fat). From 63 percent to 91 percent of females and 81 percent 
346 
to 97 percent of males consumed more than the UL for sodium (Figure D1.3). From 67 percent to 92 
347 
percent of females and from 57 percent to 84 percent of males consumed more than 10 percent of 
348 
calories from saturated fat (Figure D1.4). Therefore, sodium and saturated fat were both determined to 
349 
be overconsumed by the U.S. population (see Appendix E-2.1: Usual intake distributions, 2007-2010, 
350 
by age/sex groups and Appendix E-2.2: Usual intake distributions as a percent of energy for fatty 
351 
acids and macronutrients, 2007-2010, by age/sex groups). 
352 
 
353 
The DGAC examined population intakes of specific nutrients by age, sex, race/ethnicity, pregnancy 
354 
status, and acculturation status.  
355 
 
356 
Age and Sex 
357 
In addition to the age groups shown in Figures D1.1 and D1.2, the DGAC was interested in 
358 
understanding the intake of shortfall nutrients in older adults (71 to 79 years and 80 years and older). 
359 
Calcium intake from foods and beverages did not meet the EAR for older persons, where 71 percent of 
360 
males and 81 percent of females ages 71 years and older had intakes below the EAR. For these 
361 
analyses calcium from dietary supplements was also considered. When total intake of foods + beverage 
362 
+ dietary supplements containing calcium was considered, then the proportion of the older adults 
363 
below the EAR improved to 55 percent for men and 49 percent for women over the age of 71 years. 
364 
For vitamin D intakes from food and beverages only, about 93 percent of older males and more than 97 
365 
percent of older females had intakes below the EAR. Similar to the findings for calcium, intakes 
366 
improved when considering total intake from foods and beverages plus dietary supplements. The 
367 
proportions of older adult below the EAR dropped to 52 percent for both males and females older than 
368 
71 years.  
369 
 
370 
Fiber was a shortfall nutrient for older adults, where only 4 percent of men and 13 percent of women 
371 
had a dietary intake of fiber above the AI. Potassium also was a shortfall nutrient for both older males 
372 
and females, where less than 3 percent of both groups had intakes above the AI. Use of dietary 
373 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
11 
 
supplements containing potassium did not change the proportion of the older adults with intakes above 
374 
the AI. 
375 
 
376 
Protein was not identified as a shortfall nutrient for the overall older adult population but it should be 
377 
noted that 6 percent of men older than 80 years and 11 percent of women older than 80 years old had 
378 
protein intakes that were below the protein EAR (g/kg/body weight). 
379 
 
380 
The sample size for the older participants in WWEIA 2007-2010 is small compared to other age 
381 
groupings in the survey sample and despite the excellent population weights used in the WWEIA 
382 
dataset, the estimates should be viewed with caution because of the limited sample (see Appendix E-
383 
2.3 Usual nutrient intakes for individuals age 71 years and older). 
384 
 
385 
Race/Ethnicity 
386 
The DGAC examined the shortfall nutrients by race/ethnicity using the following groups: non-
387 
Hispanic white, non-Hispanic Black, Mexican-American, and all Hispanic combined (other race/ethnic 
388 
subgroups not available). For certain shortfall nutrients, non-Hispanic whites have the highest intakes. 
389 
These include vitamin A, vitamin E, magnesium, folate, iron, potassium, vitamin D, and calcium. 
390 
Mexican-Americans have the highest intakes of fiber, while all Hispanics combined have the highest 
391 
intakes of vitamin C. Non-Hispanic Blacks have the lowest intake for most of the shortfall nutrients 
392 
(Table D1.1). We note that evaluation of intakes relative to the EAR or AI are the most appropriate for 
393 
assessment of populations, instead of the mean intakes, but for the race/ethnicity groups, only the mean 
394 
data are available. 
395 
 
396 
Pregnancy 
397 
Many of the shortfall nutrients in the general population also were shortfall nutrients among women 
398 
who are pregnant. Among this group, 26 percent were below the EAR for vitamin A intake and 30 
399 
percent had vitamin C intakes below the EAR. For vitamin D, 90 percent had intakes below the EAR 
400 
and for vitamin E, 94 percent had intakes below the EAR. Calcium intake was also low, where 24 
401 
percent had intakes below the EAR, and for folate, 29 percent had intakes below the EAR. Notably, 96 
402 
percent of women who were pregnant had iron intakes below the EAR (Table D1.2 and  Appendix E-
403 
2.4: Usual intake distributions, 2007-2010, for pregnant and non-pregnant women in the U.S. ages 
404 
19-50 years). 
405 
 
406 
Fiber was a shortfall nutrient for women who were pregnant, as only 8 percent had fiber intakes above 
407 
the AI. For potassium only 3 percent had intakes above the AI (Table D1.2). 
408 
 
409 
It is important to note that the sample size for women who were pregnant in WWEIA 2007-2010 is 
410 
very small (n=133 respondents), so the estimates should be interpreted with caution and the 
411 
generalizability of the data to all women in the United States who were pregnant is limited.  
412 
 
413 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
12 
 
Acculturation 
414 
The U.S. population is highly diverse in terms of race, ethnicity, and cultural origin. Many people 
415 
immigrate to the United States from all over the world and each comes with distinct dietary habits and 
416 
cultural beliefs about food and food patterns.9  Acculturation is defined as the process by which 
417 
immigrants adopt the attitudes, values, customs, beliefs, and behaviors of a new culture. Acculturation 
418 
is the gradual exchange between immigrants’ original attitudes and behavior and those of the host 
419 
culture. 10, 11  The DGAC appreciates that many immigrants have difficulties purchasing and preparing 
420 
foods familiar to them either because the ingredients are not available or the ingredients may be too 
421 
expensive. A large and growing body of research suggests that the extent of an individual or family’s 
422 
acculturation status may be a predictor of dietary intake and that together, diet and acculturation status 
423 
may influence health status or disease risk.9, 10, 12, 13 For this reason, the DGAC felt it was important to 
424 
examine dietary intake by acculturation status, particularly for shortfall nutrients and nutrients of 
425 
concern. Additional information on acculturation and diet appears in Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet 
426 
and Physical Activity Behavior Change. 
427 
 
428 
NHANES collects data on some of the variables that can be used to create an acculturation variable, 
429 
including whether respondents were born outside the United States in a Spanish-speaking country or 
430 
born outside the United States in a non-Spanish speaking country, their race/ethnicity, and number of 
431 
years they have resided in the United States.14 Upon reviewing the data, however, the DGAC found 
432 
that the sample size was far too small to create meaningful variables to indicate “low acculturation 
433 
status” or “high acculturation status.” The DGAC views this lack of ability to analyze the WWEIA 
434 
data by acculturation status as a limitation of the available data. It is a very important area that needs 
435 
further research, particularly when informing nutrition programs for new residents of the United States. 
436 
 
437 
Food Insecurity Status 
438 
Readers are referred to Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and Physical Activity Behavior Change 
439 
and Part D. Chapter 5: Food Sustainability and Safety for more detailed discussions of food 
440 
insecurity and food security issues. For this section of the report, the DGAC determined that it was 
441 
important to evaluate nutrient intake, particularly for the shortfall nutrients by income status, which 
442 
can be a marker of food insecurity. For these data analyses, we used the standard cutpoints of less than 
443 
131 percent of the poverty index, 131 to 185 percent of the poverty index and more than 185 percent of 
444 
the poverty index and examined calcium, potassium, fiber and vitamin D (Table D1.3). In general, 
445 
respondents (all ages 2 years and older) from households with higher income (more than 185 percent 
446 
of the poverty index) had higher intakes of calcium, potassium, fiber, and vitamin D. Notably, in some 
447 
of the very young age groups (2 to 5 years), intakes of potassium, fiber, and vitamin D were 
448 
comparable across income groups, while calcium was highest in those coming from households at the 
449 
131 to 185 percent of the poverty index ratio. It may be that many of the households of lower income 
450 
with small children are receiving important benefits from federal nutrition assistance programs, which 
451 
could be helping to generate comparability in the intake of shortfall nutrients across the income groups. 
452 
 
453 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
13

466 
Question 2:  Of the nutrients that are underconsumed or overconsumed, including 
467 
over the Tolerable Upper Limit of Intake (UL), which present a substantial public health 
468 
concern?  
469 
Source of evidence: Data analysis 
470 
 
471 
Conclusion  
472 
Nutrient intake data, together with nutritional biomarker and health outcomes data indicate that vitamin 
473 
D, calcium, potassium, and fiber are underconsumed and may pose a public health concern. Iron also is 
474 
a nutrient of public health concern for adolescent and premenopausal females.  
475 
 
476 
Nutrient intake data, together with nutritional biomarker and health outcomes data indicate that sodium 
477 
and saturated fat are overconsumed and may pose a public health concern.  
478 
 
479 
Implications 
480 
The DGAC recommends that strategies be developed and implemented at both the individual and the 
481 
population level to improve intake of nutrients of public health concern.  
482 
 
483 
Review of the Evidence  
484 
These conclusions were reached using a 3-pronged approach, including analysis of data from What We 
485 
Eat in America, NHANES dietary survey (2007-2010) (see Appendix E-2.1: Usual intake 
486 
distributions, 2007-2010, by age/sex groups), the Second National Report on Biochemical Indices of 
487 
Diet and Nutrition in the U.S. Population, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2012,3 and data 
488 
on the prevalence of health conditions, from the CDC. The DGAC used the totality of evidence from 
489 
these sources. 
490 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
14 
 
 
491 
Nutrients of Concern for Underconsumption 
492 
Vitamin D. Vitamin D is unequivocally essential for skeletal health.15 The 2010 IOM report on 
493 
Dietary Reference Intakes for calcium and vitamin D15 established new DRIs for vitamin D based on 
494 
established and consistent evidence for vitamin D’s role in skeletal health. Numerous other functions 
495 
exist for vitamin D, including its role as a transcription factor for more than 200 genes, roles in 
496 
apoptosis and cellular proliferation, and a growing body of evidence supporting vitamin D’s role in 
497 
preventing cancer, cardiovascular disease, and other chronic diseases.16-25 
498 
 
499 
The IOM’s rationale for setting the DRI was limited to vitamin D’s role in skeletal health, as the 
500 
evidence for the other diseases was not sufficiently mature at the time of the committee’s evidence 
501 
review. Therefore, any interpretations for vitamin D intake and its classification as a shortfall nutrient 
502 
and a nutrient of public health concern are restricted to this role in skeletal health. Given the high 
503 
prevalence of osteoporosis and low bone density, particularly in the older women (see Question 17, on 
504 
health conditions, below) and due to vitamin D’s critical role in bone health, the Committee 
505 
determined that vitamin D should be classified as an underconsumed nutrient of public health concern. 
506 
 
507 
Vitamin D can be obtained from the diet by consuming fluid milk and some milk products (e.g., some 
508 
yogurts), fortified juices, finfish, fortified breakfast cereals and some fortified grain products as well as 
509 
dietary supplements (Table D1.4 and Appendix E-3.3: Meeting Vitamin D Recommended Intakes in 
510 
USDA Food Patterns). Vitamin D also is synthesized endogenously through cutaneous exposure to 
511 
ultraviolet-B sunlight. The primary biomarker to assess vitamin D status is serum/plasma 25(OH)D 
512 
concentrations. This biomarker represents dietary intake plus endogenous synthesis.  
513 
 
514 
Dietary intake of vitamin D in the United States is low and well below the EAR values (Figure D1.1) 
515 
for all age and sex groups. In addition, independent evidence of nutrient shortfall comes from data 
516 
demonstrating low serum/plasma 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentrations from the CDC biomarker data, 
517 
particularly for young adults (ages 20 to 39 years), middle-aged adults (ages 40 to 59 years), non-
518 
Hispanic Blacks and Mexican-Americans (Table D1.5). The correlation of dietary intake with the 
519 
serum measures of 25-hydroxyvitamin D) is modest. In addition several factors predict serum 
520 
concentrations of nutrients in addition to dietary intake.19 The DGAC and other expert panels, 
521 
including the IOM, acknowledge that while numerous variables, including sun exposure and 
522 
endogenous synthesis, are strong predictors of serum vitamin D status, dietary intake of vitamin D is a 
523 
critical contributor to vitamin D status.26, 27  Further, while there is some degree of unexplained 
524 
variation in serum/plasma 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentrations, the biomarker is still important for 
525 
evaluating vitamin D inadequacy. Various statistical approaches have been used to evaluate and 
526 
confirm population inadequacy using the biomarker data. 28 Of note, the CDC biomarker data reviewed 
527 
by the DGAC should be interpreted knowing that the NHANES Mobile Examination Clinics do not 
528 
sample residents of northern climates in winter months due to variable sunshine exposure and the 
529 
possibility that high levels of sunshine exposure may be overrepresented in NHANES. In other words, 
530 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
15 
 
higher values in the dataset may be over-represented due to the summer blood draws, when 25-OHD 
531 
tends to be higher from sun exposure and deficiencies may be under represented. 15p.471-473 
532 
 
533 
The DGAC’s decision to classify vitamin D as a nutrient of concern is similar to the conclusion 
534 
reached by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), which designated vitamin D as a nutrient of 
535 
“public health significance” in its recent review of evidence in publishing a Proposed Rule on the 
536 
Nutrition Facts label.29  In addition, multiple national and international groups, including the American 
537 
Academy of Pediatrics (AAP),30 the Endocrine Society31 and the National Osteoporosis Foundation 32  
538 
have recommended that strategies to achieve the RDA or higher levels of vitamin D intake could 
539 
include consumption of fortified foods, broadening the range of dairy products that are fortified, and 
540 
consideration, in some cases, of the use of a vitamin D supplement or a multivitamin including vitamin 
541 
D. Such a use is especially appropriate where sunshine exposure is more limited due to climate or 
542 
sunblock use.  
543 
 
544 
Calcium. Calcium plays a major role in skeletal health and also is essential for proper functioning of 
545 
the circulatory system, nerve transmission, muscle contractility, cell signaling pathways, and vascular 
546 
integrity.15  Dietary calcium is obtained from fluid milk and milk products, fortified juices, and some 
547 
plant foods, including soy and soy products and vegetables (see Table D1.6 and Appendix E-3.2: Food 
548 
Group Contributions). However, the bioavailability of calcium from plant foods is lower than from 
549 
animal foods, such as dairy.  
550 
 
551 
The DGAC reviewed the dietary intake data from WWEIA. Intakes of calcium were often far below 
552 
the EAR, especially among adolescent girls and adults (Figure D1.1). Even though a reliable 
553 
biomarker for calcium does not exist, because of its strong link to health outcomes and the risks 
554 
associated with osteoporosis (see Question 17 on health conditions, below), the DGAC designated 
555 
calcium as a nutrient of public health concern for underconsumption. In addition, the DGAC also notes 
556 
that calcium is an underconsumed nutrient of public health concern among pregnant women. This 
557 
conclusion concurs with the FDA’s review that designated calcium as a nutrient of “public health 
558 
significance” in its recent review of evidence in publishing a Proposed Rule on the Nutrition Facts 
559 
label.29   
560 
 
561 
Strategies to improve calcium intake include increased dairy or fortified products that are important 
562 
sources of calcium. Concern about the safety of calcium supplements and a relative lack of data about 
563 
the health benefits of such supplements limit recommendations to use supplementation as a strategy to 
564 
meet the RDA for calcium, compared to using fortified foods.  
565 
 
566 
The subgroups of particular concern with regard to intake are preadolescent and adolescent females, 
567 
pregnant females, and middle aged and older females (see Question 1, above).  
568 
 
569 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
16 
 
Potassium. Potassium is the major intracellular cation and it plays critical roles in muscle function, 
570 
cardiac function, and regulation of blood pressure. Potassium adequacy is also critical for health, as 
571 
deficiency adversely affects numerous organ systems including the musculoskeletal, renal, and 
572 
cardiovascular systems. The primary biomarker to assess potassium intake is urinary potassium, and 
573 
these data are not available in the CDC biomarker dataset. The DGAC designated potassium as a 
574 
nutrient of public health concern due to its general under consumption relative to the AI across the 
575 
U.S. population and its association with hypertension and cardiovascular diseases, two common 
576 
adverse diet-related health outcomes  in the United States (see Question 17on health conditions, 
577 
below). This conclusion concurs with the FDA’s review that designated potassium as a nutrient of 
578 
“public health significance” in its recent review of evidence in publishing a Proposed Rule on the 
579 
Nutrition Facts label.29  Even though underconsumption was evident across the population (see 
580 
Question 1, above), there is a particular concern for middle-aged and older adults, who are at increased 
581 
risk for cardiovascular diseases (see Question 17). Fruits, vegetables, and legumes are all important 
582 
sources of potassium (Table D1.7). 
583 
 
584 
Fiber. Dietary fibers are non-digestible carbohydrates, primarily from plant foods, such as whole 
585 
grains, legumes, fruits and vegetables (Table D1.8). The most important and well-recognized role for 
586 
fiber is in colonic health and maintenance of proper laxation, but a growing body of evidence also 
587 
suggests that fiber may play a role in preventing coronary heart disease, colorectal and other cancers, 
588 
type 2 diabetes, and obesity.33 The AI for fiber is based on an intake level associated with the greatest 
589 
reduction in the risk of coronary heart disease. There are no available biomarkers for fiber intake, so 
590 
the designation as a nutrient of public health concern is based on the very low dietary intakes across all 
591 
sectors of the U.S. population and its important contribution to health. Because the average intake 
592 
levels of dietary fiber are half the recommended levels, achieving the recommendation requires 
593 
selecting high-fiber cereals and whole grains and -meeting current recommendations for fruits and 
594 
vegetables. 
595 
 
596 
Iron. Iron is an essential mineral whose primary function is to transport oxygen in the blood. 
597 
Inadequate iron status in the form of iron deficiency anemia leads to poor growth and development and 
598 
the potential for cognitive deficits in children. Excellent sources of heme iron include red meats, 
599 
enriched cereal grains, and fortified breakfast cereals (Table D1.9). Dietary intake estimates, together 
600 
with the CDC nutritional biomarker data indicate that iron is a nutrient of concern for children, 
601 
premenopausal females, and during pregnancy. Among women who are pregnant, 96 percent are below 
602 
the EAR for iron intake. Serum ferritin is the biochemical marker used by NHANES and the CDC to 
603 
evaluate iron status in the U.S. population. These data show that children and women of childbearing 
604 
age are at risk of iron deficiency anemia. Risk of iron deficiency anemia also is higher among 
605 
Mexican-American and non-Hispanic Black women than among non-Hispanic white women.3 Taken 
606 
together, the DGAC concluded that iron was an underconsumed nutrient of public health concern for 
607 
adolescent and premenopausal women and women who are pregnant. This conclusion concurs with the 
608 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
17 
 
FDA’s designated iron as a nutrient of “public health significance” in its recent review of evidence in 
609 
publishing a Proposed Rule on the Nutrition Facts label.29   
610 
 
611 
Nutrients of concern for overconsumption 
612 
Sodium. Sodium is the major cation in extracellular fluid that maintains extracelluar fluid volume and 
613 
plasma volume. It also functions in membrane potential activation and active transport of molecules 
614 
across cell membranes. In excess, sodium is associated with several adverse health events, particularly 
615 
hypertension.34 The DGAC treated sodium as a cross-cutting topic for dietary intake and health 
616 
outcomes, and a sodium working group was convened. Details on sodium, including dietary sources 
617 
and health outcomes-related data are found in Part D. Chapter 6: Cross-Cutting Topics of Public 
618 
Health Importance). Current sodium intakes of the U.S. population far exceed the UL for all age and 
619 
sex groups (Figure D1.3). Due to the critical link of sodium intake to health and that intake exceed 
620 
recommendations, sodium was designated as a nutrient of public health concern for overconsumption 
621 
across the entire U.S. population. 
622 
 
623 
Saturated fat. The DGAC used the 2013 American Heart Association/American College of 
624 
Cardiology (AHA/ACC) report on lifestyle management to reduce CVD risk2 for its evaluation of 
625 
saturated fat intake. The DGAC concurred with the AHA/ACC report that saturated fat intake exceeds 
626 
current recommendations in the United States and that lower levels of consumption would further 
627 
reduce the population level risk of CVD. The DGAC also convened a working group on saturated fat 
628 
(see Part D. Chapter 6: Cross-Cutting Topics of Public Health Importance for details). In addition, 
629 
the DGAC conducted food pattern modeling to demonstrate the dietary changes that would be 
630 
necessary to have diets with various levels of saturated fat as a percent of total energy (see USDA 
631 
Food Patterns Modeling Report in Appendix E-3.5: Reducing Saturated Fats in the USDA Food 
632 
Patterns). It is important to note that the median intake of saturated fat in the United States was 11.1 
633 
percent of total energy for all age groups in the 2007-2010 WWEIA data. However, a large majority 
634 
(71 percent) of the total population consumed more than 10 percent of calories from saturated fat, with 
635 
a range by age group from 57 percent to 92 percent (Figure D1.4). Further, 65 percent to 69 percent of 
636 
the age groups at highest risk of CVD (males and females older than age 50 years) had intakes more 
637 
than 10 percent of total calories were from saturated fat, the DGAC concluded that the U.S. population 
638 
should continue to monitor saturated fat intake. Saturated fat is still a nutrient of concern for 
639 
overconsumption, particularly for those older than the age of 50 years. 
640 
 
641 
Cholesterol. Previously, the Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommended that cholesterol intake 
642 
be limited to no more than 300 mg/day. The 2015 DGAC will not bring forward this recommendation 
643 
because available evidence shows no appreciable relationship between consumption of dietary 
644 
cholesterol and serum cholesterol, consistent with the conclusions of the AHA/ACC report.2, 35  
645 
Cholesterol is not a nutrient of concern for overconsumption.  
646 
 
647 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
648 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
18 
 
• CDC report, Second National Report on Biochemical Indicators of Diet and Nutrition in the U.S. 
649 
Population 2012. Available from: 
650 
http://www.cdc.gov/nutritionreport/pdf/Nutrition_Book_complete508_final.pdf.  
651 
• 
Food Labeling: Revision of the Nutrition and Supplement Facts Labels; Proposed Rule. Available 
652 
from: http://www.gpo.gov/fdsys/pkg/FR-2014-03-03/pdf/2014-04387.pdf. 
653 
• 
Appendix E-3.2: Food Group Contributions to Nutrients in the USDA Food Patterns and Current 
654 
Nutrient Intakes

• 
Appendix E-3.3: Meeting Vitamin D Recommended Intakes in USDA Food Patterns 
656 
• 
Appendix E-3.5: Reducing Saturated Fats in the USDA Food Patterns 
657 
 
658 
 
659 
Question 3:   Is there evidence of overconsumption of any micronutrients from 
660 
consumption of fortified foods and supplements?  
661 
Source of evidence: Data analysis 
662 
Conclusion  
663 
Dietary patterns among Americans, including typical use of fortified foods, rarely lead to 
664 
overconsumption of folate, calcium, iron, or vitamin D. However, each of these nutrients, as well as 
665 
other nutrients, are overconsumed in some supplement users, especially those taking high-dose 
666 
supplements. 
667 
 
668 
Implications 
669 
The public may safely use dietary supplements containing RDA level of nutrients, so long as total 
670 
intake from diet plus supplements does not exceed the UL. Use of products with high doses of 
671 
nutrients, such that total intake exceeds the UL, should be discussed with a Registered Dietitian or 
672 
other qualified health care provider.  
673 
 
674 
Supplement users should seek guidance about factors such as whether the amount of nutrients in 
675 
supplements exceeds the UL for those nutrients. Monitoring of dietary patterns in supplement users 
676 
should continue to be done, with attention paid to the highest risk groups, such as children and women 
677 
who are pregnant.  
678 
 
679 
Review of the Evidence  
680 
These conclusions were based on analysis of usual intake data for selected nutrients from foods and 
681 
supplements from WWEIA, NHANES dietary survey (2007-2010) (see Appendix E-2.5: Usual intake 
682 
distributions for supplement users for folate, folic acid, vitamin D, calcium, and iron, 2007-2010, by 
683 
age/sex groups and Appendix E-2.6: Usual intake distributions for non-supplement users for folate, 
684 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
19 
 
folic acid, vitamin D, calcium, and iron, 2007-2010, by age/sex groups). Nutrients were selected if 
685 
the DGAC had identified them as a shortfall nutrient and if supplemental intake data were available in 
686 
WWEIA (Figure D1.5). When possible the total nutrient exposure was considered (food + 
687 
supplements). The overconsumed nutrients (saturated fat and sodium) are not contained in most dietary 
688 
supplements so that overconsumed nutrients were not considered for this question.  
689 
 
690 
Folate. The use of supplemental folic acid exceeds the established UL in a small proportion of 
691 
children, especially those younger than age 9 years. However, this UL is not based on clinical toxicity 
692 
data in this population and exceeding the UL is primarily associated with supplement use.36  The risk 
693 
associated with usual folate intakes among children in the United States is considered low, but caution 
694 
should be used in advising supplements for children younger than age 9 years. 
695 
 
696 
Calcium. Dietary calcium intake greater than 2000 mg/day (UL) are seen in up to about 20 percent of 
697 
females, and 15 percent of adult males older than age 50 years. These high intakes are driven primarily 
698 
by a historical perspective that very high calcium supplement usage may decrease the risk of 
699 
osteoporosis. Concern exists about the safety of such high intakes and the possible association with 
700 
CVD risk and little, if any, current evidence supports intakes of calcium above the UL for the purpose 
701 
of decreasing osteoporosis.15 Of note, the World Health Organization recommends high dose calcium 
702 
supplementation (1.5-2 g/day) to prevent hypertensive disorders of pregnancy.37  This recommendation 
703 
is not widely followed among low-risk women in the United States. However, use of calcium 
704 
supplements does not appear to pose a health risk related to overconsumption of calcium.37 
705 
 
706 
Iron. In adults of all ages, a small proportion of iron supplement users have intakes above the UL. 
707 
Concerns related both to cardiovascular health and oxidant damage exist, but are not well-defined. Iron 
708 
supplementation is very common during early childhood and pregnancy, but is unlikely to pose a 
709 
health risk.8 
710 
 
711 
Vitamin D. Overconsumption of vitamin D occurs when individuals take high dose supplements, 
712 
usually over a long period of time.15 The UL of 4000 IU/day is commonly exceeded by individuals 
713 
with or without the guidance of a physician.15 In general, it is unlikely that most supplement users, who 
714 
limit themselves to 10,000 IU/day or less, will have any evidence of toxicity, but a greater risk may 
715 
exist among some groups, including small children. Those who take high dose supplements often have 
716 
their serum/plasma 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentrations monitored and this can be helpful although no 
717 
clearly toxic level of 25-hydroxyvitamin D in the blood is known. Overall, the population risk of 
718 
overconsumption of vitamin D leading to toxic effects, including hypercalcemia or other clinical 
719 
symptoms, is uncommon.38 
720 
 
721 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
722 
• 
Appendix E-2.5: Usual intake distributions for supplement users for folate, folic acid, vitamin D, 
723 
calcium, and iron, 2007-2010, by age/sex groups  
724 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
20

Appendix E-2.6: Usual intake distributions for non-supplement users for folate, folic acid, vitamin 
725 
D, calcium, and iron, 2007-2010, by age/sex groups
