727 
Question 4:   What is the level of caffeine intake derived from foods and beverages on 
728 
the basis of Institute of Medicine (IOM) Dietary Reference Intakes age and sex 
729 
categories in the U.S. population?  
730 
Source of evidence: Data analysis 
731 
 
732 
Conclusion  
733 
In general, intakes of caffeine do not exceed what is currently considered safe levels in any age group. 
734 
Some young adults may have moderately high intakes. There is less certainty about the safe level of 
735 
intake in children and adolescents. However, routine consumption patterns do not suggest that 
736 
excessive intakes are common in these groups. 
737 
 
738 
Implications 
739 
The public may safely consume caffeine-containing beverages, such as coffee and tea. However, 
740 
children, adolescents, and women who are pregnant or considering pregnancy should not consume 
741 
very high levels of caffeine from beverages or supplements (e.g., energy shots, fortified foods).  
742 
 
743 
Monitoring of caffeine intake should be continued with special attention to high-risk groups, including 
744 
children and women who are pregnant. Families should monitor caffeine intake in children, and high-
745 
dose caffeine supplementations should not be used. 
746 
 
747 
For additional details on caffeine safety please see Part D. Chapter 5: Food Sustainability and Safety. 
748 
 
749 
Review of the Evidence  
750 
These conclusions were reached based on analysis of usual intake data from the WWEIA, NHANES 
751 
dietary survey (2007-2010).  Data on intakes of caffeine show that intakes in adults (Figure D1.6) peak 
752 
at ages 31 to 70 years, and that younger adults (ages 19 to 30 years), older adults (71 years and older), 
753 
have lower intakes. Relatively few individuals (less than 10 percent) have intakes above 400 mg/day 
754 
(see Appendix E-2.1: Usual intake distributions, 2007-2010, by age/sex groups), which is a level set 
755 
as a moderate intake by some groups, including Health Canada.  
756 
 
757 
In children, caffeine intakes increase with age (Figure D1.7) with median intakes remaining below 100 
758 
mg/day in adolescents (14 to 18 years). Recommended intakes from Health Canada of no more than 
759 
2.5 mg/kg/day, or about 85 mg/day total in children ages 10 to 12 years 39 are not exceeded by most 
760 
children and adolescents although recent data indicates that as many as 10 percent of children and 
761 
adolescents ages 12 to 19 years exceed this intake level.40 These data demonstrate that caregivers 
762 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
21 
 
should monitor caffeine intake in children and exercise caution with respect to time-dependent changes 
763 
in caffeine intake. 
764 
 
765 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
766 
• 
Appendix E-2.1: Usual intake distributions, 2007-2010 by age/sex groups 
767 
 
768 
Question 5:   How well do updated USDA Food Patterns♦ meet IOM Dietary Reference 
769 
Intakes and 2010 Dietary Guidelines recommendations?  How do the recommended 
770 
amounts of food groups compare to current distributions of usual intakes for the U.S. 
771 
population?  
772 
Source of evidence: Food Pattern Modeling 
773 
 
774 
Conclusion  
775 
USDA Food Patterns across a broad range of ages and energy intake meet most goals for nutrient 
776 
adequacy. The nutrients of public health concern for which the patterns do not meet recommendations 
777 
are potassium and vitamin D. Recommended amounts of food groups and their component subgroups 
778 
fall within the broad range of usual food group intake distributions for the U.S. population. 
779 
 
780 
Implications 
781 
The USDA Food Patterns provide guidance for consuming a nutrient-dense, energy-balanced diet. To 
782 
achieve nutrient adequacy, the U.S. population should be advised to consume dietary patterns 
783 
consistent with the USDA Food Patterns.  
784 
 
785 
Continued vigilance is needed to ensure that food intake patterns meet but do not exceed DRI targets in 
786 
all age groups. The Patterns meet recommended intake levels or limits for almost all nutrients, 
787 
including the following nutrients of concern: calcium, fiber, iron, sodium, and saturated fat. Two 
788 
nutrients of concern (potassium and vitamin D) are not provided in recommended levels by the 
789 
Patterns. Therefore, potassium and vitamin D intakes require assessment both of individual intake and 
790 
population intake patterns of foods or supplements to ensure that needs for physiological functioning 
791 
are met. Meeting the needs for these nutrients may require careful attention to excellent natural 
792 
sources, food enriched or fortified with the nutrients, or, in some cases, consideration of supplements. 
793 
 
794 
                                                 
♦ The USDA Food Patterns referred to in this question are the same as the “Healthy U.S.-style Food Pattern” described 
later in this chapter (see Question 20). We use the term USDA Food Patterns in this question because the development of 
the Healthy U.S.-style Food Pattern and two related USDA Food Patterns had not occurred when the Committee addressed 
this question. 
 
 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
22 
 
Following the recommended food intake pattern increases intakes of whole grains, vegetables, fruits, 
795 
and fat-free/low fat dairy and thus increases the likelihood of meeting recommendations for these food 
796 
groups while decreasing intake of the food components refined grains, solid fats, and added sugars. 
797 
Following the recommended pattern also decreases intake of the nutrients sodium and saturated fat. 
798 
 
799 
In some situations, specific foods or dietary supplements may be used to increase underconsumed 
800 
nutrient intakes not met through the USDA Food Patterns.  
801 
 
802 
Review of the Evidence  
803 
These conclusions were reached based on the results of the Food Pattern Modeling Report on 
804 
Adequacy of the USDA Food Patterns. The USDA Food Patterns are intended to represent the types 
805 
and amounts of foods that will provide nutrients sufficient to meet IOM nutrient recommendations and 
806 
Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommendations. The Food Patterns are updated every 5 years 
807 
during the deliberations of the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee, and are presented to the 
808 
Committee for their assessment of the Food Patterns’ adequacy. As part of the update, amounts 
809 
recommended from each food group may be modified to reach all or most of the specified goals. In 
810 
addition, the amounts from each food group are compared to usual dietary intake patterns of the U.S. 
811 
population, and are kept within the normal range of consumption. The current analysis, using the 2010 
812 
USDA Food Patterns as a baseline, found that the recommended amounts of each food group met 
813 
almost all nutrient goals and were within the normal range of consumption. Therefore, no updates to 
814 
the food group amounts from 2010 were needed.  
815 
 
816 
As shown in Figure D1.8, for many nutrients, amounts of a nutrient in the patterns are well above the 
817 
RDA or AI. Protein, phosphorus, zinc, copper, selenium, manganese, vitamin C, thiamin, riboflavin, 
818 
niacin, vitamin K, folate, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 are above the goal amounts for all age/sex 
819 
groups.  
820 
 
821 
In contrast, some nutrients are just above the RDA or AI, or marginally below (90 to 100%) goal 
822 
amounts for several age/sex groups. These include calcium, iron, and magnesium. The percents of the 
823 
RDA shown in Figure D1.8 are for the lowest calorie level assigned to these age/sex groups—the level 
824 
applicable for a sedentary/less active physical activity level.  
825 
 
826 
The nutrients for which adequacy goals are not met in almost all patterns are potassium, vitamin D, 
827 
vitamin E, and choline. Due to the new higher RDA for vitamin D that was recommended by the 2011 
828 
Committee to Review Dietary Reference Intakes for vitamin D and calcium,15 amounts in the patterns 
829 
are a much smaller percentage of the RDA than previously, and no pattern meets the EAR for vitamin 
830 
D. To determine if vitamin D recommendations could be met while following the food group 
831 
recommendations of the USDA Food Patterns, thorough, careful selection of specific foods within 
832 
each food group, an additional modeling analysis was conducted and reported below (see Question 6).  
833 
 
834 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
23 
 
The USDA Food Intake patterns provide a healthy pattern of food choices and to accomplish this, 
835 
these patterns deviate from typical food intakes in a number of ways. To ensure that the patterns do not 
836 
deviate too far beyond the range of what the U.S. population could feasibly consume, the 
837 
recommended intake amounts in the patterns from each food group or subgroup plus oils were 
838 
compared to the median and either the 5th or 95th percentile of usual intakes of the population, from 
839 
WWEIA/NHANES 2007-2010.41 Table A6 of the Adequacy of the USDA Food Patterns Modeling 
840 
Report (see Appendix E-3.1, Table A6) shows the comparison of food group recommended intakes to 
841 
median and 95th percentile intakes. 
842 
 
843 
For underconsumed food groups, such as fruits and vegetables, recommended amounts in the patterns 
844 
are generally between the median and 95th percentiles of usual intakes. (see Appendix E-3.1: 
845 
Adequacy of the USDA Food Patterns, Table A6)  This indicates that the Food Patterns recommend 
846 
amounts within the broad intake range for the population. However, for some specific food groups and 
847 
some age/sex groups, such as vegetables for males ages 14 to 18 years, food group amounts in the 
848 
Patterns are somewhat above the 95th percentile of usual intake. One exception to this is whole grain 
849 
recommendations in the Patterns, which are well above the 95th percentile of usual intakes for all 
850 
age/sex groups. Conversely, refined grain recommendations in the patterns are very low compared to 
851 
usual intakes—about the 5th percentile of intake for most age/sex groups. This indicates that a major 
852 
shift from refined to whole grains is needed in order to meet recommendations. 
853 
 
854 
For Food Pattern components that are overconsumed, the limits in the patterns for maximum solid fat 
855 
and added sugars (see Questions 7 and 8 for more information on solid fats and added sugars) also are 
856 
very low compared to usual intake amounts—at approximately the 5th percentile of usual intakes for 
857 
most age/sex groups, and less than the 5th percentile of usual intakes for boys and girls ages 2 to 13 
858 
years. (see Appendix E-3.1: Adequacy of the USDA Food Patterns, Table A6) 
859 
 
860 
An additional modeling analysis was conducted to answer the questions: How well do the USDA Food 
861 
Patterns meet the nutritional needs of children ages 2 to 5 years and how do the recommended amounts 
862 
compare to their current intakes?  Given the relatively small empty calorie limit for this age group, 
863 
how much flexibility is possible in food choices? (see Appendix E-3.4: USDA Food Patterns—
864 
Adequacy for Young Children) 
865 
 
866 
The nutritional needs and the diets of young children are different in some important ways from the 
867 
nutritional needs and diets of older children and adults. Therefore, this modeling analysis focused on 
868 
the adequacy of the Patterns for young children, given these differences. Nutrient profiles for the Dairy 
869 
and Fruit groups were adjusted to better reflect the food choices within these groups of young children. 
870 
The adjusted Dairy group nutrient profile for young children is based on 70 percent fluid milk, 25 
871 
percent cheese, 3.5 percent yogurt, and 1.5 percent soymilk. In contrast, the profile for the overall 
872 
population is based on 51 percent fluid milk, 45 percent cheese, 2.5 percent yogurt, and 1.5 percent 
873 
soymilk. In addition, 1 percent milk rather than fat-free milk was used as the representative food for 
874 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
24 
 
fluid milk. The adjusted Fruit group nutrient profile for young children is based on 42 percent fruit 
875 
juice and 58 percent whole fruit. In contrast, overall population intake is about 33 percent juice and 67 
876 
percent whole fruit. With these adjustments, the adequacy of the Patterns did not change, but amounts 
877 
of potassium, vitamins D, A, C, and folate increased slightly, and sodium decreased slightly. The 
878 
amounts recommended in the USDA Food Patterns fall within the broad range of usual intakes by this 
879 
age group for most food groups and subgroups (see Appendix E-3.1: Adequacy of the USDA Food 
880 
Patterns, Table A6). 
881 
 
882 
In addition, the young children’s nutrient profiles were higher in energy, mainly due to the use of 1 
883 
percent rather than fat-free milk. Therefore, the amount of calories that could be allowed from solid 
884 
fats and added sugars was adjusted down to keep the Patterns isocaloric. This resulted in limited 
885 
flexibility in food choices when following the Patterns, especially for children ages 4 and 5 years for 
886 
whom 2½ cup equivalents (cup eqs) from the Dairy group is recommended (the Patterns for children 
887 
ages 2 and 3 years recommend 2 cup eqs). Options tested to increase flexibility in food choices 
888 
included a small reduction of 1/2 ounce eq in the amount of Protein Foods, or a change from 1 percent 
889 
milk to fat-free milk at 4 years of age. These changes did not result in lower nutrient adequacy levels.  
890 
 
891 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
892 
• 
Appendix E-3.1: Adequacy of the USDA Food Patterns  
893 
• 
Appendix E-3.4: USDA Food Patterns—Adequacy for Young Children 
894 
 
895 
Question 6:  Can vitamin D Estimated Average Requirements and/or Recommended 
896 
Dietary Allowances be met with careful food choices following recommended amounts 
897 
from each food group in the USDA Food Patterns?  How restricted would food choices 
898 
be, and how much of the vitamin D would need to come from fortified dairy and other 
899 
food products? 
900 
 
901 
Source of evidence: Food Pattern Modeling 
902 
 
903 
Conclusion  
904 
Through the use of a diet rich in seafood and fortified foods, EAR, but not RDA, levels of vitamin D 
905 
can be achieved. Additional fortification or supplementation strategies would be needed to reach RDA 
906 
levels of vitamin D intake consistently, especially in individuals with low intakes of fish/seafood or 
907 
fortified dairy foods, other fortified foods (e.g. breakfast cereals) and beverages. 
908 
 
909 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
25 
 
Implications 
910 
Diet is an important aspect of achieving vitamin D intake targets. The U.S. population should be 
911 
encouraged to choose foods and beverages fortified with vitamin D. When needed, supplementation 
912 
can be considered to achieve RDA intakes of vitamin D.  
913 
 
914 
Review of the Evidence 
915 
These conclusions were reached based on the results of the Food Pattern Modeling Report titled 
916 
“Meeting Vitamin D Recommended Intakes in USDA Food Patterns” (see Appendix E-3.3). It may be 
917 
difficult for individuals to reach the RDA intake of vitamin D from food, including food as it is 
918 
currently fortified in the United States. The RDA was established by the Institute of Medicine on the 
919 
assumption of minimal or no sunshine exposure. This was done even though the majority (up to 80 to 
920 
90 percent in some parts of the United States) of vitamin D in the body is derived from conversion by 
921 
solar radiation of pre-vitamin D in the skin. However, during the winter, in much of the United States, 
922 
this conversion is minimal and furthermore, recommendations for sunscreen use have limited the 
923 
degree to which one can safely ensure sunshine exposure as a source of vitamin D.  
924 
 
925 
Vitamin D exposure, and likely status, is assessed generally through serum/plasma 25-hydroxyvitamin 
926 
D concentrations. However, this test is not recommended for routine screening of the entire 
927 
population30-32, 42, 43 due to costs and challenges in obtaining measurements throughout the year and 
928 
interpreting results in populations, including those who are obese. Because many non-screened 
929 
individuals will still need to reach the RDA for vitamin D, supplement use may be considered for this 
930 
purpose. 
931 
 
932 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
933 
• 
Appendix E-3.3 Meeting Vitamin D Recommended Intakes in USDA Food Patterns 
934 
 
935 
 
936 
FOOD GROUPS--CURRENT INTAKES AND TRENDS  
937 
Introduction 
938 
As noted for Questions 5 and 6, to help the U.S. population meet recommended dietary goals and 
939 
improve their health and well-being, the USDA recommends a food-based, total diet approach for 
940 
meeting the U.S. Dietary Guidelines.44, 45  
941 
 
942 
The USDA Food Patterns have changed over time to be consistent with emerging science that is 
943 
presented in each issuance of the Guidelines. The current USDA Food Patterns identify amounts of 
944 
foods to consume from five major food groups (fruits, vegetables, grains, protein foods, and dairy) and 
945 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
26 
 
their sub-groups (dark green vegetables, orange and red vegetables, starchy vegetables, other 
946 
vegetables, beans and peas, whole grains, enriched/refined grains, meat/poultry/eggs, nuts, seeds, soy 
947 
products, seafood) and are based on nutrient-dense foods.44, 45 In 2010, the DGAC developed a  
948 
vegetarian adaptation of the Food Patterns to provide guidance for consumers wishing to follow a 
949 
vegetarian diet. For 2015, the DGAC developed a new Healthy Vegetarian Food Pattern  based on food 
950 
intakes of vegetarians. The 2015 DGAC also provided a Mediterranean-style Food Pattern due to the 
951 
data supporting the health-related benefits of a Mediterranean-style diet (see Dietary Patterns section, 
952 
Question 20, and Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes). 
953 
The food groups chosen for all the Patterns include primarily nutrient-dense foods. The patterns are 
954 
intended to meet the RDA for nutrients so that nutritional adequacy is met without exceeding 
955 
recommended energy intake. They also are designed so that they are below the 2010 DGA limits for 
956 
sodium and saturated fat. Recommended amounts to consume from each food group differ depending 
957 
on an individual’s energy and nutrient needs. Patterns are provided for 12 different calorie levels 
958 
(Table D1.10) and assignment to one of these calorie levels is based on age, sex, and activity level 
959 
(Table D1.11). In addition, the Patterns provide for limited amounts of solid fats and added sugars. The 
960 
complete Food Pattern modeling report (including a listing of the nutrients considered for the Patterns) 
961 
is found in Appendix E3.1, and details on the methods used to derive the Patterns have been 
962 
published.44, 46, 47 
963 
 
964 
Question 7: What are current consumption patterns of USDA Food Pattern food groups 
965 
by the U.S. population?  
966 
Source of evidence:  Data analysis 
967 
 
968 
Conclusion  
969 
Positive, healthy eating habits provide an excellent foundation for a lifetime of healthy eating. Many 
970 
young children start out eating very well, particularly with regard to intakes of fruit and dairy foods. 
971 
Unfortunately, many of these early life healthy habits seem to disappear as children reach school age 
972 
and beyond. Across all age and sex groups, the vast majority of the U.S. population does not meet 
973 
recommended intakes for fruit, vegetables, whole grains, and dairy food groups. Each of these food 
974 
groups are excellent sources of shortfall nutrients and underconsumed nutrients of public health 
975 
concern. Across all age and sex groups, the vast majority of the U.S. population exceeds recommended 
976 
intakes of refined grains, solid fats, and added sugars.  
977 
 
978 
Implications 
979 
To realize the numerous health benefits from dietary patterns that are higher in fruit, vegetables, whole 
980 
grains, lean protein, and non-fat and low-fat dairy (see Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods 
981 
and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes for details on the health benefits for dietary patterns with these 
982 
characteristics), action is needed across all sectors of food production, distribution, and consumption 
983 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
27 
 
and at individual behavioral and population levels. Individuals, families, schools, worksites, healthcare 
984 
and public health settings, restaurants, and other food establishments must work together to ensure that 
985 
all segments of the population can: 
986 
• 
Increase intake of underconsumed food groups and nutrient-dense foods, while maintaining 
987 
energy balance, and without increasing saturated fat, sodium, and added sugars 
988 
Given the complexity of dietary behavior change, consumers will need access to evidence-based 
989 
educational resources and intervention programs and services in public health and healthcare settings 
990 
to facilitate adoption and maintenance of healthy dietary behaviors. (See Part D. Chapter 3: 
991 
Individual Diet and Physical Activity Behavior Change for discussion of what works at the level of 
992 
individual behavior change and Part D. Chapter 4: Food Environment and Settings for discussion of 
993 
population change through environmental strategies.) 
994 
 
995 
Within the Dairy and Vegetable groups, the following dietary changes in particular will help increase 
996 
intake of shortfall nutrients and will decrease intake of overconsumed nutrients by the U.S. population: 
997 
• 
Increasing low-fat/fat-free fluid milk and yogurt and decreasing cheese would result in higher 
998 
intakes of magnesium, potassium, vitamin A, and vitamin D while simultaneously decreasing 
999 
the intake of sodium and saturated fat.  
1000 
• 
Replacing soft drinks and other sugar-sweetened beverages (including sports drinks) with non-
1001 
fat fluid milk would substantially reduce added sugars and empty calories and increase the 
1002 
intake of shortfall nutrients, including calcium, vitamin D, and magnesium. 
1003 
• 
Consuming all vegetables, including starchy vegetables, with minimal additions of salt and 
1004 
solid fat will help minimize intake of overconsumed nutrients – sodium and saturated fat. 
1005 
 
1006 
Review of the Evidence  
1007 
This question was answered using data from the WWEIA, NHANES dietary survey (2007-2010) and 
1008 
the National Cancer Institute’s examination of the usual intake distributions and percent of the U.S. 
1009 
population meeting USDA Food Pattern recommendations for their age and sex. 41, 48, 49  It is important 
1010 
to note that the Dietary Guidelines for Americans are established only for those ages 2 years and older. 
1011 
However, the WWEIA, NHANES sample includes persons from birth. The NHANES data are 
1012 
presented in these specific age groups that cannot be further divided.  
1013 
 
1014 
Fruit. When consumed in the amounts recommended in the USDA Food Patterns, fruit contributes 
1015 
substantial amounts of two nutrients of public health concern: fiber and potassium. (Whole fruit and 
1016 
fruit juice provide about 16 percent of dietary fiber and 17 percent of potassium in the Food Patterns 
1017 
(see Appendix E-3.2: Food Group Contributions to Nutrients in USDA Food Patterns and Current 
1018 
Nutrient Intakes). 
1019 
 
1020 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
28 
 
The majority of children ages 1 to 3 years and 4 to 8 years meet the recommended intakes for total 
1021 
fruit, which is 1 cup and 1 to 1.5 cups per day, respectively. Among older children (boys and girls ages 
1022 
9 to 13 years), adolescents, and adults of all ages (both men and women), few people consume the 
1023 
recommended daily amounts, which range from 1.5 to 2 cups for older children and adolescents to 1.5 
1024 
to 2.5 cups for adults (Figure D1.9). Among the overall U.S. population, approximately 15 percent 
1025 
meet the daily fruit intake recommendation while nearly 80 percent do not meet the recommendation.  
1026 
 
1027 
More than half of the daily fruit intake for all age and sex groups in the U.S. population (ages 1 year 
1028 
and older) comes from whole fruit (Figure D1.10). Among both boys and girls ages 1 to 3 years, whole 
1029 
fruit comprises slightly more than half of the daily fruit intake and the remainder is consumed though 
1030 
100% fruit juice. The American Academy of Pediatrics (2001)50 recommends that young children limit 
1031 
their juice intake to 4 to 6 ounces per day. Six ounces of juice is 0.75 cups; the average juice intakes 
1032 
fall within this recommended limit suggesting that juice is not overconsumed among many young 
1033 
children. Among children ages 4 to 8 and 9 to 13 years, fruit intake includes both 100% juice and 
1034 
whole fruit, but whole fruit comprises the majority of intake. Among middle aged and older adults, 
1035 
most of the fruit intake is from whole fruit, albeit below recommended levels, rather than 100% juice. 
1036 
 
1037 
Vegetables. Vegetables are excellent sources of many shortfall nutrients and nutrients of public health 
1038 
concern. When vegetables are consumed in the amounts recommended in the USDA Food Patterns, 
1039 
vegetables contribute the following (expressed as averages over all the calorie levels): fiber (38 
1040 
percent), potassium (36 percent), iron (19 percent), folate (23 percent), and vitamin A as provitamin A 
1041 
carotenoids (34 percent). Note that select vegetables do contribute to calcium intake, including 
1042 
spinach, collard greens, turnip greens, but these vegetables are often consumed in smaller amounts 
1043 
than is needed to be considered important sources of calcium (Table D1.6 and Appendix E-3.2: Food 
1044 
Group Contributions to Nutrients in the USDA Food Patterns and Current Nutrient Intakes). 
1045 
 
1046 
The U.S. population consumes few vegetables (Figure D1.11). Only 10 percent and 15 percent of boys 
1047 
and girls ages 1 to 3 years, respectively, consume the recommended 1 cup of vegetables per day. For 
1048 
children ages 4 to 8 years, less than 5 percent consume the recommended amount of 1.5 to 2 cups of 
1049 
vegetables per day. Vegetable consumption is lowest among boys ages 9 to 13 years (1 percent 
1050 
consume the recommended 2 to 2.5 cups per day) and girls ages 14 to 18 years (less than 1 percent 
1051 
consume the recommended 2 to 2.5 cups/day). Vegetable intakes increase slightly during the adult 
1052 
years, but intakes are still very low. Among young adult males and females ages 19 to 30 years, less 
1053 
than 10 percent meet the 2 to 3.5 cups/day recommendation. Intakes increase only slightly in 
1054 
subsequent age decades (31 to 50 years). Middle aged adults (51 to 70 years) are somewhat closer to 
1055 
the goal as they have the highest vegetable intakes. Even so, only about 20 percent of men and about 
1056 
30 percent of women meet the daily recommendation of 2 to 3.5 cups per day. Although these intake 
1057 
levels are still below optimal, the positive gains in vegetable consumption are noteworthy. However, 
1058 
vegetable intakes fall again among older adults (71 years and older), with less than 20 percent of men 
1059 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
29 
 
and women meeting intake recommendations. Overall, nearly 90 percent of the U.S. population does 
1060 
not meet daily vegetable intake recommendations. 
1061 
 
1062 
The USDA Food Pattern food group for vegetables includes five subgroups: dark green vegetables, red 
1063 
and orange vegetables, beans and peas, starchy vegetables, and other vegetables. The U.S. population 
1064 
does not meet intake recommendations for any of these vegetable subgroups (Figures D1.12 to D1.16). 
1065 
More than 80 percent of the U.S. population does not meet the intake recommendation for dark green 
1066 
vegetables, starchy vegetables, and beans and peas, while more than 90 percent do not meet the 
1067 
recommended intakes for red and orange vegetables. “Other vegetables”  (Figure D1.16) is a broad 
1068 
group that includes iceberg lettuce, green beans, cucumbers, celery, onions, summer squash, 
1069 
mushrooms, and avocados. More than 50 percent of males and females ages 51 to 70 years meet or 
1070 
exceed the recommended intake amounts of other vegetables and among all ages, nearly 40 percent 
1071 
meet or exceed the recommended intake. Intake of “other vegetables” is more likely to meet 
1072 
recommendations than the other four subgroups, but consumers should be encouraged to increase 
1073 
intake of all vegetables. To meet total vegetable recommendations, higher intakes of all vegetable 
1074 
subgroups are needed, particularly those subgroups where intake is minimal, such as dark green and 
1075 
orange and red vegetables, which are excellent sources of vitamin C, folate, magnesium, and 
1076 
potassium.  
1077 
 
1078 
Potatoes (white potatoes) are the most commonly consumed single vegetable, and make up about 80 
1079 
percent of all starchy vegetable consumption.51 They account for 25 percent of all vegetable 
1080 
consumption and are a good source of both potassium and fiber. Among children and adolescents ages 
1081 
2 to 19 years, they account for 28 percent to 35 percent of total vegetable consumption, with a higher 
1082 
percentage of vegetables consumed as potatoes among boys than girls in each age category. Potatoes 
1083 
are consumed in a variety of forms, with about 31 percent being boiled (including mashed and in 
1084 
dishes such as potato salad, soups, and stews), 22 percent as chips, sticks, or puffs, 19 percent as 
1085 
French fries, 17 percent as baked, and 12 percent as home fries or hash browns.  
1086 
 
1087 
Grains (whole and refined). The 2010 Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommended that half of 
1088 
all grain intake should come from whole grains. The 2015 DGAC brings forward this recommendation 
1089 
and here we give rationale and results to support this decision. The background and summary of 
1090 
previous food pattern modeling with respect to grains is important to present here so as to provide 
1091 
context for the 2015 DGAC recommendations. 
1092 
 
1093 
Whole grains are those “foods made from the entire grain seed, usually called the kernel, which 
1094 
consists of the bran, germ and endosperm. If the kernel has been cracked, crushed or flaked, it must 
1095 
retain nearly the same relative proportions of bran, germ and endosperm as the original grain in order 
1096 
to be called whole grain.”52p134 Examples of whole grains are brown rice, popcorn, bulgur, whole 
1097 
wheat, oats, and barley. If whole grains were consumed in the amounts recommended in the Food 
1098 
Patterns, whole grains would provide substantial percentages of several key nutrients, such as about 32 
1099 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
30 
 
percent of dietary fiber, 42 percent of iron, 35 percent of folate, 29 percent of magnesium, and 16 
1100 
percent of vitamin A (see E-3.2: Food Group Contributions to Nutrients in USDA Food Patterns and 
1101 
Current Nutrient Intakes). 
1102 
 
1103 
Across all ages and both sexes, the U.S. population does not meet the goal for whole grain intake, as 
1104 
nearly 100 percent of the population consumes amounts that are below the recommended intake levels 
1105 
(Figure D1.17), which range from 1.5 ounce equivalents (oz eq) for young children up to 3 to 3.5 oz 
1106 
eqs for older children and adolescent and adult females. Adolescent and adult males are advised to 
1107 
consume 3 to 4 oz eqs per day. The inadequate intake of whole grains leads to underconsumption of 
1108 
several shortfall nutrients and nutrients of public health concern. Refined grains, such as white flour 
1109 
and products made with white flour, white rice, and de-germed cornmeal, are part of the intake 
1110 
recommendation because they are commonly enriched with iron and several B vitamins, including 
1111 
thiamin, niacin, and riboflavin (e.g., enriched flour, 21 CFR 137.165).53p.452  Since 1998, enriched 
1112 
grains also have been fortified with folic acid and are thus an important source of folic acid for women 
1113 
of childbearing potential.53, 54  The effect of the folic acid fortification on the health status of the U.S. 
1114 
population was extensively reviewed by the 2010 DGAC and so was not re-reviewed by the 2015 
1115 
DGAC. The 2010 DGAC concluded that strong and consistent evidence demonstrates a large reduction 
1116 
in the incidence of neural tube defects (NTDs) in the United States and Canada following mandatory 
1117 
folic acid fortification. They also found only limited evidence to suggest a decline in stroke mortality 
1118 
in the United States and Canada and an increase in colorectal cancer in those countries following 
1119 
mandatory folic acid fortification. Due to the very limited evidence, cause and effect cannot be 
1120 
attributed for folic acid fortification and either stroke or colorectal cancer incidence. The 2015 DGAC 
1121 
brings forward those results with no notable changes in the interpretation of the data presented in 2010. 
1122 
Despite the B vitamins and iron that can be obtained from enriched and fortified refined grains, 
1123 
products made with refined grains also may be a source of excess calories and added sugars.  (See 
1124 
Question 11c, food categories, below, and added sugars discussion in Part D. Chapter 6: Cross-
1125 
Cutting Topics of Public Health Importance.) Figure D1.18, documents that the U.S. population 
1126 
consumes far too many refined grains. In the overall population for all ages and for both males and 
1127 
females, about 19 percent meet the recommendation for refined grains, while more than 70 percent 
1128 
exceed the recommendation. Intake of refined grains is particularly high among boys and girls ages 4 
1129 
to 8 years and girls ages 9 to 13 years.  
1130 
 
1131 
Due to the overconsumption of refined grains and the underconsumption of whole grains relative to the 
1132 
2010 recommendation that “half of all grain intake should come from whole grains,” the DGAC 
1133 
decided that it was important to examine the impact on nutrient intake if: (1) refined/enriched grains 
1134 
intake were reduced to no more than 25 percent or 15 percent of the total grains intake; and (2) overall 
1135 
grain intake were reduced. The Committee relied on food pattern modeling analyses conducted by the 
1136 
2005 and 2010 DGACs to answer these questions, and brings forward their recommendations, as 
1137 
reiterated below.  
1138 
 
1139 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
31 
 
The key finding from the 2010 DGAC modeling report was: “As shown by food pattern modeling, 
1140 
consumption of all grains as whole grains, without including any fortified whole grain products, would 
1141 
lower dietary folate and iron intake levels to less than adequate amounts for individuals in population 
1142 
groups who may be at high risk for inadequate intakes of these nutrients. Individuals are encouraged to 
1143 
consume most of their grains as fiber-rich whole grains, and when doing so, should select some of 
1144 
these fiber-rich whole grains as products that have been fortified with folic acid and possibly other 
1145 
nutrients”. 55p146  
1146 
 
1147 
In its analysis, the 2005 DGAC reported that non-whole grains contributed important amounts of 
1148 
certain nutrients to the dietary patterns, including folate, iron, calcium, fiber, thiamin, riboflavin and 
1149 
niacin.56append G-2  The 2005 DGAC concluded that including only 3 oz eqs of whole grains, with no 
1150 
non-whole grains, in the food patterns would lower intake of many of these key nutrients and perhaps 
1151 
place certain individuals at risk of nutrient inadequacy. However, the 2010 DGAC found that 
1152 
consuming all grains as whole grains would provide for nutrient adequacy in the patterns if fortified 
1153 
ready to eat (RTE) whole grain breakfast cereals were substituted for RTE refined grain breakfast.55app 
1154 
E.7 The 2015 DGAC concluded that consumption of only whole grains with no replacement or 
1155 
substitution would result in nutrient shortfalls.  
1156 
 
1157 
Dairy. Dairy foods in the USDA Food Patterns include fluid milk, cheese, yogurt, ice cream, milk-
1158 
based replacement meals and milk products, including fortified soymilk, but do not include almond or 
1159 
other plant-based “milk-type” products. Dairy foods are excellent sources of nutrients of public health 
1160 
concern, including vitamin D, calcium, and potassium. Consumption of dairy foods provides numerous 
1161 
health benefits including lower risk of diabetes, metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease and 
1162 
obesity.57-62  When consumed in the amounts recommended by the Food Patterns, on average across 
1163 
the calorie levels, dairy foods contribute about 67 percent of calcium, 64 percent of vitamin D, and 17 
1164 
percent of magnesium (see Appendix E-3.2: Food Group Contributions to Nutrients in the USDA 
1165 
Food Patterns and Current Nutrient Intakes). The Patterns recommend consumption of low-fat and 
1166 
fat-free foods in the Dairy group to ensure intake of these key nutrients while minimizing intake of 
1167 
saturated fat, which is a nutrient of concern for overconsumption.44 
1168 
 
1169 
More than 60 percent of young boys and girls ages 1 to 3 years meet or exceed the recommended 
1170 
intake of 2 cup eqs per day, with most of this intake coming in the form of fluid milk (see Figure 
1171 
D1.19 and Appendix E-3.4: USDA Food Patterns—Adequacy for Young Children). Intake falls in 
1172 
older children to about 30 percent of boys and girls meeting or exceeding the recommended 2.5 cup 
1173 
eqs per day for those ages 4 to 8 years and 3 cup eqs per day for children ages 9 to 13 years. About 30 
1174 
percent of adolescent boys meet or exceed the recommended 3 cup eqs per day, but less than 10 
1175 
percent of adolescent females meet or exceed this recommendation. An age-related decline in dairy 
1176 
intake appears to begin in adolescence and intakes persist at very low levels among adult females 
1177 
across the age distribution. Less than 5 percent of adult females consume the recommended 3 cup 
1178 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
32 
 
equivalents per day. Overall, more than 80 percent of the entire U.S. population does not meet the 
1179 
daily dairy intake recommendation.  
1180 
 
1181 
To determine the extent to which individuals could meet recommendations for calcium and other 
1182 
shortfall nutrients intake, given various levels of dairy foods in the Food Patterns, the 2015 DGAC 
1183 
conducted a food pattern modeling analysis (see Appendix E-3: Dairy Group and Alternatives). The 
1184 
DGAC considered nutrient adequacy of the Food Patterns under the following scenarios: 1) no dairy 
1185 
was consumed; 2) calcium was obtained from non-dairy sources (including fortified foods); and 3) the 
1186 
proportions of yogurt and cheese in the patterns were modified. The DGAC further evaluated the 
1187 
relationship between changes in the types of beverages consumed (milk, fruit juices, fruit drinks and 
1188 
sports beverages) and diet quality.  
1189 
 
1190 
If no dairy is consumed, the modeling analysis shows that levels of calcium, magnesium, iron, vitamin 
1191 
A and riboflavin, drop below 100 percent of goals, and intake levels of potassium, vitamin D and 
1192 
choline also drop substantially. When no dairy is consumed, calcium intake levels drop by 68 to 88 
1193 
percent in all age and sex groups, while vitamin D intake is lowered by 20 to 30 percent (see Appendix 
1194 
E-3.6: Dairy Group and Alternatives, Table 2). Most of the milk alternatives are fortified with 
1195 
calcium, so similar amounts of calcium can be obtained from fortified rice, soy and almond milks, and 
1196 
fortified juices, but absorption of calcium is less efficient from plant beverages.63 Magnesium intake 
1197 
also is comparable from plant-based milk alternatives. However, vitamin D and potassium amounts 
1198 
vary across these milk alternatives (see Appendix E-3.6: Dairy Group and Alternatives, Table 3). 
1199 
Calorie levels also are higher for most of the plant-based alternative milk products for a given calcium 
1200 
intake level. In other words, to obtain a comparable amount of calcium as one cup eq for non-fat fluid 
1201 
milk, the portion size required to meet the calcium intake need results in higher energy intake (see 
1202 
Appendix E-3.6: Dairy Group and Alternatives, Table 4).  
1203 
 
1204 
Currently, the U.S. population consumes the recommended 3 cup equivalents/day as 53 percent fluid 
1205 
milk, 45 percent cheese, and 2 percent as yogurt. Through the food pattern modeling, the DGAC 
1206 
examined the effect on nutrient intake if fluid milk were to be increased and cheese decreased. 
1207 
Increasing the proportion of fat-free milk, while decreasing the proportion of cheese, would increase 
1208 
the intake of magnesium, potassium, vitamin A, vitamin D and would decrease intake of sodium and 
1209 
saturated fat (see Appendix E-3.6: Dairy Group and Alternatives, Table 5). A potential approach to 
1210 
increasing intake of shortfall nutrients and nutrients of public health concern while simultaneously 
1211 
decreasing intake of overconsumed nutrients of public health concern would be to increase intake of 
1212 
fat-free or low-fat fluid milk in lieu of cheese. 
1213 
 
1214 
If milk is completely eliminated from the diet and replaced by soft drinks, fruit drinks, sports 
1215 
beverages, and other sugar-sweetened beverages, diet quality deteriorates significantly, making it very 
1216 
hard for individuals to meet nutrient recommendations (see Appendix E-3.6: Dairy Group and 
1217 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
33 
 
Alternatives, Table 6). Indeed, among U.S. adolescents’ milk consumption is very low as are intakes of 
1218 
the “shortfall” nutrients. 
1219 
 
1220 
Protein Foods. Protein Foods comprise a broad group of foods including meat, poultry, fish/seafood, 
1221 
eggs, soy,∞ nuts, and seeds. Dairy also contains protein, but since it has its own food group, its nutrient 
1222 
contributions are counted in its own group. The inclusion of both animal and non-animal protein foods 
1223 
allows vegetarian options to be accommodated. In addition to providing essential amino acids, some 
1224 
protein foods are important sources of iron, and iron is a shortfall nutrient and nutrient of public health 
1225 
concern among adolescent and adult females. Meat foods in the protein group provide heme iron, 
1226 
which is more bioavailable than non-heme plant-derived iron. Heme iron is especially important for 
1227 
young children and women who are pregnant.  
1228 
 
1229 
Nearly 80 percent of boys and 75 percent of girls ages 1 to 3 years meet or exceed the protein foods 
1230 
recommendation of 2 ounce equivalents per day (Figure D1.20). Similarly, more than 60 percent of 
1231 
boys and girls ages 4 to 8 years meet or exceed the recommended intake of 3 to 4 oz  eqs/day. Intake 
1232 
declines somewhat for boys and girls ages 9 to 13 years, as approximately 40 percent and 45 percent 
1233 
meet or exceed the recommended 3 ounce equivalents/day. Although nearly 60 percent of adolescent 
1234 
males ages 14 to 18 years meet the 5.5 to 6.5 oz eq/day recommendation, less than 25 percent of 
1235 
females ages 14 to 18 meet their 5-5.5 oz eq/day recommendation. Intakes begin to increase again for 
1236 
adult males across the age distribution, and about 62 percent of males ages 31 to 50 and 78 percent of 
1237 
males 51 to 70 years meet the 5.5-6.5 oz eq/day intake recommendation. For adult females ages 19 to 
1238 
30 years, slightly more than 40 percent meet the 5 to 5.5 oz eq/day recommendation and approximately 
1239 
50 percent of those ages 31 to 50 and about 50 percent of those 51 to 70 years meet the 
1240 
recommendation. Protein foods intake declines in both men and women older than age 71 years; about 
1241 
30 percent of women and about 50 percent of men meet the recommendation. Across all age groups 
1242 
and in both males and females, nearly 60 percent of the U.S. population meets the protein foods intake 
1243 
recommendation. Although some groups in the U.S. population do not consume recommended 
1244 
amounts from the protein foods group, intakes of protein (as grams/day) are adequate across the 
1245 
population and protein is not a shortfall nutrient. Notably, protein intake also comes from dairy and 
1246 
grains in addition to the foods included in the protein foods group. 
1247 
 
1248 
Most of the protein foods intake across all age groups and for both males and females comes from 
1249 
meat, poultry, and eggs (Figure D1.21). Nearly 80 percent of the U.S. population meets the intake 
1250 
recommendation for this protein foods subgroup (although less so for adolescent girls and older 
1251 
women).  
1252 
 
1253 
                                                 
∞ Soy foods in the Protein Foods group include foods and ingredients such as tofu, soy noodles, soy flours, and soy protein 
isolates. Fortified soymilk is part of the Dairy group. Edamame and whole soybeans are part of the vegetable legume 
subgroup. 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
34 
 
In 2010, the DGAC recommended that seafood intake be increased to eight ounces per week for adults. 
1254 
In reviewing the WWEIA/NHANES data, the DGAC 2015 found that the U.S. population has low 
1255 
seafood intake. Across all age groups and for both males and females, only 10 percent of the 
1256 
population meets the 2010 intake recommendations (Figure D1.22). Intake is highest in adult men and 
1257 
women, but remains very low. In the highest intake group, males ages 51 to 70 years, 21 percent of the 
1258 
population meets the intake recommendation.  
1259 
 
1260 
In addition to reviewing WWEIA/NHANES data, the 2015 DGAC considered the potential influence 
1261 
on diet quality of substituting seafood for terrestrial animal foods (e.g., beef, poultry, pork, game 
1262 
meats). This question was addressed by the 2010 DGAC through a modeling analysis, and the 2015 
1263 
DGAC decided to bring forward those modeling results. These results indicate seafood could be 
1264 
increased to 8 ounces/week (for adults) with no negative impact on nutrient adequacy.55app E3.10 This 8 
1265 
oz amount contributes energy, protein, selenium, vitamin D, and vitamin B-12. With respect to fatty 
1266 
acids, fish is rich in the long-chain eicosapentanoic acid (EPA) and docosahexonoic acid (DHA) and 
1267 
has a higher proportion of total fatty acids coming from polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fatty 
1268 
acids relative to saturated fatty acids. The 2015 DGAC also has examined the sustainability of fish 
1269 
production and consumption, and these results are discussed in Part D. Chapter 5: Food Sustainability 
1270 
and Safety. 
1271 
 
1272 
Nuts, seeds, and soy. Nuts, seeds, and soy provide protein, selenium, polyunsaturated fatty acids, 
1273 
fiber, magnesium, and zinc. Nuts, seeds, and soy are less commonly consumed protein foods (Figure 
1274 
D1.23). Even so, overall approximately 40 percent of the U.S. population meets or exceeds the food 
1275 
pattern recommended intake of these protein foods.  
1276 
 
1277 
Empty calories. Solid fats that occur naturally in foods such as meat, dairy, and some tropical foods 
1278 
(e.g.,  coconut), and sugars that are added to foods either by the consumer or by food manufacturers are 
1279 
referred to as “empty calories” because both provide calories, but few or no nutrients. For the purposes 
1280 
of the USDA Food Pattern Food Groups, the term solid fats and added sugars is an analytic grouping, 
1281 
but going forward for 2015, the DGAC has elected to use the term “empty calories.” 
1282 
 
1283 
Calories from solid fats and added sugars are included for the USDA Food Patterns because they are a 
1284 
component of the diet that should be limited because they are not nutrient-dense and the solid fats 
1285 
contribute to saturated fat intake, which is overconsumed in the U.S. population (see Nutrient 
1286 
Intake/Nutrients of Concern section, Questions 1 and 2). Solid fats and added sugars are not food 
1287 
groups on their own, as are protein foods, dairy, grains, fruits, and vegetables, but they are included in 
1288 
the Food Patterns because they are an integral component of many foods consumed by the U.S. 
1289 
population either because they occur naturally (in the case of some solid fats) or they are added to 
1290 
foods, such as added sugars or fat added during processing, cooking, or other aspects of food 
1291 
preparation. Additional details about added sugars and saturated fat are provided in Part D. Chapter 6: 
1292 
Cross-Cutting Topics of Public Health Importance.  
1293 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
35 
 
 
1294 
Because added sugars and solid fats are not nutrient dense and solid fats contribute to saturated fat 
1295 
intake, the USDA Food Patterns recommend that intake be limited. The guidance on the approximate 
1296 
amounts of solid fats and added sugars that can be part of a healthful diet is as follows: children ages 2 
1297 
to 8 years: 120 calories/day; children 9 to 13 years: 120 to 250 calories/day; girls ages 14 to 18 years: 
1298 
120 to 250 calories/day; boys ages 14 to 18: 160 to 330 calories/day; adult women: 120 to 250 
1299 
calories/day; and adult men: 160 to 330 calories/day. Intake limits varies by age and sex and are based 
1300 
on residual calories after all food group intakes are met. The intake limits include solid fats and added 
1301 
sugars from all sources in the diet: from sugar in sugar-sweetened beverages, including coffee and tea, 
1302 
and breakfast cereals, to solid fats in burgers, sandwiches, and pizza, to the combination of solid fats 
1303 
and added sugars in snacks and desserts such as cookies, cakes, ice cream, and donuts. Question 11 of 
1304 
the Food Categories section of this Chapter provides information on food sources of solid fats and 
1305 
added sugars. 
1306 
 
1307 
The intake of solid fats and added sugars is very high across all age groups and for both males and 
1308 
females in the United States, with nearly 90 percent exceeding the recommended daily limits (Figure 
1309 
D.1.24). Particularly noteworthy is that nearly 100 percent of boys and girls ages 1 to 3 and 4 to 8 
1310 
years exceed the recommended limit for solid fats and added sugars (see Part B. Chapter 6: Cross 
1311 
Cutting Topics of Public Health Importance).  
1312

Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee. USDA and HHS, 2010, Appendix E3.7. Available from: 
1328 
http://www.cnpp.usda.gov/sites/default/files/dietary_guidelines_for_americans/AppendixE-3-7-
1329

1331 
Advisory Committee. U.S. HHS and USDA, 2005, appendix G-2. Available from:

1333 
n. 
1334 
 
1335 
 
1336 
Question 8:  What are the trends in USDA Food Pattern food group consumption by 
1337 
the U.S. population? 
1338 
Source of Evidence: Data analysis   
1339 
 
1340 
Conclusion  
1341 
The U.S. population has made few dietary changes over time: 
1342 
• 
Fruit intake has remained low but stable.  
1343 
• 
Vegetable intake has declined, particularly among children of all ages, adolescents, and young 
1344 
adult males.  
1345 
• 
Whole grain intake has slightly increased between 2001-2004 and 2007-2010, particularly 
1346 
among middle aged and older adults.  
1347 
• 
Dairy intake has been relatively constant over time, but has decreased for girls ages 4 to 8 years 
1348 
and young adult males, and has increased for adults ages 51 to 70 years.  
1349 
• 
Added sugars intake has decreased for both males and females across all age groups between 
1350 
2001-2004 and 2007-2010, but intakes still exceed the limit in the USDA food patterns. 
1351 
Implications 
1352 
Individuals and families must make conscious and focused decisions about choosing nutrient-dense 
1353 
foods. In addition, to continue progress toward consumption of a healthy diet among all age and sex 
1354 
groups, action is needed along the entire food processing, delivery, and service supply chain in order to 
1355 
provide the U.S. population with affordable and accessible foods that are nutrient dense and low in 
1356 
added sugars and sodium.  
1357 
 
1358 
Poor nutritional intake is linked to numerous diet-related chronic diseases (see Part D. Chapter 2: 
1359 
Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes) and the prevalence of these conditions 
1360 
is too high in the United States (see Health Conditions section, Questions 15 to 17, below). The health 
1361 
of the nation hinges in part on improving dietary intake at individual and population levels, and 
1362 
changes in line with those suggested here could have a measurable positive impact on the health of the 
1363 
population.  
1364 
 
1365 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
37 
 
Given the complexity of dietary behavior change, consumers will need access to evidence-based 
1366 
educational resources and intervention programs and services in public health and healthcare settings 
1367 
to facilitate adoption and maintenance of healthy dietary behaviors. (See Part D. Chapter 3: 
1368 
Individual Diet and Physical Activity Behavior Change for discussion of what works at the level of 
1369 
individual behavior change.) In addition, these efforts should be complemented with research-driven 
1370 
environmental strategies that make access to affordable healthy foods possible in retail, community, 
1371 
worksite, and educational settings. (See Part D. Chapter 4: Food Environment and Settings for 
1372 
discussion of effective environmental approaches to promote dietary change across the lifespan.)  
1373 
 
1374 
Review of the Evidence  
1375 
This question was answered using data from WWEIA, NHANES dietary survey data and the National 
1376 
Cancer Institute’s examination of usual intake distributions for 2001-200464 and 2007-2010.41 
1377 
 
1378 
Fruit. Fruit intake remained relatively stable across the 2001-2004 and 2007-2010 time periods 
1379 
(Figure D1.25). The only group with significant changes over time was males ages 31 to 50 years, for 
1380 
whom mean fruit intake decreased.  
1381 
 
1382 
Vegetables. Vegetable intake declined from 2001-2004 to 2007-2010 (Figure D1.26). Across the 
1383 
overall population, the mean daily vegetable intake significantly declined. Significant declines in mean 
1384 
intake occurred among males ages 1 to 3, 4 to 8, 9 to 13, 14 to 18, and 19 to 30 years. For females, 
1385 
significant decreases in mean vegetable intake occurred for those ages 1 to 3, 4 to 8, and 9 to 13 years.  
1386 
 
1387 
Grains (whole and refined). Whole grain intake significantly increased among the overall population 
1388 
between 2001-2004 and 2007-2010 (Figure D1.27). Among males, significant increases in mean intake 
1389 
occurred for those ages 1 to 3, 4 to 8, 14 to 18, 31 to 50, and 51 to 70 years. Among females, 
1390 
significant increases in mean whole grain intake occurred for those ages 9 to 13, 19 to 30, 31 to 50, 51 
1391 
to 70, and 71 years and older (Figure D1.27). Similarly, refined grain intake has declined in all age and 
1392 
sex groups between 2001-2004 and 2007-2010 (Figure D1.28). 
1393 
 
1394 
Dairy. Dairy intake remained stable over the entire population between 2001-2004 and 2007-2010 
1395 
(Figure D1.29). Significant declines in mean daily intake occurred between the two time periods for 
1396 
males ages 19 to 30 years and females ages 4 to 8 years. Significant increases in mean daily dairy 
1397 
intake occurred for both males and females ages 51 to 70 years. 
1398 
 
1399 
Protein Foods. Protein food intake remained relatively stable for the U.S. population between 2001-
1400 
2004 and 2007-2010 (Figure D1.30). Females ages 31 to 50 and 51 to 70 years had significantly higher 
1401 
mean intake in 2007-2010 compared to 2001-2004. These were the only groups with any significant 
1402 
change over time. 
1403 
 
1404 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
38 
 
Added Sugars. Some improvements have been made in added sugars intake, with noticeable declines 
1405 
in mean intakes for all age groups and among both males and females when comparing 2007-2010 data 
1406 
with 2001-2004 data (Figure D1.31). As seen in Figure D1.31, intakes of added sugars are still very 
1407 
high, however, and are well above recommended limits, but the improvements provide some optimism 
1408 
for improved diets.  
1409 
 
1410 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
1411 
• Usual Dietary Intakes: Food Intakes, US Population, 2007-10: Applied Research Program. 
1412 
National Cancer Institute; [updated May 22, 2014]. Available from: 
1413 
http://appliedresearch.cancer.gov/diet/usualintakes/pop/2007-10/.

• Usual Dietary Intakes: Food Intakes, US Population, 2001-04: Applied Research Program. 
1416

1420 
FOOD CATEGORIES—CURRENT INTAKES AND SOURCES OF ENERGY, 
1421 
NUTRIENT, AND FOOD GROUP INTAKES 
1422 
The food sources of nutrients and the patterns in which they are consumed are informative in 
1423 
identifying strategies to modify dietary intake and eating behaviors and help Americans to choose and 
1424 
consume higher quality diets. We examined four questions related to the foods that are top contributors 
1425 
to intakes of energy, food groups, and selected nutrients in the U.S. diet. This section describes those 
1426 
food sources and the implications for meeting recommended or optimal intakes of various food groups 
1427 
and nutrients. 
1428 
 
1429 
Question 9: What are current consumption patterns by food categories (i.e., foods as 
1430 
consumed) in the U.S. population? 
1431 
Source of evidence:  Data analysis 
1432 
 
1433 
Conclusion  
1434 
The mixed dishes food category, which includes foods commonly used as entrees, such as sandwiches, 
1435 
burgers, pizza, pasta or rice mixed dishes, stir-fries, soups, and meat or poultry mixed dishes, is the 
1436 
major contributor to three USDA Food Pattern food groups—grains, vegetables, and protein foods. 
1437 
Fruit and fluid milk intake are seldom consumed as part of mixed dishes. The mixed dishes food 
1438 
category contributes heavily to intake of energy, saturated fat, and sodium; however, mixed dishes do 
1439 
provide vegetables, fiber, grains, and dairy. 
1440 
 
1441 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
39 
 
Implications 
1442 
An important strategy for meeting recommended intake levels of calories, saturated fat, and sodium is 
1443 
to change the composition of mixed dishes that are high in calories, saturated fat, and sodium to better 
1444 
meet these nutrition goals. Food manufacturers and the food service sector (e.g., restaurants, schools) 
1445 
should reformulate mixed dishes to improve their nutritional profiles. Americans should be encouraged 
1446 
to modify recipes to lower the sodium and saturated fat content when cooking, to use appropriate 
1447 
portion sizes, and choose reformulated mixed dish options when available. 
1448 
 
1449 
Review of the Evidence  
1450 
These conclusions were reached by examining data from the WWEIA Food Categories for the 
1451 
NHANES 2009-2010 dietary survey.65 The WWEIA Food Categories provide an application that 
1452 
allows analysts to examine foods and beverages as consumed in the U.S. diet. Each food or beverage 
1453 
item (as consumed) that is included in WWEIA is placed in one of 150 mutually exclusive food 
1454 
categories. The focus of this categorization system is on grouping similar foods and beverages together 
1455 
based on usage and nutrient content.  
1456 
 
1457 
An adaptation of the food categories was used by the 2015 DGAC for this analysis related to the 
1458 
“sandwiches and burgers” and “salads” categories. We placed all food items reported to be eaten as a 
1459 
sandwich, burger, taco, or salad item into the “sandwiches and burgers” or the “salads” categories 
1460 
regardless of whether the components were reported as separated ingredients or as a single combined 
1461 
item. For example, a food reported as a “cheeseburger” (a single item) would always be classified in 
1462 
the category of “burgers, sandwiches, and tacos,” but a food reported as the individual food items of a 
1463 
hamburger bun, a hamburger patty, and cheese, eaten as a combination, would have been classified in 
1464 
the categories of  “rolls and buns,” “ground meat,” and “cheese.”  The adaptation recoded these 
1465 
individually reported foods that were eaten in combination to “burgers, sandwiches, and tacos.” By 
1466 
doing this, the categories used for this analysis more fully represented foods as consumed rather than 
1467 
as ingredients.  
1468 
 
1469 
The 150 categories from WWEIA were condensed into 9 major and 32 sub-categories for analysis of 
1470 
the percent of total intake for energy, nutrients, and food groups from each major and sub-category 
1471 
(see Appendix E-2.7: Major categories and subcategories used in DGAC analyses of WWEIA Food 
1472 
Categories). Analysis was conducted for the population ages 2 and older as a whole; analysis of the 
1473 
percent of energy intake also was conducted for males and females ages 2 to 5, 6 to 11, 12 to 19, 20 to 
1474 
40, 41 to 50, 51 to 70, and 71 years and older; for race/ethnic groups including Non-Hispanic Whites, 
1475 
Non-Hispanic Blacks, and Hispanics ages 2 years and older; and for those with incomes less than or 
1476 
equal to 185 percent, or greater than 185 percent of the Poverty Index Ratio by three age groups: 2 to 
1477 
11, 12 to 19, and 20 years and older. 
1478 
 
1479 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
40 
 
WWEIA data show that Americans consume a substantial amount of foods in the form of mixed dishes 
1480 
(Figure D1.32). More specifically, 31 percent of vegetables, 45 percent of grains, 30 percent of dairy, 
1481 
and 45 percent of protein foods come from mixed dishes. Mixed dishes (which include foods such as 
1482 
sandwiches, burgers, pizza, pasta or rice mixed dishes, stir-fries, soups, and meat or poultry mixed 
1483 
dishes) make up 28 percent of total energy intake. Of note, only small amounts of fruits (1 percent) and 
1484 
fluid milk (3 percent) are consumed in mixed dishes—most are consumed as single food items, such as 
1485 
an apple or glass of milk (see Appendix E-2.8: Percent of total food group intake, 2009-2010, for 
1486 
U.S. population ages 2 years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories). 
1487 
 
1488 
When mixed dishes contribute to dairy foods, the majority of intake is in the form of cheese. Data 
1489 
show that about two-thirds of all cheese intake is from mixed dishes such as pizza, burgers, 
1490 
sandwiches, and casseroles. Given that cheese is generally higher in saturated fat and sodium and 
1491 
lower in potassium and vitamin D than is fluid milk (see Question 7b, above, and Appendix E-3.6: 
1492 
Dairy Group and Alternatives), modifying the types of cheese products used in these mixed dishes to 
1493 
lower fat and sodium versions would improve their nutritional profile. 
1494 
 
1495 
When mixed dishes contribute to the grains group, a larger percentage of refined (48 percent) than 
1496 
whole (19 percent) grains are consumed as part of these dishes. Substitution of whole for refined grains 
1497 
in mixed dishes such as burgers, sandwiches, pizza, and casseroles containing pasta or rice could 
1498 
improve the nutritional profile of grains that are consumed this way. 
1499 
 
1500 
Although mixed dishes account for a substantial amount of intake of some overconsumed nutrients (43 
1501 
percent of sodium, 36 percent of saturated fat), they also account for 28 percent of fiber, 29 percent of 
1502 
calcium, 24 percent of potassium, and 16 percent of vitamin D, all of which are underconsumed 
1503 
nutrients. Other food categories that contribute substantially to overall energy, sodium, saturated fat, 
1504 
and added sugars intake are discussed in the following two questions—Question 10: “What are the top 
1505 
foods contributing to energy intake in the U.S. population?” and Question 11: “What are the top foods 
1506 
contributing to sodium, saturated fat, and added sugars intake in the U.S. population?” 
1507 
 
1508 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
1509 
• 
What We Eat in America. Food Categories for the NHANES 2009-2010 dietary survey. Available 
1510 
from: http://seprl.ars.usda.gov/Services/docs.htm?docid=23429.  
1511 
• 
Appendix E-2.7: Major categories and subcategories used in DGAC Analyses of WWEIA Food 
1512 
Categories  
1513 
• 
Appendix E-2.8: Percent of total food group intake, 2009-10 for U.S. population ages 2 years and 
1514 
older

Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
41 
 
Question 10: What are the top foods contributing to energy intake in the U.S. 
1518 
population? 
1519 
Source of evidence:  Data Analysis 
1520 
 
1521 
Conclusion  
1522 
Seventy-five percent of total energy intake in the U.S. population comes from 16 of the 32 food sub-
1523 
categories, with mixed dishes, snacks and sweets, and beverages together contributing to more than 
1524 
half (56 percent) of energy intake in the U.S. population. 
1525 
 
1526 
Implications 
1527 
The foods with the highest contribution to energy intake are burgers, sandwiches, and tacos; desserts 
1528 
and sweet snacks; and sugar-sweetened beverages. Given the link to energy intake, reduced 
1529 
consumption of these foods and beverages or modifying the ways these foods are prepared, as well as 
1530 
consumption of smaller portion sizes, may help prevent excess weight gain or may help with weight 
1531 
reduction. 
1532 
 
1533 
Public health strategies (e.g., programs, regulations, and policies) and product reformulation are 
1534 
needed to help individuals achieve recommendations.  
1535 
 
1536 
Review of the Evidence  
1537 
These conclusions were reached by examining data from the WWEIA Food Categories for the 
1538 
NHANES 2009-2010 dietary survey,65 as described in relation to  question 9 (current consumption 
1539 
patterns by food categories  in the U.S. population). 
1540 
 
1541 
The top foods contributing to energy intake in the U.S. population are concentrated in several food 
1542 
categories, as shown in Figure D1.33. Three food categories  account for more than half (56 percent) of 
1543 
all energy consumed: 1) Mixed dishes (which include foods such as sandwiches, burgers, pizza, pasta 
1544 
or rice mixed dishes, stir-fries, soups, and meat or poultry mixed dishes); 2) snacks and sweets, which 
1545 
includes foods such as chips, cakes, pies, cookies, doughnuts, ice cream, and candy.), and 3) beverages 
1546 
other than milk and 100% fruit juice (such as soft drinks, fruit drinks, coffee and tea, and alcoholic 
1547 
beverages)  
1548 
 
1549 
Examining energy intake from the more specific 32 food subcategories shows that almost half of total 
1550 
energy intake comes from just 7 of these sub-categories (Table D1.12): Burgers and sandwiches (13.8 
1551 
percent); desserts and sweet snacks (8.5 percent); sugar-sweetened beverages (6.5 percent); rice, pasta, 
1552 
and grain-based mixed dishes (5.5 percent); chips, crackers, and savory snacks (4.6 percent); pizza (4.3 
1553 
percent); and meat, poultry, and seafood mixed dishes (3.9 percent). Further examination of the 32 
1554 
subcategories shows that 75 percent of all energy intake comes from the 7 subcategories previously 
1555 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
42 
 
described, plus vegetables (including starchy vegetables), alcoholic beverages, yeast breads and 
1556 
tortillas, whole and 2 percent milk and yogurt, breakfast cereals and bars, poultry, and candy and 
1557 
sugars. 
1558 
 
1559 
As noted in Question 9, (current consumption patterns by food categories in the U.S. population), some 
1560 
of the food sub-categories that provide substantial amounts of energy also provide underconsumed 
1561 
food groups and nutrients. On the other hand, several of these subcategories, notably desserts and 
1562 
sweet snacks and sugar-sweetened beverages, tend to contribute to energy intake with little 
1563 
contribution to underconsumed food groups (see Appendix E-2.8: Percent of total food group intake, 
1564 
2009-2010, for the U.S. population ages 2 years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories) and 
1565 
nutrients (see Appendix E-2.9: Percent of total energy and nutrient intake, 2009-2010, for the U.S. 
1566 
population ages 2 years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories), but major contributions to one or 
1567 
more overconsumed food components (see Question 11: What are the top foods contributing to 
1568 
sodium, saturated fat, and added sugars intake in the U.S. population?)  
1569 
 
1570 
Analysis of the food sources of energy by age and sex groups showed the expected higher percent of 
1571 
energy from dairy among children, especially young children, but no other major differences. Analysis 
1572 
by racial/ethnic groups and by income groups did not show major differences (see Appendix 2.10: 
1573 
Percent of total energy intake, 2009-2010, for age/sex groups of the U.S. population, from WWEIA 
1574 
Food Categories, Appendix E-2.11: Percent of total energy intake, 2009-2010, for racial/ethnic 
1575 
groups of the U.S. population, from WWEIA Food Categories, and Appendix E-2.12: Percent of 
1576 
total energy intake, 2009-2010, for age/income groups of the U.S. population, from WWEIA Food 
1577 
Categories). 
1578
