1596 
 
1597 
Question 11: What are the top foods contributing to sodium, saturated fat, and added 
1598 
sugars intake in the U.S. population? 
1599 
Source of Evidence: Data analysis  
1600 
 
1601 
Conclusion  
1602 
Mixed dishes are the largest contributor to intake of sodium (44 percent) and saturated fat (38 percent). 
1603 
Sodium and saturated fat have both been identified as nutrients of concern for overconsumption. 
1604 
Within mixed dishes, the sub-category of burgers and sandwiches is the largest contributor for both 
1605 
nutrients. 
1606 
 
1607 
Sodium is ubiquitous in the food supply and many food categories contribute to intake. 
1608 
Beverages supply 47 percent of added sugars intake.  
1609 
 
1610 
Snacks and sweets also are a major contributor to added sugars (31 percent) and saturated fat intake 
1611 
(18 percent).  
1612 
 
1613 
Implications 
1614 
To decrease dietary intake from added sugars, the U.S. population should reduce consumption of 
1615 
sugar-sweetened beverages and of desserts and sweet snacks.  
1616 
 
1617 
The U.S. population can use a variety of strategies to reduce consumption of sodium, saturated fat, and 
1618 
added sugars, including smaller portion sizes, reduced frequency of consumption, and recipe 
1619 
modification.  
1620 
 
1621 
Given the ubiquity of sodium in the food supply, concerted efforts to reduce sodium in commercially 
1622 
prepared and processed foods, as well as encouragement of home cooking using recipes with small 
1623 
amounts of sodium are needed to decrease intake toward recommended levels.  
1624 
 
1625 
Review of the Evidence  
1626 
These conclusions were reached by examining data from the WWEIA Food Categories for the 
1627 
NHANES 2009-2010 dietary survey,65 as described in relation to Question 9 (current consumption 
1628 
patterns by food categories in the U.S. population). 
1629 
 
1630 
The category of mixed dishes contributes substantially more saturated fat (36 percent) and sodium (43 
1631 
percent) to diets of the U.S. population than does any other category. Within this category, the largest 
1632 
share of both saturated fat (19 percent) and sodium (21 percent) comes from the subcategory of 
1633 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
44 
 
burgers, sandwiches, and tacos. The other subcategories that also contribute notable amounts of 
1634 
saturated fat and sodium are pizza (approximately 6 percent for both); rice, pasta, and other grain-
1635 
based mixed dishes (5 percent and 7 percent); and meat, poultry, and seafood mixed dishes (5 percent 
1636 
and 7 percent). Soups contribute a notable amount of sodium (4 percent) but less saturated fat (1 
1637 
percent). (Figures D1.34 and D1.35). 
1638 
 
1639 
Other food categories contributing substantial amounts of saturated fat include snacks and sweets (18 
1640 
percent), protein foods (15 percent), and dairy (13 percent). Within snacks and sweets, the subcategory 
1641 
providing the largest share is desserts and sweet snacks (12 percent). Within protein foods, saturated 
1642 
fat comes from meats, in general (3 percent), deli and cured meats and poultry (3 percent), poultry (3 
1643 
percent), and eggs (3 percent), with seafood and nuts, seeds, and soy each contributing less than 3 
1644 
percent. Within the dairy category, higher fat (whole and 2 percent) milk and yogurt (7 percent) and 
1645 
cheese (4 percent) contribute the most saturated fat. 
1646 
 
1647 
Sodium is more ubiquitous in the food supply than are other nutrients, and the food categories 
1648 
contributing the highest amounts of sodium include protein foods (14 percent), grains (11 percent), 
1649 
vegetables (11 percent), and snacks and sweets (8 percent). Sodium is distributed throughout many 
1650 
food categories and subcategories with the exception of fruits and fruit juice, which are notably low in 
1651 
sodium (0.1 percent). 
1652 
 
1653 
The distribution of added sugars in foods as consumed differs from saturated fat and sodium (Figure 
1654 
D1.36) The vast majority of added sugars intake comes from the major categories of beverages (not 
1655 
including milk and 100% fruit juice) (47 percent) and snacks and sweets (31 percent). Grains, 
1656 
including breakfast cereals and bars, contribute 8 percent, mixed dishes contribute 6 percent, and dairy, 
1657 
including sweetened flavored milks and yogurts contribute only 4 percent of total added sugars intake 
1658 
(see Appendix E-2.8: Percent of total food group intake, 2009-2010, for the U.S. population ages 2 
1659 
years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories).  
1660 
 
1661 
Four additional questions were examined using the WWEIA Food Categories data. They are: 
1662 
11a. What is the current contribution of fruit products with added sugars to intake of added sugars?  
1663 
11b. What is the current contribution of vegetable products with added sodium to intake of sodium? 
1664 
11c. What is the current contribution of refined grains to intake of added sugars, saturated fat, some 
1665 
forms of polyunsaturated fat, and sodium? 
1666 
11d. What are the sources of caffeine from foods and beverages on the basis of age and sex categories? 
1667 
 
1668 
With regard to Question 11a, the DGAC found that: 
1669 
• 
Less than 1 percent of total added sugars come from fruits and 100% fruit juice foods 
1670 
(including fresh, canned, frozen, dried fruit and fruit salads) (see Appendix E-2.8: Percent of 
1671 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
45 
 
total food group intake, 2009-2010, for the U.S. population ages 2 years and older, from 
1672 
WWEIA Food Categories). 
1673 
 
1674 
With regard to Question 11b, the DGAC found that: 
1675 
• 
11 percent of total sodium comes from all vegetables (with starchy vegetables), including beans 
1676 
and peas, vegetable mixtures, lettuce salads, pasta sauces, and vegetable juice (see Appendix E-
1677 
2.9: Percent of total energy and nutrient intake, 2009-2010, for the U.S. population ages 2 
1678 
years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories).  
1679 
• 
When vegetables are categorized by starchy or non-starchy, we found that: 
1680 
o 7 percent of total sodium comes from all vegetables, excluding starchy vegetables, and 
1681 
o 4 percent comes from starchy vegetables, including French fries and other fried potatoes, 
1682 
mashed potatoes, all other potatoes, corn, and other starchy vegetables. 
1683 
 
1684 
With regard to Question 11c:  
1685 
• 
The DGAC could not directly determine the contribution of refined grains to the nutrients of 
1686 
interest with the currently available data. However, the food categories that make up more than 
1687 
90 percent of all refined grain intake (i.e., burgers, sandwiches, and tacos; breads and tortillas; 
1688 
rice and pasta mixed dishes; desserts and sweet snacks; pizza; chips, crackers, and savory 
1689 
snacks; quick breads; rice and pasta; and meat, poultry, and seafood mixed dishes) account for: 
1690 
o 28 percent of all added sugars intake 
1691 
o 47 percent of all saturated fat intake 
1692 
o 50 percent of all sodium intake  
1693 
(see Appendix E-2.8: Percent of total food group intake, 2009-2010, for the U.S. population 
1694 
ages 2 years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories and Appendix E-2.9: Percent of total 
1695 
energy and nutrient intake, 2009-2010 for the U.S. population ages 2 years and older, from 
1696 
WWEIA Food Categories) 
1697 
 
1698 
With regard to Question 11d, the DGAC found that (Figure D1.37): 
1699 
• 
Among children and adolescents, sugar-sweetened and diet beverages and coffee and tea 
1700 
contribute to overall caffeine intake at approximately equal levels.  
1701 
• 
Among adults, the primary sources of caffeine from all foods and beverages are coffee and tea. 
1702 
 
1703 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
1704

46 
 
• 
What We Eat in America. Food Categories for the NHANES 2009-10 dietary survey. Available 
1705 
from: http://seprl.ars.usda.gov/Services/docs.htm?docid=23429.  
1706 
• 
Appendix E-2.7: Major categories and subcategories used in DGAC analyses of WWEIA Food 
1707 
Categories

1711 
ages 2 years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories  
1712 
 
1713 
 
1714 
Question 12: What is the contribution of beverage types to energy intake by the U.S. 
1715 
population? 
1716 
Source of evidence: Data analysis  
1717 
 
1718 
Conclusion  
1719 
 
1720 
Beverages contribute 19 percent of total energy intake. Of this 19 percent of energy, major sources are 
1721 
sugar-sweetened beverages (35 percent), milk and milk drinks (26 percent), and 100% fruit juices (10 
1722 
percent).  
1723 
 
1724 
Implications 
1725 
The beverages that contribute the most to energy intake, particularly sugar-sweetened beverages, are 
1726 
those that are not nutrient dense and could be targeted for reduction. Others, like milk, fortified low-
1727 
and non-fat milk, and milk beverage are good sources of key nutrients. Modifying the types of 
1728 
beverages consumed can reduce calories (e.g., switching from sugar-sweetened beverages to water) or 
1729 
improve nutrient intakes (e.g., switching from sugar-sweetened beverages to low-fat or fat-free milk). 
1730 
This may be an important strategy for individuals who need to reduce their energy intake and/or 
1731 
control their weight. Public health strategies (e.g., programs, regulations, and policies) are needed to 
1732 
reduce consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages.  
1733 
 
1734 
Strategies are needed to encourage the U.S. population to drink water when they are thirsty. Water 
1735 
provides a healthy, low-cost, zero-calorie beverage option. Free, clean water should be available in 
1736 
public settings, as well as child care facilities, schools, worksites, publically funded athletic stadiums 
1737 
and arenas, transportation hubs (e.g., airports) and other community places and should be promoted in 
1738 
all settings where beverages are offered. 
1739 
 
1740 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
47 
 
Review of the Evidence  
1741 
These conclusions were reached by examining data from the WWEIA Food Categories data from the 
1742 
NHANES 2009-2010 dietary survey,65 as described in relation to question 9 (current consumption 
1743 
patterns by food categories  in the U.S. population). For this question, a new grouping of all beverages, 
1744 
including fluid milk and 100% fruit juice, was created. The conclusions and details below are based on 
1745 
this category of all beverages (see Appendix E-2.7: Major categories and subcategories used in 
1746 
DGAC analyses of WWEIA Food Categories). 
1747 
 
1748 
All beverages account for about one-fifth (19 percent) of total energy intake. Within that amount, 
1749 
about one-third (35 percent) is from sugar-sweetened beverages, mostly soft drinks and sweetened fruit 
1750 
drinks (see Appendix E-2.9: Percent of total energy and nutrient intake, 2009-2010, for the U.S. 
1751 
population ages 2 years and older, from WWEIA Food Categories). About 20 percent of the calories 
1752 
from beverages come from alcoholic beverages (21 percent), and milk and milk drinks made with 
1753 
whole and 2 percent fat (18 percent). About 10 percent of the calories from beverages come from 
1754 
100% fruit and vegetable juice (10 percent), fat-free and low-fat milk and milk drinks (8 percent), and 
1755 
coffee and tea (8 percent) (Figure D1.38). 
1756

1765 
 
1766 
EATING BEHAVIORS—CURRENT STATUS AND TRENDS  
1767 
Diet quality and energy balance directly affect health and weight status. Eating behaviors, such as 
1768 
when people eat (e.g., patterns of meals and snacks, meal and snack frequency), meal skipping, and the 
1769 
locations where food is obtained and consumed (e.g., retail and restaurants) influence dietary intake 
1770 
and quality. Assessing and understanding eating behaviors of the U.S. population can shed light on 
1771 
ways to improve food choices, weight status, and health outcomes of Americans.  
1772 
 
1773 
Question 13: What are the current status and trends in the number of daily eating 
1774 
occasions and frequency of meal skipping? How do diet quality and energy content 
1775 
vary based on eating occasion? 
1776 
Source of evidence:  Data analysis  
1777 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
48 
 
 
1778 
Conclusion  
1779 
The majority of the U.S. population consumes three meals a day plus at least one snack. Children ages 
1780 
2 to 5 years are most likely to consume three meals a day and adolescent females, young adult males, 
1781 
non-Hispanic Blacks, Hispanics, and individuals with lower incomes are least likely to consume three 
1782 
meals a day. Trend data from 2005-2006 to 2009-2010 show little change in meal and snack intake 
1783 
patterns. 
1784 
 
1785 
Breakfast tends to have a higher overall dietary quality because of its higher nutrient density compared 
1786 
to other meals and snacks. Adolescents and young adults are the least likely to eat breakfast. Snacks 
1787 
contribute about one-fourth of daily energy intake for the U.S. population and are lower in nutrients of 
1788 
concern relative to energy intake than are meals. For young children ages 2 to 5 years, 29 percent of 
1789 
daily energy is from snacks. 
1790 
 
1791 
Implications 
1792 
Understanding eating behaviors is important for designing and implementing strategies to reduce 
1793 
obesity and other diet-related chronic diseases and for improving overall health. Breakfast eating is 
1794 
associated with more favorable nutrient intakes compared to nutrient intakes from other meals or 
1795 
snacks. Adolescents and young adults are the least likely to eat breakfast, and targeted promotion 
1796 
efforts are needed to reach these groups. For children and adolescents, the school breakfast program is 
1797 
an important venue for promoting breakfast consumption and efforts are needed to increase student 
1798 
participation rates.  
1799 
 
1800 
Americans are frequent snackers and snacks contribute substantially to daily energy intake and tend to 
1801 
be lower than meals in shortfall nutrients of public health concern relative to energy intake. Because 
1802 
snack foods and beverages are readily available and accessible in multiple settings throughout the day, 
1803 
both population-level environmental changes and individual behavioral interventions and 
1804 
communications are needed to ensure that healthy choices are available in these settings and to 
1805 
minimize their contribution to excess energy intake.  
1806 
 
1807 
Individuals with lower incomes are less likely to eat three meals a day compared to higher income 
1808 
individuals and low-income households are more likely to be food insecure. The federal nutrition 
1809 
programs play a key role in reducing food insecurity and improving nutritional health.  
1810 
 
1811 
Review of the Evidence 
1812 
These conclusions were reached by examining existing WWEIA NHANES data tables,5 from 
1813 
NHANES 2009-2010 for current intakes, and WWEIA, NHANES 2003-2004, 2005-2006 and 2007-
1814 
2008 data for trends. Respondents self-identified the specific meal or snack occasion for each food and 
1815 
beverage consumed.  
1816 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
49 
 
 
1817 
Eating Occasions: Meals. Three meals a day is the current norm for most of the U.S. population ages 
1818 
2 years and older, with almost two-thirds (63 percent) eating breakfast, lunch, and dinner (Figure 
1819 
D1.39). However, there are differences by age, sex, racial/ethnicity group, and income level. By age 
1820 
group, consuming three meals a day follows a modest U-shaped curve where it is most likely for 
1821 
children ages 2 to 5 years (84 percent). It then declines, and reaches its lowest point during 
1822 
adolescence and young adulthood, and then increases with age through the adult years. Adolescent 
1823 
females (12 to 19 years) and young adult males (20 to 29 years) are the most likely to not eat three 
1824 
meals a day (49 percent). For all other age/sex groups, eating three meals a day is reported by 59 to 73 
1825 
percent of respondents. Eating only one meal a day is most likely for young adult males (12 percent) 
1826 
and adolescent females (10 percent). However, all but 1 percent of these respondents, consumed at 
1827 
least two or more snacks a day (Table D1.13).  
1828 
 
1829 
Among the U.S. population ages 2 years and older, 15 percent do not eat breakfast, 20 percent do not 
1830 
eat lunch, and 7 percent do not eat dinner. Breakfast is most likely to be skipped by young adults ages 
1831 
20 to 29 years (28 percent of males, 22 percent of females) and adolescents (25 percent of females, 26 
1832 
percent of males). Breakfast skipping declines sharply with advancing age. Lunch is not eaten by 25 
1833 
percent of adolescent females and from 17 to 28 percent of all adult age groups (Table D1.14). 
1834 
 
1835 
Non-Hispanic whites are most likely to report consuming three meals a day, across all 
1836 
age/sex/racial/ethnic groups, with 68 percent reporting breakfast, lunch, and dinner consumption. For 
1837 
non-Hispanic Blacks, slightly less than half (48 percent) consumed all three meals, and for all 
1838 
Hispanics, slightly more than half (52 percent). Non-Hispanic Blacks ages 12 to 19 years and 20 years 
1839 
and older, and Hispanics ages 12 to 19 years, were least likely to consume three meals a day (42 
1840 
percent, 45 percent, and 45 percent, respectively) and most likely to consume only one meal a day (18 
1841 
percent, 11 percent, and 10 percent).66 
1842 
 
1843 
The percent of individuals consuming three meals a day increases with higher income levels. For those 
1844 
below 131 percent and from 131 to 185 percent of the poverty threshold, 53 percent and 56 percent 
1845 
report three meals a day, while for those above 185 percent of the threshold, 70 percent report three 
1846 
meals a day. For lower income individuals, the lower number of meals consumed per day is much 
1847 
more evident for older children and adults. Among children ages 2 to 5 years in the three income 
1848 
groupings, 81 percent, 82 percent, and 88 percent, respectively, report consuming three meals a day, 
1849 
while for adults ages 20 years and older, the corresponding percentages are 48 percent, 54 percent, and 
1850 
70 percent, respectively.67 
1851 
 
1852 
Eating Occasions: Snacks. Nearly all of the U.S. population ages 2 years and older consume at least 
1853 
one snack a day (96 percent). The most common snacking pattern for most age, sex, racial/ethnic and 
1854 
income groups is two to three snacks per day. Females and males ages 70 years and older are most 
1855 
likely to report eating one or fewer snacks per day (26 percent), and children ages 2 to 5 years are the 
1856 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
50 
 
least likely (10 percent). Children ages 2 to 5 years are most likely of any age group to report four or 
1857 
more snacks per day, across all racial/ethnic groups.68 
1858 
 
1859 
The number of individuals reporting one or fewer snacks per day is highest (25 percent) for those 
1860 
below 131 percent of the poverty threshold, and lowest (17 percent) for those above 185 percent of the 
1861 
threshold. Consumption of four or more snacks per day is lowest (25 percent) for those below 131 
1862 
percent of the poverty threshold and highest (35 percent) for those above 185 percent of the threshold. 
1863 
However, for all income groups, 2 to 3 snacks per day is the modal number and similar across income 
1864 
groups (51 percent, 48 percent, 48 percent).67 
1865 
 
1866 
Trends. Trend data from NHANES from 2005-2006 to 2009-2010 show little change in number of 
1867 
daily eating occasions or frequency of meal skipping (Table D1.15). 
1868 
 
1869 
Diet Quality and Energy content by Eating Occasion. For this analysis, diet quality is defined as a 
1870 
comparison of nutrient or food group content to energy content of a specified set of foods or beverages. 
1871 
In this question, diet quality compares the proportion of total nutrient intake at a given eating occasion 
1872 
to the proportion of energy intake at that eating occasion. 
1873 
 
1874 
This analysis is summarized in Figure D1.40 and described below. In looking at this Figure, it should 
1875 
be noted that percent of total intake of nutrients of concern are shown in comparison to percent of total 
1876 
energy. If a nutrient is above the energy line, the meal/snack is a relatively higher source of that 
1877 
nutrient. If it is below the energy line, it is a relatively lower source.  
1878 
 
1879 
Breakfast has a higher overall diet quality compared to lunch, dinner or snacks. Breakfast consists of 
1880 
15 to 20 percent of the day’s total energy intake (Table D1.16) but has a higher percent of nutrients. 
1881 
For all the shortfall nutrients of public health concern (fiber, folate, vitamin D, calcium, iron, and 
1882 
potassium), a higher percent of the day’s total intake was consumed compared to the percent of energy 
1883 
consumed (Figure D1.40) 
1884 
 
1885 
Among the U.S. population ages 2 years and older, about one fourth (24 percent) of daily energy intake 
1886 
is consumed at lunch and about one-third (35 percent) is consumed at dinner (Table D1.16). In terms 
1887 
of dietary quality, lunch is neutral, with similar percents of total nutrients and energy intakes for most 
1888 
nutrients. Dinner, which provides the greatest amount of daily total energy intake, has a higher percent 
1889 
of fiber, and potassium in comparison to percent energy, but calcium and several other nutrients are 
1890 
lower in comparison to percent energy. Sodium and saturated fat are higher as a percent of their total 
1891 
intakes than is energy intake. Further, the percent of total daily intake of sodium and saturated fat 
1892 
consumed at dinner is higher compared to other meals and snacks (Figure D1.40). 
1893 
 
1894 
About one-fourth (24 percent) of daily energy intake comes from snacks. For young children ages 2 to 
1895 
5 years, 29 percent of daily energy is from snacks (Table D1.17). Snacks provide the lowest percent of 
1896 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
51 
 
key nutrients (protein, iron, vitamin D, fiber, and potassium) relative to the percent of energy provided. 
1897 
Snacks provide 42 percent of the daily intake of added sugars. A lower percent of total sodium than of 
1898 
energy is provided by snacks. Snacks provide roughly the same percent of total intake of calcium as 
1899 
they do energy. This is also true of saturated fat for females (Table D1.17).  
1900 
 
1901 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
1902 
• 
Percent of the U.S. population consuming or skipping meals and snacks, 2001-2002, 2005-2006, 
1903 
2007-2008, and 2009-2010 by age/sex groups, race/ethnicity, and percent of the poverty threshold. 
1904 
Available from: http://seprl.ars.usda.gov/Services/docs.htm?docid=18349.  
1905 
• 
Percent of total energy and nutrient intake by meal/snack, 2001-2002, 2005-2006, 2007-2008 and 
1906 
2009-2010 by age/sex groups, race/ethnicity, and percent of the poverty threshold. Available from: 
1907 
http://seprl.ars.usda.gov/Services/docs.htm?docid=18349. 
1908 
 
1909 
 
1910 
Question 14:  What are the current status and trends in the location of meal and snack 
1911 
consumption and sources of food and beverages consumed at home and away from 
1912 
home? How do diet quality and energy content vary based on the food and beverage 
1913 
source? 
1914 
Source of evidence: Data analysis 
1915 
 
1916 
Conclusion  
1917 
About two-thirds of the calories consumed by the U.S. population are purchased at a store (69 percent), 
1918 
such as a grocery store or supermarket, and consumed in the home. The percent of calories eaten away 
1919 
from home (32 percent) has remained about the same since 2003-2004. 
1920 
 
1921 
Food group and nutrient quality as measured by the Healthy Eating Index (HEI) vary by where food is 
1922 
obtained. Despite this, no matter where the food is obtained, diet quality of the U.S. populations does 
1923 
not meet recommendations for fruit, vegetables, dairy, whole grains, and exceeds recommendations for 
1924 
sodium, saturated fats, refined grains, solid fats, and added sugars.  
1925 
 
1926 
Implications 
1927 
The overall diet quality of the U.S. population’s dietary patterns, regardless of where the food is 
1928 
purchased and eaten, is of major public health concern. Given that fruit, vegetables, dairy, and whole 
1929 
grains are consumed in less than recommended amounts and that sodium, saturated fats, refined grains, 
1930 
solid fats, and added sugars exceed recommended levels, urgent action is needed at individual and 
1931 
population levels to alter food purchasing and consumption habits.  
1932 
 
1933 
Efforts are needed by the food industry and food retail (food stores and restaurants) sectors to market 
1934 
and promote healthy foods. The general public needs to be encouraged to purchase these healthier 
1935 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
52 
 
options. Making healthy options the default choice in restaurants (e.g., fat-free/low-fat milk instead of 
1936 
sugar-sweetened beverages, and fruit and non-fried vegetables in Children’s Meals, whole wheat buns 
1937 
instead of refined grain buns for sandwich meals) would facilitate the consumption of more nutrient 
1938 
dense diets. Food manufacturers and restaurants should reformulate foods to make them lower in 
1939 
overconsumed nutrients (sodium, added sugars and saturated fat) and calories and higher in whole 
1940 
grains, fruits and vegetables.  
1941 
 
1942 
In addition, Federal regulations for food labeling need to be updated. Food labels are an important tool 
1943 
to enable the public to follow the Dietary Guidelines and to make healthy food choices. They provide 
1944 
consumers with quick, easy to use information about the food they are purchasing. They also lead food 
1945 
companies to reformulate their food products to meet consumer demand. As recently proposed by the 
1946 
FDA, updates are needed in the Nutrition Facts label on packaged foods to emphasize calories, serving 
1947 
sizes, and nutrients of concern (including overconsumed nutrients such as sodium). Consumers also 
1948 
may benefit from a standardized Front of Pack label that gives clear guidance such as proposed by the 
1949 
IOM panel on FOP labeling.69 
1950 
 
1951 
In addition to regulatory, policy, environmental and organizational changes, individual behavioral 
1952 
strategies are also needed to help Americans improve dietary behaviors. Comprehensive lifestyle 
1953 
interventions in a variety of settings and nutrition counseling by professionals in health care settings 
1954 
can modify dietary behaviors and improve health outcomes. 
1955 
 
1956 
Review of the Evidence  
1957 
This conclusion was reached by examining a new analysis of WWEIA, NHANES food intake data, 
1958 
from WWEIA NHANES 2009-2010 for current status, and WWEIA NHANES 2003-2004, 2005-2006 
1959 
and 2007-2008 for trends (see Appendix E-2.13: Percent of energy intake from major points of 
1960 
purchase and location of eating, 2003-2004, 2005-2006, 2007-2008, 2009-2010, for the U.S. 
1961 
population ages 2 years and older and Appendix E-2.14: Food group and nutrient content of foods 
1962 
per 1000 calories obtained from major points of purchase, 2003-2004, 2005-2006, 2007-2008, 2009-
1963 
2010, for the U.S. population ages 2 years and older). This analysis was requested by the DGAC to 
1964 
answer the question. In addition, the DGAC reviewed the ERS publication Nutritional Quality of Food 
1965 
Prepared at Home and Away from Home, 1977-200870 to ascertain longer-term trends. 
1966 
 
1967 
Respondents self-identified the food source (point of purchase) for each food or beverage they 
1968 
reported. For this analysis, food sources were grouped into the following categories: stores (grocery, 
1969 
supermarket, convenience/corner stores), full-service restaurants (defined as table service restaurants), 
1970 
quick-serve restaurants (includes fast food, counter service, and vending machines), school (includes 
1971 
child care). The location of eating, either at home or away from home, also was examined (Figure 
1972 
D1.41). 
1973 
 
1974 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
53 
 
Americans increased their away-from-home share of caloric intake from 18 percent in 1977-1978 to 32 
1975 
percent in 2005-2008, mainly from full service and fast food restaurants.70 The percent of calories 
1976 
eaten away from home has remained roughly the same since 2003-2004. In 2009-2010, 69 percent of 
1977 
calories consumed by Americans were purchased from a store and 58 percent were eaten at home. This 
1978 
is about the same percent from 2003-2008 (Figure D1.41). 
1979 
 
1980 
Diet quality was assessed using a density approach expressed as the amount of food group or nutrient 
1981 
per 1000 calories consumed, for each source from which food is obtained. The point of purchase (e.g., 
1982 
food store) is used as a proxy for where the food is consumed (e.g., home) because most food from 
1983 
stores are consumed at home, and most foods from other points of purchase are consumed away from 
1984 
home. Diet quality for a food group or nutrient for each food source obtained/consumed was then 
1985 
compared to the standard for a optimal HEI score per 1000 calories.71  For saturated fat intake, the 
1986 
amount from each source was compared to the 2010 Dietary Guidelines limit for saturated fat intake. 
1987 
 
1988 
Fruit. Fruit group density (cups per 1000 calories) is well below the HEI standard regardless of where 
1989 
the food is obtained or consumed. Amounts of fruit obtained and consumed differ by source, with full 
1990 
service and fast-food restaurants providing much less fruit per 1000 calories compared to other 
1991 
sources. This changed little from 2003-2004 to 2009-2010. Amount of fruit per 1000 calories is highest 
1992 
from schools/day care, and increased from 2003-2004 to 2009-2010, especially from 2007-2008 on 
1993 
(Figure D1.42). 
1994 
 
1995 
Vegetables. Density for vegetables (cups per 1000 calories) falls below recommended intakes 
1996 
regardless of where food is obtained (Figure D1.43). Amounts of total vegetables and the starchy and 
1997 
other vegetable subgroups are shown in Figures D1.43 and D1.44. (Other vegetables are those not in 
1998 
the dark green, red orange, or starchy subgroups, such as green beans, iceberg lettuce, onions, cabbage, 
1999 
cucumbers.) Amounts of total vegetables and other vegetables per 1000 calorie are highest for 
2000 
restaurants, especially full service restaurants, with a slight downward trend from 2007-2008 to 2009-
2001 
2010 (Figures D1.43 and D1.44). Amounts of total vegetables and starchy vegetables per 1000 calories 
2002 
from schools/daycare show a suggestive decrease in 2009-2010 compared to earlier years. Density for 
2003 
all vegetable subgroups by source for 2003-2004 through 2009-2010 are listed in Table D1.18.  
2004 
 
2005 
Dairy. Amounts of total dairy products (fluid milk, cheese, and yogurt) are highest from schools/day 
2006 
care sources and are above the HEI standard, with an increase from 2007-2008. Amounts from other 
2007 
sources are far below recommendations (Figure D1.45). 
2008 
 
2009 
Whole and refined grains. Whole grain density per 1000 calories is far below the HEI standard and is 
2010 
low for all food sources with little change since 2003-2004. On the other hand, refined grains exceed 
2011 
the HEI limit for all food sources, with the highest amount coming from quick serve restaurants 
2012 
(Figure D1.46). 
2013 
 
2014 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
54 
 
Protein foods. Amounts of total protein foods per 1000 calories are above the HEI standard for full 
2015 
service restaurants and fast food restaurants (Figure D1.47). 
2016 
 
2017 
Sodium. Amounts of sodium per 1000 calories are well above the HEI limit and do not differ greatly 
2018 
across sources. However, the density from full service and fast food restaurants are somewhat higher 
2019 
than from stores. There has been little change from 2003-2004 to 2009-2010 (Figure D1.48). 
2020 
 
2021 
Saturated fats. Amounts of saturated fat per 1000 calories is well above the Dietary Guidelines limit 
2022 
and do not differ greatly across sources. However, the density from fast food restaurants is somewhat 
2023 
higher than from stores. There has been little change from 2003-2004 to 2009-2010 (Figure D1.49). 
2024 
 
2025 
Empty calories. (defined as the total calories from solid fats and added sugars). Empty calories are 
2026 
well above the HEI limit (190 calories per 1000 calories) for all food sources, with the highest amount 
2027 
from fast food restaurants, but no large differences among sources. Empty calories have trended 
2028 
downward since 2003-2004 (Figure D1.50). The HEI does not have a separate HEI standard for added 
2029 
sugars and solid fats. Both added sugars and solid fats have decreased since 2003-2004. (Figures 
2030 
D1.51, D1.52)  The highest amounts of added sugars are obtained from stores and the highest amounts 
2031 
of solid fats are obtained from fast food restaurants. 
2032 
 
2033 
Food group density by age group. For children ages 2 to 5 years, fruit group density per 1000 
2034 
calories from schools and stores reaches the HEI standard. School foods provide the highest fruit 
2035 
density among all food sources for 6-11 year olds, with an increase since 2007-2008. All other age 
2036 
groups do not reach the HEI standard for fruit from any source, although the store location is 
2037 
consistently the top source for adults. Vegetable density from full service restaurants reaches the HEI 
2038 
standard for ages 51-70 and 71 years and older. All sources of vegetables are below the standard for 
2039 
children, adolescents and adults under age 50. Dairy product density from child care and stores meet 
2040 
the HEI standard for children ages 2-5 and from schools for children ages 6-19. School foods provide 
2041 
the highest dairy product density among all food sources in children’s diets. For school age children 
2042 
and adolescents, school foods are the only food source that meets the recommended amount of dairy 
2043 
products. Among adults, dairy product density is low for all sources. For children ages 6-11, there is a 
2044 
difference in the added sugars density by source, with schools having less added sugars per 1000 
2045 
calories than other sources. This difference is not as clear for younger children or adolescents. For 
2046 
adults the highest amount of added sugars per 1000 calories is from stores. For most age groups, there 
2047 
is a slight downward trend, especially in the density of added sugars from stores (see Appendix E-
2048 
2.15:  Amount of key nutrients and food groups by age group per 1000 calories from each point of 
2049 
purchase, 2003-2004, 2005-2006, 2007-2008, and 2009-2010). 
2050 
 
2051 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
2052 
• 
Appendix E-2.13: Percent of energy intake from major points of purchase and location of eating, 
2053 
2003-2004, 2005-2006, 2007-2008, and 2009-2010, for the U.S. population ages 2 years and older  
2054 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
55 
 
• 
Appendix E-2.14: Food group and nutrient content of foods per 1000 calories obtained from major 
2055

Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
56 
 
PREVALENCE OF HEALTH CONDITIONS AND TRENDS  
2064 
Preventable, diet- and lifestyle-related chronic diseases, including high blood pressure, CVD, type 2 
2065 
diabetes, and certain cancers, contribute to the high and rising costs of U.S. health care. Adults with 
2066 
overweight or obesity frequently have co-morbid conditions and higher chronic disease risk profiles 
2067 
that contribute substantially to higher health care costs. These health problems are persistent in the 
2068 
population and pose major public health concerns. Increasing rates of overweight and obesity among 
2069 
American youth have resulted in rising rates of CVD risk factors, including borderline high blood 
2070 
pressure and diabetes, in this population. Health disparities in risk profiles and disease rates are evident 
2071 
across racial, ethnic, and income strata. In a new health care and public health vision, prevention of 
2072 
chronic diseases and other lifestyle-related health problems would become a major focus. Examining 
2073 
the status and trends in these health conditions provides a framework for discussing their relationship 
2074 
to dietary intake and lifestyle factors and can help in identifying evidence-based strategies for 
2075 
prevention. 
2076 
 
2077 
Question15:   What is the current prevalence of overweight/obesity and distribution of 
2078 
body weight, BMI, and abdominal obesity in the U.S. population and in specific age, 
2079 
sex, racial/ethnic, and income groups? What are the trends in prevalence?  
2080 
Source of evidence:  Data analysis 
2081 
 
2082 
Conclusion  
2083 
The current rates of overweight and obesity are extremely high among children, adolescents, and 
2084 
adults. These high rates have persisted for more than 25 years. 
2085 
 
2086 
Overall, 65 percent of adult females and 70 percent of adult males are overweight or obese, and rates 
2087 
are highest in adults ages 40 years and older. Rates of overweight and obesity in adults vary by age and 
2088 
race/ethnicity.  
2089 
• 
Overweight (excluding obesity) is most prevalent in those ages 40 years and older, and in 
2090 
Hispanic American adults.  
2091 
• 
Obesity is most prevalent in those 40 years of age or older and in African American adults. 
2092 
Obesity is least prevalent in adults with highest incomes (400+ percent the poverty threshold).  
2093 
 
2094 
Abdominal obesity is present in U.S. adults of all ages, increases with age, and varies by sex and 
2095 
race/ethnicity. 
2096 
• 
Abdominal obesity rates are highest in individuals ages 60 years and older, and are higher in 
2097 
women than men at all ages. 
2098 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
57 
 
• 
In men, abdominal obesity rates are slightly higher among non-Hispanic whites than Mexican 
2099 
Americans or African Americans. In women, abdominal obesity rates are lower in non-
2100 
Hispanic whites than in Mexican Americans or African Americans. 
2101 
 
2102 
Nearly one in three youth (31 percent), ages 2 to 19 years, is now overweight (85th-94th percentile) or 
2103 
obese (>95th percentile) and these rates vary by age and ethnicity.  
2104 
• 
In youth ages 2 to 19 years, obesity prevalence increases with age, and the age category with 
2105 
the highest prevalence is 12-19 year olds. 
2106 
• 
In youth ages 2 to 19 years, the race categories with the highest prevalence of obesity are 
2107 
African Americans and Hispanics. 
2108 
 
2109 
Implications 
2110 
The persistent high levels of overweight and obesity require urgent population- and individual-level 
2111 
strategies across multiple settings, including health care, communities, schools, worksites, and 
2112 
families. 
2113 
 
2114 
Comprehensive lifestyle interventions and evidence-based dietary interventions for weight 
2115 
management in individuals and small groups should be developed and implemented by trained 
2116 
interventionists  and professional nutrition service providers in healthcare settings as well as in 
2117 
community locations, including public health facilities and worksites. 
2118 
 
2119 
Quality of care standards in health care settings should include the provision and impact of preventive 
2120 
nutrition services provided by multidisciplinary teams of trained interventionists, as appropriate, and 
2121 
nutrition professionals. Incentives should be offered to providers and systems to develop preventive 
2122 
services. 
2123 
 
2124 
The public should be encouraged to monitor their body weight and engage with their health care 
2125 
providers at least annually to assess their body weight and BMI. As appropriate, providers should use 
2126 
evidence-based approaches aimed at achieving and maintaining healthy body weight. Health care 
2127 
providers should encourage achieving and maintaining a healthy weight through healthy eating and 
2128 
physical activity behaviors.  
2129 
 
2130 
The persistent high rates of obesity across the lifespan show the limited impact of our efforts to date. 
2131 
Accelerating progress in reversing obesity trends will require a more targeted, comprehensive, and 
2132 
coordinated strategy and a renewed commitment and action for sustained, large-scale, integrated multi-
2133 
sectoral and cross-sectoral collaborations. Government at local, state, and national levels, the health 
2134 
care system, schools, worksites, community organizations, businesses, and the food industry all have 
2135 
critical roles in developing creative and effective solutions. 
2136 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
58 
 
 
2137 
Behavioral change at the individual level is important. However, policy interventions that make 
2138 
healthy dietary and activity choices easier, more routine, and affordable and that reduce unhealthy 
2139 
options are likely to achieve population-wide benefits. 
2140 
 
2141 
Age-appropriate nutrition and food preparation education should be a mandatory part of primary and 
2142 
secondary school curricula.  
2143 
 
2144 
Review of the Evidence  
2145 
To reach these conclusions, the DGAC examined evidence from NHANES 2009-2012, and additional 
2146 
survey years including 1988-1994 to 2011-2012 for trends data. These data are available in summary 
2147 
NHANES data table format on the CDC website, in published peer-reviewed articles by CDC,72-74 and 
2148 
in analyses requested by the DGAC and provided by CDC/NCHS (see Appendix E-2.16: Body mass 
2149 
index, adults ages 20 years and older, NHANES 2009-2012 and  Appendix E-2.17: Body mass index, 
2150 
children and adolescents ages 2-19 years, NHANES 2009-2012). 
2151 
 
2152 
The prevalence rates of overweight and obesity among U.S. adults have been extremely high for the 
2153 
past 25 years and appear to be at record high levels in women and to have plateaued at near record high 
2154 
levels in men (Figure D1.53). In 2009-2012, combined rates of overweight and obesity in adult men, 
2155 
ages 20 years and older, were 72.6 percent (38.1 percent for overweight and 34.5 percent for obesity) 
2156 
and 64.8 percent (28.8 percent for overweight and 36 percent for obesity) in women (Table D1.19). 
2157 
Rates of overweight and obesity in adults vary by age and ethnicity and are most pronounced in adults 
2158 
ages 40 years and older and in Hispanic and African American adults (Table D1.19). 
2159 
 
2160 
Overweight affects 29.5 percent of adults ages 20 to 39 years, 35.9 percent of adults ages 40 to 59 
2161 
years, and 35.7 percent of adults ages 60 years and older, while obesity affects 31.5 percent of adults 
2162 
ages 20 to 39 years, 38 percent of those ages 40 to 59 years, and 37.5 percent of those ages 60 years 
2163 
and older (Table D1.19).  
2164 
 
2165 
Overweight affects 31.7 percent of adult African American men and 24.5 percent of adult African 
2166 
American women, while obesity affects 37.9 percent of adult African American men and 57.5 percent 
2167 
of adult African American women. Among adult Hispanic men, overweight affects 41.5 percent and 
2168 
obesity affects 38.5 percent, and among adult Hispanic women, overweight affects 33.5 percent and 
2169 
obesity affects 43 percent (Table D1.19). 
2170 
 
2171 
Obesity is least prevalent (about 31 percent) in adults ages 20 years and older with highest incomes 
2172 
(400 + percent the poverty threshold) in 2007-2010 (Table D1.20), while affecting 37.2 percent of 
2173 
those with incomes below 100 percent of the poverty threshold, 37.3 percent of those with incomes 
2174 
from 100 percent to 199 percent of the poverty threshold, and 36.8 percent of those with incomes from 
2175 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
59 
 
200 percent to 399 percent of the poverty threshold (Table D1.20). Across all income strata, combined 
2176 
rates of overweight and obesity and particularly obesity rates have risen over the past 25 years.  
2177 
 
2178 
Abdominal obesity, as measured by waist circumference (WC), and defined as WC more than 102 cm 
2179 
in men and more than 88 cm in women, is a risk factor for CVD and diabetes .6 Abdominal obesity is 
2180 
prevalent in U.S. adults of all ages and varies by age and sex. In 2011-2012, overall rates of abdominal 
2181 
obesity were about 54 percent in adults ages 20 years and older, with a prevalence of about 44 percent 
2182 
in adult men and 65 percent in adult women (Table D1.21). Data from the NHANES 2007-2008 survey 
2183 
shows that men ages 20 to 39 years have the lowest rates of abdominal obesity (28.5 percent) 
2184 
compared to men ages 40 to 59 years (49.4 percent) and those ages 60 years and older (60.4 percent) 
2185 
(Table D1.21). Women ages 60 years and older have the highest rates of abdominal obesity (73.8 
2186 
percent) compared to women ages 40 to 59 and 20 to 39 years (65.5 percent and 51.3 percent, 
2187 
respectively). Data from the 2011-2012 survey show that the highest prevalence of abdominal obesity 
2188 
among men is in non-Hispanic white men (44.5 percent), followed by Mexican American men (43.2 
2189 
percent) and African American men (41.5 percent), while the highest prevalence among women is in 
2190 
African American women (75.9 percent), followed by Mexican American (71.6 percent) and non-
2191 
Hispanic white women (63.3 percent) (Table D1.21). For 2007-2010, the prevalence of abdominal 
2192 
obesity is very high in obese adults ages 18 years and older (97 percent), and overweight adults (57 
2193 
percent), compared to normal/underweight adults (8 percent).75  Since 1999 rates of abdominal obesity 
2194 
have risen in all age and racial strata of both adult males and females (Table D1.21). 
2195 
 
2196 
After increasing from the 1980s until about 2004, rates of overweight and obesity in children and 
2197 
adolescents ages 2 to 19 years have since remained at very high levels (Figure D1.54). A significant 
2198 
decrease in obesity among children ages 2 to 5 years old was observed in an analysis comparing the 
2199 
survey data from 2003-2004 (13.9 percent) to 2011-2012 (8.4 percent).74 However, it is not clear 
2200 
whether this comparison of only two time periods reflects an actual downward trend. Currently, 14.9 
2201 
percent of boys ages 2 to 19 years are overweight (85th to 94th percentile) and 17.6 percent are obese 
2202 
(95th percentile and greater); rates in girls ages 2 to 19 years are 14.9 percent and 16.1 percent, 
2203 
respectively. Furthermore, rates of obesity in youth increase with age and vary by ethnicity, with 
2204 
obesity found in 22.1 percent of African American and 21.8 percent of Hispanic Americans ages 2 to 
2205 
19 years (Table D1.22).  
2206 
 
2207 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:

Question 16:  What is the relative prevalence of metabolic and cardiovascular risk 
2213 
factors (i.e., blood pressure, blood lipids, and diabetes) by BMI/body weight/waist 
2214 
circumference (abdominal obesity) in the U.S. population and specific population 
2215 
groups?  
2216 
Source of evidence:  Data analysis  
2217 
 
2218 
Conclusion 
2219 
Approximately 50 percent of adults who are normal weight have at least one cardiometabolic risk 
2220 
factor. Approximately 70 percent of adults who are overweight and 75 percent of those who are obese 
2221 
have one or more cardiometabolic risk factors. 
2222 
 
2223 
Rates of elevated blood pressure, adverse blood lipid profiles (i.e., low high density lipoprotein 
2224 
cholesterol [HDL-C], high low density lipoprotein choldesterol [LDL-C], and high triglycerides), and 
2225 
diabetes are highest in adults with elevated abdominal obesity (waist circumference greater than 102 
2226 
cm in men, greater than 88 cm in women). 
2227 
 
2228 
Ninety-three percent of the children with type 2 diabetes are ages 12 to 19 years and 90 percent of 
2229 
these children with type 2 diabetes are overweight or obese. In children with type 2 diabetes, the 
2230 
prevalence of obesity is higher in African Americans, followed by American Indians and Hispanics, 
2231 
compared to non-Hispanic whites or Asian Pacific Islander youth. 
2232 
Dyslipidemia and rates of borderline high blood pressure vary by weight status in boys and girls; rates 
2233 
are particularly high in obese boys.  
2234 
Nearly three-fourths of the overweight or obese populations have at least one cardiometabolic risk 
2235 
factor.  
2236 
 
2237 
Implications 
2238 
The rates of cardiometabolic risk factors in adult Americans are extremely high and reflect the high 
2239 
rates of population overweight and obesity. Many adults have personal health profiles in which 
2240 
multiple metabolic risk factors co-exist and substantially increase risks for coronary heart disease, 
2241 
hypertension and stroke, diabetes, and other obesity-related co-morbidities. These are the most costly 
2242 
health problems in the Nation today and they can be prevented or better managed with intensive, 
2243 
comprehensive, and evidence-based lifestyle interventions carried out by multidisciplinary teams of 
2244 
trained professionals or through medical nutrition therapy provided by registered dietitians or 
2245 
nutritionists (AHA/ACC/TOS).2 Program plans and interventions needed to confront the nation’s 
2246 
obesity epidemic and its devastating metabolic consequences. A shift in the healthcare paradigm 
2247 
toward prevention is critical. Nutrition and lifestyle services for obesity prevention and weight 
2248 
management should be expanded and integrated. As part of this approach, quality of care guidelines 
2249 
need to be revised to incentivize the provision of personalized lifestyle and nutrition interventions to 
2250 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
61 
 
combat obesity and obesity-related chronic diseases and their metabolic risk factors and co-
2251 
morbidities. As emphasized in Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and Physical Activity Behavior 
2252 
Change and Part D. Chapter 4: Food Environment and Settings, the most effective approach to 
2253 
preventing and treating overweight and obesity in our nation across the lifespan requires both 
2254 
individual and population-based, environmental strategies. Initiatives in health care and public health 
2255 
and other government sectors should be complemented with collaborative approaches in retail, 
2256 
educational, and social service and agricultural settings to make the long-term adoption of healthy 
2257 
nutrition and lifestyle behavior not only feasible but normative.  
2258 
 
2259 
The high rates of overweight and obesity in youth and their concomitant cardiometabolic risk factors 
2260 
require early preventive interventions at individual and population levels. Evidence-based strategies in 
2261 
health and public health settings also should be implemented and complemented by environmental 
2262 
approaches across wide-ranging sectors to reverse these priority health problems.  
2263 
 
2264 
Review of the Evidence   
2265 
To reach these conclusions, the DGAC examined evidence from NHANES 2007-2010 and 2009-2012 
2266 
data and SEARCH for Diabetes in Youth Study (SEARCH). These data were available in published 
2267 
peer-reviewed articles by CDC,76 or SEARCH77 authors and in analyses requested by the DGAC and 
2268 
provided by CDC/NCHS (see Appendix E-2.18: Total cholesterol and high density lipoprotein 
2269 
cholesterol (HDL), adults ages 20 years and older, NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-2.19: Low 
2270 
density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and triglycerides, adults ages 20 years and older, NHANES 
2271 
2009-2012, Appendix E-2.20: Prevalence of high blood pressure, adults ages 18 years and older, 
2272 
NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-2.21: Total diabetes, adults ages 20 years and older, NHANES 
2273 
2009-2012, Appendix E-2.22: Total cholesterol, high density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL), and 
2274 
non-HDL-cholesterol, children and adolescents ages 6–19 years, NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-
2275 
2.23: Low density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and triglycerides, adolescents ages 12-19 years, 
2276 
NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-2.24: Prevalence of high and borderline high blood pressure 
2277 
(BP), children and adolescents ages 8-17 years, NHANES 2009-2012). 
2278 
 
2279 
In U.S. adults ages 18 years and older, weight status is related to prevalent CVD risk. About two-thirds 
2280 
(66.6 percent) of U.S. adults, including more than half (56.1 percent) of normal weight adults (BMI 
2281 
18.5-<25 kg/m2), have one or more CVD risk factors (including type I and type II diabetes, 
2282 
hypertension, or dyslipidemia, or self-reported smoking) (Figure D1.55). About 70 percent (69.6 
2283 
percent) of adults who are overweight (BMI 25-<30 kg/m2) have at least one or more CVD risk factors, 
2284 
making them candidates for preventive weight management interventions, according to expert 
2285 
guidelines established by the American College of Cardiology, American Heart Association, and The 
2286 
Obesity Society for preventative weight management (see Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods 
2287 
and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes). Furthermore, more than one-quarter (27.8 percent) have two or 
2288 
more CVD risk factors (Figure D1.55). About three-quarters (74.6 percent) of adults who are obese 
2289 
(BMI >30 kg/m2) have one or more CVD risk factors and about 39 percent have two or more CVD risk 
2290 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
62 
 
factors (Figure D1.55). Cardio-metabolic risk factors also are substantially more prevalent in adult men 
2291 
and women who have abdominal obesity (Table D1.23).  
2292 
 
2293 
In terms of plasma lipids, the prevalence of low HDL-C (<40 mg/dl), high LDL-C (≥160 mg/dl), and 
2294 
high triglycerides (≥ 200 mg/dl) is highest in obese adults (ages 20 years and older) compared to 
2295 
normal weight adults (Table D1.23). Similar patterns are observed in those who are overweight 
2296 
compared to normal weight adults (Table D1.23). These lipid profiles also are highest in men with 
2297 
abdominal obesity (> 102 cm) or women (>88 cm). (Table D1.23). High total cholesterol (≥ 200 
2298 
mg/dl), low HDL-C (<40 mg/dl), and high triglycerides (≥ 130 mg/dl) also are most prevalent in obese 
2299 
compared to overweight or normal weight children and adolescents (Table D1.24). There does not 
2300 
appear to be a difference in the prevalence of high LDL-C (≥ 130 mg/dl) by weight status in children 
2301 
and adolescents (Table D1.24).  
2302 
 
2303 
In adults ages18 years and older, rates of elevated blood pressure (defined as having measured systolic 
2304 
pressure of at least 140 mm Hg or diastolic pressure of at least 90 mm Hg and/or taking 
2305 
antihypertensive medication) are highest with obesity (39.2 percent) compared to normal weight (20 
2306 
percent) or overweight (26.4 percent). It is also highest in those with elevated waist circumferences 
2307 
(men > 102cm (37.2 percent vs 23.3 percent; and > 88 cm in women (32.9 percent vs 17.8 percent) 
2308 
(Table D1.23). Similar to adults, the rate of borderline high blood pressure (defined as a systolic or 
2309 
diastolic blood pressure ≥ 90th percentile but < 95th percentile or blood pressure levels ≥ 120/80 mm 
2310 
Hg) in youth ages 8 to 17 years was highest in with obesity (16.2 percent) compared to those who are 
2311 
normal weight (5.4 percent) or overweight (10.9 percent) (Table D1.25). Diabetes in adults ages 20 
2312 
years and above also increases with body mass index from 5.5 percent in those who are of normal 
2313 
weight, to 9 percent in overweight and 20.3 percent in obese adults and is more prevalent in those with 
2314 
abdominal obesity (men > 102cm (19.6 percent vs 8.3 percent); and > 88 cm in women (13.9 percent 
2315 
vs 2.6 percent) (Table D1.23).  
2316 
 
2317 
Data from 2001 to 2004 in children (ages 3 to 19 years) participating in the SEARCH for Diabetes in 
2318 
Youth Study (SEARCH) show that 93 percent of youth with type 2 diabetes are ages 12 to 19 years. 
2319 
The prevalence of obesity among youth with type 2 diabetes is 79.4 percent and an additional 10.4 
2320 
percent are overweight (Table D1.26). The percentage of overweight among youth with type 2 diabetes 
2321 
is not significantly different than rates in U.S. youth who do not have type 2 diabetes.77  However, the 
2322 
prevalence of obesity among youth with type 2 diabetes (79.4 percent) is much higher than in U.S. 
2323 
youth without type 2 diabetes (16.9 percent) (Table D1.26). The prevalence of obesity in those with 
2324 
type 2 diabetes was higher in African Americans (91.1 percent), followed by American Indians (88 
2325 
percent), and Hispanics (75 percent) in comparison to non-Hispanic white or Asian Pacific Islander 
2326 
youths (about 68 percent for each) (Table D1.26). 
2327

2344 
Question 17:   What are the current rates of nutrition-related health outcomes (i.e., 
2345 
incidence of and mortality from cancer [breast, lung, colorectal, prostate] and 
2346 
prevalence of CVD, high blood pressure, diabetes, bone health, congenital anomalies, 
2347 
neurological and psychological illness) in the overall U.S. population? 
2348 
Source of evidence:  Data analysis  
2349 
 
2350 
Conclusion 
2351 
Adults have high rates of nutrition-related chronic diseases, including high blood pressure, CVD, 
2352 
diabetes, and various forms of cancer. Children and adolescents also have nutrition-related chronic 
2353 
diseases, including borderline high blood pressure and type 2 diabetes. At all ages, rates of chronic 
2354 
disease risk are linked to overweight and obesity. The rates of these chronic diseases vary by 
2355 
race/ethnicity and income status. Prevalence of osteoporosis and of low bone mass increases with age, 
2356 
particularly in post-menopausal women. Among the less common health outcomes: 
2357 
• 
Nutrition-related neurological and psychological conditions are a growing concern. 
2358 
• 
Congenital anomalies are a relatively rare, but important pregnancy outcome. 
2359 
 
2360 
Implications  
2361 
Given the high rates of nutrition-related chronic diseases in the adult population and rising rates in 
2362 
youth, it is imperative to develop prevention policies and programs that target all age groups and 
2363 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
64 
 
address nutrition and lifestyle issues with evidence-based interventions that are appropriate for delivery 
2364 
in multiple settings. 
2365 
 
2366 
Qualified professionals should deliver multidisciplinary interventions and medical nutrition therapies, 
2367 
as appropriate, that are effective in reducing nutrition-related chronic diseases. 
2368 
 
2369 
More studies are needed to understand the complex etiology of congenital anomalies and neurological 
2370 
and psychological conditions, and factors that influence bone health as well as healthy outcomes of 
2371 
pregnancy so as to inform potential dietary choices by the U.S. population. 
2372 
 
2373 
Review of the Evidence  
2374 
To reach these conclusions, the DGAC examined evidence from NHANES  2007-2010 and 2009-2012 
2375 
(see Appendix E-2.18: Total cholesterol and high density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL), adults ages 
2376 
20 years and older, NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-2.19: Low density lipoprotein cholesterol 
2377 
(LDL-C) and triglycerides, adults ages 20 years and older, NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-2.20: 
2378 
Prevalence of high blood pressure, adults ages 18 years and older, NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix 
2379 
E-2.21: Total diabetes, adults ages 20 years and older, NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-2.22: Total 
2380 
cholesterol, high density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL), and non-HDL-cholesterol, children and 
2381 
adolescents ages 6-19 years, NHANES 2009-2012, Appendix E-2.23: Low density lipoprotein 
2382 
cholesterol (LDL-C) and triglycerides, adolescents ages 12-19 years, NHANES 2009-2012, 
2383 
Appendix E-2.24: Prevalence of high and borderline high blood pressure (BP), children and 
2384 
adolescents ages 8-17 years, NHANES 2009-2012), the National Health Interview Survey (NHIS) 
2385 
2012,78 SEARCH for Diabetes in Youth Study,79 American Heart Association, 2014 report,6 and the 
2386 
Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results (SEER) Program of the National Cancer Institute.80  The 
2387 
DGAC also examined evidence from CDC’s population-based birth defects surveillance system,81 
2388 
Alzheimer’s Association 2014 Facts and Figures,82 and published data by CDC authors.83  
2389 
 
2390 
Cardiovascular Diseases 
2391 
Cardiovascular diseases, including coronary heart disease, hypertension, and stroke, affect an 
2392 
estimated 83.6 million (35.3 percent) men and women ages 20 years and older in the United States.6  
2393 
CVD increases with age, meaning that about half of those with CVD, 42.2 million adults, are ages 60 
2394 
years and older.6  Rates of coronary heart disease also vary by race/ethnicity and income. Coronary 
2395 
heart disease is most prevalent in Hispanics (7.8 percent of those reporting the disease) and Native 
2396 
Americans (including Alaskan natives 12.5 percent) adults.78  Stroke is most prevalent in Native 
2397 
Americans (4.3 percent of those reporting the disease) and African Americans (3.9 percent).78  
2398 
Coronary heart disease rates are inversely related to income. Rates are about 9.8 percent and 7.7 
2399 
percent in those with lower income (less than 100 percent of the poverty threshold and 100 to 199 
2400 
percent, respectively) compared to those with higher income (200 percent and greater of the poverty 
2401 
threshold; 1.9 percent). Stroke also is more prevalent in those with incomes less than 100 percent of 
2402 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
65 
 
the poverty threshold (4.8 percent) and 100 to 199 percent of the poverty threshold (3.7 percent) 
2403 
compared to those with higher incomes (1.9 percent).78  
2404 
 
2405 
The prevalence of elevated blood pressure (measured systolic pressure of at least 140 mm Hg or 
2406 
diastolic pressure of at least 90 mm Hg and/or taking antihypertensive medication), in adults ages 18 
2407 
years and older (29 percent) is similar in adult men (29.8 percent) and women (28.3 percent) and varies 
2408 
by age and race/ethnicity (Table D1.27). Rates of elevated blood pressure are highest in adults ages 60 
2409 
years and older (66.3 percent), and African Americans (41.5 percent), relative to non-Hispanic whites 
2410 
(27.9 percent) or Hispanics (26.1 percent) (Table D1.27). A similar pattern is seen in youth ages 8 to 
2411 
17 years, with borderline high blood pressure in 8.3 percent overall (Table D1.25). Boys (12 percent) 
2412 
are much more likely to have borderline high blood pressure than are girls (4.6 percent), as are those 
2413 
ages 13 to 17 years (12.4 percent) compared to those ages 8 to 12 years (3.8 percent), and African 
2414 
Americans (12.1 percent) compared to non-Hispanic whites (7.2 percent) and Hispanics (8.5 percent) 
2415 
(Table D1.25).  
2416 
 
2417 
Diabetes 
2418 
Total diabetes (type I plus type II) is the sum of self-reported diabetes and undiagnosed diabetes. 
2419 
Diagnosed diabetes was obtained by self-report and excludes women who reported having diabetes 
2420 
only during pregnancy. Undiagnosed diabetes was defined as fasting plasma glucose of at least 126 
2421 
mg/dL or a hemoglobin A1C value of at least 6.5% and was not reported as a physician diagnosis. The 
2422 
prevalence of diabetes in U.S. adults, is 14 percent for men and 10.8 percent for women 20+ years of 
2423 
age (Table D1.27). Rates increase with age, to 26 percent for adults ages 60 years and older, and are 
2424 
higher in African Americans (18.4 percent) and Hispanics (19.3 percent) compared to non-Hispanic 
2425 
whites (9.8 percent) (Table D1.27). Between 2001 and 2009, rates of type 2 diabetes in children and 
2426 
adolescents ages 10 to 19 years increased 30.5 percent79 and the disease now affects  about 1 in 2,000 
2427 
youth (0.46 per 1000)  (Table D1.28 ). In 2009, type 2 diabetes appeared to be more common in girls 
2428 
than boys (0.58, vs. 0.35 /1000 youth), in older adolescents (ages15 to 19 years; 0.68) compared to 
2429 
those ages 10 to 14 years (0.23), and in American Indian (1.2), African American (1.06), and Hispanic 
2430 
(0.79) youth compared to non-Hispanic Whites (0.17) (Table D1.28).  
2431 
 
2432 
Nutrition-related Major Cancers  
2433 
Breast cancer: Breast cancer represents approximately 14 percent of all new cancer cases and 6.8 
2434 
percent of all cancer deaths in the United States. In 2011, an estimated 2,899,726 (2.9 million) women 
2435 
in the United States had a history of breast cancer. About 232,670 new cases of breast cancer and 
2436 
40,000 deaths from this disease are estimated for 2014. Breast cancer is the third leading cause of 
2437 
cancer death in the U.S.80, 84 New cases of breast cancer are highest in the middle age and older women 
2438 
(about 22, 25.5, and 21.3 percent of new cases occur in women ages 45 to 54, 55 to 64 and 65 to 74 
2439 
years, respectively) (Table D1.29) and in non-Hispanic white women (128/100,000 women per year), 
2440 
followed by African American (122.8/100,000 women). The death rate from this disease is also highest 
2441 
among women ages 55 to 84 years old (ranges 20.6 percent to 21.7 percent of deaths) and African 
2442

Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
66 
 
Americans (30.6 of death/100,000), followed by non-Hispanic white women (21.7/100,000) (Table 
2443 
D1.29).  
2444 
 
2445 
Prostate cancer: Prostate cancer represents approximately 14 and 5 percent of all new cancer cases 
2446 
and all cancer death, respectively in U.S. men. In 2011, an estimated 2,707,821 (2.7 million) men had a 
2447 
history of prostate cancer. About 233,000 new cases of prostate cancer and 29,480 deaths from this 
2448 
disease are estimated for 2014. Prostate cancer is the fifth leading cause of cancer death in the United 
2449 
States.84, 85  New cases of prostate cancer are most prevalent in older men (about 32.7, 36.3 and 16.8 
2450 
percent of new cases in men ages 55 to 64, 65 to 74, and 75 to 84 years, respectively) (Table D1.29) 
2451 
and African American (223.9 of new cases/100,000 men). The death rate from this disease is highest 
2452 
among men ages 75 to 84 years old (36.8 percent of deaths) and African Americans (48.9/100,000) 
2453 
(Table D1.29).  
2454 
 
2455 
Colorectal cancer: Colorectal cancer represents approximately 8.2 and 8.6 percent of all new cancer 
2456 
cases and all cancer death, respectively in the United States. In 2011, an estimated 1,162,426 (1.2M) 
2457 
adult men and women had a history of colorectal cancer. About 136,830 new cases of colorectal cancer 
2458 
and 50,310 deaths from this disease are estimated for 2014, respectively. Colorectal cancer is the 
2459 
second leading cause of cancer death in the United States.84, 86  The incidence (new cases) of this 
2460 
cancer is more common in men than women and is more common in those older than age 55 years 
2461 
(highest frequency observed among those ages 65 to 74 years (23.9 percent) (Table D1.29) and in 
2462 
African Americans (62.3 and 47.5 new cases/100,000 persons in African American men and women, 
2463 
respectively). The death rate from this disease also is highest in people older than age 55 years old 
2464 
(highest frequency observed among those ages 75 to 84 years old (27.3 percent of deaths) and in 
2465 
African American (27.7, and 18.5 deaths/100,000 persons in men and women, respectively) (Table 
2466 
D1.29).  
2467 
 
2468 
Lung and Bronchus cancer: Lung and bronchus cancer represents approximately 13.5 and 27.2 
2469 
percent of all new cancer cases and all cancer deaths, respectively in the United States. In 2011, an 
2470 
estimated 402,326 people had a history of lung and bronchus cancer. About 224,210 new cases of lung 
2471 
and bronchus cancer and 159,260 deaths from this disease are estimated in 2014, respectively. This 
2472 
cancer is the first leading cause of cancer death in the United States.84, 87 The incidence of lung and 
2473 
bronchus cancer is more common in men than women and is more common in those older than age 55 
2474 
years (highest frequency observed among those ages 65 to 74 years (31.7 percent) in African American 
2475 
men (93 new cases/100,000 persons), and in white women (53.8/100,000 persons) (Table D1.29). The 
2476 
death rate from this disease also is higher in older people (highest frequency observed among those 
2477 
ages 65 to 84 years (about 30 percent of deaths) and in African American men (75.7 deaths/100,000 
2478 
persons), and non-Hispanic white women (39.8/100,000 persons) (Table D1.29).

67 
 
Bone Health 
2481 
Approximately 10 million (10.3 percent) American adults ages 50 years and older were reported to 
2482 
have osteoporosis (defined as T-score ≤ -2.5 at either the femoral neck or the lumbar spine) and 43 
2483 
million (44 percent) to have low bone mass (defined as T-scores between -1.0 and -2.5 at either 
2484 
skeletal site) in NHANES 2005-2010 (Table D1.30). A higher percent of women are affected by 
2485 
osteoporosis (15 percent) and low bone mass (51 percent) than men (about 4 percent and 35 percent, 
2486 
respectively). Osteoporosis increases with advancing age, occurring in about 35 percent in women ages 
2487 
80 years and older compared to 26 percent in those ages 70 to 79 years old. The prevalence of low 
2488 
bone mass is similar in women ages 50 to 59 year and 80 years and older (ranges from 49 to 53 
2489 
percent). Osteoporosis and low bone mass are more prevalent in Mexican American (20 percent, 48 
2490 
percent) and non-Hispanic white (16 percent, 53 percent) relative to African American (8 percent, 36 
2491 
percent) women (Table D1.30).  
2492 
 
2493 
Congenital Anomalies  
2494 
Each year, about 3 percent (one in every 33 babies) is born with spina bifida (without anencephaly); 
2495 
cleft lip (with and without cleft palate), or cleft palate (without cleft lip).88 The estimated national 
2496 
prevalence of spina bifida was 3.17 per 10,000 live births in 1999-2007.81 During this same time 
2497 
period, the prevalence of having a baby with spina bifida was reported to be more common in Native 
2498 
Americans/Alaska Natives (4.02/10,000 live birth), followed by Hispanics (3.8/10,000), non-Hispanic 
2499 
whites (3.09/10,000), African-Americans (2.73/10,000), and Asian/Pacific Islanders (1.2/10,000).81 
2500 
The estimated national prevalence of cleft palate and cleft lip is 5.67 and 9.3 per 10,000 live birth, 
2501 
respectively.81 The prevalence of both of these congenital anomalies was highest in non-Hispanic 
2502 
Native Americans/Alaskan Natives (20/10,000 [cleft lip] and 6.5/10,000 [cleft palate]), and was lowest 
2503 
in African-Americans (6/10,000 [cleft lip] and 4.2/10,000 [cleft palate]).81  
2504 
 
2505 
Congenital heart defects affect about 40,000 births (about 1 percent of births) per year in the United 
2506 
States.89  The number of babies with congenital heart defects, especially those forms that are less 
2507 
severe (ventricular septal defects and atrial septal defects), is increasing compared to the total number 
2508 
of births, while the prevalence of other types has remained stable.89    
2509 
  
2510 
Neurological and Psychological Conditions  
2511 
There are numerous types of neurological and psychological conditions, and the DGAC focused only 
2512 
on depression and Alzheimer’s disease. The prevalence of depression was estimated at 8 percent for 
2513 
the U.S. population ages 12 years and older in the NHANES 2007-2010 survey.90 Depression is higher 
2514 
in females (10 percent) than in males (6 percent), and highest in those ages 40 to 59 years (12 percent 
2515 
women, 7 percent men).90 Depression also is reported to be higher in African Americans (8 percent), 
2516 
followed by Mexican-Americans (6.3 percent) and non-Hispanic whites (4.8 percent) (NHANES 2005 
2517 
-2006).91 
2518 
 
2519

68 
 
In 2014, about 3.2 million women and 1.8 million men in the United States, ages 65 years and older 
2520 
are reported to be living with Alzheimer’s disease.82 This disease is most prevalent in those ages 75 to 
2521 
84 years (44 percent of those with Alzheimer’s) and those ages 85 years and older (38 percent).82  
2522 
About 63, 59, and 30 percent of those ages 85 years and older with Alzheimer’s disease are reported to 
2523 
be Hispanics (primarily Caribbean-American), African Americans, and non-Hispanic white adults, 
2524 
respectively.82  It has been projected that the number of people with Alzheimer’s disease will increase 
2525 
by about  threefold from 4.8 million in 2010 to 13.7 million in 2050.92
