2570 
 
2571 
DIETARY PATTERNS COMPOSITION 
2572 
Dietary patterns with positive health benefits are described as high in vegetables, fruit, whole grains, 
2573 
seafood, legumes, and nuts; moderate in low- and non-fat dairy products; lower in red and processed 
2574 
meat; and low in sugar-sweetened foods and beverages and refined grains. The primary dietary 
2575 
patterns examined and described in Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and 
2576 
Health Outcomes included both a priori, investigator-derived scoring systems such as DASH/OMNI, 
2577 
Mediterranean diet scores, and the Healthy Eating Index, as well as data-driven approaches using 
2578 
factor/cluster analysis or reduced rank regression. The findings presented come from controlled 
2579 
intervention trials, cohort studies, and nested case-control studies. The DGAC examined these patterns 
2580 
in an attempt to quantify, for the first time, the approximate amounts of each food group in these 
2581 
patterns. The DGAC also examined the range of and commonalities across food group intakes in 
2582 
healthy dietary patterns and compared these ranges to the range of usual adult consumption in the 
2583 
United States and to the range recommended by the USDA Food Patterns. 
2584 
 
2585 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
70 
 
Question 18:  What is the composition of dietary patterns with evidence of positive 
2586 
health outcomes (e.g., Mediterranean-style patterns, Dietary Approaches to Stop 
2587 
Hypertension-style patterns, patterns that closely align with the Healthy Eating Index, 
2588 
and vegetarian patterns), and of patterns commonly consumed in the United States? 
2589 
What are the similarities (and differences) within and among the dietary patterns with 
2590 
evidence of positive health outcomes and the commonly consumed dietary patterns? 
2591 
Source of evidence: Data analysis 
2592 
 
2593 
Conclusions 
2594 
Dietary patterns with varying food group composition, but certain common elements were observed 
2595 
across intervention and cohort studies to have health benefits. A healthful diet can be achieved by 
2596 
following any of these dietary patterns.  
2597 
 
2598 
In general, the ranges of intake in dietary patterns with positive health benefits are very close to those 
2599 
recommended by the USDA Food Patterns, but amounts of some specific food groups vary across the 
2600 
various diet pattern types.  
2601 
• 
DASH-style diets, Mediterranean-style diets, and the USDA Food Patterns are similar with 
2602 
respect to amounts of fruits and vegetables, and the OMNI diets are slightly higher than the 
2603 
USDA Food Patterns. 
2604 
• 
Dairy intake is comparable between DASH-style diets and the USDA Food Patterns, but dairy 
2605 
is lower for Mediterranean-style diets than for the USDA Food Patterns. 
2606 
• 
Red and processed meats are higher in the Mediterranean-style diets but lower in the DASH-
2607 
style diet than is recommended by the USDA Food Patterns.  
2608 
• 
Seafood intake is similar in DASH-style and higher in Mediterranean-style diets than in the 
2609 
USDA Food Patterns. 
2610 
The data from the intervention trials and the cohort studies examined provide empirical data that the 
2611 
USDA Food Patterns provide an evidence-based guide to healthy patterns of food consumption. 
2612 
 
2613 
Implications 
2614 
The quality of the diets currently consumed by the U.S. population is suboptimal overall and has major 
2615 
adverse health consequences. Several options exist for dietary patterns that can be followed to improve 
2616 
the population’s diet quality. The approaches that can be taken are varied and can be adapted to 
2617 
personal and cultural preferences. The ability to offer the U.S. population alternative dietary pattern 
2618 
options and to tailor them to personal preferences may increase the likelihood of long term success of 
2619 
maintaining a healthy diet pattern, ultimately leading to improved health in the U.S. population. 
2620 
 
2621 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
71 
 
Review of the Evidence 
2622 
The DGAC analyzed data on food group composition reported in research articles on dietary patterns 
2623 
and health outcomes. These articles were drawn from those included in the questions on dietary 
2624 
patterns and health examined by the Committee (see Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Food and 
2625 
Nutrients, and Health Outcomes). The studies reported in that chapter D2 were reviewed to identify 
2626 
those that reported semi-quantitative data on food group intakes among the sample or population group 
2627 
with positive health outcomes (Table D1.31).93-112 These sample or population groups included the 
2628 
intervention group in intervention studies, the highest category (usually the top quintile) in cohorts and 
2629 
nested case-control studies measuring diet with an a priori index, or a specific cluster or factor analysis 
2630 
group. Approximate quantified food group intakes for these subsets of the population or samples with a 
2631 
beneficial health outcome were identified. These intakes were converted to grams per day if not 
2632 
reported this way in the original manuscripts. Then, all data were converted to grams per 1000 calories 
2633 
to allow for comparisons across studies. 
2634 
 
2635 
For comparison to usual intake levels of each food group in the United States., data from NHANES 
2636 
2007-2010 for usual intake by adult age/sex groups41 in cup or ounce equivalents were converted into 
2637 
grams using average weights based on Food Patterns Equivalents Database (FPED) data.48, 49  The 
2638 
gram weights were divided by the usual calorie intake for that group, and multiplied times 1000 for an 
2639 
estimate of the food group intake per 1000 calories for each adult age/sex group. The range of these 
2640 
intakes was used as a comparator. For comparison to the food group amounts recommended in the 
2641 
USDA Food Patterns (also called the Healthy U.S.-style Patterns; see Question 20) the recommended 
2642 
amount for adult age/sex groups in the patterns at 1600 to 2400 calories were converted to grams per 
2643 
1000 calories by the same procedure used for the usual intakes (see Figures D1.56 to D1.60).  
2644 
 
2645 
Vegetable intake in the OMNI diets was higher than both the USDA Food Patterns and current 
2646 
consumption estimates, but DASH-style, PREDIMED, most of the Mediterranean scores, and data 
2647 
driven approaches were very similar to vegetable amounts recommended by the USDA Food Patterns. 
2648 
Fruit intake was higher in the OMNI diets and PREDIMED relative to the USDA Food Patterns and 
2649 
current consumption, but DASH, the Mediterranean score diets, and many of the data driven scores are 
2650 
all within the range of the USDA Food Pattern recommendations. Dairy intakes in OMNI, DASH, and 
2651 
some of the Mediterranean and data driven scores were all within the ranges recommended by the 
2652 
USDA Food Patterns, while PREDIMED and some other scores had lower intakes of dairy. 
2653 
Consumption of red and processed meats was higher in PREDIMED and in some studies using 
2654 
Mediterranean diet scores relative to the USDA Food Patterns, whereas several cohorts using data-
2655 
driven approaches to assessing diet patterns reported ranges of red and processed meat intake that 
2656 
aligned very well with the USDA Food Pattern recommendations. Intakes of red and processed meat 
2657 
were lower in the OMNI and DASH dietary interventions than in either the USDA Food Patterns or the 
2658 
range of usual intake in the United States. Seafood intakes for the OMNI diets and some of the data-
2659 
driven dietary pattern studies aligned very well with the USDA Food Patterns. Seafood intake ranges 
2660 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
72 
 
for all the other studies were much higher than both the USDA Food Patterns and the ranges of usual 
2661 
intake in the United States. 
2662 
 
2663 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
2664 
• Usual Dietary Intakes: Food Intakes, U.S. Population, 2007-10: Applied Research Program. 
2665 
National Cancer Institute; [updated May 22, 2014]. Available from: 
2666 
http://appliedresearch.cancer.gov/diet/usualintakes/pop/2007-10/. 
2667 
• Appendix E-3.1: Adequacy of the USDA Food Patterns  
2668 
 
2669 
Question 19:  To what extent does the U.S. population consume a dietary pattern that 
2670 
is similar to those observed to have positive health benefits [e.g., Mediterranean-style 
2671 
pattern, Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH)-style patterns, patterns that 
2672 
closely align with the Healthy Eating Index, and vegetarian patterns] overall and by 
2673 
age/sex and race/ethnic groups? 
2674 
Source of evidence: Data analysis 
2675 
 
2676 
Conclusion  
2677 
Data from WWEIA show that the average HEI score in the U.S. population is 57 points out of a total 
2678 
of 100 points. The best scores (average scores) were observed for the following components: total 
2679 
protein foods (average score of 100 percent of possible points), seafood and plant protein (84 percent 
2680 
of possible points), and dairy (69 percent of possible points), while the poorest scores were observed 
2681 
for whole grains (25 percent of possible points), sodium (37 percent of possible points), fatty acid ratio 
2682 
(41 percent of possible points), greens and beans (46 percent of possible points), and empty calories 
2683 
(60 percent of  possible points).  
2684 
 
2685 
Young children ages 2 to 3 years and middle aged and older adults (ages 51 years and older) have the 
2686 
best HEI scores (total scores of 63 percent and 66 percent, respectively), while preadolescents and 
2687 
adolescents have the poorest HEI scores (total scores of 49 percent and 48 percent, respectively). 
2688 
 
2689 
Implications 
2690 
To improve diet quality, the U.S. population should replace most refined grains with whole grains, 
2691 
decrease sodium, decrease saturated fat, consume fewer calories from added sugars, and replace these 
2692 
calories with more varied vegetable choices, seafood, plant proteins, and low-fat dairy. 
2693 
 
2694 
Young children and middle-aged and older adults have the highest HEI scores. These positive healthy 
2695 
eating habits should continue to be encouraged. Because preadolescents and adolescents have the 
2696 
lowest HEI scores, significant intervention is needed at the level of the individual, family, school, day 
2697 
care, and community settings to help this age group adopt and maintain healthful dietary patterns. 
2698 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
73 
 
 
2699 
Review of the Evidence  
2700 
The DGAC examined mean HEI scores and component scores for the entire U.S. population ages 2 
2701 
years and older (see Appendix E-2.25: Average Healthy Eating Index-2010 scores for Americans 
2702 
ages 2 years and older). These data were examined for the entire population, for males and females 
2703 
and by age subgroups. In general, the best scores for the HEI components were for protein and seafood 
2704 
and plant proteins, while the poorest score was for whole grains. For nearly all of the component 
2705 
scores as well as the total HEI score, females tended to have better scores than males, indicating 
2706 
slightly healthier dietary patterns in females compared to males. Analyses by age showed that the 
2707 
youngest and oldest segments of the population had the best component and total HEI scores (Figure 
2708 
D1.61). For these groups, the component scores were very good to excellent for total fruit and whole 
2709 
fruit. Young children also had excellent scores for dairy, and middle-aged and older adults had 
2710 
excellent scores for total protein and seafood and plant protein. All age groups have poor scores for 
2711 
whole grains. 
2712 
 
2713 
Data were not available to examine how closely the U.S. population’s dietary patterns align with a 
2714 
Mediterranean-style or DASH-style dietary pattern. 
2715 
 
2716 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
2717 
• Healthy Eating Index, Center for Nutrition Policy and Promotion. Available from: 
2718 
http://www.cnpp.usda.gov/HealthyEatingIndex. 
2719 
• Appendix E-2.25: Average Healthy Eating Index-2010 scores for Americans ages 2 years and 
2720 
older (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey 2009-2010)  
2721 
 
2722 
Question 20:  Using the Food Pattern Modeling process, can healthy eating patterns 
2723 
for vegetarians and for those who want to follow a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern 
2724 
be developed?  How do these patterns differ from the USDA Food Patterns previously 
2725 
updated for potential inclusion in the 2015 DGAs? 
2726 
Source of evidence: Food Pattern Modeling 
2727 
 
2728 
Conclusion  
2729 
Food Pattern Modeling demonstrates that healthy eating patterns can be achieved for a variety of 
2730 
eating styles, including the “Healthy U.S.-style Pattern,” the “Healthy Mediterranean-style Pattern,” 
2731 
and the “Healthy Vegetarian-style Pattern”. Although some differences exist across the three eating 
2732 
patterns, comparable amounts of nutrients can be obtained using nutrient dense foods while 
2733 
maintaining energy balance. 
2734 
 
2735 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
74 
 
Implications 
2736 
The U.S. population has a variety of options to help achieve healthful eating patterns that adhere to the 
2737 
Dietary Guidelines. These include the Healthy U.S.-style Pattern, Mediterranean-style Pattern, or 
2738 
Vegetarian Patterns. (Detailed information on these patterns can be found in Table D1.32 and 
2739 
Appendix E-3.7: Developing Vegetarian and Mediterranean-style Food Patterns.)  These diets meet 
2740 
nutritional goals without excess calories and use a variety of foods. Importantly, these diets reflect the 
2741 
range of foods that can be used to achieve a healthful eating pattern, and they support the inclusion of 
2742 
diverse foods that are consistent with personal, cultural and religious preferences. These diets can be 
2743 
used in a variety of settings, including homes, schools, worksites, health care facilities, and places of 
2744 
worship. 
2745 
 
2746 
Review of the Evidence  
2747 
These conclusions were reached based on the results of the Food Pattern Modeling analysis for 
2748 
vegetarian and Mediterranean-style food patterns. Data from WWEIA from self-reported vegetarians 
2749 
were used to inform the vegetarian eating pattern (Figure D1.62) and data from the Dietary Patterns 
2750 
composition project reviewed above were used to select foods for the Mediterranean-style pattern.113  
2751 
 
2752 
From three dietary patterns (“Healthy U.S.-style,” “Healthy Mediterranean-style Patter,” and “Healthy 
2753 
Vegetarian Pattern”), selected food group intakes across calorie levels were compared (Table D1.32). 
2754 
Notably, fruit and seafood were higher in the Mediterranean-style diet, while dairy was lower, based 
2755 
on the data presented above (Figures D1.56 to D1.60). For the Vegetarian Pattern, meat and seafood 
2756 
are absent, but eggs and dairy are included because self-reported vegetarians in WWEIA reported 
2757 
consumption of these foods. Legumes, nuts/seeds, and processed soy are all higher in the Vegetarian 
2758 
Pattern compared to the Healthy U.S.-style and the Healthy Mediterranean-style Patterns.  
2759 
 
2760 
When comparing nutrient intake across these three dietary patterns, as a percent of the RDA using a 
2761 
woman age 19 to 30 years as an example, modest difference emerged (Table D1.33). The Vegetarian 
2762 
pattern is lower in sodium and all three patterns are low in vitamin D. 
2763 
 
2764 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:   
2765 
• Usual Dietary Intakes: Food Intakes, US Population, 2007-10: Applied Research Program. 
2766 
National Cancer Institute; [updated May 22, 2014]. Available from: 
2767 
http://appliedresearch.cancer.gov/diet/usualintakes/pop/2007-10/. 
2768 
• Appendix E-3.7: Developing Vegetarian and Mediterranean-style Food Patterns  
2769 
 
2770 
 
2771 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
75 
 
CHAPTER SUMMARY 
2772 
The DGAC conducted data analyses to address a series of questions related to the current status and 
2773 
trends in the Nation’s dietary intake. The questions focused on: intake of specific nutrients and food 
2774 
groups; food categories (i.e., foods as consumed) that contribute to intake; eating behaviors; and the 
2775 
composition of various dietary patterns shown to have health benefits, including Mediterranean-style 
2776 
diets, the Healthy US-style and DASH-style diets. These topics were addressed using data from the 
2777 
WWEIA dietary survey, which is the dietary intake component of the ongoing NHANES. Food pattern 
2778 
modeling using the USDA Food Pattern food groups also was used to address some of the questions of 
2779 
interest. In addition, the DGAC examined the prevalence and trends of health conditions that may have 
2780 
a nutritional origin, or where the course of disease may be influenced by diet. 
2781 
 
2782 
The DGAC found that several nutrients are underconsumed and the Committee characterized them as 
2783 
shortfall nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E, vitamin C, folate, calcium, magnesium, fiber, and 
2784 
potassium. For adolescent and premenopausal females, iron also is a shortfall nutrient. Important to 
2785 
note, on the basis of nutrient biomarkers or health outcomes, calcium, vitamin D, fiber, and potassium 
2786 
also are classified as nutrients of public health concern because their underconsumption has been 
2787 
linked in the scientific literature to adverse health outcomes. Iron is included as a shortfall nutrient of 
2788 
public health concern for adolescent females and adult females who are premenopausal due to the 
2789 
increased risk of iron-deficiency in these groups. The DGAC also found that two nutrients—sodium 
2790 
and saturated fat—are overconsumed by the U.S. population and that the overconsumption poses 
2791 
health risks. 
2792 
 
2793 
The majority of the U.S. population has low intakes of key food groups that are important sources of 
2794 
the shortfall nutrients including vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and dairy. Furthermore, population 
2795 
intake is too high for refined grains and added sugars. The data suggest cautious optimism about 
2796 
dietary intake of the youngest members of the U.S. population because many young children ages 2 to 
2797 
5 years consume recommended amounts of fruit and dairy. However, a better understanding is needed 
2798 
on how to maintain and encourage the good habits that are started early in life. Analysis of data on 
2799 
food categories, such as burgers, sandwiches, mixed dishes, desserts, and beverages, because they 
2800 
represent such a large proportion of the calories consumed, are prime targets for reformulation to 
2801 
increase population intake of vegetables, whole grains, and other underconsumed food groups and to 
2802 
lower population intake of the nutrients sodium and saturated fat, and the food component refined 
2803 
grains. Dramatically reducing the intake of sugar-sweetened beverages and limiting sweets and 
2804 
desserts would help lower intakes the food component added sugars. 
2805 
 
2806 
The U.S. population purchases its food in a variety of locations, including supermarkets, convenience 
2807 
stores, schools, and the workplace, and consumes prepared food outside the home. The DGAC found 
2808 
that while diet quality varies somewhat by the setting where food is obtained, overall, independent of 
2809 
where the food is prepared or obtained, the diet quality of the U.S. population does not meet 
2810 
recommendations for fruit, vegetables, dairy, or whole grains, and exceeds recommendations, leading 
2811 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
76 
 
to overconsumption, for the nutrients sodium and saturated fat, and the food components refined 
2812 
grains, solid fats, and added sugars.  
2813 
 
2814 
Obesity and chronic diseases with a nutritional origin are very common. The Nation must accelerate 
2815 
progress toward reducing the incidence and prevalence of overweight and obesity and chronic disease 
2816 
risk across the U.S. population throughout the lifespan and reduce the disparities in obesity and chronic 
2817 
disease rates that exist in the United States for certain ethnic and racial groups and for those with lower 
2818 
incomes.  
2819 
 
2820 
The DGAC identified key aspects of several different dietary patterns that are associated with lower 
2821 
risk of many nutrition-related outcomes such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, some cancers, 
2822 
psychological health and bone health. These patterns and their associated health benefits are described 
2823 
in greater detail in the next chapter.  
2824 
 
2825 
The DGAC had enough descriptive information from existing research and data to model three dietary 
2826 
patterns and to examine their nutritional adequacy. These patterns are the Healthy U.S.-style Pattern, 
2827 
the Healthy Mediterranean-style Pattern, and the Healthy Vegetarian Pattern. These patterns include 
2828 
the components of a dietary pattern associated with health benefits.   
2829 
 
2830 
The findings from this chapter and the remainder of the 2015 DGAC report can be used by individuals, 
2831 
families, communities, schools, local, state and federal agencies and the food industry to address the 
2832 
high prevalence of obesity and other nutrition-related health conditions in the United States and help 
2833 
all sectors of the population consume a diet that is healthful, accessible, and affordable. 
2834 
 
2835 
 
2836 
NEEDS FOR FUTURE RESEARCH 
2837 
1. Expand WWEIA participation to include more respondents from race/ethnic minorities and non-
2838 
U.S. born residents.  
2839 
Rationale: Very little is known about the dietary habits of many of the cultural subgroups in the 
2840 
United States. This knowledge is essential to moving forward any nutrition programs for first and 
2841 
second generation immigrants. More data on the impact of acculturation also are needed on food 
2842 
and health behaviors. The number of participants in WWEIA using the derived acculturation 
2843 
variable was too small for any analysis. Finally, “Hispanic” is a very broad term and a better 
2844 
understanding is needed of the nutritional profiles (including shortfalls and excesses) across 
2845 
various Spanish-speaking people in the United States, who come from different cultural 
2846 
backgrounds with distinct eating patterns. 
2847 
 
2848 
2. Include higher proportion of older Americans as respondents in WWEIA. 
2849 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
77 
 
Rationale: More data are needed on dietary intake of older adults; the sample sizes in WWEIA 
2850 
were too small for any meaningful analyses for those older than the age of 71 years. In addition to 
2851 
nutrient intake, additional information is needed on whether older adults are able to shop and cook, 
2852 
whether polypharmacy plays a role in nutritional adequacy, and whether co-morbidities, such as 
2853 
poor dentition, musculo-skeletal difficulties, arthralgias and other age-related symptoms, affect 
2854 
their ability to establish and maintain proper nutritional status. 
2855 
 
2856 
3. Increase the number of pregnant women as respondents in WWEIA. 
2857 
Rationale: The number of pregnant women in WWEIA is currently too small to properly evaluate 
2858 
the status and trends in food and nutrient intake in pregnant women. Since good nutrition in 
2859 
pregnancy is critical to proper growth development of the infant it is critical to properly evaluate 
2860 
food and nutrient intake, which will inform recommendations and public policies for pregnant 
2861 
women. 
2862 
 
2863 
4. Conduct research on nutrition transitions from childhood to shed light on how and why dietary 
2864 
intake changes so rapidly from early childhood through pre-adolescence and adolescence, and to 
2865 
identify the driving forces behind dietary intake change in these age groups and what programs are 
2866 
most effective at maintaining positive nutrition habits established in very young children. 
2867 
Rationale: Young children have better dietary intake than older children and adolescents. It is 
2868 
important to maintain the positive gains made in early childhood and identify factors responsible 
2869 
for the declines in intakes of fruit, dairy, and other food groups and increases in added sugars and 
2870 
refined grains as children become enter the elementary school age years, as poor eating patterns in 
2871 
elementary school seem to persist into adolescence and beyond. 
2872 
 
2873 
5. Evaluate the effects of common variations in dietary patterns in small children on nutrient intakes. 
2874 
Rationale: Children from 2 to 4 years of age have a highly variable diet and often do not fit readily 
2875 
into the USDA Food Pattern food groups diet pattern analyses. Further information is needed to 
2876 
understand the broad range of diets and supplement use in small children and how this relates to 
2877 
nutrient intake and growth. Research is needed to better characterize their diets so that appropriate 
2878 
guidance can be offered. 
2879 
 
2880 
6. Increase the quantity and quality of food composition databases available for research.  
2881 
Rationale: Accurate assessment of nutrient intake and trends over time in the U.S. population is 
2882 
dependent upon the quality of food composition data. Tens of thousands of foods are available for 
2883 
purchase and consumption in the United States, but accurate nutrient content data are available 
2884 
only for less than 10,000 foods and are almost non-existent for many ready-to-eat and restaurant-
2885 
type foods. Analytic values from foods are needed on specific nutrients and components, such as 
2886 
vitamin D, fiber, added sugars, and sodium. Improved food composition data also is critical for 
2887 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
78 
 
needed research to better define, identify, and quantify total grain, whole grain consumption, and 
2888 
refined grain consumption in dietary studies. 
2889 
 
2890 
7. Investigate the validity, reliability, and reproducibility of new biomarkers of nutrient intake and 
2891 
biomarkers of nutritional status. 
2892 
Rationale: Limited biomarkers are available and some that are available are difficult to interpret 
2893 
due to other contributing factors to the biomarker measure (e.g., vitamin D is obtained in the diet 
2894 
and is also endogenously synthesized). 
2895 
 
2896 
8. Evaluate effects of fortification strategies and supplement use on consumer behavior related to the 
2897 
intake of foods and supplements containing key nutrients, including calcium, vitamin D, potassium, 
2898 
iron, and fiber 
2899 
Rationale: The intake of key nutrients of concern is considerably affected by the rapidly evolving 
2900 
marketplace of food fortification and supplementation. Understanding consumer behavior related 
2901 
to fortification and supplementation would be important in predicting the effects of interventions 
2902 
and marketplace changes in content of these nutrients. Special interest exists regarding fortification 
2903 
strategies of foods, including whole grains and yogurts, in allowing individuals to reach the RDA 
2904 
for vitamin D without using supplements. Data are needed on how supplements may help meet 
2905 
nutrients shortfalls and/or how use of supplements may place individuals at risk of 
2906 
overconsumption. Research on effective consumer guidance is needed. 
2907 
 
2908 
9. Understand the rationale for and consequences of the use of supplements above the UL for 
2909 
vitamins and minerals. Identify biochemical markers that would indicate the effects of high-dose 
2910 
supplement use. 
2911 
Rationale: Consumer use of high-dose supplements has increased. Understanding the influences 
2912 
guiding this use would be helpful in considering how to educate consumers about safe upper intake 
2913 
limits. 
2914 
 
2915 
10. Develop a standardized research definition for meals and snacks.  
2916 
Rationale: Multiple different criteria are used in studies to define a snack or meal occasion, such 
2917 
as time of day, the types or amounts of food consumed, or subjective assessment by the study 
2918 
respondent. Researchers should work toward a consensus on the use of standard definitions. 
2919 
 
2920 
11. Understand better the concept of dietary patterns and design approaches to quantify the diet in 
2921 
large population-based studies. 
2922 
Rationale: More methodological work on dietary patterns is needed. For example, food frequency 
2923 
questionnaires, which are used in most diet assessment studies, do not capture data on meal timing, 
2924 
meal frequency, or the types of foods consumed together. Studies using diet recalls and records are 
2925 
better at capturing specific foods and their quantities consumed (portion sizes) and the types of 
2926 

 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
79 
 
foods eaten together, but often these detailed assessment methods are not feasible for large 
2927 
population-based studies. Quantification of food group intake is needed. In addition, dietary 
2928 
patterns research encompasses a broader scope of issues than can  e addressed by diet scores and 
2929 
data drive approaches.  
2930 
 
2931 
12. Consistently report the nutrients, foods, and food groups that are used to evaluate dietary patterns 
2932 
in published studies.  
2933 
Rationale: The current scientific literature evaluating dietary patterns and health is inconsistent in 
2934 
its provision of dietary patterns composition information. This makes it difficult to compare, across 
2935 
studies, the components of healthful patterns that are associated with health benefits. 
2936 
 
2937 
13. Conduct population surveillance on the prevalence and trends of nutrition-related chronic diseases 
2938 
including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, some cancers osteoporosis and neurocognitive 
2939 
disorders.  
2940 
Rationale: Current data on diabetes in adults cannot be stratified by disease type (type I or type II), 
2941 
making it very difficult to monitor incidence and prevalence of type 2 diabetes. Continued 
2942 
population surveillance is needed to effectively link nutritional factors with risk of these diseases.  
2943

Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
 
101 
 
Table D1.10. USDA Food Intake Patterns (Healthy U.S.-Style Patterns), continued 
 
Quantity equivalents for each food group are:  
• 
Grains, 1 ounce equivalent is:  ½ cup cooked rice, pasta, or cooked cereal; 1 ounce dry pasta or rice; 1 slice bread; 1 small muffin (1 oz); 1 cup RTE 
cereal flakes. 
• 
Fruits and vegetables, 1 cup equivalent is:  1 cup raw or cooked fruit or vegetable, 1 cup fruit or vegetable juice, 2 cups leafy salad greens. 
• 
Protein Foods , 1 ounce equivalent is:  1 ounce lean meat, poultry, or fish; 1 egg; ¼ cup cooked dry beans or tofu; 1 Tbsp peanut butter; ½ ounce nuts 
or seeds. 
• 
Milk, 1 cup equivalent is:  1 cup milk or yogurt, 1½ ounces natural cheese such as Cheddar cheese or 2 ounces of processed cheese.  
 
Source:  Center for Nutrition Policy and Promotion, USDA. USDA Food Patterns. For more information see Appendix E-3.1: Adequacy of the USDA Food 
Patterns

1Sedentary means a lifestyle that includes only the physical activity of independent living. 
2Moderately Active means a lifestyle that includes physical activity equivalent to walking about 1.5 to 3 miles per day at 3 
to 4 miles per hour, in addition to the activities of independent living. 
3Active means a lifestyle that includes physical activity equivalent to walking more than 3 miles per day at 3 to 4 miles per 
hour, in addition to the activities of independent living.

* Adults ages 18 years and older. 
@ Hypertension is defined as having measured systolic pressure of at least 140 mm Hg or diastolic pressure of at least 90 mm Hg and/or taking antihypertensive 
medication. Estimates are based on the average of up to 3 measurements.  
**Total diabetes is the sum of self-reported diabetes and undiagnosed diabetes. Diagnosed diabetes was obtained by self-report and excludes women who 
reported having diabetes only during pregnancy. Undiagnosed diabetes is defined as fasting plasma glucose (FPG) of at least 126 mg/dL or a hemoglobin A1c of 
at least 6.5% and no reported physician diagnosis. Respondents had fasted for at least 8 hours and less than 24 hours. The definition of undiagnosed diabetes was 
based on recommendations from the American Diabetes Association. For more information, see Standards of medical care in diabetes – 2010. Diabetes Care 
2010: 33 (suppl 1): S11-S61.

* Prevalence from NHANES 2005-2010 has been adjusted to the age, sex, and race/ethnic distribution of the US 
3 
population at the time of the 2010 Census using the direct method. 
4 
**Count expressed in millions; 95% CI=95% confidence limits 
5 
@ Other races not shown separately 
6 
OP = osteoporosis; LBM= low bone mass; NH= non-Hispanic. SE = standard error.  
7 
Osteoporosis and low bone mass were defined using the WHO criteria. Specifically, osteoporosis was defined as a 
8 
T-score ≤ -2.5 at either the femoral neck or the lumbar spine. Among those without osteoporosis, low bone mass 
9 
was defined as those with T-scores between -1.0 and -2.5 at either skeletal site. The reference group for calculation 
10 
of the scores at the femoral neck for both men and women, consisted of 20-29 non-Hispanic White females from 
11 
NHANES III. As there is no internationally recommended reference group for the lumbar spine, the reference group 
12 
for calculation of these scores at the lumbar spine consisted of 30-year old White females from the DXA 
13 
manufacturer reference database. These reference groups were used to calculate T-scores for all race/ethnic groups 
14 
and for both sexes.  
15 
Source:  Wright NC, Looker AC, Saag KG, Curtis JR, Delzell ES, Randall S, et al. The Recent Prevalence of 
16 
Osteoporosis and Low Bone Mass in the United States Based on Bone Mineral Density at the Femoral Neck or 
17 
Lumbar Spine. J Bone Miner Res. 2014. PMID: 24771492. http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24771492.

Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, 
1 
and Health Outcomes 
2 
INTRODUCTION 
3 
A healthy diet is a pillar of well-being throughout the lifespan. It promotes the achievement of 
4 
healthy pregnancy outcomes; supports normal growth, development and aging; helps maintain 
5 
healthful body weight; reduces chronic disease risks; and promotes overall health and well-
6 
being. Previous Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committees focused on examining specific foods, 
7 
nutrients, and dietary components and their relationships to health outcomes. In its review, 
8 
however, the 2010 DGAC noted that it is often not possible to separate the effects of individual 
9 
nutrients and foods, and that the totality of diet—the combinations and quantities in which foods 
10 
and nutrients are consumed—may have synergistic and cumulative effects on health and 
11 
disease.1 This approach has been adopted by others as well (e.g. American Heart Association, 
12 
American College of Cardiology and the National Cancer Institute) and is being used by the 
13 
2015 DGAC. The 2010 Committee acknowledged the importance of dietary patterns and 
14 
recommended additional research in this area. After the release of the 2010 Dietary Guidelines 
15 
for Americans, the USDA Nutrition Evidence Library (NEL) completed a systematic review 
16 
project examining the relationships between dietary patterns and several health outcomes, 
17 
including cardiovascular disease (CVD), body weight and type 2 diabetes.2 Their report has been 
18 
used by the 2015 DGAC.  
19 
 
20 
As also noted in the 2010 Dietary Guidelines for Americans, individuals can achieve a healthy 
21 
diet in multiple ways and preferably with a wide variety of foods and beverages. Optimal 
22 
nutrition can be attained with many dietary patterns and a single dietary pattern approach or 
23 
prescription is unnecessary. Indeed, for long-term maintenance, a dietary pattern to support 
24 
optimal nutrition and health should be based on the biological and medical needs as well as 
25 
preferences of the individual. 
26 
 
27 
Dietary patterns are defined as the quantities, proportions, variety or combinations of different 
28 
foods and beverages in diets, and the frequency with which they are habitually consumed. 
29 
Americans consume many habitual dietary patterns, rather than a “typical American pattern,” 
30 
which reflect their life experiences and wide-ranging personal, socio-cultural and other 
31 
environmental influences. The nutritional quality of a dietary pattern can be determined by 
32 
assessing the nutrient content of its constituent foods and beverages and comparing these 
33 
characteristics to age- and sex-specific nutrient requirements and standards for nutrient 
34 
adequacy, as shown in Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current 
35 
Status and Trends for the USDA Food Patterns, including the “Healthy U.S.-style Pattern,” the 
36 
“Healthy Mediterranean-style Pattern,” and the “Healthy Vegetarian Pattern.” Understanding the 
37 
array of dietary patterns in a population and their nutrient quality allows a more complete 
38 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
characterization of individual eating behaviors and enables their examination in relationship with 
39 
diverse health outcomes. For these reasons, the DGAC focused on considering the evidence for 
40 
overall dietary patterns in addition to key foods and nutrients. A major goal was to describe the 
41 
common characteristics of a healthy diet, which informed and is complementary to the 
42 
quantitative description of dietary patterns provided in Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient 
43 
Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends. 
44 
 
45 
Dietary patterns can be characterized in three main ways, drawing from Dr. Susan Krebs-Smith’s 
46 
presentation to the DGAC during the second public meeting (available at 
47 
www.DietaryGuidelines.gov). The first is by the use of an a priori index that is based on a set of 
48 
dietary recommendations for a healthy dietary pattern as a result of scientific consensus or 
49 
proposed by investigators using an evidence-based approach. An individual’s index/score is 
50 
derived by comparing and quantifying their adherence to the criterion food and/or nutrient 
51 
component of the index and then summed up over all components. A population’s average mean 
52 
and individual component scores can be similarly determined. Examples of dietary quality scores 
53 
include: the Healthy Eating Index (HEI)-2005 and 2010,3 the Alternate HEI (AHEI) and updated 
54 
AHEI-2010,4 the Recommended Food Score (RFS),5 the Dietary Approaches to Stop 
55 
Hypertension (DASH) score,6 the Mediterranean Diet Score (MDS),7 and the Alternate 
56 
Mediterranean Diet Score (aMed).8   
57 
 
58 
The second method of dietary pattern assessment is through data-driven approaches, such as 
59 
cluster analysis (which addresses the question, “Using the self-reported food and beverage intake 
60 
data are there groups of people with distinct (non-overlapping) dietary patterns?”) and factor 
61 
analysis (which addresses the question, “Which components of the diet track together to explain 
62 
variations in food or beverage intake across diet patterns?”). These data-driven approaches are 
63 
outcome-independent. That is, the relationships between the dietary patterns and intermediate or 
64 
longer-term health outcomes are examined once the patterns themselves are defined. Other data-
65 
driven approaches are outcome-dependent, such as reduced rank regression (which addresses the 
66 
question, “What combination of foods explains the most variation in one or more intermediate 
67 
health markers?”).  
68 
 
69 
The third method examines individuals’ food and beverage intake preferences as they are 
70 
commonly defined by foods included or eliminated. In cohort studies, this pattern is usually 
71 
based upon qualitative self-reported behaviors rather than detailed questionnaires. Vegetarianism 
72 
and its various forms (e.g., ovo-lacto vegetarianism) are examples of this type of dietary pattern.  
73 
 
74 
The dietary patterns approach has a number of major strengths. The method captures the 
75 
relationship between the overall diet and its constituent foods, beverages and nutrients in 
76 
relationship to outcomes of interest and quality, thereby overcoming the collinearity among 
77 
single foods and nutrients. In so doing, it considers the inherent interactions between foods and 
78 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
3 
 
nutrients in promoting health or increasing disease risk. Because foods are consumed in 
79 
combinations, it is difficult, if not impossible, to determine their separate effects on health. 
80 
Relationships or effects attributed to a particular food or nutrient may be accurate or reflect those 
81 
of other dietary components acting in synergy. The dietary pattern approach has advanced 
82 
nutrition research by capturing overall food consumption behaviors and its quality in relationship 
83 
to health.  
84 
 
85 
Despite these considerable strengths, however, the approach has several limitations that are 
86 
important to consider. First, the dietary assessment instruments used to define the dietary 
87 
patterns (e.g., food frequency questionnaires [FFQ] and 24–hour or multi-day dietary recalls or 
88 
records) are based upon self-report and may introduce levels of report bias that can attenuate 
89 
diet-health relationships. The FFQ has been evaluated as a valid and reliable measure of usual 
90 
food and nutrient intake. However, the extent to which data from FFQs are valid measures of 
91 
dietary patterns is not well established. Second, dietary patterns are not uniformly defined by 
92 
investigators and vary substantially from one study to the next even though studies may use the 
93 
same nomenclature. This may hamper cross-study comparisons and limits reproducibility. Third, 
94 
scoring algorithms used to evaluate dietary pattern adherence may differ and affect the results of 
95 
studies examining specific health outcomes. Fourth, data-driven methods may not derive 
96 
comparable patterns in different populations because these patterns may be population specific. 
97 
Lastly, dietary patterns do not assess the frequency of meal and snack consumption, specific 
98 
combinations of foods consumed together, and aspects of food purchase and preparation, all of 
99 
which may influence the overall dietary pattern. 
100 
 
101 
Another challenge to examining dietary patterns is that randomized dietary intervention studies 
102 
have used different approaches for ensuring that subjects comply with the intervention diet when 
103 
testing their relationships with health outcomes. For example, randomized controlled trials 
104 
(RCTs), such as Prevencion con Dieta Mediterranean (PREDIMED), coached participants to 
105 
follow a dietary pattern and provided them with key foods (e.g., olive oil or nuts) to facilitate 
106 
adherence. In contrast, feeding studies (another form of intervention study), such as those 
107 
conducted in the DASH and the Optimal Macronutrient Intake Trial for Heart Health 
108 
(OmniHeart), provided all food to be consumed to each participant. These study designs across 
109 
randomized trials and feeding studies provide strong evidence for the benefits and risks of 
110 
particular dietary patterns because a prescribed intervention allows relatively precise definition 
111 
of dietary exposures, and randomization helps ensure that any potential confounding variables 
112 
are randomly distributed between study arms. However, some trials (i.e. DASH, OmniHeart) are 
113 
necessarily restricted to testing a dietary pattern’s effect on an intermediate outcome or a 
114 
surrogate endpoint, such as blood lipids, because of the complexities involved in maintaining 
115 
dietary compliance over long study duration. Additionally, the feeding trials fail to represent 
116 
what happens in real world situations. Thus, well-conducted observational cohort studies provide 
117 
an important evidentiary complement to RCTs because they enable the study of hard endpoints 
118 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
4 
 
for disease in addition to intermediate outcomes and often provide a wider range of exposures for 
119 
study.  
120 
 
121 
Dietary patterns and their food and nutrient characteristics are at the core of the conceptual 
122 
model that has guided the DGAC’s work (see Part B. Chapter 2: 2015 DGAC Themes and  
123 
Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence), and the relationship of dietary patterns to health 
124 
outcomes is the centerpiece of this chapter. The Committee considered evidence about the 
125 
relationship of diet with several health outcomes that are listed as major public health outcomes 
126 
of concern in Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and 
127 
Trends. Several of these outcomes—CVD, overweight and obesity, type 2 diabetes, congenital 
128 
anomalies, and bone health—also were addressed by the 2010 DGAC. Others—cancers (lung, 
129 
colon, prostate and breast) and neurological and psychological illness—while previously 
130 
addressed, are considered here in more depth and represent an expanded list of health outcomes 
131 
for which there is growing evidence of a diet-disease relationship. The 2015 Committee was not 
132 
able to consider the relationship between dietary patterns during the peri- and prenatal period and 
133 
pregnancy outcomes (e.g., birth weight, preterm birth, pregnancy complications) or other cancer 
134 
outcomes, such as total cancer mortality or gynecological, pancreatic, and gastric-esophageal 
135 
cancers due to time limitations and limited work done in these areas involving dietary patterns. 
136 
However, it is important to note that recently the NIH-AARP Diet and Health Study (n = 
137 
492,823) conducted in the United States demonstrated that high adherence on several indices (the 
138 
HEI-2010, the AHEI-2010, the aMED, and DASH) was associated with lower risk of overall 
139 
CVD and cancer mortality.9  The authors concluded that this finding provides further credence 
140 
for using the dietary pattern approach, indicating that multiple dietary indices reflecting core 
141 
tenets of a healthy diet may lower the risk of mortality outcomes.9 
142 
 
143 
Over the course of the DGAC’s review, when strong or moderate evidence related to dietary 
144 
patterns and a particular health outcome was available, the Committee focused its discussion on 
145 
dietary patterns and, as possible, highlighted the most consistent common food and nutrient 
146 
characteristics identified in the dietary patterns literature. When only limited or insufficient 
147 
evidence related to dietary patterns and a particular health outcome was available (as in the case 
148 
of congenital anomalies and neurological and psychological illnesses), the Committee 
149 
summarized these findings and also provided a brief summary of existing evidence on specific 
150 
foods and/or nutrients and selected health outcomes.  
151 
 
152 
In addition to its work on dietary patterns, the DGAC considered conducting an evidence review 
153 
on the relationship between the role of the microbiome and various health outcomes. This novel 
154 
area of research has generated considerable interest in the scientific community and the lay 
155 
public. Investigators are examining the diversity of organisms (i.e., microbes) that inhabit 
156 
different parts of the body such as the gut, mouth, skin, and vagina, and are attempting to 
157 
understand how the microbial communities are influenced by diet, environment, host genetics 
158 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
5 
 
and other microbes, as well as their association with various health outcomes. The DGAC 
159 
conducted an exploratory search but did not find sufficient evidence to address this question in 
160 
the 2015 report. However, the Committee considers the microbiome to be an emerging topic of 
161 
potential importance to future DGACs. 
162 
 
163 
LIST OF QUESTIONS 
164 
Dietary Patterns and Cardiovascular Disease 
165 
1. What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of cardiovascular disease? 
166 
 
167 
Dietary Patterns and Body Weight 
168 
2. What is the relationship between dietary patterns and measures of body weight or obesity? 
169 
 
170 
Dietary Patterns and Type 2 Diabetes 
171 
3. What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of type 2 diabetes? 
172 
 
173 
Dietary Patterns and Cancer 
174 
4. What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of cancer? 
175 
 
176 
Dietary Patterns and Congenital Anomalies  
177 
5. What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of congenital anomalies? 
178 
 
179 
Dietary Patterns and Neurological and Psychological Illnesses 
180 
6. What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of neurological and psychological 
181 
illnesses? 
182 
 
183 
Dietary Patterns and Bone Health 
184 
7. What is the relationship between dietary patterns and bone health? 
185 
 
186 
METHODOLOGY 
187 
For the first time, the 2015 DGAC included a chapter focusing solely on the relationship between 
188 
dietary patterns and health outcomes. Although the 2010 DGAC considered some research on 
189 
certain dietary patterns and specific health outcomes, notably body weight, they did not complete 
190 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
6 
 
NEL systematic reviews on this research. The 2015 DGAC began by acknowledging a desire to 
191 
continue and expand on the total diet approach initiated by the 2010 DGAC. They then identified 
192 
outcomes of public health concern on which to focus their reviews.  
193 
 
194 
For the purposes of the 2015 DGAC, dietary patterns were defined as the quantities, proportions, 
195 
variety or combinations of different foods and beverages in diets, and the frequency with which 
196 
they are habitually consumed. Because the purpose of the Dietary Guidelines is to develop food-
197 
based recommendations to promote health and reduce risk of diet-related disease, one of the key 
198 
aspects of the research that the DGAC considered was a description of the foods and beverages 
199 
consumed by participants in the studies that the Committee reviewed. This was particularly 
200 
important for the NEL systematic reviews, for which a description of foods and beverages was a 
201 
key criterion for inclusion. Data on nutrients were not required for inclusion, but were considered 
202 
when provided as part of the dietary pattern description. 
203 
 
204 
Self-reported food and beverage intake was typically assessed using a qualitative or semi-
205 
quantitative food intake questionnaire (i.e., FFQ). However, some studies used other methods, 
206 
such as 24-hour recalls. When reviewing the evidence, the Committee attempted to adhere to the 
207 
language used by the study authors in describing food groupings. There was variability across 
208 
the food groupings, and this was particularly apparent in the meat group; for example, “total 
209 
meat” may have been defined as “meat, sausage, fish, and eggs,” “red meat, processed meat, and 
210 
poultry,” or various other combinations of meat. Similarly, “vegetables” seemed to most often 
211 
exclude potatoes, but some studies included potatoes, yet they rarely provided information on 
212 
how the potatoes were consumed (e.g., fried versus baked). When reported in the studies, the 
213 
Committee considered these definitions in their review.  
214 
 
215 
Because of the variability in dietary patterns methodology and food groupings reported, the 
216 
Committee focused on providing a qualitative description of healthy dietary patterns. 
217 
Additionally, as most studies reported intake in relative terms (e.g., comparing the first and fifth 
218 
quintiles or across tertiles), the Committee has presented its conclusions with relative 
219 
terminology (e.g., “higher” and “lower” in a certain component). Quantitative information on 
220 
dietary patterns is provided in Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: 
221 
Current Status and Trends as part of the Dietary Patterns Composition section. 
222 
 
223 
A number of studies in the scientific literature describe diets based on macronutrient proportion 
224 
or test only a specific food group or nutrient in the diet. For example, a low-carbohydrate diet fits 
225 
this description and has been of public interest. The DGAC reviewed the body of evidence 
226 
related to this type of diet as part of Question 2. Additionally, the Committee examined the 
227 
results of exploratory searches on low-carbohydrate diets (defined as less than 45 percent of 
228 
calories from carbohydrate) and all of the health outcomes considered in this chapter published 
229 
since 2000. Overall, it appears that only limited evidence is available to address the relationship 
230 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
7 
 
between low-carbohydrate diets and health, particularly evidence derived from U.S.-based 
231 
populations. The most evidence available focuses on low-carbohydrate diets and body weight. 
232 
The 2010 DGAC examined the relationship between macronutrient proportion and various body 
233 
weight outcomes, concluding that:  
234 
“1) There is strong and consistent evidence that when calorie intake is controlled, 
235 
macronutrient proportion of the diet is not related to losing weight; 2) A moderate body of 
236 
evidence provides no data to suggest that any one macronutrient is more effective than any 
237 
other for avoiding weight re-gain in weight reduced persons; 3) A moderate body of evidence 
238 
demonstrates that diets with less than 45% of calories as carbohydrates are not more 
239 
successful for long-term weight loss (12 months). There is also some evidence that they may 
240 
be less safe. In shorter-term studies, low-calorie, high-protein diets may result in greater 
241 
weight loss, but these differences are not sustained over time; and 4) A moderate amount of 
242 
evidence demonstrates that intake of dietary patterns with less than 45% calories from 
243 
carbohydrate or more than 35% calories from protein are not more effective than other diets 
244 
for weight loss or weight maintenance, are difficult to maintain over the long term, and may 
245 
be less safe.”  
246 
The published literature since that review does not provide sufficient evidence to change these 
247 
conclusions. Thus, in summary, although studies that examine macronutrient proportion or that 
248 
test only a specific food group or nutrient are important, they answer different questions related 
249 
to diet and health than those proposed by the DGAC. In addition, these studies generally did not 
250 
meet the DGAC’s definition of a dietary pattern study unless a full description of the dietary 
251 
pattern consumed was provided and appropriate methods were used to adjust for the confounding 
252 
of foods and nutrients.  
253 
 
254 
Questions 1, 2, and 3 were answered using existing reports, systematic reviews, and meta-
255 
analyses. All three of these questions were addressed in the NEL Dietary Patterns Systematic 
256 
Review Project. This project was supported by USDA’s Center for Nutrition Policy and 
257 
Promotion and was informed by a Technical Expert Collaborative of experts in dietary patterns 
258 
research.2 Additionally, the DGAC reviewed reports from systematic reviews recently conducted 
259 
by the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI) that included dietary patterns 
260 
research. For Question 1, the DGAC used the NHLBI Lifestyle Interventions to Reduce 
261 
Cardiovascular Risk: Systematic Evidence Review from the Lifestyle Work Group10 and the 
262 
associated American Heart Association (AHA)/ American College of Cardiology (ACC) 
263 
Guideline on Lifestyle Management to Reduce Cardiovascular Risk.11 For Question 2, the DGAC 
264 
used the NHLBI Managing Overweight and Obesity in Adults: Systematic Evidence Review from 
265 
the Obesity Expert Panel12 and the associated AHA/ACC/The Obesity Society (TOS) Guideline 
266 
for the Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults.13 For all three questions, in an attempt 
267 
to capture new research published since the searches for these systematic reviews were 
268 
completed, the Committee considered existing systematic reviews and meta-analyses published 
269 
in peer-reviewed journals since 2008. The existing systematic reviews and meta-analyses 
270 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
8 
 
considered by the DGAC had to meet the general inclusion criteria of the DGAC, and were 
271 
required to consider dietary patterns and the outcomes of interest. A description of the process 
272 
the DGAC used to answer existing report questions is provided in Part C: Methodology. The 
273 
DGAC followed this approach, including consideration of reference overlap, for all three 
274 
questions. For more information on the existing reports, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses 
275 
considered by the DGAC, the reader is encouraged to review the original sources, which are 
276 
referenced within each evidence review. 
277 
 
278 
Questions 4, 5, 6, and 7 were answered using NEL systematic reviews. A description of the NEL 
279 
process is provided in Part C: Methodology. All reviews were conducted in accordance with 
280 
NEL methodology, and the DGAC made all substantive decisions required throughout the 
281 
process to ensure that the most complete and relevant body of evidence was identified and 
282 
evaluated to answer each question. All steps in the process were documented to ensure 
283 
transparency and reproducibility. Specific information about individual systematic reviews can 
284 
be found at www.NEL.gov, including the search strategy, inclusion and exclusion criteria, a 
285 
complete list of included and excluded articles, and a detailed write-up describing the included 
286 
studies and the body of evidence. A link for each question is provided following each evidence 
287 
review. 
288 
 
289 
Introductory sections were written for Questions 4, 5, 6, and 7 because the conclusion statements 
290 
for these questions were graded limited or insufficient. The purpose of the introduction was to 
291 
provide a brief description of the current evidence available related to foods and nutrients and the 
292 
health outcome of interest. However, this evidence was not considered in developing the dietary 
293 
pattern conclusion statements. During the course of the dietary pattern reviews, the DGAC chose 
294 
to highlight particular components of the diet, which are discussed further in Part D. Chapter 6: 
295 
Cross-Cutting Topics of Public Health Importance. 
296 
 
297 
Question 1: What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of 
298 
cardiovascular disease? 
299 
Source of evidence: Existing reports 
300 
 
301 
Conclusion  
302 
The DGAC concurs with the conclusions of the NEL Dietary Patterns Systematic Review Project 
303 
and AHA/ACC Guideline on Lifestyle Management to Reduce Cardiovascular Risk that strong 
304 
and consistent evidence demonstrates that dietary patterns associated with decreased risk of CVD 
305 
are characterized by higher consumption of vegetables, fruits, whole grains, low-fat dairy, and 
306 
seafood, and lower consumption of red and processed meat, and lower intakes of refined grains, 
307 
and sugar-sweetened foods and beverages relative to less healthy patterns. Regular consumption 
308 
of nuts and legumes and moderate consumption of alcohol also are shown to be components of a 
309 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
9 
 
beneficial dietary pattern in most studies. Randomized dietary intervention studies have 
310 
demonstrated that healthy dietary patterns exert clinically meaningful impact on cardiovascular 
311 
risk factors, including blood lipids and blood pressure. Additionally, research that includes 
312 
specific nutrients in their description of dietary patterns indicate that patterns that are lower in 
313 
saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium and richer in fiber, potassium, and unsaturated fats are 
314 
beneficial for reducing cardiovascular disease risk. DGAC Grade: Strong 
315 
 
316 
Implications  
317 
Individuals are encouraged to consume dietary patterns that emphasize vegetables, fruits, whole 
318 
grains, legumes, and nuts; include low-fat dairy products and seafood; limit sodium, saturated 
319 
fat, refined grains, and sugar-sweetened foods and beverages; and are lower in red and processed 
320 
meats. Multiple dietary patterns can achieve these food and nutrient patterns and are beneficial 
321 
for cardiovascular health, and they should be tailored to individuals’ biological needs and 
322 
cultural as well as individual food preferences. The Committee recommends the development 
323 
and implementation of programs and services at the individual and population levels that 
324 
facilitate the improvement in eating behaviors consistent with the above dietary patterns.  
325 
 
326 
Review of the Evidence 
327 
The DGAC examined research compiled in the NEL Dietary Patterns Systematic Review 
328 
Project, which included 55 articles summarizing evidence from 52 prospective cohort studies and 
329 
7 RCTs, and the 2013 AHA/ACC Lifestyle Guideline and associated NHLBI Lifestyle Report, 
330 
which included primarily RCTs. The Committee drew additional evidence and effect size 
331 
estimates from six published systematic reviews/meta-analyses published since 2008 that 
332 
included one or more studies not covered in the NEL or NHLBI Lifestyle reports.14-19 In total, 
333 
142 articles were considered in these reports, of which 35 were included in two or more reviews. 
334 
Little evidence on the contribution of dietary patterns to CVD risk factors in the pediatric 
335 
populations was available, and that which was published was not systematically reviewed.  
336 
 
337 
Most evidence examining hard disease endpoints comes from large, prospective cohort studies in 
338 
adults using a priori scores to rank individuals with respect to adherence to dietary patterns of 
339 
interest. Though the observational design allows the necessary duration of follow-up to observe 
340 
CVD endpoints, comparison across studies was difficult because of different methods for 
341 
deriving scores and different versions of scores measuring adherence to the same dietary pattern. 
342 
In the Mediterranean dietary indices and the AHEI scores, moderate alcohol was included as a 
343 
“positive” component (associated with potential benefits). Red and processed meats were 
344 
“negative” (potentially detrimental) components in the Mediterranean scores, AHEI scores, and 
345 
DASH. Certain scores also included sugars or sugar-sweetened beverages as negative 
346 
components. Poultry was considered as a positive component in the original AHEI. Total high-
347 
fat dairy was a negative component in the Mediterranean diet scores, but dairy was a positive 
348 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
10 
 
component when meeting recommended intakes for the HEI-2005, and low-fat dairy was 
349 
positive in the DASH scores. As the NEL systematic review points out, several components of 
350 
scores associated with decreased CVD risk recurred in multiple dietary patterns and were 
351 
associated as part of scores and as individual components with reduced CVD risk. These 
352 
included consumption of vegetables, fruits, whole grains, nuts, legumes, unsaturated fats, and 
353 
fish. 
354 
 
355 
The NHLBI Lifestyle Report summarized the evidence from two RCTs of the DASH dietary 
356 
pattern and two trials testing DASH variations with differing levels of sodium or macronutrients. 
357 
The diet provided to participants in standard DASH intervention trials was high in vegetables, 
358 
fruits, low-fat dairy products, whole grains, poultry, fish, and nuts. It also was low in sweets, 
359 
sugar-sweetened beverages, and reduced in (or lower in) red and processed meats. The DASH 
360 
dietary pattern is high in fiber and potassium and low in sodium, saturated fat, total fat, and 
361 
cholesterol. It is rich in potassium, magnesium, and calcium, as well as protein and fiber.  
362 
 
363 
In contrast to the patterns described above, vegetarian diets were defined by what they excluded. 
364 
Variations included: vegan (no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy); lacto-ovo vegetarian (includes eggs 
365 
and dairy, but no fish or meat), and pesco-vegetarian (includes fish, but no meat) diets. The 
366 
content of these diets varied substantially, though they tended to emphasize plant based foods, 
367 
especially fruits and vegetables, legumes, nuts, and whole grains.  
368 
 
369 
Dietary Patterns and Blood Pressure (BP) 
370 
DASH or DASH-style Dietary Patterns  
371 
The NEL systematic review and AHA/ACC Lifestyle Guideline conclude that strong and 
372 
consistent evidence from RCTs demonstrates that compared to a dietary pattern that is relatively 
373 
high in saturated fat and sodium and low in vegetables and fruits, the DASH-style dietary pattern 
374 
reduced BP by approximately 6/3 mmHg (systolic blood pressure/diastolic blood pressure) 
375 
across subgroups defined by sex, race, age, and hypertension status. The DASH trial provided all 
376 
food to participants for 8 weeks. Fat intake was relatively low at 26 percent of energy (7 percent 
377 
each monounsaturated and saturated, 10 percent polyunsaturated), compared to 36 percent in the 
378 
control group. Carbohydrates accounted for 57 percent of energy and protein for 18 percent. 
379 
Sodium was stable at 3,000 mg/day and body weight did not change. Variations of the DASH 
380 
diet also lowered blood pressure: in the OmniHeart Trial, compared to the standard DASH, 
381 
replacing 10 percent of calories from carbohydrate with either the same calorie content of protein 
382 
or with unsaturated fat (8 percent MUFA and 2 percent PUFA) lowered systolic BP by 1 mmHg. 
383 
Among adults with BP 140–159/90–95 mmHg, these substitutions lowered systolic BP by 3 
384 
mmHg relative to standard DASH.2, 11  
385 
 
386 
Observational evidence summarized in the NEL report included one cohort showing that 
387 
increased DASH score was associated with small, but decreased levels of systolic and diastolic 
388 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
11 
 
BP over time;20 two others cohorts showed no relationship between DASH scores and risk of 
389 
hypertension.21, 22 
390 
 
391 
Mediterranean-Style Dietary Patterns  
392 
Several RCTs provide limited to moderate evidence on the benefits of a Mediterranean-style diet 
393 
for reducing blood pressure. The AHA/ACC Lifestyle Guideline conclude that consuming a 
394 
Mediterranean dietary pattern instead of a lower-fat dietary pattern had beneficial effects on 
395 
blood pressure. The NHLBI Lifestyle Report reviewed two RCTs of free-living middle-aged or 
396 
older adults (with type 2 diabetes  or at least three CVD risk factors) in which a Mediterranean 
397 
diet intervention reduced BP by 6–7/2–3 mmHg.23, 24 The report also reviewed one observational 
398 
study of healthy younger adults. Higher adherence to a Mediterranean-style diet, as measured 
399 
through a Mediterranean score, was associated with a decrease in BP of 2–3/1–2 mmHg.25  
400 
 
401 
Vegetarian Dietary Patterns  
402 
Evidence for the blood pressure benefits of vegetarian dietary patterns is more limited, but 
403 
moderately consistent trends appear to exist. A recent meta-analysis of seven RCTs found that 
404 
consumption of vegetarian diets was associated with a reduction in mean systolic blood pressure 
405 
(-4.8 mm Hg; 95% CI = -6.6 to -3.1; p<0.01) and diastolic blood pressure (-2.2 mm Hg; 95% CI 
406 
= -3.5 to -1.0) compared with the consumption of omnivorous diets.19 The AHA/ACC Lifestyle 
407 
Guideline did not find sufficient evidence to examine vegetarian dietary patterns, and the NEL 
408 
systematic review summarized only three studies comparing blood pressure outcomes in lacto-
409 
ovo vegetarian diets versus non-vegetarian diets in which meat and fish were consumed. Of the 
410 
two studies, one was a large prospective cohort that found no association with blood pressure,26 
411 
and the other was a RCT among individuals with hypertension that demonstrated a decrease in 
412 
systolic blood pressure, but not diastolic blood pressure.27 The more recent EPIC-Oxford cohort 
413 
found lower systolic, but not diastolic blood pressure compared to the findings of Crowe, 2013.28  
414 
 
415 
Other Dietary Patterns 
416 
As summarized in the NEL systematic review, adherence to the 2005 Dietary Guidelines for 
417 
Americans was related to lower blood pressure in one study of healthy young adults. Zamora et 
418 
al reported 20-year findings from the CARDIA study including 4,381 Black and White young 
419 
adults.29 Participants in the highest (vs. lowest) quartile of adherence to the 2005 Dietary 
420 
Guidelines had significantly less increase in systolic and diastolic blood pressure over time. 
421 
 
422 
Dietary Patterns and Blood Lipids  
423 
DASH or DASH-style Dietary Patterns 
424 
As reviewed in the NHLBI Lifestyle Report, RCTs of the DASH diet show favorable effects on 
425 
low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and total cholesterol: high-density lipoprotein 
426 
cholesterol (total-C: HDL-C) ratio, and no effect on triglycerides (TG). Benefits were seen with a 
427 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
12 
 
variety of different macronutrient compositions, though they were enhanced when some 
428 
carbohydrates in the standard DASH pattern were replaced with protein or unsaturated fat. In the 
429 
standard DASH, when food was supplied to adults with a total cholesterol level of less than 260 
430 
mg/dL and LDL-C less than 160 mg/dL, and body weight was kept stable, the DASH dietary 
431 
pattern compared to the control diet decreased LDL-C by 11 mg/dL, decreased HDL-C by 4 
432 
mg/dL, and had no effect on TG. The OmniHeart trial tested the DASH dietary pattern with 
433 
different macronutrient compositions among adults with average baseline LDL-C 130 mg/dL, 
434 
HDL-C 50 mg/dL, and TG 100 mg/dL. Modifying the DASH diet by replacing 10 percent of 
435 
calories from carbohydrate with 10 percent of calories from protein decreased LDL-C by 3 
436 
mg/dL, decreased HDL-C by 1 mg/dL, and decreased TG by 16 mg/dL compared to the DASH 
437 
dietary pattern. Replacing 10 percent of calories from carbohydrate with 10 percent of calories 
438 
from unsaturated fat (8 percent MUFA and 2 percent PUFA) decreased LDL-C similarly, 
439 
increased HDL-C by 1 mg/dL, and decreased TG by 10 mg/dL compared to the DASH dietary 
440 
pattern.11 
441 
 
442 
Mediterranean-style Dietary Patterns 
443 
As with blood pressure, few trials have evaluated the effects of Mediterranean dietary patterns on 
444 
blood lipids. According to the AHA/ACC Lifestyle Guideline, consuming a Mediterranean-style 
445 
diet (compared to minimal or no dietary advice) resulted in no consistent effect on plasma LDL-
446 
C, HDL-C, and TG. In part, this was due to substantial differences in dietary interventions 
447 
conducted among free-living middle aged or older adults with or without CVD or at high risk for 
448 
CVD.11 In the PREDIMED trial (reviewed in both the NHLBI Lifestyle and NEL reports), both 
449 
treatment groups (Mediterranean diet +olive oil or +nuts) had favorable changes in HDL-C, 
450 
total-C: HDL-C ratio and TG when compared to the control group, which received minimal 
451 
advice to follow a lower-fat diet.23 One of the prospective cohort studies reviewed by the NEL 
452 
showed each one-point increase in alternate Mediterranean diet score assessed in adolescence 
453 
and early adulthood was associated with a -6.19 (-10.44, -1.55) mg/dL lower total cholesterol in 
454 
adulthood but no significant effects on HDL-C.30 Of other observational cohorts reviewed, one 
455 
reported adherence to a Mediterranean diet was associated with favorable changes in HDL-C and 
456 
TG,31 and another found no associations between adherence to a Mediterranean diet and blood 
457 
lipids.32  
458 
 
459 
Vegetarian Dietary Patterns 
460 
The NEL systematic review included three articles on vegetarian patterns that measured blood 
461 
pressure or blood lipids.26-28 One study reported decreased total-C26 and another reported 
462 
decreased non-HDL-C in vegetarian versus non-vegetarian participants.28  
463 
 
464 
Other Dietary Patterns 
465 
Of note, adherence to the 2005 Dietary Guidelines for Americans also was related to higher 
466 
HDL-C levels in a cohort of Black and White young adults.29 
467 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
13 
 
 
468 
Dietary Patterns and Cardiovascular Disease Outcomes 
469 
The NHLBI Lifestyle review did not include any trials examining the evidence of particular 
470 
dietary patterns with CVD outcomes. Overall, the NEL systematic review found that individuals 
471 
whose diets mirrored the dietary patterns of interest (typically compared with diets having lower 
472 
scores) was associated with lower CVD incidence and mortality in 14 out of 17 studies. The 
473 
studies were predominantly observational, but included some trial evidence, and they typically 
474 
assessed dietary intakes through self-report. The effect sizes varied substantially, with the 
475 
decrease in risk of CVD ranging from 22 to 59 percent for increased adherence to various 
476 
Mediterranean-style dietary patterns and from 20 to 44 percent for increased adherence to a U.S. 
477 
Dietary Guidelines-related pattern (e.g., HEI or AHEI and updates). The majority of studies that 
478 
assessed coronary heart disease (CHD) incidence or mortality also reported a favorable 
479 
association between adherence to a healthy dietary pattern and CHD risk. The lower CHD risk 
480 
ranged from 29 to 61 percent for greater adherence to Mediterranean-style dietary patterns, from 
481 
24 to 31 percent for greater adherence to a U.S. Dietary Guidelines-related pattern, and from 14 
482 
to 27 percent for greater adherence to DASH. Similarly, the majority of studies assessing stroke 
483 
incidence or mortality reported favorable associations, with the lower stroke risk ranging from 13 
484 
to 53 percent for greater adherence to a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern and from 14 to 60 
485 
percent for greater adherence to a U.S. Dietary Guidelines-related pattern.2  
486 
 
487 
Mediterranean-style Dietary Patterns 
488 
To gather additional information on dietary patterns and CVD outcomes, the DGAC consulted 
489 
two meta-analyses,15, 18 which included many of the same observational prospective cohort 
490 
studies as one another and as the NEL systematic review. These meta-analyses each reported 
491 
summary estimates across studies as a 10 percent reduction in risk of CVD (fatal or nonfatal 
492 
clinical CVD event) per 2-increment increase in adherence to the Mediterranean-style diet. The 
493 
NEL report also included results from the largest Mediterranean diet trial, PREDIMED, which 
494 
found that a Mediterranean diet (plus extra virgin olive oil or nuts) had favorable effects in high-
495 
risk participants compared to the control group who were advised to reduce dietary fat intake. An 
496 
approximately 30 percent decrease in risk of major CVD events (a composite endpoint including 
497 
myocardial infarction, stroke, and deaths) was observed and the trial was stopped early for 
498 
meeting benefit requirements.2, 33 According to food questionnaires measuring adherence to the 
499 
assigned diet by the end of follow-up, the intervention groups had significantly increased 
500 
consumption of fish and legumes and non-significant reductions in refined grains and red meat 
501
