from baseline, in addition to increased intake of supplemental foods (olive oil or nuts depending 
502 
on the intervention arm), compared to the control group.  
503 
 
504 
DASH-style Dietary Patterns 
505 
A recent meta-analysis17 of six prospective cohort studies with CVD endpoints assessed DASH-
506 
style diet through the Fung et al. method,6 which assigns points based on population-specific 
507 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
14 
 
quintiles of eight DASH dietary pattern components: fruits, vegetables, nuts and legumes, whole 
508 
grains, low-fat dairy, sodium, red and processed meats, and sweetened beverages. This meta-
509 
analysis reported that greater adherence to a DASH-style diet significantly reduced CVD 
510 
(Relative Risk [RR]=0.80; 95% CI = 0.74 to 0.86), CHD (RR=0.79; 95% CI = 0.71 to 0.88), and 
511 
stroke (RR=0.81; 95% CI = 0.72 to 0.92). All of the studies meta-analyzed also were included 
512 
the NEL’s evidence base for the DASH-style diet. 
513 
 
514 
Vegetarian Dietary Patterns 
515 
The NEL systematic review concluded that evidence for the effects of vegetarian dietary patterns 
516 
on cardiovascular endpoints is limited. Most of this evidence was from prospective cohort 
517 
studies; four out of six studies suggested that a vegetarian dietary pattern was associated with 
518 
reduced incidence of ischemic heart disease (IHD) or CVD mortality. A meta-analysis of seven 
519 
studies related to CVD mortality and vegetarian diet14 (including two of the studies from the 
520 
NEL systematic review) found that mortality from IHD was significantly lower in vegetarians 
521 
than in non-vegetarians (RR=0.71; 95% CI = 0.56 to 0.87). The authors estimated a 16 percent 
522 
lower mortality from circulatory diseases (RR=0.84; 95% CI = 0.54 to 1.14) and a 12 percent 
523 
lower mortality from cerebrovascular disease (RR=0.88; 95% CI = 0.70 to 1.06) in vegetarians 
524 
compared to non-vegetarians. 
525 
 
526 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit: References 2, 10, 11, 14-19 and Appendix 
527 
E-2.26 
528 
 
529 
 
530 
DIETARY PATTERNS AND BODY WEIGHT 
531 
Question 2: What is the relationship between dietary patterns and measures of 
532 
body weight or obesity? 
533 
Source of evidence: Existing reports  
534 
 
535 
Conclusion 
536 
The DGAC concurs with the 2013 AHA/ACC/TOS Guideline for the Management of 
537 
Overweight and Obesity that strong evidence demonstrates that, preferably as part of a 
538 
comprehensive lifestyle intervention carried out by multidisciplinary teams of professionals or 
539 
nutrition professionals, overweight and obese adults can achieve weight loss through a variety of 
540 
dietary patterns that achieve an energy deficit. Clinically meaningful weight losses that were 
541 
achieved ranged from 4 to 12 kg at 6-month follow-up. Thereafter, slow weight regain is 
542 
observed, with total weight loss at 1 year of 4 to 10 kg and at 2 years of 3 to 4 kg. However, 
543 
some dietary patterns may be more beneficial in the long-term for cardiometabolic health. 
544 
DGAC Grade: Strong 
545 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
15 
 
 
546 
The DGAC concurs with the NEL Dietary Patterns Systematic Review Project that moderate 
547 
evidence indicates dietary patterns that are higher in vegetables, fruits, and whole grains; include 
548 
seafood and legumes; are moderate in dairy products (particularly low and non-fat dairy) and 
549 
alcohol; lower in meats (including red and processed meats), and low in sugar-sweetened foods 
550 
and beverages, and refined grains are associated with favorable outcomes related to healthy body 
551 
weight (including lower BMI, waist circumference, or percent body fat) or risk of obesity. 
552 
Components of the dietary patterns associated with these favorable outcomes include higher 
553 
intakes of unsaturated fats and lower intakes of saturated fats, cholesterol, and sodium. DGAC 
554 
Grade: Moderate 
555 
 
556 
Evidence for children is limited, but studies in the NEL Dietary Patterns Systematic Review 
557 
Project and the systematic review focused on this age group by Ambrosini et al.34 suggest that 
558 
dietary patterns in childhood or adolescence that are higher in energy-dense and low-fiber foods, 
559 
such as sweets, refined grains, and processed meats, as well as sugar-sweetened beverages, 
560 
whole milk, fried potatoes, certain fats and oils, and fast foods increase the risk of obesity later 
561 
on in life. DGAC Grade: Limited 
562 
 
563 
Implications 
564 
To achieve and maintain a healthy body weight, individuals are encouraged to consume dietary 
565 
patterns that are higher in vegetables, fruits, and whole grains; include seafood and legumes; are 
566 
moderate in dairy products (with an emphasis on low- and non-fat dairy), and alcohol; and are 
567 
lower in meats (including red and processed meats), sugar-sweetened foods and beverages, and 
568 
refined grains. During childhood and adolescence, a time period critical for the prevention of 
569 
obesity later in life, a dietary pattern similar to that associated with a healthy weight in adults 
570 
should be encouraged.  
571 
 
572 
Among overweight and obese individuals, an energy deficit is necessary to achieve weight loss. 
573 
This can be achieved through a variety of evidence-based dietary patterns and should be 
574 
approached with comprehensive lifestyle interventions. While it is possible to lose weight on 
575 
his/her own, it is more successful if conducted by trained professionals or by referral to a 
576 
nutrition professional for individual or group counseling (for more details refer to 
577 
AHA/ACC/TOS Guideline for the Management of Overweight and Obesity13 algorithm Box 
578 
11B). Strategies should be based on the individual’s preferences and health status and consider 
579 
the socio-cultural influences on lifestyle behaviors that relate to long-term behavior maintenance. 
580 
These approaches are best complemented with population-based approaches, as mentioned in 
581 
Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and Physical Activity Behavior Change and Part D. 
582 
Chapter 4: Food Environment and Settings, which will allow all factors influencing lifestyle 
583 
behaviors to be addressed as defined in the socio-ecological model.  
584 
 
585 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
16 
 
Review of the Evidence 
586 
The DGAC considered evidence from the 2013 AHA/ACC/TOS Obesity Guideline and 
587 
associated NHLBI Obesity Report, which included only randomized trials,12, 13 the NEL Dietary 
588 
Patterns Systematic Review Project,2 which included 38 studies predominately of prospective 
589 
cohort design and a few randomized trials, and two systematic reviews/meta-analyses published 
590 
since 2008.34, 35 In total, 81 articles were considered in these reports. The published reviews 
591 
provided evidence for the pediatric population (included 7 studies of which 2 overlapped with 
592 
those in the NEL review) and further evidence for dietary patterns related to the Mediterranean-
593 
style diet and its effect on obesity and weight loss (all randomized trials of which 1 out of the 16 
594 
studies overlapped with the NEL review). 
595 
 
596 
Dietary Patterns and the Management of Overweight and Obesity 
597 
In the NHLBI Obesity Report, the 12 randomized studies described in summary Table 3.1 of the 
598 
report all confirm that to lose weight, a variety of dietary pattern approaches can be used and a 
599 
reduction in caloric intake is required. The energy balance equation requires that for weight loss, 
600 
one must consume less energy than one expends or expend more energy than one consumes. The 
601 
report states that any one of the following methods can be used to reduce food and calorie intake: 
602 
prescription of 1,200 to 1,500 kcal/day for women and 1,500 to 1,800 kcal/day for men (kcal 
603 
levels are usually adjusted for the individual’s body weight); prescription of a 500 kcal/day or 
604 
750 kcal/day energy deficit; or prescription of an evidence-based diet that restricts certain food 
605 
types (such as high-carbohydrate foods, low-fiber foods, or high-fat foods) in order to create an 
606 
energy deficit by reduced food intake. 
607 
 
608 
For the different dietary approaches (provided either as part of a comprehensive lifestyle change 
609 
intervention carried out by a multi-disciplinary team of trained professionals or within nutrition 
610 
interventions conducted by nutrition professionals) that the authors of the report evaluated, it is 
611 
evident that all prescribed diets that achieved an energy deficit were associated with weight loss. 
612 
There was no apparent superiority of one approach when behavioral components were balanced 
613 
in the treatment arms. Results indicated that average weight loss is maximal at 6 months with 
614 
smaller losses maintained for up to 2 years, while treatment and follow-up taper. Weight loss 
615 
achieved by dietary techniques aimed at reducing daily energy intake ranges from 4 to 12 kg at 
616 
6-month follow-up. Thereafter, slow weight regain is observed, with total weight loss at 1 year of 
617 
4 to 10 kg and at 2 years of 3 to 4 kg. The following dietary approaches are associated with 
618 
weight loss if reduction in dietary energy intake is achieved: 
619 
 
620 
 A diet from the European Association for the Study of Diabetes Guidelines, which 
621 
focuses on targeting food groups, rather than formal prescribed energy restriction while 
622 
still achieving an energy deficit.  
623 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
17 
 
 Higher protein (25 percent of total calories from protein, 30 percent of total calories from 
624 
fat, 45 percent of total calories from carbohydrate) with provision of foods that realized 
625 
energy deficit. 
626 
 Higher protein Zone™-type diet (5 meals/day, each with 40 percent of total calories from 
627 
carbohydrate, 30 percent of total calories from protein, 30 percent of total calories from 
628 
fat) without formal prescribed energy restriction but realized energy deficit. 
629 
 Lacto-ovo-vegetarian-style diet with prescribed energy restriction. 
630 
 Low-calorie diet with prescribed energy restriction. 
631 
 Low-carbohydrate (initially less than 20 g/day carbohydrate) diet without formal 
632 
prescribed energy restriction but realized energy deficit. 
633 
 Low-fat (10 percent to 25 percent of total calories from fat) vegan-style diet without 
634 
formal prescribed energy restriction but realized energy deficit. 
635 
 Low-fat (20 percent of total calories from fat) diet without formal prescribed energy 
636 
restriction but realized energy deficit. 
637 
 Low-glycemic load diet, either with formal prescribed energy restriction or without 
638 
formal prescribed energy restriction but with realized energy deficit. 
639 
 Lower fat (≤30 percent fat), high dairy (4 servings/day) diets with or without increased 
640 
fiber and/or low-glycemic index/load foods (low-glycemic load) with prescribed energy 
641 
restriction. 
642 
 Macronutrient-targeted diets (15 percent or 25 percent of total calories from protein; 20 
643 
percent or 40 percent of total calories from fat; 35 percent, 45 percent, 55 percent, or 65 
644 
percent of total calories from carbohydrate) with prescribed energy restriction. 
645 
 Mediterranean-style diet with prescribed energy restriction. 
646 
 Moderate protein (12 percent of total calories from protein, 58 percent of total calories 
647 
from carbohydrate, 30 percent of total calories from fat) with provision of foods that 
648 
realized energy deficit. 
649 
 Provision of high-glycemic load or low-glycemic load meals with prescribed energy 
650 
restriction. 
651 
 The AHA-style Step 1 diet (with prescribed energy restriction of 1,500 to 1,800 kcal/day, 
652 
<30 percent of total calories from fat, <10 percent of total calories from saturated fat). 
653 
Although these dietary patterns with an energy deficit will result in weight loss during a 6-
654 
months to 2-year period, long-term health implications with certain patterns may be detrimental 
655 
to cardiometabolic health. These associations have been discussed in the dietary patterns and 
656 
cardiovascular health section as well as the saturated fat and cardiovascular health section.  
657 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
18 
 
 
658 
As presented in Table D2.1 at the end of the chapter, the results of the randomized studies 
659 
considered in the AHA/ACC/TOS Guideline provide evidence for what works in terms of the 
660 
components of a comprehensive lifestyle intervention or nutrition interventions that are needed 
661 
to achieve weight loss with the variety of dietary approaches described above. 
662 
 
663 
Dietary Patterns and their Association with Body Weight 
664 
A total of 14 studies met the inclusion criteria for the index/score question of the NEL systematic 
665 
review and were categorized based on dietary pattern exposure. Two major categories were 
666 
identified: (1) studies that examined exposure based on a Mediterranean-designated dietary 
667 
pattern and (2) studies that examined exposure based on expert dietary guidelines 
668 
recommendations. Taken together, there were six studies on Mediterranean-designated diet 
669 
scores,23, 31, 32, 36-38 five studies on dietary guidelines-based indices,39-43 two studies on 
670 
Mediterranean-designated scores and dietary guidelines indices,44, 45 and one study that used a 
671 
trial-based customized score.46 Two of the studies were RCTs of positive quality23, 46 and 12 
672 
were prospective cohort studies. The studies were carried out between 2006 and 2012. 
673 
 
674 
The sample sizes for prospective cohort studies ranged from 732 to 373,803 participants, with 
675 
follow-up times from 1.5 to 20 years. Ten out of 12 of the prospective cohort studies were 
676 
conducted with generally healthy adults with a mean age of 25 to 63 years. Two studies were 
677 
conducted with children and adolescents (one with girls).39, 40 The two RCTs were conducted in 
678 
adults with elevated chronic disease risk: one study with a Mediterranean-designated diet 
679 
intervention on older adults at increased CVD risk with more than 90 percent overweight or 
680 
obese23 and one study using an a priori diet intervention on men with pre-existing metabolic 
681 
syndrome.46 The sample sizes for the RCTs were from 187 to 769 subjects and duration of 
682 
follow-up ranged from 3 to 12 months.  
683 
 
684 
Mediterranean-style Dietary Pattern 
685 
Four out of the six studies evaluating the Mediterranean style dietary pattern were conducted in 
686 
Spain.23, 32, 36, 37 Of the other two, one study was the European multicenter study that was part of 
687 
the EPIC-Physical Activity, Nutrition, Alcohol Consumption, Cessation of Smoking, Eating out 
688 
of Home, and Obesity (EPIC-PANACEA) study,38 and one was conducted in the United States.31  
689 
 
690 
Dietary Patterns and Body Weight and Incidence of Overweight and/or Obesity 
691 
The Prevencion con Dieta Mediterranean (PREDIMED) study tested the effects of a 
692 
Mediterranean diet on the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease in a high-risk group of 
693 
men and women. Subjects either had type 2 diabetes or three cardiovascular disease risk factors 
694 
(such as hypertension or current smoking) and 90 percent were overweight or obese defined as 
695 
BMI ≥25 kg/m2. The PREDIMED trial randomly assigned participants to three interventions: (1) 
696 
Mediterranean diet with extra virgin olive oil, (2) Mediterranean diet with mixed nuts, and (3) 
697 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
19 
 
low-fat diet. At end of 3 months of a 4-year clinical trial, the authors found that the 
698 
Mediterranean diet score increased in the two Mediterranean diet groups of the trial and 
699 
remained unchanged in the low-fat group. However, no significant changes in body weight and 
700 
adiposity occurred within or between groups from baseline to the 3 months. Beunza et al., 2010 
701 
reported on a prospective cohort study in Spain, the Seguimiento Universidad de Navarra (SUN) 
702 
study.36 Participants with the highest adherence to a Mediterranean dietary pattern, assessed 
703 
using the Trichopoulou Mediterranean Diet Score (MDS) were found to have lower average 
704 
yearly weight gain, -0.059 kg/y (95% CI = -0.111 to -0.008 kg/y; p for trend = 0.02), than 
705 
participants in the lowest adherence group.7 However, the MDS was not associated with 
706 
incidence of overweight or obesity in participants who were normal weight at baseline. Mendez 
707 
et al., 2006 reported on the EPIC-Spain prospective cohort study.37  Adherence to a 
708 
Mediterranean diet was assessed using a slight modification of the Trichopoulou MDS, with 
709 
exposure categorized in tertiles of low (0-3), medium (4-5), and high (6-8) adherence. 
710 
Participants with highest MDS adherence had reduced incidence of obesity when overweight at 
711 
baseline; overweight women and men were 27 percent and 29 percent, respectively, less likely to 
712 
become obese. High MDS adherence was not associated with incidence of overweight in subjects 
713 
who were normal weight at baseline. The EPIC-PANACEA study examined the association 
714 
between adherence to the relative Mediterranean dietary pattern (rMDS), prospective weight 
715 
change, and the incidence of overweight or obesity. Participants with high rMED adherence 
716 
gained less weight in 5 years than did participants with low rMED adherence (-0.16 kg; 95% CI 
717 
= -0.24 to -0.07 kg) and had a 10 percent lower odds of becoming overweight or obese (OR = 
718 
0.90; 95% CI = 0.82 to 0.96). The contribution of each rMED scoring component also was 
719 
assessed and it was found that the association between rMED and weight change was no longer 
720 
significant when meat and meat products were not part of the score. Lastly, a meta-analysis of 
721 
the odds ratio scores of all 10 European countries showed that a 2-point increase in rMED score 
722 
was associated with 3 percent (95% CI = 1 to 5%) lower odds of becoming overweight or obese 
723 
over 5 years.  
724 
 
725 
Dietary Patterns and Waist Circumference 
726 
Rumawas et al., 2009 conducted a prospective cohort study using a subset of the Framingham 
727 
Offspring and Spouse (FOS) study.31 Dietary exposure was assessed in quintiles of low to high 
728 
adherence to the Mediterranean style dietary pattern score (MSDPS). Participants with a higher 
729 
MSDPS had significantly lower waist circumference (p for trend < 0.001). Tortosa et al., 2007 
730 
reported on the association of the Mediterranean dietary pattern and metabolic syndrome in the 
731 
SUN study conducted in Spain.32 Participants in the highest tertile of adherence to the MDS had 
732 
lower waist circumference, -0.05 cm over 6 years (p for trend = 0.038), compared to the lowest 
733 
tertile.  
734 
 
735 
Although some mixed results from prospective studies may be due to differences in the length of 
736 
follow up, definition of the Mediterranean dietary pattern and population included, the results of 
737 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
20 
 
randomized studies indicate a significant reduction in body weight when calories are restricted. 
738 
A high quality meta-analysis (AMSTAR rating of 11) on the association of a Mediterranean-
739 
style diet with body weight conducted by Esposito included 16 randomized studies of which 
740 
one32 overlapped with the NEL systematic review was included in the DGAC body of evidence 
741 
for this question. The meta-analysis included studies conducted in the United States, Italy, Spain, 
742 
France, Israel, Greece, Germany, and the Netherlands that lasted from 4 weeks to 24 months 
743 
with a total of 3,436 participants. Using a random effects model, participants in the 
744 
Mediterranean diet group had significant weight loss (mean difference between Mediterranean 
745 
diet and control diet, -1.75 kg; 95% CI = -2.86 to -0.64) and reduction in BMI (mean difference, 
746 
-0.57 kg/m2; 95% CI = 0.93 to 0.21 kg/m2) compared to those in the control arm. The effect of 
747 
Mediterranean diet on body weight was greater in association with energy restriction (mean 
748 
difference, -3.88 kg; 95% CI = -6.54 to -1.21 kg), increased physical activity (-4.01 kg; 95% CI 
749 
= -5.79 to -2.23 kg), and follow up longer than 6 months (-2.69 kg; 95% CI = -3.99 to -1.38 kg). 
750 
Across all 16 studies, the Mediterranean style dietary pattern did not cause weight gain. 
751 
 
752 
Dietary Guidelines-Based Indices 
753 
Of the seven studies conducted on dietary guidelines-based indices, three studies were conducted 
754 
in the United States with U.S.-based indices.39, 41, 43 One study was conducted in Germany with 
755 
an index developed in the United States,40 and two studies were conducted in France (one used a 
756 
French index,42 and the other compared six different dietary scores).44  
757 
 
758 
Dietary Patterns and Body Weight and Incidence of Overweight and/or Obesity 
759 
Gao et al., 2008 reported on a prospective cohort study of White, African American, Hispanic, 
760 
and Chinese men and women in the Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA) in the US. 
761 
Two versions of the 2005 HEI were used: the original and a modified version that adjusted the 
762 
food group components to incorporate levels of caloric need based on sex, age, and activity 
763 
level.41 For the overall population, there was an inverse association between quintiles of each 
764 
HEI score and BMI (p<0.001). The risk of obesity in normal weight participants was inversely 
765 
associated with HEI scores only for Whites (p<0.05). A comparison of the HEI-1995 and HEI-
766 
2005 scores indicated that beta-coefficients, as predictors of body weight and BMI, were higher 
767 
for the HEI-2005 scores in Whites. Zamora et al., 2010 analyzed data from the prospective 
768 
cohort study, Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults (CARDIA), conducted in the 
769 
United States, to examine the association between diets consistent with the 2005 Dietary 
770 
Guidelines and subsequent weight gain in Black and White young adults.43  The Diet Quality 
771 
Index (DQI)  included 10 components of the 2005 Dietary Guidelines relating to the 
772 
consumption of total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, added sugars, reduced-fat milk, fruit, 
773 
vegetables, whole grains, nutrient-dense foods, and limited sodium and alcohol intake. They 
774 
found, a 10-point increase in DQI score was associated with a 10 percent lower risk of gaining 
775 
10 kg in normal-weight Whites. However, the same magnitude increase in score was associated 
776 
with a 15 percent higher risk in obese Blacks (p<0.001). Kesse-Guyot et al., 2009 conducted a 
777 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
21 
 
prospective cohort study in France to examine the association between adherence to a dietary 
778 
score based on the French 2001 nutritional guidelines (Programme National Nutrition Sante´ 
779 
guidelines score (PNNS-GS) and changes in body weight, body fat distribution, and obesity 
780 
risk.42  The PNNS-GS includes 12 nutritional components: fruit and vegetables, starchy foods, 
781 
whole grains, dairy products, meat, seafood, added fat, vegetable fat, sweets, water and soda, 
782 
alcohol, and salt. The last PNNS-GS component is physical activity. In fully adjusted models, an 
783 
increase of one PNNS-GS unit was associated with lower weight gain (p=0.004), and lower BMI 
784 
gain (p=0.002). An increase of 1 PNNS-GS unit was associated with a lower probability of 
785 
becoming overweight (including obese) (OR = 0.93; 95% CI = 0.88 to 0.99). Similarly, an 
786 
increase of 1 PNNS-GS unit was associated with a lower probability of becoming obese (OR = 
787 
0.89; 95% CI = 0.80 to 0.99). 
788 
 
789 
Two studies were conducted in children. Cheng et al., 2010 analyzed data from a prospective 
790 
cohort study conducted in Germany, the Dortmund Nutritional and Anthropometric 
791 
Longitudinally Designed (DONALD) study, to examine whether the diet quality of healthy 
792 
children before puberty was associated with body composition at onset of puberty.40  Adherence 
793 
to a diet pattern was assessed by the Revised Children’s Diet Quality Index (RC-DQI) which was 
794 
based on the Dietary Guidelines for Americans. In this study, a higher dietary quality was 
795 
associated with a higher energy intake, and children with a lower diet quality had lower BMI and 
796 
Fat Mass Index (FMI) Z-scores at baseline (p<0.01) but not at onset of puberty. Berz et al., 2011 
797 
reported on a prospective cohort study to assess the effects of the DASH eating pattern on BMI 
798 
in adolescent females over a 10-year period.39 Only seven out of the 10 original components of 
799 
the DASH score were used; the three excluded were added sugars, discretionary fats and oils, 
800 
and alcohol. Overall, girls in the highest vs. lowest quintile of DASH score had an adjusted mean 
801 
BMI of 24.4 vs. 26.3 kg/m2 (p<0.05). 
802 
 
803 
Dietary Patterns and Waist Circumference  
804 
Gao et al, found, for the overall population in the MESA study, an inverse association between 
805 
quintiles of each HEI score and waist circumference (WC) (p<0.001).41 The study by Kesse-
806 
Guyot conducted in France showed, in fully adjusted models, an increase of one PNNS-GS unit 
807 
was associated with lower waist circumference gain (p=0.01) and lower waist-to-hip ratio gain 
808 
(p=0.02).42  
809 
 
810 
Other Indices 
811 
Jacobs et al., 2009 conducted an RCT in Norway, the Oslo Diet and Exercise Study, to examine 
812 
the effect of changes in diet patterns on body weight and other outcomes among men who met 
813 
the criteria for the metabolic syndrome (n=187 men).46 Study participants were randomly 
814 
assigned to: (1) the diet protocol, (2) the exercise protocol, (3) the diet + exercise protocol, or (4) 
815 
the control protocol. The trial duration was 12 months. The authors created their own diet score 
816 
to assess adherence to the intervention. The score was based on summing the participants 
817 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
22 
 
ranking of intake (across tertiles) of 35 food groups that, based on the literature, had a beneficial 
818 
neutral or detrimental effect on health. A higher score reflected greater adherence to the diet 
819 
intervention. Over the course of the intervention, the diet score increased by 2 points (SD ±5.5) 
820 
in both diet groups, with a decrease of an equivalent amount in the exercise and control groups. 
821 
A 10-point change in the diet score during the intervention period was associated with a 3.5 kg 
822 
decrease in weight, a 2.8 cm decrease in waist circumference and 1.3 percent decrease in percent 
823 
body fat (all significant at p<0.0001). 
824 
 
825 
Studies that Compared Various Dietary Indices 
826 
In a study by Lassale et al., subjects were participants in the SUpplementation en VItamines et 
827 
Minereaux AntioXydants (SU.VI.MAX) study and diet quality was assessed using a 
828 
Mediterranean Score (MDS, rMED, MSDPS), the Diet Quality Index-International (DQI-I), the 
829 
2005 Dietary Guidelines for Americans Adherence Index (DGAI), and the French Programme 
830 
National Nutrition Sante-Guidelines Score (PNNS-GS).44 Overall, better adherence to a 
831 
Mediterranean diet (except for the MSDPS) or expert dietary guidelines was associated with 
832 
lower weight gain in men who were normal weight at baseline (p for trend = <0.05). In addition, 
833 
among the 1,569 non-obese men at baseline, the odds of becoming obese associated with one 
834 
standard deviation increase in dietary score ranged from OR = 0.63 (95% CI = 0.51 to 0.78) for 
835 
the DGAI to OR = 0.72 (95% CI = 0.59 to 0.88) for the MDS, only the MSDPS was non-
836 
significant. In women, no association between diet scores and weight gain or incidence of 
837 
obesity was found. Woo et al., 2008 reported on a prospective cohort study in Hong Kong to 
838 
examine adherence to a diet pattern using the MDS and the Diet Quality Index International 
839 
(DQI-I).45  They found that increased adherence to either the MDS or DQI-I was not associated 
840 
with becoming overweight. 
841 
 
842 
Dietary Patterns from Data-Driven Methods 
843 
In the NEL review, a total of 11 studies from prospective cohort studies were included that either 
844 
used factor or cluster analyses to derive dietary patterns. Eight of the eleven studies were 
845 
conducted in the United States, with additional studies from the United Kingdom, Iran, and 
846 
Sweden. The sample sizes ranged from 206 to 51,670 participants with follow-up times from 3 to 
847 
20 years. The majority of the studies were conducted with generally healthy adult men and 
848 
women,47-52 five studies included women only,53-57 and one was conducted in children to 
849 
examine weight gain in adolescence over the period of follow-up.56 Outcomes examined 
850 
included change in body weight (3 studies), BMI (7 studies), and waist circumference (6 
851 
studies); one study examined both percent body fat and incidence of overweight/obesity. 
852 
 
853 
Most of the studies found at least two generic food patterns: a “healthy/prudent” food pattern and 
854 
an “unhealthy/western” pattern. Generally, healthy patterns were associated with more favorable 
855 
body weight outcomes, while the opposite was seen for unhealthy patterns. However, not all 
856 
studies reported significant associations. There was a potential difference in associations found 
857 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
23 
 
by sex: of the three studies that analyzed men and women separately, men tended to have null 
858 
results. However, data were insufficient to draw conclusions about population subgroups. 
859 
Furthermore, because the patterns are data-driven, they represent what was consumed by the 
860 
study population, and thus it is difficult to compare across the disparate patterns. The one study 
861 
that analyzed the dietary patterns of pre-pubescent children transitioning into adolescence 
862 
showed that patterns vary widely at this age and caution should be observed when analyzing 
863 
these data because the diet of children changes rapidly, as does their weight. 
864 
 
865 
The DGAC considered the systematic review by Ambrosini et al. that included seven articles, 
866 
two of which overlapped with the NEL review.34  Results demonstrated a positive association 
867 
between a dietary pattern high in energy-dense, high fat, and low fiber foods and later obesity (4 
868 
of the 7 studies), while three studies demonstrated null associations. The seven longitudinal 
869 
studies of children from the United Kingdom, United States, Australia, Norway, Finland, and 
870 
Colombia had follow-up periods ranging from 2 to 21 years and had sample sizes from 427 to 
871 
6772 individuals. The studies determined dietary patterns using factor or cluster analysis (5) or 
872 
reduced rank regression (2). 
873 
  
874 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit: References 2, 13, 34, 35 and Appendix E-
875 
2.27 
876 
 
877 
DIETARY PATTERNS AND TYPE 2 DIABETES 
878 
Question 3: What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of type 2 
879 
diabetes? 
880 
Source of evidence: Existing reports  
881 
 
882 
Conclusion 
883 
Moderate evidence indicates that healthy dietary patterns higher in vegetables, fruits, and whole 
884 
grains and lower in red and processed meats, high-fat dairy products, refined grains, and 
885 
sweets/sugar-sweetened beverages reduce the risk of developing type 2 diabetes. DGAC Grade: 
886 
Moderate 
887 
 
888 
Evidence is lacking for the pediatric population. 
889 
 
890 
Implications 
891 
To reduce the risk of developing type 2 diabetes, individuals are encouraged to consume dietary 
892 
patterns that are rich in vegetables, fruits, and whole grains and lower in red and processed 
893 
meats, high-fat dairy, refined grains, and sweets/sugar-sweetened beverages in addition to 
894 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
24 
 
maintaining a healthy body weight. Diabetes can be prevented through the consumption of a 
895 
variety of healthy dietary patterns that share these components and that are tailored to the 
896 
biological needs and socio-cultural preferences of the individual and carried out preferably 
897 
through counseling by a nutrition professional.  
898 
 
899 
Review of the Evidence 
900 
The Committee considered two sources of evidence. The primary source was the NEL Dietary 
901 
Patterns Systematic Review Project which included 37 studies predominantly of prospective 
902 
cohorts design and some randomized trials (n=8).2 This primary source was supplemented by a 
903 
published meta-analysis58 that included 15 cohort studies of which 13 overlapped with the NEL 
904 
review.58 The meta-analysis provided an estimate of the effect size of incident type 2 diabetes 
905 
associated with a healthy and unhealthy dietary pattern.  
906 
 
907 
Although the NEL rated the overall body of evidence for type 2 diabetes as limited, this was 
908 
primarily a result of examining the different methods for defining dietary patterns (e.g. indices, 
909 
data driven, and reduce rank regression) separately. As such, the NEL noted these 
910 
methodological inconsistencies across studies but stated general support for the consumption of a 
911 
dietary pattern rich in vegetables and fruits and low in high-fat dairy and meats. The DGAC 
912 
concurred with this conclusion. However, the DGAC has elevated the grade of the entire body of 
913 
evidence to moderate given that the NEL findings were corroborated by the results of a high 
914 
quality meta-analysis (AMSTAR rating of 11) and the magnitude of the associations that showed 
915 
when the results of 15 cohort studies are pooled, evidence indicated a 21 percent reduction in the 
916 
risk of developing type 2 diabetes associated with dietary patterns characterized by high 
917 
consumption of whole grains, vegetables, and fruit. Conversely, a 44 percent increased risk of 
918 
developing type 2 diabetes was seen with an unhealthy dietary pattern characterized by higher 
919 
consumption of red or processed meats, high-fat dairy, refined grains, and sweets.  
920 
 
921 
Dietary Patterns and Incident Type 2 Diabetes 
922 
Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH)  
923 
One study used the DASH score in a cohort of 820 U.S. adults ages 40 to 69 years and with  
924 
equal sex distribution and racial diversity.59 Liese et al. found adherence to the DASH score was 
925 
associated with markedly reduced odds of type 2 diabetes in Whites but not in the total 
926 
population, or in the Blacks and Hispanics, which comprised the majority of this cohort. 
927 
 
928 
Mediterranean-style Dietary Patterns 
929 
Three studies assessed Mediterranean-style dietary pattern adherence (Mediterranean Diet Score 
930 
[MDS]) with sample sizes ranging from 5,000 to more than 20,000 in both Mediterranean and 
931 
U.S. populations. One study conducted in Spain with the SUN cohort (n=13,380) found a 
932 
favorable association between the MDS (the original MDS of Trichopoulou) and risk of type 2 
933 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
25 
 
diabetes. Overall, a 2-point increase in MDS was associated with a 35 percent reduction in risk 
934 
of type 2 diabetes.60 Another study, conducted in Greece with the EPIC-Greece cohort 
935 
(n=22,295), also assessed the relationship between the MDS and type 2 diabetes. In this second 
936 
Mediterranean population, adherence to the MDS also was favorably associated with decreased 
937 
risk of diabetes.61 Conversely, a study conducted in the United States, using the authors’ 
938 
MedDiet Score with the Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA) cohort (n=5,390) found 
939 
no association between their MedDiet Score and type 2 diabetes incidence in the total 
940 
population, in men or women, or in specific racial/ethnic groups.62  
941 
 
942 
Dietary Indices based on the Dietary Guidelines 
943 
Four studies used dietary guidelines-based indices such as the AHEI and the Diet Quality Index 
944 
(DQI). The sample sizes of the studies ranged from 1,821 to 80,029. A study that assessed 
945 
adherence to the AHEI in the United States found a favorable association between AHEI score 
946 
and risk of incident type 2 diabetes in women in the Nurses’ Health Study (n=80,029).63 In the 
947 
CARDIA study (n=4,381), also from the United States, the authors found no association between 
948 
DQI-2005 score and type 2 diabetes incidence in the total population or in Blacks or Whites.29 
949 
Studies from outside the United States included one conducted in Australia using a Total Diet 
950 
score in the Blue Mountains Eye Study (BMES, n=1,821) and one from Germany using a 
951 
German Food Pyramid Index with the EPIC-Potsdam cohort (n=23,531). Neither found an 
952 
association between these scores and incident type 2 diabetes.64, 65 Thus, evidence for an 
953 
association only exists with the AHEI, which does contain slightly different components from 
954 
the other indices, such as nuts and legumes, trans fat, EPA + DHA (n–3 FAs), PUFAs, alcohol, 
955 
red and processed meat.  
956 
 
957 
Data-Driven Approaches 
958 
Eleven studies used factor analysis and one study used cluster analysis. These analyses were all 
959 
conducted using data from prospective cohort studies published between 2004 and 2012 and had 
960 
sample sizes ranging from 690 to more than 75,000 individuals. Five studies were conducted in 
961 
the United States and the rest from developed countries around the world. Each study identified 
962 
one to four dietary patterns, with the most common comparison between "western"/"unhealthy" 
963 
and "prudent"/"healthier" patterns; a total of 35 diverse dietary patterns were identified within 
964 
the body of evidence. Many studies had null findings, particularly studies with duration of less 
965 
than 7 years of follow up.66-69 Patterns associated with lower risk of type 2 diabetes were 
966 
characterized by higher intakes of vegetables, fruits, low-fat dairy products, and whole grains, 
967 
and those associated with increased risk were characterized by higher intakes of red meat, sugar-
968 
sweetened foods and drinks, French fries, refined grains, and high-fat dairy products. However, 
969 
the food groups identified varied substantially, even among patterns with the same name. 
970 
 
971 
Three prospective cohort studies used reduced rank regression to examine the relationship 
972 
between dietary patterns and type 2 diabetes.70-72 Two of the studies were conducted in the 
973 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
26 
 
United States and one in the United Kingdom. The sample sizes were 880 for Liese (2009), 
974 
2,879 for Imamura (2009), and 6,699 for McNaughton (2008). The independent variables in 
975 
these studies were dietary pattern scores, and biomarkers were used as response variables in two 
976 
of the studies. Dietary patterns that included meat intake and incident type 2 diabetes were 
977 
positively associated in the two studies that used biomarkers as response variables, though the 
978 
definitions of meat differed.70, 71 However, because so few studies were available and the 
979 
methodology used and different populations considered varied so much, the information was 
980 
insufficient to assess consistency or draw conclusions.  
981 
 
982 
Other Dietary Patterns 
983 
The body of evidence examined included seven studies conducted between 2004 and 2013, 
984 
consisting of six RCTs73-79 and one prospective cohort study (PCS).80 Two studies were 
985 
conducted in the United States; one in the United States and Canada; one in Spain (2 
986 
PREDIMED articles); and one each in Greece, Italy, and Sweden. The sample sizes of the RCTs 
987 
ranged from 82 to 1,224 participants and the PCS had a sample size of 41,387 participants. All 
988 
eight studies were conducted in adults. RCT duration ranged from 6 weeks to a median of 4 
989 
years and the PCS duration was 2 years. The RCTs were primary prevention studies of at-risk 
990 
participants. Baseline health status in the study participants included those with mild 
991 
hypercholesterolemia, overweight or obesity, metabolic syndrome, abdominal obesity, and three 
992 
or more CVD risk factors, including metabolic syndrome. The PCS participants were individuals 
993 
in the Adventist Health Study who did not have type 2 diabetes.80 Three studies looked at a 
994 
Mediterranean-style diet,75, 77-79 one study examined the Nordic diet (defined by the authors of 
995 
the study as a diet rich in high-fiber plant foods, fruits, berries, vegetables, whole grains, 
996 
rapeseed oil, nuts, fish and low-fat milk products, but low in salt, added sugars, and saturated 
997 
fats),73 and three studies looked at either the DASH diet or a variation of the DASH diet,74, 76 or a 
998 
vegetarian diet.80  
999 
 
1000 
Two of the seven studies examined the association between adherence to a dietary pattern and 
1001 
incidence of type 2 diabetes.79, 80 Although the results of both studies showed a favorable 
1002 
association between either a Mediterranean-style or a vegetarian dietary pattern and incidence of 
1003 
type 2 diabetes the studies differed in design and dietary pattern used to assess diet exposure. 
1004 
The other studies examined the intermediate outcomes of impaired glucose tolerance and/or 
1005 
insulin resistance and are discussed in the next section. 
1006 
 
1007 
Dietary Patterns and Intermediate Outcomes 
1008 
Five studies examined adherence to a dietary pattern and intermediate outcomes related to 
1009 
glucose tolerance and/or insulin resistance: two RCTs23, 46 and three prospective cohort studies.29, 
1010 
31, 64 It was difficult to assess food components across these studies, as numerous different scores 
1011 
were used and no compelling number of studies used any one score or index. Even so, favorable 
1012 
associations between dietary patterns and intermediate outcomes were found.  
1013 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
27 
 
 
1014 
The two RCTs were conducted in populations in Europe that were at risk of diabetes. An early 
1015 
report from the PREDIMED trial showed that a Mediterranean diet decreased fasting blood 
1016 
glucose, fasting insulin, and HOMA-IR scores in a Spanish population at risk of CVD.23 In the 
1017 
Oslo Diet and Exercise Study (ODES), increased adherence to the authors’ a priori diet score 
1018 
resulted in decreased fasting insulin and insulin after a glucose challenge, but not fasting glucose, 
1019 
in Norwegian men with metabolic syndrome.46 Results from prospective cohort studies were 
1020 
consistent in showing a favorable association between diet score and fasting glucose, fasting 
1021 
insulin or HOMA-IR,29, 31 with the exception of one study that found the association with fasting 
1022 
glucose only in men.64  
1023 
 
1024 
Data-Driven Approaches 
1025 
Variations in populations studies, definition of outcomes, dietary assessment methodologies, and 
1026 
methods used to derive patterns resulted in a highly variable set of dietary patterns, thus making 
1027 
it difficult to draw conclusions from studies using data-driven approaches. For example, one 
1028 
study measured fasting blood glucose with a cutoff of 6.1 and greater mmol/L;47 another study 
1029 
measured plasma glucose with a cutoff of 5.1 and greater mmol/L,81 while a third study 
1030 
measured plasma glucose after an overnight fast and after a standard 75 g oral glucose tolerance 
1031 
test.82 Three prospective cohort studies assessed the association between dietary patterns and 
1032 
plasma glucose levels. Two U.S. studies derived patterns using cluster analysis47, 81 and one 
1033 
study conducted in Denmark used factor analysis.82 Duffey et al. identified two diet clusters: 
1034 
“Prudent Diet” and “Western Diet”;47 Kimokoti et al. identified five clusters: “Heart Healthier,” 
1035 
“Lighter Eating,” “Wine and Moderate Eating,” “Higher Fat,” and “Empty Calories”;81 and Lau 
1036 
et al. derived two factors: “Modern” and “Traditional.”82  
1037 
  
1038 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:  References 2, 58, and Appendix E-2.28 
1039 
 
1040 
 
1041 
DIETARY PATTERNS AND CANCER 
1042 
Existing Evidence around Foods and Nutrients and Cancer  
1043 
The role of dietary composition in cancer risk has been postulated since ancient times, yet 
1044 
scientific evidence for such relationships was sparse until nearly a century ago. Experimental 
1045 
models of cancer based upon chemical carcinogens, radiation, viral-transmission, and inherited 
1046 
genetic variations gradually emerged in first half of the 20th century and were soon found to be 
1047 
influenced by dietary and nutritional interventions. The establishment of population-based cancer 
1048 
registries around the globe in the years following World War II clearly indicated that the 
1049 
incidence and mortality of specific cancers and the patterns of cancers varied widely between 
1050 
countries. Soon, studies of migrant populations demonstrated that in parallel with acculturation, 
1051 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
28 
 
cancer risk evolved toward that observed in the adopted country, implicating a strong role for 
1052 
environmental influences, such as dietary patterns, in cancer risk. When coupled with national 
1053 
food consumption data, relationships between dietary patterns or components and cancer risk 
1054 
were hypothesized. The development of dietary assessment tools, such as FFQs, paved the way 
1055 
for large prospective epidemiologic cohort studies designed to examine more precisely the role 
1056 
of dietary patterns, foods, and specific nutrients in the risk of various cancers.83 Additional diet 
1057 
assessment tools, such as food diaries, and single and multi-day 24-hr recalls enhanced the 
1058 
ability to undertake population studies and mechanism-based RCTs. These studies were made 
1059 
possible by USDA support of research to advance laboratory methods to define the nutrient 
1060 
content of foods in the U.S. food supply and establish a database that, when coupled with diet 
1061 
assessment tools, provides an estimated intake of energy, macronutrients, vitamins, minerals and 
1062 
other dietary variables. More recently, inclusion into the database of non-nutrient bioactive 
1063 
components primarily found in vegetables and fruits has enhanced the ability to define human 
1064 
intake of bioactive components that may affect health and disease.  
1065 
 
1066 
In 1982, the American Institute for Cancer Research (AICR), a part of the World Cancer 
1067 
Research Fund (WCRF) global philanthropic network, was established. Together, the mission of 
1068 
WCRF/AICR is to fund research and disseminate evidence-based cancer prevention guidelines to 
1069 
the public. In 1997, the AICR/WCRF published the results of a comprehensive multi-year effort 
1070 
to systematically review the published scientific literature and develop dietary guidelines for 
1071 
cancer prevention.84  With a rapid expansion of available data in the subsequent years, the 
1072 
process was repeated for the 2007 AICR/WCRF report.85 This effort has been enhanced in 
1073 
subsequent years by the AICR/WCRF Continuous Update Project (CUP), in which data are 
1074 
reviewed and updated on a continuous, rolling basis for specific cancers, with several reports 
1075 
completed annually.86 This effort is accomplished through a rigorous systematic review process 
1076 
in which scientific evidence is gathered, reviewed and judged by panels of experts in nutrition 
1077 
and cancer in order to generate nutrition and cancer prevention goals for policy makers, the 
1078 
general population, and individuals seeking to reduce cancer risk.87   The most recent summary 
1079 
of the systematic review which documents important information about the relationship between 
1080 
specific foods, nutrients and other lifestyle behavior and cancer risk is found in Table D2.2.  
1081 
 
1082 
As previously mentioned, the 2015 DGAC chose to determine whether an examination of dietary 
1083 
patterns, could inform the understanding of diet and cancer risk. As this scientific literature is 
1084 
relatively early in its development, we limited our search to the four most common malignancies 
1085 
affecting the American public—lung, breast, colon/rectal, and prostate—which account for the 
1086 
majority of the cancer burden in the United States. Although the published literature on dietary 
1087 
patterns and cancer risk is relatively young, the DGAC felt it was important to examine the 
1088 
evidence and conclusions, consider the implications for development of dietary guidelines, and 
1089 
indicate areas for future research. 
1090 
 
 
1091 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
29
