Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
30 
 
AICR/WCRF Evidence Stratification87  
1096 
Convincing: The evidence for a convincing grade is strong enough to support a causal relationship. This relationship is 
1097 
robust enough that it is unlikely to be modified of research in the foreseeable future. A grade of “convincing” requires evidence 
1098 
from more than one study type, data from at least two cohort studies, no unexplained heterogeneity between study types with 
1099 
regard to the presence or absence of an association, good quality studies where random or systematic errors are unlikely, presence 
1100 
of a dose-response relationship, and strong and plausible experimental evidence relating typical human exposures to relevant 
1101 
cancer outcomes. 
1102 
Probable: The criteria for determining a probable diet and cancer relationship include: evidence from at least two cohort 
1103 
studies or at least five case-control studies, no substantial unexplained heterogeneity between or within study types in the 
1104 
presence or absence of an association or direction of effect, good quality studies where the likelihood of random or systematic 
1105 
error is low, and evidence for biologic plausibility. 
1106 
Limited—suggestive:  This grade is assigned when the evidence is too limited to permit a probable or convincing judgment, 
1107 
but there is evidence of a direction of effect. The evidence may have methodological flaws, or there may be a limited number of 
1108 
studies. A grade of “limited-suggestive” requires the following: evidence from at least two cohort studies or five case-control 
1109 
studies, there is some evidence for biologic plausibility, and the direction of the effect is generally consistent, although there may 
1110 
be some unexplained heterogeneity. 
1111 
Limited—no conclusion:  This grade describes diet and cancer relationships where the evidence was ample for review by 
1112 
the panel, but it was too limited to receive one of the other grades. The available studies may be of good quality, but limited in 
1113 
number or yielding inconsistent results. 
1114 
Substantial effect on risk unlikely: This grade is assigned when the evidence is strong that a particular nutrient, food, 
1115 
dietary pattern, or physical activity is unlikely to have a substantial causal relationship to a cancer outcome. Data must be strong 
1116 
enough that modification in the foreseeable future is unlikely. 
1117 
 
1118 
Question 4: What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of cancer? 
1119 
Source of evidence: NEL systematic review  
1120 
 
1121 
Conclusions 
1122 
Colon/Rectal Cancer: Moderate evidence indicates an inverse association between dietary 
1123 
patterns that are higher in vegetables, fruits, legumes, whole grains, lean meats/seafood, and low-
1124 
fat dairy and moderate in alcohol; and low in red and/or processed meats, saturated fat, and 
1125 
sodas/sweets relative to other dietary patterns and the risk of colon/rectal cancer. Conversely, 
1126 
diets that are higher in red/processed meats, French fries/potatoes, and sources of sugars (i.e., 
1127 
sodas, sweets, and dessert foods) are associated with a greater colon/rectal cancer risk. DGAC 
1128 
Grade: Moderate 
1129 
 
1130 
Breast Cancer: Moderate evidence indicates that dietary patterns rich in vegetables, fruit, and 
1131 
whole grains, and lower in animal products and refined carbohydrate, are associated with 
1132 
reduced risk of post-menopausal breast cancer. The data regarding this dietary pattern and pre-
1133 
menopausal breast cancer risk point in the same direction, but the evidence is limited due to 
1134 
fewer studies. DGAC Grade: Moderate for postmenopausal breast cancer risk; Limited for 
1135 
premenopausal breast cancer risk 
1136 
 
1137 
Lung Cancer: Limited evidence from a small number of studies suggests a lower risk of lung 
1138 
cancer associated with dietary patterns containing more frequent servings of vegetables, fruits, 
1139 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
31 
 
seafood, grains/cereals, and legumes, and lean versus higher fat meats and lower fat or non-fat 
1140 
dairy products. Despite reported modest significant reductions in risk, definitive conclusions 
1141 
cannot be established at this time due to the small number of articles, as well as wide variation in 
1142 
study design, dietary assessment, and case ascertainment. DGAC Grade: Limited 
1143 
 
1144 
Prostate Cancer: No conclusion can be drawn regarding the relationship between dietary 
1145 
patterns and the risk of prostate cancer. This is due to limited evidence from a small number of 
1146 
studies with wide variation in study design, dietary assessment methodology and prostate cancer 
1147 
outcome ascertainment. DGAC Grade: Grade not assignable 
1148 
 
1149 
Implications 
1150 
The data accumulating regarding the impact of dietary patterns on risk of certain types of cancers 
1151 
supports the concept that a healthy dietary pattern may significantly reduce the overall burden of 
1152 
cancer in the United States. Emerging studies on dietary patterns support the findings of expert 
1153 
reviews regarding individual foods and nutrients. Effective strategies to initiate early in life and 
1154 
maintain a healthy dietary pattern and body weight, coupled with regular physical activity, will 
1155 
significantly reduce the cancer burden in America.  
1156 
 
1157 
Review of the Evidence  
1158 
Dietary Patterns and Colorectal Cancer 
1159 
This systematic review included 21 articles from prospective cohort studies and one article from 
1160 
an RCT published since 2000 that examined the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of 
1161 
colorectal cancer.88-109 The articles used diverse methodology to assess dietary patterns. Nine 
1162 
articles used indices/scores to assess dietary patterns, 10 articles used data-driven methods, and 
1163 
three used other approaches.  
1164 
 
1165 
The dietary patterns examined in this systematic review were defined in various ways, making 
1166 
comparisons between articles difficult. However, despite general heterogeneity in this body of 
1167 
evidence, some protective dietary patterns emerged, particularly in articles where patterns were 
1168 
defined by index or score; articles using data-driven methods were less consistent. Patterns 
1169 
emphasizing vegetables, fruits, fish/seafood, legumes, low-fat dairy, and whole grains were 
1170 
generally associated with reduced risk of colorectal cancer. Patterns higher in red/processed 
1171 
meats, potatoes/French fries, and sodas/sweets/added sugars were generally associated with 
1172 
increased risk of colorectal cancer.  
1173 
 
1174 
The relationship between dietary patterns and colorectal cancer risk often varied by sex and 
1175 
tumor location. Results based on analysis by sex were mixed, while analysis in tumor subgroups 
1176 
seemed to indicate that dietary patterns may be more strongly associated with tumor 
1177 
development in distal regions of the colon/rectum. Although most cohort studies make extensive 
1178 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
32 
 
efforts to include participants across a wide range of race/ethnic groups and across the socio-
1179 
economic continuum, there still may be some groups for which the association between dietary 
1180 
patterns and colorectal cancer risk cannot be reliably assessed and therefore conclusions cannot 
1181 
be drawn.  
1182 
 
1183 
Dietary Patterns and Breast Cancer 
1184 
This systematic review included 25 prospective cohort studies and one RCT published since 
1185 
2000 that examined the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of breast cancer.94, 101, 104, 
1186 
110-131 The studies used multiple approaches to assess dietary patterns and cancer risk. Eight 
1187 
studies used indices/scores to assess dietary patterns, 13 studies used factor or principal 
1188 
components analysis, two used reduced rank regression, two made comparisons on the basis of 
1189 
animal product consumption, and one conducted an RCT of a low-fat dietary pattern. 
1190 
 
1191 
This moderate body of evidence encompassed a large diversity in methods to assess or determine 
1192 
dietary patterns, making comparison across studies challenging. Despite this variability, 17 of the 
1193 
included studies found statistically significant relationships between dietary patterns and breast 
1194 
cancer risk, particularly among certain groups of women. Because a variety of different 
1195 
methodologies were employed to derive dietary patterns, and the patterns, while similar in many 
1196 
respects, were composed of different combinations of foods and beverages, it was difficult to 
1197 
determine which patterns had the greatest impact on breast cancer risk reduction.  
1198 
 
1199 
The relationship between dietary patterns and breast cancer risk may be more consistent among 
1200 
postmenopausal women, but additional research is needed to explore the relationships for both 
1201 
pre- and post- menopausal cancer. Certain histopathologic and molecular phenotypes of breast 
1202 
cancer may be affected more by certain dietary patterns, but this has not yet been explored 
1203 
sufficiently. For example, limited studies to date suggest that estrogen or progesterone receptor 
1204 
status of breast cancers may define subgroups with unique dietary risk profiles, but no 
1205 
conclusions can be drawn at this time. More research is needed to explore other factors that may 
1206 
influence the relationship between dietary patterns during various stages of life and breast cancer 
1207 
risk, such as anthropometrics, BMI (including weight change over adulthood), physical activity, 
1208 
sedentary behavior, and reproductive history, including ages of menarche, age of menopause, 
1209 
parity, and breast feeding.  
1210 
 
1211 
Dietary Patterns and Lung Cancer 
1212 
This systematic review included three prospective cohort studies and one nested case-cohort 
1213 
study published since 2000 that examined the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of 
1214 
lung cancer.101, 104, 132, 133 The studies used different methods to assess dietary patterns. Two 
1215 
studies used an index/score to measure adherence to a dietary pattern, one study derived dietary 
1216 
patterns using principal components analysis, and another based dietary patterns on participant 
1217 
reports of animal product intake. With only four relevant studies that used different approaches 
1218 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
33 
 
for assessing or determining dietary patterns, the evidence available to examine the relationship 
1219 
between dietary patterns and risk of lung cancer is limited. 
1220 
 
1221 
Dietary Patterns and Prostate Cancer 
1222 
This systematic review included seven prospective cohort studies (from six different cohorts) 
1223 
published since 2000 that examined the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of prostate 
1224 
cancer.101, 134-139 The studies used different methods to assess dietary patterns. Three studies used 
1225 
index/scores to assess dietary patterns, two studies used factor analysis, one study used principle 
1226 
components analysis, and one made comparisons on the basis of animal product consumption. 
1227 
 
1228 
Most of the seven studies included in this systematic review did not detect clear or consistent 
1229 
relationships between dietary patterns and risk of prostate cancer, though one found that 
1230 
adherence to the Dietary Guidelines (assessed using the HEI-2005 and AHEI-2010) was 
1231 
associated with a lower risk of prostate cancer, particularly among men who had a prostate-
1232 
specific antigen screening in the past 3 years. Because these studies used a range of different 
1233 
approaches for assessing dietary patterns in populations with variable cancer screening patterns, 
1234 
had heterogeneous prostate cancer outcome ascertainment, and were typically limited to dietary 
1235 
exposure late in life, the results were inconclusive regarding risk for clinically significant 
1236 
prostate cancer. 
1237 
 
1238 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:  http://NEL.gov/topic.cfm?cat=3344 
1239 
 
1240 
DIETARY PATTERNS AND CONGENITAL ANOMALIES 
1241 
Existing Evidence around Foods and Nutrients and Congenital Anomalies  
1242 
It is well established that adequate folate status is critical for the prevention of neural tube 
1243 
defects, specifically anencephaly and spina bifida, as well as other birth defects.140  Folate is 
1244 
often described by its source, with “folate” referring to naturally occurring folate from food 
1245 
sources, and “folic acid” referring to the synthetic form used in dietary supplements and food 
1246 
fortification. After mandatory fortification of enriched cereal products with folic acid in 1998, 
1247 
serum folate concentrations in the U.S. population more than doubled, and rates of neural tube 
1248 
defects decreased by 20 to 30 percent.141, 142  
1249 
 
1250 
Despite this decrease, nearly one fifth of females ages 14 to 30 years do not meet the estimated 
1251 
average requirement for folate, the level deemed to be adequate for one half of healthy females 
1252 
in the age group.143 The current U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends that women 
1253 
capable of becoming pregnant should take 400 to 800 micrograms of folic acid daily from 
1254 
fortified food or supplements in addition to a healthy diet rich in food sources of folate and folic 
1255 
acid to reduce risk of neural tube and other birth defects.144 Women with a history of a pregnancy 
1256 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
34 
 
affected by a neural tube defect or who are at high risk of neural tube defects require 4 mg of 
1257 
synthetic folic acid supplements daily under the supervision of a physician.145  Given the 
1258 
emphasis on a healthy diet, the DGAC was interested in understanding which dietary patterns, if 
1259 
any, were associated with a decreased risk of congenital anomalies among women of 
1260 
reproductive age. 
1261 
 
1262 
Question 5: What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of 
1263 
congenital anomalies? 
1264 
Source of evidence: NEL systematic review  
1265 
 
1266 
Conclusion 
1267 
Limited evidence suggests that healthy maternal dietary patterns during the preconception period 
1268 
that are higher in vegetables, fruits, and grains, and lower in red and processed meats, and low in 
1269 
sweets were associated with lower risk of developing of neural tube defects, particularly among 
1270 
women who do not take folic acid supplements. Whereas some dietary patterns were associated 
1271 
with lower risk of developing anencephaly, others were associated with lower risk of developing 
1272 
spina bifida.  
1273 
   
1274 
Evidence is insufficient to determine an association between maternal dietary patterns and 
1275 
congenital heart defects or cleft lip/palate.  
1276 
   
1277 
All studies were consistent in demonstrating that folic acid supplementation periconceptionally 
1278 
was associated with a decreased risk of having a child with a birth defect (e.g. neural tube 
1279 
defects, congenital heart defects, and cleft lip/palate). DGAC Grade: Neural Tube Defects – 
1280 
Limited; Congenital Heart Defects – Grade not assignable; Cleft Lip/Palate – Grade not 
1281 
assignable 
1282 
 
1283 
Implications 
1284 
Women of reproductive age should consume folic acid in the form of a supplement or through 
1285 
fortified foods in the range recommended by the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (400 to 
1286 
800 micrograms) in addition to consuming a diet rich in vegetables, fruits, and grains; lower in 
1287 
red and processed meats; and low in sweets. 
1288 
 
1289 
Review of the Evidence  
1290 
This series of systematic reviews included five case-control studies (using data from three 
1291 
cohorts) published since 1980 that examined the relationship between maternal dietary patterns 
1292 
and congenital anomalies in infants.146-150 Three articles examined neural tube defects,146, 147, 149 
1293 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
35 
 
two articles examined congenital heart defects,147, 150  and two articles examined orofacial 
1294 
clefts.146, 148 
1295 
 
1296 
Although all five case-control studies reported significant associations between dietary patterns 
1297 
and risk of congenital anomalies in women not taking folic acid supplementation, the variability 
1298 
of dietary patterns methodology used and composition of dietary patterns identified made it 
1299 
difficult to draw conclusions. All studies were consistent in finding that folate delivered 
1300 
periconceptionally in food or as a supplement as a key nutrient was associated with lower risk of 
1301 
developing congenital anomalies. It should be noted that some of the included studies were 
1302 
conducted in countries with mandatory folate fortification, while others were from countries that 
1303 
prohibit such fortification. 
1304 
 
1305 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:  http://NEL.gov/topic.cfm?cat=3356  
1306 
 
1307 
DIETARY PATTERNS AND NEUROLOGICAL AND PSYCHOLOGICAL 
1308 
ILLNESSES 
1309 
Existing Evidence around Foods and Nutrients and Neurological and 
1310 
Psychological Illnesses  
1311 
Neuropsychological development and function is increasingly recognized as a high national 
1312 
priority for health promotion and chronic disease prevention. Two major components of 
1313 
neuropsychological function are cognition, the ability to reason, and mood, balanced and 
1314 
appropriate to enable optimal cognition.  
1315 
 
1316 
Nutrition for optimal neurodevelopment in very young children has long been a subject of 
1317 
research. The 2010 DGAC concluded that moderate evidence supported a positive relationship 
1318 
between maternal dietary intakes of n-3 from seafood and improved cognitive ability in 
1319 
infants.151 The rising numbers of U.S. older adults and the potential human and financial cost of 
1320 
age-related cognitive impairments, such as Alzheimer’s disease and other dementias, also have 
1321 
helped drive national interest in chronic mental disease.152, 153 Separately, depression affected 8 
1322 
percent of Americans for at least two weeks annually from 2007-2010, and of these, 80 percent 
1323 
report functional impairment.154 Many preclinical and human studies have established 
1324 
relationships between traditional nutrients (e.g., omega-3 fatty acids) and central nervous system 
1325 
composition and function. Studies appearing in the last few years reflect the increasing research 
1326 
interest in the links between diet and neurological health.  
1327 
 
1328 
The hypothesis that nutrition can reduce and/or play a role in the treatment of these mental 
1329 
diseases and their related burdens has been studied in relation to several nutrients and foods, 
1330 
including the B vitamins, vitamin E, and selenium.155, 156  The omega-3 fatty acids 
1331 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
36 
 
eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) are among the most studied 
1332 
nutrients for neural health, in part because DHA is a major component of the brain, specifically 
1333 
gray matter and its synapses, and the specialized light detecting cells of the retina. DHA, in 
1334 
particular, supports the amplitude and signaling speed of neural response. EPA has emerged as a 
1335 
nutrient with antidepressive properties and continued studies to define its role in prevention and 
1336 
therapy are underway. Sufficiently strong medical evidence has been obtained for EPA and DHA 
1337 
such that supplements are now considered as complementary therapy for major depressive 
1338 
disorder by the American Psychiatric Association157 and more recent data from a meta-analysis 
1339 
has found them effective.158 Before 2010, the number of published dietary pattern studies was 
1340 
small. However, a more substantial literature on dietary patterns and neuropsychological health 
1341 
has been published since 2010. The DGAC was therefore able to consider prevention of adult 
1342 
neuropsychological ill health for the first time.  
1343 
 
1344 
Question 6: What is the relationship between dietary patterns and risk of 
1345 
neurological and psychological illnesses? 
1346 
Source of evidence: NEL systematic review  
1347 
 
1348 
Conclusion 
1349 
Limited evidence suggests that a dietary pattern containing an array of vegetables, fruits, nuts, 
1350 
legumes and seafood consumed during adulthood is associated with lower risk of age-related 
1351 
cognitive impairment, dementia, and/or Alzheimer’s disease. Although the number of studies 
1352 
available on dietary patterns and neurodegenerative disease risk is expanding, this body of 
1353 
evidence, which is made up of high-quality observational studies, has appeared only in recent 
1354 
years and is rapidly developing. It employs a wide range of methodology in study design, 
1355 
definition and measurement ascertainment of cognitive outcomes, and dietary pattern 
1356 
assessment. DGAC Grade: Limited 
1357 
 
1358 
Limited evidence suggests that dietary patterns emphasizing seafood, vegetables, fruits, nuts, and 
1359 
legumes are associated with lower risk of depression in men and non-perinatal women. However, 
1360 
the body of evidence is primarily composed of observational studies and employs a range of 
1361 
methodology in study design, definition, and measurement of dietary patterns and ascertainment 
1362 
of depression/depressive signs and symptoms. Studies on dietary patterns in other populations, 
1363 
such as women in the post-partum period, children and adolescents, as well as those in various 
1364 
ethnic and cultural groups, are too limited to draw conclusions. DGAC Grade: Adults – 
1365 
Limited; Children, adolescents, and women in the post-partum period – Grade not 
1366 
assignable  
1367 
 
1368 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
37 
 
Implications 
1369 
Dietary patterns emphasizing vegetables, fruits, seafood, legumes and nuts similar to those that 
1370 
achieve chronic disease risk reduction are consistent with maintaining neurocognitive health, 
1371 
including cognitive ability in healthy aging, and balanced mood. 
1372 
 
1373 
Review of the Evidence  
1374 
Dietary Patterns and Cognitive Impairment, Dementia, and Alzheimer’s Disease 
1375 
This systematic review includes 30 articles (two articles analyzed data taken from RCTs and 28 
1376 
articles used data from prospective cohort studies) published since 1980 (with all but two 
1377 
published since 2008) that examined the relationship between dietary patterns and age-related 
1378 
cognitive impairment, dementia, and/or Alzheimer’s disease.159-188 Twenty of the articles 
1379 
included in this review assessed the relationship between dietary patterns and cognitive 
1380 
impairment, 10 articles examined cognitive impairment or dementia, and eight articles looked at 
1381 
Alzheimer’s disease. 
1382 
 
1383 
The articles used several different methods to assess dietary patterns. Two articles analyzed data 
1384 
from RCTs that tested or described dietary patterns, 23 articles used indices/scores to assess 
1385 
dietary patterns quality or adherence, three articles used data-driven methods, and three used 
1386 
reduced rank regression. Most (18 of 28) articles found an association between dietary patterns 
1387 
and age-related cognitive impairment, dementia, and/or Alzheimer’s disease. Despite some 
1388 
heterogeneity in this body of evidence, some common elements of dietary patterns were 
1389 
associated with measures of cognitive impairment, dementia, and/or Alzheimer’s disease: 
1390 
 
1391 
 Patterns higher in vegetables, fruits, nuts, legumes, and seafood were generally associated 
1392 
with reduced risk of age-related cognitive impairment, dementia, and/or Alzheimer’s 
1393 
disease.  
1394 
 Patterns higher in red and/or processed meats were generally associated with greater age-
1395 
related cognitive impairment. Relatively few studies reported on refined sugar and added 
1396 
salt, and patterns including these nutrients tended to report greater cognitive impairment. 
1397 
Although some studies included participants from a range of race/ethnic and socioeconomic 
1398 
groups, the results are most applicable to the general healthy aging population. In addition, 
1399 
dietary patterns were derived using dietary intake measured at baseline only, and therefore, may 
1400 
not reflect patterns consumed throughout relevant periods of life before enrollment in the study, 
1401 
or changes in intake that may have occurred over the duration of the study. Similarly, several 
1402 
studies measured cognitive function only at a single time point (follow-up), and therefore, could 
1403 
not assess change in cognitive function over time. Finally, though these studies controlled for a 
1404 
number of confounders, not all apparently relevant potential confounders were adjusted for (e.g., 
1405 
existing or family history of cognitive decline, dementia, or Alzheimer’s disease; baseline health 
1406 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
38 
 
status; changes in dietary intake over time) and, as with all association studies, residual 
1407 
confounding is possible. 
1408 
   
1409 
Dietary Patterns and Depression 
1410 
This systematic review includes nineteen articles (17 from prospective cohort studies, and 2 
1411 
using data from RCTs) published since 1980 (all of which were published since 2008) that 
1412 
assessed the relationship between dietary patterns and depression.175, 182, 189-205  
1413 
 
1414 
The articles used several different methods to assess dietary patterns. Two studies tested the 
1415 
effects of dietary patterns as part of an RCT, six articles used indices/scores to assess dietary 
1416 
patterns, 10 articles used data-driven methods, and one used reduced rank regression. Despite 
1417 
methodological and outcome heterogeneity in this body of evidence, some protective dietary 
1418 
patterns emerged: 
1419 
 
1420 
 Patterns emphasizing seafood, vegetables, fruits, and nuts, were generally associated with 
1421 
reduced risk of depression. 
1422 
 Patterns emphasizing red and processed meats and refined sugar were generally 
1423 
associated with increased risk of depression. 
1424 
This body of evidence did have several limitations. There was considerable variability in how the 
1425 
outcome of depression was assessed, with some studies using various depression scales, some 
1426 
using physician diagnosis/hospital discharge records, and others using proxies such as use of 
1427 
depression medication. Although most studies make extensive efforts to include participants 
1428 
across a wide range of race/ethnic groups and across the socio-economic continuum, there still 
1429 
may be some subgroups for which the association between dietary patterns and depression risk 
1430 
cannot be reliably assessed and therefore conclusions cannot be drawn for them. Research is 
1431 
needed to determine whether dietary patterns are associated with risk of depression in 
1432 
particularly vulnerable subgroups, specifically children, adolescents, young adults, and women 
1433 
during the post-partum period. Additional limitations within this body of evidence make it 
1434 
difficult to draw stronger conclusions, including assessment of dietary patterns and depression 
1435 
outcomes at a single point in time, potential for residual confounding despite adjustment for a 
1436 
number of factors, and few studies conducted in U.S.-based populations. 
1437 
 
1438 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:  http://NEL.gov/topic.cfm?cat=3352  
1439 
 
1440 
 
1441 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
39 
 
DIETARY PATTERNS AND BONE HEALTH 
1442 
Existing Evidence around Foods and Nutrients and Bone Health  
1443 
Low bone mineral density and osteoporosis are common in the United States, particularly in 
1444 
older adults, and its contribution to disability and cost to the health care system continues to rise 
1445 
in parallel to longer life expectancy. As described in Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient 
1446 
Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends, more than half of women ages 60 to 69 years 
1447 
have low bone mass and approximately 12 percent meet established criteria for osteoporosis. The 
1448 
prevalence of osteoporosis increases with age; about one-quarter of women ages 70 to 79 years 
1449 
and about one-third of women older than age 80 years have osteoporosis. Low bone mass is less 
1450 
common in older men but is increasingly recognized. Among U.S. men ages 60 to 69 years, 
1451 
about a third have low bone mass and this increases to about 40 percent and slightly more than 
1452 
50 percent for men ages 70 to 79 years and 80 years and older, respectively. 
1453 
 
1454 
Poor bone health and osteoporotic fractures are a major cause of morbidity and mortality in the 
1455 
elderly and account for significant health care costs. Understanding the extent to which dietary 
1456 
factors can help improve bone health and reduce the incidence of fractures across all segments of 
1457 
the population, particularly in the elderly, is important for the health and well-being of the 
1458 
nation. 
1459 
 
1460 
The most critical nutrients for healthy bone are calcium, vitamin D, and phosphorous. As part of 
1461 
their 2011 report on Calcium and Vitamin D, the Institute of Medicine extensively reviewed the 
1462 
available data and updated the Dietary Reference Intakes (DRIs) for calcium and vitamin D for 
1463 
men and women across life stages.206  The new reference values were based upon a strong body 
1464 
of evidence regarding bone growth and maintenance. At the time of the report, these bone health 
1465 
outcomes (in particular bone mass [bone mineral content]) were the only indicators on which 
1466 
there was sufficient scientific evidence to define DRIs; a thorough review of other outcomes 
1467 
(bone mineral density, risk of fractures, and osteoporosis) provided mixed and inconclusive 
1468 
results, and thus did not inform the DRIs. Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and 
1469 
Health: Current Status and Trends of this DGAC report concluded that calcium and vitamin D 
1470 
were shortfall nutrients of public health concern. The estimated low levels of intake in various 
1471 
age and sex groups place many at risk for suboptimal bone health. The DGAC asked additional 
1472 
questions regarding bone health that went beyond those relating to the role of specific and well-
1473 
known nutrients on bone remodeling. Specifically, the DGAC considered the influence of dietary 
1474 
patterns and their relationship to bone health and specific bone health outcomes across the 
1475 
lifespan, including bone density and fractures. This approach enabled the DGAC to consider the 
1476 
relationship between the total diet and its component foods and nutrients, acting in combination, 
1477 
on bone health outcomes. This section reviews this evidence and forms the basis for the DGAC 
1478 
recommendation for action at individual and population level as well as its research 
1479 
recommendations.  
1480 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
40 
 
 
1481 
Question 7: What is the relationship between dietary patterns and bone health? 
1482 
Source of evidence: NEL systematic review  
1483 
 
1484 
Conclusion 
1485 
Limited evidence suggests that a dietary pattern higher in vegetables, fruits, grains, nuts, and 
1486 
dairy products, and lower in meats and saturated fat, is associated with more favorable bone 
1487 
health outcomes in adults, including decreased risk of fracture and osteoporosis, as well as 
1488 
improved bone mineral density. Although a growing number of studies are examining the 
1489 
relationship between dietary patterns and bone health in adults, the number of high-quality 
1490 
studies is modest and those available employ a wide range of methodologies in study design, 
1491 
dietary assessment techniques, and varying bone health outcomes.  
1492 
 
1493 
Definitive conclusions regarding the relationship between dietary patterns and bone health 
1494 
outcomes (bone mineral density and bone mineral content) in children and adolescents cannot be 
1495 
drawn due to the limited evidence from a small number of studies with wide variation in study 
1496 
design, dietary assessment methodology, and bone health outcomes. DGAC Grade: Adults – 
1497 
Limited; Children and Adolescents - Grade not assignable 
1498 
 
1499 
Implications 
1500 
Only limited evidence is available on the relationships between dietary patterns and bone health 
1501 
outcomes in adults and other age groups. Although there is strong evidence on the roles of 
1502 
vitamin D and calcium in bone health across the age spectrum, further research is needed on 
1503 
dietary patterns that are most beneficial.  
1504 
 
1505 
Review of the Evidence 
1506 
This systematic review included two articles that used data from RCTs and 11 articles from 
1507 
prospective cohort studies published since 2000 that examined the relationship between dietary 
1508 
patterns and bone health.207-219 
1509 
 
1510 
The articles employ diverse methodologies to assess dietary patterns. Four articles used an index 
1511 
or score, six articles used factor analysis/principal components analysis, two articles used 
1512 
reduced rank regression, and two articles tested dietary patterns in an intervention study where 
1513 
bone health or fractures were either secondary or tertiary trial outcomes. Seven studies assessed 
1514 
risk of fracture, six studies assessed bone mineral density, bone mineral content, or bone mass, 
1515 
and one study examined risk of osteoporosis. The dietary patterns examined in this systematic 
1516 
review were defined in various ways, making comparisons between articles difficult. However, 
1517 
despite heterogeneity in this body of evidence, some common characteristics of dietary patterns 
1518 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
41 
 
associated with better or adverse bone health outcomes emerged, particularly in articles where 
1519 
patterns were defined by index or score. Articles using data-driven methods were less consistent. 
1520 
The following overall conclusions can be drawn:   
1521 
 
1522 
 Patterns emphasizing vegetables, fruits, legumes, nuts, dairy, and 
1523 
cereals/grains/pasta/rice, and unsaturated fats were generally associated with more 
1524 
favorable bone health outcomes. 
1525 
 Patterns higher in meats and saturated fats were generally associated with increased risk 
1526 
of adverse bone health outcomes. 
1527 
 Results were far less consistent for added sugars, alcohol, and sodium in relation to bone 
1528 
health.  
1529 
Although many cohort studies make extensive efforts to include participants across a wide range 
1530 
of race/ethnic groups and across the socio-economic continuum, there still may be some groups 
1531 
for which the association between dietary patterns and bone health cannot yet be determined 
1532 
(i.e., children, adolescents). 
1533 
 
1534 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit:  http://NEL.gov/topic.cfm?cat=3360  
1535 
 
1536 
 
1537 
CHAPTER SUMMARY 
1538 
The dietary patterns approach captures the relationship between the overall diet and its 
1539 
constituent foods, beverages, and nutrients in relationship to outcomes of interest. Numerous 
1540 
dietary patterns were identified, with the most common ones defined using indices or scores such 
1541 
as the HEI-2010, the AHEI-2010, or various Mediterranean-style dietary patterns, the DASH 
1542 
pattern, vegetarian patterns, and data-driven approaches.  
1543 
 
1544 
The Committee’s examination of the association between dietary patterns and various health 
1545 
outcomes revealed remarkable consistency in the findings and implications that are noteworthy. 
1546 
When looking at the dietary pattern conclusion statements across the various health outcomes, 
1547 
certain characteristics of the diet were consistently identified (see Table D2.3). Common 
1548 
characteristics of dietary patterns associated with positive health outcomes include higher intake 
1549 
of vegetables, fruits, whole grains, low- or non-fat dairy, seafood, legumes, and nuts; moderate 
1550 
intake of alcohol (among adults); lower consumption of red and processed meat, and low intake 
1551 
of sugar-sweetened foods and drinks, and refined grains. Vegetables and fruits are the only 
1552 
characteristics of the diet that were consistently identified in every conclusion statement across 
1553 
the health outcomes. Whole grains were identified slightly less consistently compared to 
1554 
vegetables and fruits, but were identified in every conclusion with moderate to strong evidence. 
1555 
For studies with limited evidence, grains were not as consistently defined and/or they were not 
1556 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
42 
 
identified as a key characteristic. Low- or non-fat dairy, seafood, legumes, nuts, and alcohol 
1557 
were identified as beneficial characteristics of the diet for some, but not all, outcomes. For 
1558 
conclusions with moderate to strong evidence, higher intake of red and processed meats was 
1559 
identified as detrimental compared to lower intake. Higher consumption of sugar-sweetened 
1560 
foods and beverages as well as refined grains were identified as detrimental in almost all 
1561 
conclusion statements with moderate to strong evidence.  
1562 
 
1563 
 
 
1564 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
43

red/processed meats, French fries/potatoes, and sources of sugars (i.e., sodas, sweets, and dessert foods) are associated with a greater risk.)

a The DGAC Grade presented represents the grade the Committee provided for the conclusion statement with the dietary pattern components described. Some health outcomes had 
1569 
more than one graded conclusion. Only the conclusion statements that describe dietary pattern components are presented here. Post = Post-menopausal; Pre = Pre-menopausal 
1570 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
44 
 
As alcohol is a unique aspect of the diet, the DGAC considered evidence from several sources to 
1571 
inform recommendations. As noted above, moderate alcohol intake among adults was identified 
1572 
as a component of a healthy dietary pattern associated with some health outcomes, which 
1573 
reaffirms conclusions related to moderate alcohol consumption by the 2010 DGAC. The 
1574 
Committee also concurs with the conclusions reached by the 2010 DGAC on the relationship 
1575 
between alcohol intake and unintentional injury and lactation.1 However, as noted in Table D2.1, 
1576 
evidence also suggests that alcoholic drinks are associated with increased risk for certain cancers, 
1577 
including pre- and post-menopausal breast cancer. After consideration of this collective 
1578 
evidence, the Committee concurs with the 2010 DGAC that if alcohol is consumed, it should be 
1579 
consumed in moderation, and only by adults. However, it is not recommended that anyone begin 
1580 
drinking or drink more frequently on the basis of potential health benefits because moderate 
1581 
alcohol intake also is associated with increased risk of violence, drowning, and injuries from falls 
1582 
and motor vehicle crashes. Women should be aware of a moderately increased risk of breast 
1583 
cancer even with moderate alcohol intake. There are many circumstances in which people should 
1584 
not drink alcohol:  
1585 
 Individuals who cannot restrict their drinking to moderate levels.  
1586 
 Anyone younger than the legal drinking age.  
1587 
 Women who are pregnant or who may be pregnant.  
1588 
 Individuals taking prescription or over-the-counter medications that can interact with 
1589 
alcohol.  
1590 
 Individuals with certain specific medical conditions (e.g., liver disease, 
1591 
hypertriglyceridemia, pancreatitis).  
1592 
 Individuals who plan to drive, operate machinery, or take part in other activities that 
1593 
require attention, skill, or coordination or in situations where impaired judgment could 
1594 
cause injury or death (e.g., swimming). 
1595 
Finally, because of the substantial evidence clearly demonstrating the health benefits of 
1596 
breastfeeding, occasionally consuming an alcoholic drink does not warrant stopping 
1597 
breastfeeding. However, women who are breastfeeding should be very cautious about drinking 
1598 
alcohol, if they choose to drink at all.§§  
1599 
 
1600 
The common characteristics of a healthy dietary pattern found in the conclusion statements 
1601 
across the outcomes examined implies that following a dietary pattern associated with reduced 
1602 
risk of CVD, overweight, and obesity will have positive health benefits beyond these categories 
1603 
                                                 
§§ If the infant’s breastfeeding behavior is well established, consistent, and predictable (no earlier than at 3 months 
of age), a mother may consume a single alcoholic drink if she then waits at least 4 hours before breastfeeding. 
Alternatively, she may express breast milk before consuming the drink and feed the expressed milk to her infant 
later.  
 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
45 
 
of health outcomes. Thus, the U.S. population should be encouraged and guided to consume 
1604 
dietary patterns that are rich in vegetables, fruits, whole grains, seafood, legumes, and nuts; 
1605 
moderate in low- and non-fat dairy products and alcohol (among adults); lower in red and 
1606 
processed meat; and low in sugar-sweetened foods and beverages and refined grains. These 
1607 
dietary patterns can be achieved in many ways and should be tailored to the individual’s 
1608 
biological and medical needs as well as socio-cultural preferences. As described in the DGAC’s 
1609 
conceptual model, a multi-level process at individual and population levels is required to help 
1610 
achieve a healthy diet and other lifestyle behaviors so as to achieve chronic disease risk 
1611 
reduction and overall well-being. The Committee recommends the development and 
1612 
implementation of programs and services that facilitate the improvement in eating behaviors 
1613 
consistent with healthy dietary patterns in various settings, including preventive services in our 
1614 
healthcare and public health systems as well as those that reach populations in other settings of 
1615 
influence such as preschool and school settings and workplaces.  
1616 
 
1617 
The dietary pattern characteristics being recommended by the 2015 DGAC reaffirms the dietary 
1618 
pattern characteristics recommended by the 2010 DGAC, despite the fact that different 
1619 
approaches were employed. Additionally, this dietary pattern aligns with recommendations from 
1620 
other groups, including AICR and AHA/ACC. The majority of evidence considered focuses on 
1621 
dietary patterns consumed in adulthood on health risks, primarily risks of chronic disease 
1622 
development and, in the case of pregnancy, birth defects. Very little evidence considered here 
1623 
was directed to dietary patterns in children, and risk reduction studies evaluating children’s diets 
1624 
and risk of overweight and obesity provided limited evidence. No conclusions on chronic disease 
1625 
apply directly to evidence developed in children. Recommendations based on adult studies have 
1626 
implications for children based on general nutritional principles but caution is warranted, 
1627 
considering the fact that children with developing bodies and neurocognitive capabilities present 
1628 
unique nutritional issues. 
1629 
 
1630 
 
1631 
NEEDS FOR FUTURE RESEARCH 
1632 
1. Conduct additional dietary patterns research for other health outcomes to strengthen the 
1633 
evidence beyond CVD and body weight in populations of various ethnic backgrounds and 
1634 
life course stages in order for future DGACs to draw stronger conclusions. 
1635 
 
1636 
Rationale: The NEL systematic reviews demonstrated that considerable CVD research 
1637 
related to dietary patterns is available. However, it also is important to note, that unlike CVD, 
1638 
some of the other health outcomes are more heterogeneous and thus may require greater 
1639 
specificity in the examination of diet and disease risk. There is a clear need for all studies 
1640 
examining the relationship between dietary patterns and health outcomes to include the full 
1641 
age spectrum and to take a life course perspective (including pregnancy); insufficient 
1642 
research is being devoted to children and how diseases may evolve over time. An increased 
1643 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
46 
 
emphasis should be placed on understanding how the diets of all those in the U.S. population 
1644 
from various ethnic backgrounds may be associated with health outcomes, thereby 
1645 
broadening knowledge beyond Hispanics and African Americans to include the diversity that 
1646 
exists in the United States today. This may require our national nutrition monitoring 
1647 
programs to over-sample individuals from other national origins to conduct subgroup 
1648 
analysis.  
1649 
 
1650 
2.  Improve the understanding of how to more precisely characterize dietary patterns by their 
1651 
food constituents and the implications of the food constituents on nutrient adequacy through 
1652 
the use of Food Pattern Modeling. More precise characterization, particularly of protein 
1653 
foods, is needed. 
1654 
 
1655 
Rationale:  Researchers are characterizing dietary patterns very differently and yet 
1656 
sometimes use similar nomenclatures. This makes it difficult to compare results across 
1657 
studies and as demonstrated in the NEL systematic reviews, can impair the grading of the 
1658 
body of evidence as strong. The reason why researchers are not replicating others findings in 
1659 
different populations may be a function of publication bias. It is important for editors of 
1660 
scientific journals and peer reviewers to appreciate the replication of findings first and then 
1661 
value a research group’s methodological nuance that may improve the examination of the 
1662 
association between dietary patterns and health outcomes. Perhaps what should be stressed is 
1663 
a harmonization of research methods across various cohorts or randomized trials, similar to 
1664 
what is being done at the National Cancer Institute’s Dietary Patterns Methods Project9, 220 
1665 
led by Drs. Krebs-Smith and Reedy. The use of Food Pattern Modeling as demonstrated in 
1666 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends 
1667 
allows questions about the adequacy of the dietary patterns given specific food constituents 
1668 
to be addressed and how modifications of the patterns by altering the foods for specific 
1669 
population groups or to meet specific nutrient targets can be achieved. 
1670 
 
1671 
3.  Examine the long-term cardio-metabolic effects of the various dietary patterns identified in 
1672 
the AHA/ACC/TOS Guidelines for the Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults that 
1673 
are capable of resulting in short-term weight loss (see Question 2, above).  
1674 
 
1675 
Rationale: Although the research to date demonstrates that to lose weight, a variety of 
1676 
dietary pattern approaches can be used if a reduction in caloric intake is achieved, the long-
1677 
term effects of these diets on cardio-metabolic health are not well known. Emerging research 
1678 
is exploring health effects of variations of the low-carbohydrate, higher protein/fat dietary 
1679 
pattern. In some approaches (such as Atkins), the dietary pattern which emphasizes animal 
1680 
products, may achieve a macronutrient composition that is higher in saturated fat. Others 
1681 
may emphasize plant-based proteins and fats and may achieve a lower saturated fat content 
1682 
and may be higher in polyunsaturated fats and dietary fiber. Research is needed to determine 
1683 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
47 
 
the impact of these alternative approaches, and perhaps others, on CVD risk profiles as well 
1684 
as other health outcomes. As mentioned in the review of the literature associated with 
1685 
saturated fat and cardiovascular disease in Part D. Chapter 6: Cross-Cutting Topics of 
1686 
Public Health Importance, substituting one macronutrient for another may result in 
1687 
unintended consequences. Careful consideration to the types of foods that are used in these 
1688 
diets and in particular the type of fat and amount of added sugars should be taken into 
1689 
account.  
1690 
 
1691

Table D2.1. AHA/ACC/TOS Guideline for the Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults, 2013 
2514 
Critical Question 4a. Among overweight and obese adults, what is the efficacy/effectiveness of a comprehensive lifestyle intervention program (i.e., comprised of 
diet, physical activity, and behavior therapy) in facilitating weight loss or maintenance of lost weight? 
Critical Question 4b. What characteristics of delivering comprehensive lifestyle interventions (e.g., frequency and duration of treatment, individual versus group 
sessions, onsite versus telephone/email contact) are associated with greater weight loss or weight loss maintenance? 
Intervention/Question   
Included 
Studies 
Evidence Statement (Strength of Evidence)   
3.4.1. Description of the Diet, Physical 
Activity, and Behavior Therapy 
Components in High-Intensity, Onsite 
Lifestyle  Interventions 
12 RCTs 
ES1. The principal components of an effective high-intensity, on-site comprehensive-lifestyle intervention 
include: 1) prescription of a moderately-reduced calorie diet; 2) a program of increased physical activity; 
and 3) the use of behavioral strategies to facilitate adherence to diet and activity recommendations. (High) 
3.4.2. Comprehensive Interventions 
Compared with Usual Care, Minimal Care, 
or No-Treatment Control   
15 RCTs 
ES 2a (Short-Term Weight Loss). In overweight and obese individuals in whom weight loss is indicated 
and who wish to lose weight, comprehensive lifestyle interventions consisting of diet, physical activity, 
and behavior therapy (all 3 components) produce average weight losses of up to 8 kg in 6 months of 
frequent (i.e., initially weekly), onsite treatment provided by a trained interventionist* in group or 
individual sessions. Such losses (which can approximate reductions of 5% to 10% of initial weight) are 
greater than those produced by usual care (i.e., characterized by the limited provision of advice or 
educational materials). Comparable 6-month weight losses have been observed in treatment comparison 
studies of comprehensive lifestyle interventions, which did not include a usual care group. (High) 
ES 2b (Intermediate-Term Weight Loss). Longer-term comprehensive lifestyle interventions, which 
additionally provide weekly to monthly on-site treatment for another 6 months, produce average weight 
losses of up to 8 kg at 1 year, losses which are greater than those resulting from usual care. Comparable 1-
year weight losses have been observed in treatment comparison studies of comprehensive lifestyle 
interventions, which did not include a usual care group. (Moderate) 
ES 2c (Long-Term Weight Loss). Comprehensive lifestyle interventions which, after the first year, 
continue to provide bimonthly or more frequent intervention contacts, are associated with gradual weight 
regain of 1 to 2 kg/year (on average), from the weight loss achieved at 6 to 12 months. Long-term (>1 
year) weight losses, however, remain larger than those associated with usual care. Comparable findings 
have been observed in treatment comparison studies of comprehensive lifestyle interventions, which did 
not include a usual care group. (High) 
3.4.3. Efficacy/Effectiveness of 
Electronically Delivered, Comprehensive 
Interventions in Achieving Weight Loss 
Evidence Statement 
13 RCTs 
ES 3. Electronically delivered, comprehensive weight loss interventions developed in academic settings, 
which include frequent self-monitoring of weight, food intake, and physical activity—as well as 
personalized feedback from a trained interventionist*—can produce weight loss of up to 5 kg at 6 to 12 
months, a loss which is greater than that resulting from no or minimal intervention (i.e., primarily 
knowledge based) offered on the internet or in print. (Moderate) 
3.4.4. Efficacy/Effectiveness of 
Comprehensive, Telephone-Delivered 
Lifestyle Interventions in Achieving 
Weight Loss 
3 RCTs 
ES 4. In comprehensive lifestyle interventions that are delivered by telephone or face-to-face counseling, 
and which also include the use of either commercially-prepared prepackaged meals or an interactive web 
based program, the telephone delivered and face-to-face delivered interventions produced similar mean net 
weight losses of approximately 5 kg at 6 months and 24 months, compared with a usual care control group. 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
67 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(Low) 
3.4.5. Efficacy/Effectiveness of 
Comprehensive Weight Loss Programs in 
Patients Within a Primary Care Practice 
Setting Compared With Usual Care 
4 RCTs 
ES 5. In studies to date, low to moderate-intensity lifestyle interventions for weight loss provided to 
overweight or obese adults by primary care practices alone, have not been shown to be effective. (Low) 
3.4.6. Efficacy/Effectiveness of 
Commercial-Based, Comprehensive 
Lifestyle Interventions in Achieving 
Weight Loss 
4 RCTs 
ES 6. Commercial-based, comprehensive weight loss interventions that are delivered in person have been 
shown to induce an average weight loss of 4.8 kg to 6.6 kg at 6 months in 2 trials when conventional foods 
are consumed and 6.6 kg to 10.1 kg at 12 months in 2 trials with provision of prepared food, losses that are 
greater than those produced by minimal-treatment control interventions. (Low) 
3.4.7. Efficacy/Effectiveness of Very Low-
Calorie Diets, as Used as Part of a 
Comprehensive Lifestyle Intervention, in 
Achieving Weight Loss 
4 RCTs 
ES 7a. Comprehensive, high intensity on-site lifestyle interventions that include a medically supervised 
very low-calorie diet (often defined as <800 kcal/day), as provided by complete meal replacement 
products, produce total weight loss of approximately 14.2 kg to 21 kg over 11 to 14 weeks, which is larger 
than that produced by no intervention or a usual care control group (i.e., advice and education only). (High) 
ES 7b. Following the cessation of a high intensity lifestyle intervention with a medically supervised very-
low calorie diet of 11 to 14 weeks, weight regain of 3.1 kg to 3.7 kg has been observed during the ensuing 
21 to 38 weeks of non-intervention follow-up. (High) 
ES 7c. The prescription of various types (resistance or aerobic training) and doses of moderate intensity 
exercise training (e.g., brisk walking 135 to 250 minutes/week), delivered in conjunction with weight loss 
maintenance therapy does not reduce the amount of weight regained after the cessation of the very-low 
calorie diet, as compared with weight loss maintenance therapy alone. (Low) 
3.4.8. Efficacy/Effectiveness of 
Comprehensive Lifestyle Interventions in 
Maintaining Lost Weight 
14 RCTs 
ES 8a. After initial weight loss, some weight regain can be expected, on average, with greater regain 
observed over longer periods of time. Continued provision of a comprehensive weight loss maintenance 
program (onsite or by telephone), for periods of up to 2.5 years following initial weight loss, reduces 
weight regain, as compared to the provision of minimal intervention (e.g., usual care). The optimal 
duration of weight loss maintenance programs has not been determined. (Moderate) 
ES 8b. 35% to 60% of overweight/obese adults who participate in a high intensity long-term 
comprehensive lifestyle intervention maintain a loss of ≥5% of initial body weight at ≥2 year’s follow-up 
(post-randomization). (Moderate) 
3.4.9. Characteristics of Lifestyle 
Intervention Delivery That May Affect 
Weight Loss: Intervention 
10 RCTs 
ES 9a (Moderate-Intensity Interventions). Moderate intensity, on-site comprehensive lifestyle 
interventions, which provide an average of 1 to 2 treatment sessions per month typically produce mean 
weight losses of 2 kg to 4 kg in 6 to 12 months, losses which generally are greater than those produced by 
usual care (i.e., minimal intervention control group). (High) 
ES 9b (Low-intensity Interventions). Low intensity, on-site comprehensive lifestyle interventions, which 
provide fewer than monthly treatment sessions do not consistently produce weight loss when compared to 
usual care. (Moderate) 
ES 9c (Effect of intervention intensity). When weight loss with each intervention intensity (i.e., low, 
moderate, and high) is compared to usual care, high-intensity lifestyle interventions (≥14 sessions in 6 
months) typically produce greater net-of-control weight losses than low-to-moderate intensity 
interventions. (Moderate) 

 
Part D. Chapter 2: Dietary Patterns, Foods and Nutrients, and Health Outcomes 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
68 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.4.10. Characteristics of Lifestyle 
Intervention Delivery That May Affect 
Weight Loss or Weight 
15 RCTs 
ES 10. There do not appear to be substantial differences in the size of the weight losses produced by 
individual- and group-based sessions in high-intensity, comprehensive lifestyle intervention delivered on 
site by a trained interventionist*. (Low) 
3.4.11. Characteristics of Lifestyle 
Intervention Delivery That May Affect 
Weight Loss or Weight Loss Maintenance: 
Onsite Versus Electronically Delivered 
Interventions 
 
ES 11. Weight losses observed in comprehensive lifestyle interventions, which are delivered onsite by a 
trained interventionist* in initially weekly and then biweekly group or individual sessions, are generally 
greater than weight losses observed in comprehensive interventions that are delivered by Internet or email 
and which include feedback from a trained interventionist. (Low)

 Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and PA Behavior Change 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
1 
 
Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and Physical Activity 
1 
Behavior Change  
2 
INTRODUCTION 
3 
Individual behavior change lies at the inner core of the social-ecological model that forms the 
4 
basis of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines for American Advisory Committee (DGAC) conceptual 
5 
model (see Part B. Chapter 2: 2015 DGAC Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the 
6 
Evidence). For this reason, it is crucial to identify the behavioral strategies that individuals living 
7 
in the United States can follow to improve their healthy lifestyle behaviors as well as the key 
8 
contextual factors that facilitate the ability of individuals to consume healthy diets. 
9 
 
10 
In the past, American families seldom consumed food prepared outside their homes and, for the 
11 
most part, consumed their meals as a family unit. However, these behaviors have changed 
12 
dramatically in recent years. Today, 33 percent of calories are consumed outside the home and it 
13 
is becoming more common for individuals to eat alone and to bring meals prepared outside into 
14 
their homes (see Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status 
15 
and Trends). Eating away from home is associated with increased caloric intake and poorer 
16 
dietary quality compared to eating at home.1 As recognized by the 2010 DGAC these major 
17 
changes in eating behaviors can be expected to have a negative impact on the quality of the diets 
18 
consumed and the risk of obesity among the U.S. population.2 
19 
 
20 
Other individual lifestyle behaviors related to dietary intakes and obesity risk also have changed 
21 
in recent decades. The U.S. population has become increasingly sedentary,3 with daily hours of 
22 
screen time exposure becoming a serious public health concern due to its potential negative 
23 
influence on dietary and weight outcomes. For example, it has been hypothesized that TV 
24 
viewing time has a negative influence on dietary habits of individuals because of unhealthy 
25 
snacking while watching TV and through exposure to advertisements of unhealthy food 
26 
products.4 In turn, excess caloric intake coupled with sedentary time directly resulting from 
27 
excessive TV may increase the risk of obesity. Suboptimal sleep patterns associated with today’s 
28 
busy lives also have been identified as a potential risk factor for poor dietary behaviors and body 
29 
weight outcomes.5  
30 
 
31 
In response to these trends, interest has grown in the potential of behavioral strategies that 
32 
individuals can use to improve their dietary behaviors. Specifically, self-monitoring of diet, 
33 
physical activity, and body weight has been identified as a potential key component of successful 
34 
healthy lifestyle interventions.6 Diet self-monitoring may, in turn, be facilitated by the 
35 
availability and use of menus displaying calorie labels and the Nutrition Facts label on packaged 
36 
foods.  
37 

 
 Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and PA Behavior Change 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
2 
 
Recognizing the importance of these dietary and lifestyle behaviors to the health and well-being 
38 
of the U.S. population, the DGAC reviewed recent evidence to address questions on the 
39 
relationship between eating out, family shared meals, sedentary behavior, and diet and weight 
40 
outcomes. The DGAC also sought to examine associations between sleep patterns, dietary 
41 
intakes, and obesity risk. However, after conducting preliminary literature searches, the 
42 
Committee determined sleep patterns was an emerging area with an insufficient body of 
43 
evidence and did not include specific questions on this topic.  
44 
 
45 
The DGAC also focused on identifying evidence that could provide individuals with tools to 
46 
improve their dietary choices and body weight status. Specifically, the Committee reviewed 
47 
recent evidence on the impact of diet and weight self-monitoring, and on use of food and menu 
48 
labels on dietary intake and weight outcomes. The DGAC was interested in reviewing the 
49 
evidence on the use of mobile health (m-health) technologies to improve dietary and weight 
50 
outcomes, and after a preliminary review was conducted, determined that this, too, was an 
51 
emerging area and that a full evidence review was premature. However, key m-health studies 
52 
focused on self-monitoring were identified, and thus were reviewed as part of the body of 
53 
evidence on self-monitoring. This chapter addresses sedentary behaviors, but not physical 
54 
activity behaviors in general because these are addressed in Part D. Chapter 7: Physical 
55 
Activity.  
56 
 
57 
Consistent with the DGAC conceptual model presented in Part B. Chapter 1: Introduction, this 
58 
chapter also addresses major contextual factors that influence the ability of individuals to 
59 
implement healthy dietary and other lifestyles, including the prevention of sedentary behaviors. 
60 
The Committee focused on the association between diet, body weight, and chronic disease 
61 
outcomes and two contextual factors that are highly relevant in the United States—household 
62 
food insecurity and acculturation.  
63 
 
64 
Household food insecurity is defined as “access to enough food for an active, healthy life. It 
65 
includes at a minimum (a) the ready availability of nutritionally adequate and safe foods, and (b) 
66 
an assured ability to acquire acceptable foods in socially acceptable ways (e.g., without resorting 
67 
to emergency food supplies, scavenging, stealing, or other coping  strategies)”.7 Thus, household 
68 
food insecurity is a condition that exists whenever the availability of nutritionally adequate and 
69 
safe foods, or the ability to acquire acceptable foods in socially acceptable ways, is limited or 
70 
uncertain.7 In 2013, 49.1 million people in the United States lived in food insecure households, 
71 
and of these, 8.6 million are children.1  Household food insecurity is suggested to be an 
72 
independent risk factor for poor physical and mental health outcomes across the lifespan.8, 9  
73 
 
74 
The second contextual factor the DGAC addressed—acculturation—reflects that the United 
75 
States continues to be a nation of immigrants.10, 11  Acculturation has been defined both as the 
76 
“process by which immigrants adopt the attitudes, values, customs, beliefs, and behaviors of a 
77 

 
 Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and PA Behavior Change 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
3 
 
new culture”,12 and as the “gradual exchange between immigrants’ original attitudes and 
78 
behavior and those of the host culture”.13 Acculturation is relevant for individual dietary 
79 
behaviors because evidence suggests that the healthy lifestyles with which immigrants arrive 
80 
deteriorate as they integrate or assimilate into mainstream American culture.14 Moreover, 
81 
evidence suggests that to be effective in helping immigrants retain their healthy lifestyles, 
82 
nutrition education programs, including those that are a part of food assistance programs, must 
83 
be tailored to their different levels of acculturation.14  
84 
 
85 
Given the strong relevance of household food insecurity and acculturation as contextual factors 
86 
influencing healthy lifestyles, the DGAC examined associations between them and diet, obesity 
87 
risk, and whenever possible, corresponding chronic disease risk factors.  
88 
 
89 
LIST OF QUESTIONS 
90 
Eating Out 
91 
1. What is the relationship between eating out and/or take away meals and body weight in 
92 
children and adults? 
93 
 
94 
Family Shared Meals  
95 
2. What is the relationship between frequency and regularity of family shared meals and 
96 
measures of dietary intake in U.S. population groups? 
97 
3. What is the relationship between frequency and regularity of family shared meals and 
98 
measures of body weight and obesity in U.S. population groups? 
 
99 
 
100 
Sedentary Behavior, Including Screen Time  
101 
4. What is the relationship between sedentary behavior and measures of dietary intake and body 
102 
weight in adults?   
103 
5. How effective are behavioral interventions in youth that focus on reducing recreational 
104 
sedentary screen time and improving physical activity and/or diet? 
105 
 
106 
Self-Monitoring  
107 
6. What is the relationship between use of diet and body weight self-monitoring strategies and 
108 
body weight outcomes in adults and youth? 
109 
 
110 

 
 Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and PA Behavior Change 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
4 
 
Food and Menu Labeling  
111 
7. What is the relationship between knowledge and use of food and menu labels and measures 
112 
of dietary intake in U.S. population groups?   
113 
 
114 
Household Food Insecurity (HFI) 
115 
8. What is the relationship between household food insecurity (HFI) and measures of dietary 
116 
intake and body weight?  
117 
 
118 
Acculturation  
119 
9. What is the relationship between acculturation and measures of dietary intake?   
120 
10. What is the relationship between acculturation and body weight? 
121 
11. What is the relationship between acculturation and risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD)? 
122 
12. What is the relationship between acculturation and risk of type 2 diabetes?  
123 
 
124 
METHODOLOGY  
125 
All of the questions covered in this chapter— eating out, family shared meals, sedentary 
126 
behavior, self-monitoring, food and menu labeling, household food insecurity, and 
127 
acculturation—were answered using Nutrition Evidence Library (NEL) systematic reviews. A 
128 
description of the NEL process is provided in Part C: Methodology. All reviews were conducted 
129 
in accordance with NEL methodology, and the DGAC made all substantive decisions required 
130 
throughout the process to ensure that the most complete and relevant body of evidence was 
131 
identified and evaluated to answer each question. All steps in the process were documented to 
132 
ensure transparency and reproducibility. Specific information about individual systematic 
133 
reviews can be found at www.NEL.gov, including the search strategy, inclusion and exclusion 
134 
criteria, a complete list of included and excluded articles, and detailed documentation describing 
135 
the included studies and the body of evidence. A link to this website is provided following each 
136 
evidence review. 
137 
 
138 
EATING OUT 
139 
The majority of Americans consume meals outside of the home one or more times per week (see 
140 
Part D. Chapter 1: Food and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends). The 
141 
2010 DGAC concluded that “strong and consistent evidence indicates that children and adults 
142 
who eat fast food are at increased risk of weight gain, overweight, and obesity”.2 With this 
143 
relationship as a foundation, the 2015 DGAC updated and expanded the review of the “eating 
144 
out” topic. Specifically, the “fast food” category was broadened to capture other types of eating 
145 
out venues (e.g., quick serve, casual, formal restaurants, and grocery store take-out). 
146 

 
 Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and PA Behavior Change 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
5 
 
Terminology used to define the exposure was modified from “eating out,” to the broader term 
147 
“eating out and/or take away meals” to reflect the inclusion of meals eaten out at a broader array 
148 
of restaurant venues as well as takeout or ready-to-eat foods or meals purchased and consumed 
149 
either away from or in the home. The population of interest remained healthy individuals ages 2 
150 
years and older. 
151 
 
152 
Question 1: What is the relationship between eating out and/or take away meals 
153 
and body weight in children and adults? 
154 
Source of evidence: Update to 2010 DGAC’s NEL systematic review 
155 
Conclusion 
156 
Among adults, moderate evidence from prospective cohort studies in populations ages 40 years or 
157 
younger at baseline indicates higher frequency of fast food consumption is associated with higher 
158 
body weight, body mass index (BMI), and risk for obesity. DGAC Grade: Moderate 
159 
 
160 
Among children, limited evidence from prospective cohort studies in populations ages 8 to 16 
161 
years at baseline suggests that higher frequency of fast food consumption is associated with 
162 
increased adiposity, BMI z-score, or risk of obesity during childhood, adolescence, and during the 
163 
transition from adolescence into adulthood. DGAC Grade: Limited 
164 
 
165 
Insufficient evidence is available to assess the relationship between frequency of other types of 
166 
restaurant and takeout meals and body weight outcomes in children and adults. DGAC Grade: 
167 
Grade Not assignable 
168 
 
169 
Implications 
170 
Given that one-third of calories are consumed outside of the home (see Part D. Chapter 1: Food 
171 
and Nutrient Intakes, and Health: Current Status and Trends), individuals should limit the 
172 
frequency of eating at fast-food establishments. When eating out, one should choose healthy foods 
173 
and beverages within their calorie needs to avoid increases in body weight.  
174 
 
175 
Review of the Evidence  
176 
Fifteen prospective studies examined the relationship between eating out and/or take away meals 
177 
and measures of body weight in adults and children.15-29 Eleven studies in the United States 16-18, 
178 
20-23, 25-28 and four international studies (one each from Canada, the United Kingdom, Australia, 
179 
and Spain)15, 19, 24, 29 were reviewed. Men and women and boys and girls were well represented 
180 
and the majority of studies within the United States included diverse populations.  
181 
 
182 

 
 Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and PA Behavior Change 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
6 
 
In children, seven prospective cohort studies19, 21, 22, 24, 27-29 examined the relationship between 
183 
frequency of fast-food meals, or consumption of other types of meals and anthropometric 
184 
outcomes and, overall, found mixed results. Six studies examined fast-food meals19, 21, 22, 24, 28, 29: 
185 
three studies19, 28, 29 indicated increased fast food intake, particularly more than twice per week, 
186 
was associated with increased risk of obesity, BMI/BMI z-score or body fat, two22, 24 found no 
187 
association, and one21 found no association in boys and a negative association in girls. Two 
188 
studies looked at a variety of non-fast-food meals away from home, using varying definitions of 
189 
food establishments and meal types and reported mixed findings for a relationship with weight-
190 
related outcomes.27, 28  
191 
 
192 
In adolescents transitioning to adulthood, one study found high baseline frequency of fast food 
193 
intake was associated with increased BMI z-scores at 5-year follow-up.25 In adults, evidence 
194 
consistently demonstrated a relationship between higher frequency of fast-food meal 
195 
consumption and body weight outcomes. Five prospective cohort studies (three cohorts) reported 
196 
a higher frequency of intake of meals from fast food locations, or intake exceeding once per 
197 
week, was associated with higher weight gain, BMI, and risk of obesity.17, 18, 20, 23, 26 A 
198 
“moderate” grade was assigned (as opposed to the “strong” grade assigned by the 2010 DGAC) 
199 
because the evidence based was small (five studies focused on fast food, three from the same 
200 
cohort), all of which were prospective cohort studies; few studies controlled for energy intake 
201 
and no study reported actual food consumed; and the method of measurement of “eating out” 
202 
varied among studies. Evidence related to the association between frequency of meals from other 
203 
types of restaurants and intake of all takeout meals and weight is limited, but indicates traditional 
204 
restaurant meal frequency may not be associated with weight outcomes.17, 18 Two studies15, 16 
205 
examined total meals away from home or meal types eaten away from home, which came from 
206 
both fast food and restaurant locations, and reported frequency was associated with increased 
207 
body weight outcomes for most meal types. Two studies from the same cohort found no 
208 
significant relationship between frequency of meals from restaurants (non-fast-food 
209 
establishments), and weight-related outcomes.  
210 
 
211 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit: http://NEL.gov/topic.cfm?cat=3371  
212 
 
213 
FAMILY SHARED MEALS 
214 
Data from cross-sectional studies suggest that when families share meals, they achieve better diet 
215 
quality and improved nutrient intake, and to some extent, are better able to maintain appropriate 
216 
body weight.30-36  The definition of family shared meals in the literature varies, with some 
217 
defining it as the number of a specific meal eaten together (e.g., dinner), or any meal, prepared at 
218 
home or outside of home, that is shared among individuals living in the same household.37 
219 
Family mealtime may act as a protective factor for many nutritional health-related problems. For 
220 
example, they provide an opportunity for parents to model good eating behaviors and create a 
221 

 
 Part D. Chapter 3: Individual Diet and PA Behavior Change 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee  
7 
 
positive atmosphere by providing time for social interaction and thus a sense of social support 
222 
for all members.38, 39 Shared meals may be important in every stage of the lifecycle to support 
223 
healthy growth, development, and weight, though the evidence for adults is mixed. The 
224 
importance of the family in supporting positive behaviors is clearly part of the life course 
225 
approach embodied in the DGAC’s conceptual model (see Part B. Chapter 2: 2015 DGAC 
226 
Themes and Recommendations: Integrating the Evidence). As a result, the Committee decided 
227 
to explore the relationship between family shared meals and dietary intake as well as weight 
228 
outcomes from high-quality epidemiological studies to determine if there is a cause and effect 
229 
association.  
230 
 
231 
Question 2: What is the relationship between frequency/regularity of family 
232 
shared meals and measures of dietary intake in U.S. population groups? 
233 
Source of evidence: NEL systematic review 
234 
Conclusion 
235 
Insufficient evidence on the association between frequency of family shared meals and measures of 
236 
dietary intake is available to draw a conclusion. DGAC Grade: Grade not assignable 
237 
 
238 
Implications 
239
