369 
PHYSICAL ACTIVITY DOSE  
370 
Question 11: What dose of physical activity is most likely to provide health 
371 
benefits in children and adolescents? 
372 
Source of Evidence: Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Report, 2008 
373 
Conclusion 
374 
The DGAC concurs with the 2008 PAGAC, which found that substantial evidence indicates 
375 
important health and fitness benefits can be expected to accrue to most children and adolescents 
376 
who participate daily in 60 or more minutes of moderate to vigorous physical activity. Also, 
377 

 
Part D. Chapter 7: Physical Activity 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
14 
 
certain specific types of physical activity should be included in an overall physical activity 
378 
pattern in order for children and adolescents to gain comprehensive health benefits. These 
379 
include regular participation in each of the following types of physical activity on 3 or more days 
380 
per week: resistance exercise to enhance muscular strength in the large muscle groups of the 
381 
trunk and limbs, vigorous aerobic exercise to improve cardiorespiratory fitness and 
382 
cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk factors, and weight-loading activities to promote bone 
383 
health. DGAC Grade: Strong 
384 
 
385 
Based on these conclusions from the 2008 PAGAC, the PAG provides recommendations on 
386 
physical activity for children and adolescents (Table D7.1). The DGAC concurs with these 
387 
recommendations. 
388 
 
389 
Review of Evidence 
390 
A body of RCTs, meta-analyses, non-randomized trials, well-designed prospective cohort 
391 
studies, case-control studies, and other observational studies supports the dose of physical 
392 
activity most likely to provide health benefits in children and adolescents.  
393 
 
394 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit: Appendix E-2.49 and Physical Activity 
395 
Guidelines Advisory Committee Report, 2008 at 
396 
http://www.health.gov/paguidelines/Report/pdf/CommitteeReport.pdf.   
397 
For evidence reviews on: 
398 
 Children and adolescents, see Part G, Section 9: Youth 
399 
 
400 
For additional details about the PAG recommendations, visit: 
401 
http://www.health.gov/paguidelines/pdf/paguide.pdf.  
402 
 
403 
Question 12: What dose of physical activity is most likely to provide health 
404 
benefits in adults? 
405 
Source of Evidence: Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Report, 2008 
406 
 
407 
Conclusion 
408 
The DGAC concurs with the 2008 PAGAC, which found that for overall public health benefit, 
409 
data from a large number of studies evaluating a wide variety of benefits in diverse populations 
410 
generally support 30 to 60 minutes per day of moderate- to vigorous-intensity physical activity 
411 
on 5 or more days of the week. For a number of benefits, including all-cause mortality, coronary 
412 
heart disease, stroke, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes in adults and older adults, lower risk is 
413 
consistently observed at 2.5 hours per week of moderate- to vigorous-intensity activity. The 
414 

 
Part D. Chapter 7: Physical Activity 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
15 
 
amount of moderate- to vigorous-intensity activity most consistently associated with 
415 
significantly lower rates of colon and breast cancer and the prevention of unhealthy weight gain 
416 
or significant weight loss by physical activity alone is in the range of 3 to 5 hours per week. The 
417 
available evidence suggests that the major health benefits of physical activity and the dose 
418 
needed for major health benefits are similar for all adults, regardless of race or ethnicity. For a 
419 
variety of health and fitness outcomes, including chronic disease prevention, improvement of 
420 
various disease biomarkers and the maintenance of a healthy weight, reasonably strong evidence 
421 
demonstrates that amounts of moderate- to vigorous-intensity activity that exceed 150 minutes 
422 
per week are associated with greater health benefits. DGAC Grade: Strong 
423 
 
424 
Based on these conclusions from the 2008 PAGAC, the PAG provides recommendations on 
425 
physical activity for adults ages 18 years and older (Table D7.1). The DGAC concurs with these 
426 
recommendations. 
427 
 
428 
Review of Evidence 
429 
A body of well-designed prospective cohort studies and case control studies provides evidence 
430 
on physical activity dose most likely to provide health benefits in adults.  
431 
 
432 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit: Appendix E-2.49 and Physical Activity 
433 
Guidelines Advisory Committee Report, 2008 at 
434 
http://www.health.gov/paguidelines/Report/pdf/CommitteeReport.pdf.   
435 
For evidence reviews on: 
436 
 Adults, see Part E: Integration and Summary of the Science (pages E-23 to E-24) 
437 
 
438 
For additional details about the PAG recommendations, visit: 
439 
http://www.health.gov/paguidelines/pdf/paguide.pdf.  
440 
 
441 
Question 13: Are there any special considerations for dose of physical activity for 
442 
older adults? 
443 
Source of Evidence: Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Report, 2008 
444 
Conclusion 
445 
The DGAC concurs with the 2008 PAGAC, which found that, because the exercise capacity of 
446 
adults tends to decrease as they age, older adults generally have lower exercise capacities than 
447 
younger persons. Thus, they may need a physical activity plan that is of lower absolute intensity 
448 
and amount (but similar in self-perceived relative intensity and amount) than is appropriate for 
449 
more fit people, especially when they have been sedentary and are starting an activity program.  
450 
 
451 

 
Part D. Chapter 7: Physical Activity 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
16 
 
For older adults at risk of falling, strong evidence exists that regular physical activity is safe and 
452 
reduces falls by about 30 percent. Most evidence supports a program of exercise with the 
453 
following characteristics: 3 times per week of balance training and moderate-intensity muscle-
454 
strengthening activities for 30 minutes per session and with additional encouragement to 
455 
participate in moderate-intensity walking activities 2 or more times per week for 30 minutes per 
456 
session. Some evidence, albeit less consistent, suggests that tai chi exercises also reduce falls. 
457 
Successful reduction in falls by tai chi interventions resulted from programs conducted from 1 to 
458 
3 hours or more per week. No evidence indicates that planned physical activity reduces falls in 
459 
adults and older adults who are not at risk of falls. DGAC Grade: Strong 
460 
 
461 
Based on these conclusions from the 2008 PAGAC, the PAG provides recommendations on 
462 
physical activity for adults ages 65 years and older (Table D7.1). The DGAC concurs with these 
463 
recommendations. 
464 
 
465 
Review of Evidence 
466 
A body of RCTs, meta-analyses, and non-randomized trials provides evidence on physical 
467 
activity dose in older adults.  
468 
 
469 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit: Appendix E-2.49 and Physical Activity 
470 
Guidelines Advisory Committee Report, 2008 at 
471 
http://www.health.gov/paguidelines/Report/pdf/CommitteeReport.pdf.   
472 
For evidence reviews on: 
473 
 Older adults, see Part E: Integration and Summary of the Science (pages E-23 to E-24) 
474 
 
475 
For additional details about the PAG recommendations, visit: 
476 
http://www.health.gov/paguidelines/pdf/paguide.pdf.  
477 

 
Part D. Chapter 7: Physical Activity 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee

PHYSICAL ACTIVITY INTERVENTIONS FOR CHILDREN AND 
478 
ADOLESCENTS  
479 
Question 14:  What is the relationship between physical activity participation and 
480 
interventions in school-based settings? 
481 
Question 15:  What is the relationship between physical activity participation and 
482 
interventions to change the built environment? 
483 
Question 16:  What is the relationship between physical activity participation and 
484 
interventions based in home settings? 
485 
Question 17:  What is the relationship between physical activity participation and 
486 
interventions based in early care and education centers? 
487 
Question 18:  What is the relationship between physical activity participation and 
488 
interventions based in primary health care settings? 
489 
Source of Evidence: Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans Midcourse Report: 
490 
Strategies to Increase Physical Activity Among Youth  
491 
 
492 
Conclusion 
493 
The DGAC concurs with the Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans Midcourse Report: 
494 
Strategies to Increase Physical Activity Among Youth, which found that multi-component school-
495 
based interventions that include strategies such as physical education, active transportation, and 
496 
activity breaks can increase physical activity in children and adolescents during school hours. 
497 
DGAC Grade: Strong  
498 
 
499 
Reasonably consistent evidence suggests that changing the built environment as well as 
500 
interventions in early care and education centers can increase physical activity in children and 
501 
adolescents. DGAC Grade: Moderate 
502 
 
503 
Evidence to date is insufficient to conclude that intervention strategies in home or primary health 
504 
care settings increase physical activity in children and adolescents. DGAC Grade: Grade Not 
505 
Assignable 
506 
 
507 
Review of Evidence 
508 
A body of systematic reviews and meta-analyses supports interventions to increase physical 
509 
activity in children and adolescents.  
510 
 
511 

 
Part D. Chapter 7: Physical Activity 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
18 
 
For additional details on this body of evidence, visit: Appendix E-2.49 and Physical Activity 
512 
Guidelines for Americans Midcourse Report: Strategies to Increase Physical Activity Among 
513 
Youth at http://www.health.gov/paguidelines/midcourse/pag-mid-course-report-final.pdf.  
514 
For evidence reviews on: 
515 
 School-based interventions, see School Setting (pages 9 to 14)

521 
 
522 
IMPLICATIONS 
523 
Given the strong evidence for health benefits of regular physical activity as well as the low levels 
524 
of adherence to national recommendations, every effort should be made to encourage and 
525 
facilitate programs at multiple levels so that children, adults, and older adults can meet the 2008 
526 
PAG in combination with the Dietary Guidelines for Americans. This can be achieved if 
527 
programs, policies, and communication strategies are developed across sectors to increase 
528 
opportunities for engaging in physical activity and to improve the built environment. Ultimately, 
529 
these actions can create a culture of health that facilitates participation in regular physical 
530 
activity. Individuals, communities, schools, health care, and the private and public sectors 
531 
should: 
532 
• Ensure that all individuals have access to safe, affordable, and enjoyable modes of physical 
533 
activity throughout the day in the environments where they live, learn, work, and play.  
534 
These opportunities must include structured programming and informal modes of 
535 
transportation and play. 
536 
• Focus particular attention on people with the greatest health disparities, as these individuals 
537 
have the lowest physical activity participation rates but can gain the most health benefits by 
538 
being physically active. 
539 
• Support policies and promote programs for children, adolescents, adults, and older adults that 
540 
help set and reinforce a personal value system that instills a lifetime of physical activity.  
541 
• Enact effective policies and strengthen existing policies within schools, communities, health 
542 
care settings, housing, and worksites that promote opportunities for regular physical activity.  
543 
• Enact effective policies and strengthen existing policies that promote active transport (e.g., 
544 
walking and bicycling) within and between communities. 
545 
• Develop and promote programs to create or enhance access to safe and enjoyable places to be 
546 
physically active, including public spaces and local, state, and national parks.  
547 

 
Part D. Chapter 7: Physical Activity 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
19 
 
• Develop and implement ongoing physical activity promotion campaigns that involve high-
548 
visibility and multiple delivery channels and multiple sectors of influence.  
549 
• Coordinate efforts between numerous Federal and non-Federal initiatives, such as the 
550 
President’s Council on Fitness, Sports and Nutrition, Let’s Move!, the National Physical 
551 
Activity Plan, and Active Schools Acceleration Project. 
552 
 
553 
 
554 
CHAPTER SUMMARY 
555 
The findings outlined in this chapter provide strong evidence supporting the importance of 
556 
regular physical activity for health promotion and disease prevention in the U.S. population. 
557 
Physical activity is important for all people—children, adolescents, adults, older adults, women 
558 
during pregnancy and the postpartum period, and individuals with disabilities. The findings 
559 
further provide guidance on the dose of physical activity needed across the lifecycle to realize 
560 
these significant health benefits.  
561 
 
562 
Future Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committees will be asked to carefully review the 
563 
most recent evidence so that the Federal government can fully update the PAG. Given the 
564 
exceedingly low physical activity participation rates in this country, it will be critically important 
565 
for the next PAGAC to identify proven strategies and approaches to increase population-level 
566 
physical activity across the lifespan.  
567 
 
568 
 
569 
NEEDS FOR FUTURE RESEARCH 
570 
1. Evaluate best practices in programming at the community and national level and identify 
571 
which local and national policies in the public and private sector have demonstrated the 
572 
greatest effect on increasing physical activity participation across the lifespan, especially in 
573 
populations with the greatest health disparities.  
574 
Rationale:  Physical activity participation rates are exceptionally low across all age groups, 
575 
and are especially low in individuals with the greatest health disparities. Many different 
576 
initiatives are currently underway in the private and public sector to help increase physical 
577 
activity on a population level. Understanding which programs and policies are having the 
578 
greatest impact will help focus valuable resources and national recommendations for 
579 
maximum public health benefit. 
580 
 
581 
2. Identify the dose of physical activity needed to achieve health benefits, as well as appropriate 
582 
growth and development, for children younger than age 6 years.  
583 
Rationale: Until recently, very little effort has been focused on understanding the health 
584 
benefits of physical activity for young children. Given that this is a critical age of growth and 
585 
development, considerable research should be focused on this age group. 
586 

 
Part D. Chapter 7: Physical Activity 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
20 
 
 
587 
3. Evaluate the effects of various modes and doses of physical activity on health outcomes in 
588 
older adults. 
589 
Rationale:  Older adults are the fastest growing segment of the population. They also have 
590 
the greatest burden of disease and functional (mental and physical) limitations. To reduce 
591 
burden of disease and related economic impacts, research regarding mode and dose of 
592 
physical activity should be focused on this age group. 
593 
 
594 
4. Further evaluate the importance of light activity, short bouts of physical activity (i.e., 10-
595 
minutes or less) and modes of activity on health outcomes across the lifespan.  
596 
Rationale:  The review of the evidence in the 2008 PAGAC Report focused primarily on 
597 
moderate- and vigorous-intensity activity. Emerging research highlights the positive effects 
598 
of light activity as well as shorter bouts of vigorous activity on health outcomes. 
599 
Understanding the health impact of the full range of mode, intensity, duration, frequency, and 
600 
setting will help to further refine the PAG to support maximum public health benefit. 
601 
 
602 
5. Further investigate the effects of sedentary behaviors on health outcomes, including duration, 
603 
frequency, and mode of sedentary activities. 
604 
Rationale: Increasing evidence demonstrates the negative health consequences of sedentary 
605 
behaviors. Clarity on the types and duration of sedentary behaviors that have the most 
606 
negative health impact would help to identify meaningful evidence-based public health 
607 
recommendations. 
608
