Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Appendix E-1: Needs for Future Research 
1 
CHAPTER 1: FOOD AND NUTRIENT INTAKES AND HEALTH: 
2 
CURRENT STATUS AND TRENDS 
3 
1. Expand WWEIA participation to include more respondents from race/ethnic minorities and 
4 
non-U.S. born residents.  
5 
Rationale: Very little is known about the dietary habits of many of the cultural subgroups in 
6 
the United States. This knowledge is essential to moving forward any nutrition programs for 
7 
first and second generation immigrants. More data on the impact of acculturation also are 
8 
needed on food and health behaviors. The number of participants in WWEIA using the 
9 
derived acculturation variable was too small for any analysis. Finally, “Hispanic” is a very 
10 
broad term and a better understanding is needed of the nutritional profiles (including 
11 
shortfalls and excesses) across various Spanish-speaking people in the United States, who 
12 
come from different cultural backgrounds with distinct eating patterns. 
13 
 
14 
2. Include higher proportion of older Americans as respondents in WWEIA. 
15 
Rationale: More data are needed on dietary intake of older adults; the sample sizes in 
16 
WWEIA were too small for any meaningful analyses for those older than the age of 71 years. 
17 
In addition to nutrient intake, additional information is needed on whether older adults are 
18 
able to shop and cook, whether polypharmacy plays a role in nutritional adequacy, and 
19 
whether co-morbidities, such as poor dentition, musculo-skeletal difficulties, arthralgias and 
20 
other age-related symptoms, affect their ability to establish and maintain proper nutritional 
21 
status. 
22 
 
23 
3. Increase the number of pregnant women as respondents in WWEIA. 
24 
Rationale: The number of pregnant women in WWEIA is currently too small to properly 
25 
evaluate the status and trends in food and nutrient intake in pregnant women. Since good 
26 
nutrition in pregnancy is critical to proper growth development of the infant it is critical to 
27 
properly evaluate food and nutrient intake, which will inform recommendations and public 
28 
policies for pregnant women. 
29 
 
30 
4. Conduct research on nutrition transitions from childhood to shed light on how and why 
31 
dietary intake changes so rapidly from early childhood through pre-adolescence and 
32 
adolescence, and to identify the driving forces behind dietary intake change in these age 
33 
groups and what programs are most effective at maintaining positive nutrition habits 
34 
established in very young children. 
35 
Rationale: Young children have better dietary intake than older children and adolescents. It 
36 
is important to maintain the positive gains made in early childhood and identify factors 
37 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
responsible for the declines in intakes of fruit, dairy, and other food groups and increases in 
38 
added sugars and refined grains as children become enter the elementary school age years, as 
39 
poor eating patterns in elementary school seem to persist into adolescence and beyond. 
40 
 
41 
5. Evaluate the effects of common variations in dietary patterns in small children on nutrient 
42 
intakes. 
43 
Rationale: Children from 2 to 4 years of age have a highly variable diet and often do not fit 
44 
readily into the USDA Food Pattern food groups diet pattern analyses. Further information is 
45 
needed to understand the broad range of diets and supplement use in small children and how 
46 
this relates to nutrient intake and growth. Research is needed to better characterize their diets 
47 
so that appropriate guidance can be offered. 
48 
 
49 
6. Increase the quantity and quality of food composition databases available for research.  
50 
Rationale: Accurate assessment of nutrient intake and trends over time in the U.S. 
51 
population is dependent upon the quality of food composition data. Tens of thousands of 
52 
foods are available for purchase and consumption in the United States, but accurate nutrient 
53 
content data are available only for less than 10,000 foods and are almost non-existent for 
54 
many ready-to-eat and restaurant-type foods. Analytic values from foods are needed on 
55 
specific nutrients and components, such as vitamin D, fiber, added sugars, and sodium. 
56 
Improved food composition data also is critical for needed research to better define, identify, 
57 
and quantify total grain, whole grain consumption, and refined grain consumption in dietary 
58 
studies. 
59 
 
60 
7. Investigate the validity, reliability, and reproducibility of new biomarkers of nutrient intake 
61 
and biomarkers of nutritional status. 
62 
Rationale: Limited biomarkers are available and some that are available are difficult to 
63 
interpret due to other contributing factors to the biomarker measure (e.g., vitamin D is 
64 
obtained in the diet and is also endogenously synthesized). 
65 
 
66 
8. Evaluate effects of fortification strategies and supplement use on consumer behavior related 
67 
to the intake of foods and supplements containing key nutrients, including calcium, vitamin 
68 
D, potassium, iron, and fiber 
69 
Rationale: The intake of key nutrients of concern is considerably affected by the rapidly 
70 
evolving marketplace of food fortification and supplementation. Understanding consumer 
71 
behavior related to fortification and supplementation would be important in predicting the 
72 
effects of interventions and marketplace changes in content of these nutrients. Special 
73 
interest exists regarding fortification strategies of foods, including whole grains and yogurts, 
74 
in allowing individuals to reach the RDA for vitamin D without using supplements. Data are 
75 
needed on how supplements may help meet nutrients shortfalls and/or how use of 
76 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
3 
 
supplements may place individuals at risk of overconsumption. Research on effective 
77 
consumer guidance is needed. 
78 
 
79 
9. Understand the rationale for and consequences of the use of supplements above the UL for 
80 
vitamins and minerals. Identify biochemical markers that would indicate the effects of high-
81 
dose supplement use. 
82 
Rationale: Consumer use of high-dose supplements has increased. Understanding the 
83 
influences guiding this use would be helpful in considering how to educate consumers about 
84 
safe upper intake limits. 
85 
 
86 
10. Develop a standardized research definition for meals and snacks.  
87 
Rationale: Multiple different criteria are used in studies to define a snack or meal occasion, 
88 
such as time of day, the types or amounts of food consumed, or subjective assessment by the 
89 
study respondent. Researchers should work toward a consensus on the use of standard 
90 
definitions. 
91 
 
92 
11. Understand better the concept of dietary patterns and design approaches to quantify the diet 
93 
in large population-based studies. 
94 
Rationale: More methodological work on dietary patterns is needed. For example, food 
95 
frequency questionnaires, which are used in most diet assessment studies, do not capture data 
96 
on meal timing, meal frequency, or the types of foods consumed together. Studies using diet 
97 
recalls and records are better at capturing specific foods and their quantities consumed 
98 
(portion sizes) and the types of foods eaten together, but often these detailed assessment 
99 
methods are not feasible for large population-based studies. Quantification of food group 
100 
intake is needed. In addition, dietary patterns research encompasses a broader scope of issues 
101 
than can be addressed by diet scores and data drive approaches.  
102 
 
103 
12. Consistently report the nutrients, foods, and food groups that are used to evaluate dietary 
104 
patterns in published studies.  
105 
Rationale: The current scientific literature evaluating dietary patterns and health is 
106 
inconsistent in its provision of dietary patterns composition information. This makes it 
107 
difficult to compare, across studies, the components of healthful patterns that are associated 
108 
with health benefits. 
109 
 
110 
13. Conduct population surveillance on the prevalence and trends of nutrition-related chronic 
111 
diseases including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, some cancers osteoporosis and 
112 
neurocognitive disorders.  
113 
Rationale: Current data on diabetes in adults cannot be stratified by disease type (type I or 
114 
type II), making it very difficult to monitor incidence and prevalence of type 2 diabetes. 
115 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
4 
 
Continued population surveillance is needed to effectively link nutritional factors with risk of 
116 
these diseases.  
117 
 
118 
 
119 
CHAPTER 2: DIETARY PATTERNS, FOODS AND NUTRIENTS, AND 
120 
HEALTH OUTCOMES 
121 
1. Conduct additional dietary patterns research for other health outcomes to strengthen the 
122 
evidence beyond CVD and body weight in populations of various ethnic backgrounds and 
123 
life course stages in order for future DGACs to draw stronger conclusions. 
124 
Rationale: The NEL systematic reviews demonstrated that considerable CVD research 
125 
related to dietary patterns is available. However, it also is important to note, that unlike CVD, 
126 
some of the other health outcomes are more heterogeneous and thus may require greater 
127 
specificity in the examination of diet and disease risk. There is a clear need for all studies 
128 
examining the relationship between dietary patterns and health outcomes to include the full 
129 
age spectrum and to take a life course perspective (including pregnancy); insufficient 
130 
research is being devoted to children and how diseases may evolve over time. An increased 
131 
emphasis should be placed on understanding how the diets of all those in the U.S. population 
132 
from various ethnic backgrounds may be associated with health outcomes, thereby 
133 
broadening knowledge beyond Hispanics and African Americans to include the diversity that 
134 
exists in the United States today. This may require our national nutrition monitoring 
135 
programs to over-sample individuals from other national origins to conduct subgroup 
136 
analysis.  
137 
 
138 
2.  Improve the understanding of how to more precisely characterize dietary patterns by their 
139 
food constituents and the implications of the food constituents on nutrient adequacy through 
140 
the use of Food Pattern Modeling. More precise characterization, particularly of protein 
141 
foods, is needed. 
142 
Rationale:  Researchers are characterizing dietary patterns very differently and yet 
143 
sometimes use similar nomenclatures. This makes it difficult to compare results across 
144 
studies and as demonstrated in the NEL systematic reviews, can impair the grading of the 
145 
body of evidence as strong. The reason why researchers are not replicating others findings in 
146 
different populations may be a function of publication bias. It is important for editors of 
147 
scientific journals and peer reviewers to appreciate the replication of findings first and then 
148 
value a research group’s methodological nuance that may improve the examination of the 
149 
association between dietary patterns and a health outcomes. Perhaps what should be stressed 
150 
is a harmonization of research methods across various cohorts or randomized trials, similar to 
151 
what is being done at the National Cancer Institute’s Dietary Patterns Methods Project9, 220 
152 
led by Drs. Krebs-Smith and Reedy. The use of Food Pattern Modeling as demonstrated in 
153 
Chapter 1 allows questions about the adequacy of the dietary patterns given specific food 
154 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
5 
 
constituents to be addressed and how modifications of the patterns by altering the foods for 
155 
specific population groups or to meet specific nutrient targets can be achieved. 
156 
 
157 
3.  Examine the long-term cardio-metabolic effects of the various dietary patterns identified in 
158 
the AHA/ACC/TOS Guidelines for the Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults 
159 
that are capable of resulting in short-term weight loss (see Question 2, above).  
160 
Rationale: Although the research to date demonstrates that to lose weight, a variety of 
161 
dietary pattern approaches can be used if a reduction in caloric intake is achieved, the long-
162 
term effects of these diets on cardio-metabolic health are not well known. Emerging research 
163 
is exploring health effects of variations of the low-carbohydrate, higher protein/fat dietary 
164 
pattern. In some approaches (such as Atkins), the dietary pattern which emphasizes animal 
165 
products, may achieve a macronutrient composition that is higher in saturated fat. Others 
166 
may emphasize plant-based proteins and fats and may achieve a lower saturated fat content 
167 
and may be higher in polyunsaturated fats and dietary fiber. Research is needed to determine 
168 
the impact of these alternative approaches, and perhaps others, on CVD risk profiles as well 
169 
as other health outcomes. As mentioned in the review of the literature associated with 
170 
saturated fat and cardiovascular disease in Chapter 6: Cross-Cutting Topics of Dietary 
171 
Guidance and Public Health Importance, substituting one macronutrient for another may 
172 
result in unintended consequences. Careful consideration to the types of foods that are used 
173 
in these diets and in particular the type of fat and amount of added sugars should be taken 
174 
into account.  
175 
 
176 
 
177 
CHAPTER 3: INDIVIDUAL DIET AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY BEHAVIOR 
178 
CHANGE 
179 
Eating Out  
180 
1. Develop a standard methodology to collect and characterize various types of eating venues. 
181 
Rationale: This recommendation is fundamental to conducting rigorous research, evaluating 
182 
findings from multiple studies, and developing policies to promote healthy eating among 
183 
people who frequent eating out venues and/or consume take away meals. 
184 
 
185 
2. Conduct rigorously designed research to examine the longitudinal impact of obtaining or 
186 
consuming meals away from home from various types of commonly frequented venues on 
187 
changes in food and beverage intakes (frequency, quantity, and composition), body weight, 
188 
adiposity, and health profiles from childhood to adulthood in diverse (racial/ethnic, 
189 
socioeconomic, cultural, and geographic) groups of males and females. 
190 
Rationale: Most groups in the U.S. population regularly consume meals that are prepared 
191 
away from home and the landscape of fast food and other types of food procurement and 
192 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
6 
 
consumption venues is increasingly complex. The potential for eating out and/or take away 
193 
meals to influence diet quality, energy balance, body mass and composition, and the risks of 
194 
health-related morbidities across the life course among our diverse population underscores the 
195 
importance of understanding this issue. 
196 
 
197 
Family Shared Meals  
198 
3. Conduct studies in diverse populations that assess not only frequency of family shared meals, 
199 
but also quality of family shared meals. 
200 
Rationale: Our understanding of the importance of family shared meals in terms of how they 
201 
contribute in a positive way to body weight and overall health and well-being requires a 
202 
rigorous examination of the dietary quality of these meals compared to other meals consumed 
203 
by family members. 
204 
 
205 
4. Conduct RCTs to isolate the effect of interventions that increase the frequency of family 
206 
meals from other health and parenting behaviors that may be associated with dietary intake 
207 
and weight status. 
208 
Rationale:  Family shared meals are commonly implemented as one component of lifestyle 
209 
interventions that include an array of other behavioral and parenting strategies for weight 
210 
management. To improve our understanding of the causal pathway of how family shared meals 
211 
contributes to maintaining or achieving a health weight, the specific contribution of family 
212 
shared meals to weight outcomes independent of other behavioral strategies needs to be 
213 
ascertained.  
214 
 
215 
Sedentary Behavior  
216 
5. Develop improved and better standardized and validated tools to assess sedentary behaviors 
217 
and activities that children, adolescents, and adults regularly engage in. 
218 
Rationale: Our understanding of the impact of sedentary behaviors on diet, energy balance, 
219 
body mass, adiposity, and health is currently compromised by reliance on subjective 
220 
assessments, including self-reports of daily activity patterns, and by inadequate techniques to 
221 
document and quantify the array of sedentary activities people engage in (beyond TV viewing 
222 
and (or) computer screen time). It also would be beneficial for researchers to document the 
223 
potential benefits and implications of reducing one type of sedentary behavior (e.g. screen 
224 
time) on other sedentary behaviors (e.g., reading for leisure, arts and crafts, listening to music) 
225 
and indices of health (e.g. sleep quality and duration). 
226 
 
227 
6. Conduct prospective research to examine the effects and mechanisms of the quantity, 
228 
patterns, and changes of sedentary behaviors on diet quality, energy balance, body weight, 
229 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
7 
 
adiposity, and health across the life span in groups within the U.S. population with diverse 
230 
personal, cultural, economic, and geographic characteristics. 
231 
Rationale: Emerging, but limited, evidence implicates sedentary behaviors with adverse 
232 
health-related outcomes, especially in children and adolescents as they transition into 
233 
adulthood. However, an improved understanding of why these relationships exist will help in 
234 
developing appropriate and effective approaches and policies to reduce the amount of time 
235 
people spend engaging in sedentary behaviors. 
236 
 
237 
Self-Monitoring  
238 
7. Evaluate the impact of different types, modalities, and frequencies of self-monitoring on 
239 
body weight outcomes during both the weight loss intervention and maintenance periods. 
240 
Rationale:  Self-monitoring is associated with improved weight management. However, the 
241 
current practice of recommending daily self-monitoring may represent a barrier to its 
242 
implementation and/or continued use. Hence, it is important to determine whether lower 
243 
frequencies of self-monitoring can produce beneficial effects on weight outcomes. 
244 
 
245 
8. Evaluate the comparative effectiveness of performance feedback from self-monitoring 
246 
delivered through automated systems versus personal interactions with a counselor.  
247 
Rationale:  Automated feedback derived from self-monitoring data and delivered 
248 
electronically can produce beneficial changes on weight outcomes. However, the comparative 
249 
effectiveness and cost efficiency of feedback delivered through non-personal modalities versus 
250 
personal interactions has yet to be determined. 
251 
 
252 
9. Test the effectiveness of self-monitoring on weight outcomes in understudied groups, 
253 
including ethnic/racial minorities, low education, low literacy, and low numeracy 
254 
populations, males, and subjects younger than age 30 years and older than age 60 years.  
255 
Rationale:  Evidence regarding the effectiveness of self-monitoring has been derived largely 
256 
from research conducted on well educated, middle-class, white women. Hence, it is important 
257 
to determine whether the beneficial effects of self-monitoring on weight outcomes are 
258 
generalizable to understudied groups. 
259 
 
260 
10. Conduct RCTs based on sound behavioral change theories that incorporate self-monitoring, 
261 
employ heterogeneous populations, and are powered for small effect sizes and high attrition 
262 
rates, to test the short- (e.g., 3 months) and long-term (e.g., 12 months) effects of mobile health 
263 
technologies on dietary and weight outcomes. 
264 
Rationale: Mobile health technologies have the potential to reach larger portions of the 
265 
populations than face-to-face interventions, but the effect sizes of mobile technologies may be 
266 
small and the attrition rates may be large. Larger, more representative study populations and 
267 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
8 
 
longer study periods will permit an assessment of the generalizability and sustainability of 
268 
mobile health technologies.  
269 
 
270 
Food and Menu Labeling  
271 
11. Develop novel labeling approaches to provide informative strategies to convey caloric intake 
272 
values on food items consumed at home and in restaurant settings. 
273 
Rationale: Menu labels can include different types of information in addition to calories. 
274 
These include physical activity equivalents, and daily caloric needs. Very few studies have 
275 
been designed to examine the optimal combination of menu label information to prevent 
276 
excessive caloric intake. This will be very valuable evidence to inform the calorie label policy 
277 
that has just been enacted by the FDA. 
278 
 
279 
12. Compare labeling strategies across various settings, such as restaurants, stores, and the home 
280 
to determine their efficacy in altering food selection and health outcomes, including weight. 
281 
Rationale: The great majority of menu labeling RCT's have been conducted under laboratory 
282 
conditions. Given the recent FDA regulations, future studies will be able to impact the 
283 
effectiveness of these polices across settings as accessed by diverse free living populations.  
284 
 
285 
13. Evaluate the process and impact of recent FDA menu labeling regulation. 
286 
Rationale: The new FDA regulation provides a unique opportunity to understand the impact of 
287 
menu labeling on consumers dietary behaviors in "real world" settings. 
288 
 
289 
Household Food Insecurity  
290 
14. Conduct prospective cohort studies that cover a wide age range and include children, 
291 
families, older adults, and ethnically/racially diverse populations and describe potential effect 
292 
modifiers such as gender, ethnic and cultural factors, family structure, area of residence (i.e., 
293 
urban vs. rural), employment, and use of social support systems while examining the 
294 
relationship between household food insecurity, dietary intake, and body weight.  
295 
Rationale: Understanding the temporal process of when and how long food insecurity occurs 
296 
within a family/individual’s lifetime and their response to this economic stressor is critical to 
297 
conducting rigorous research and comparing finding across studies in order to develop and 
298 
implement intervention studies and policies to alleviate this public health problem. 
299 
 
300 
15. Standardize research methodology, including developing a consistent approach to measuring 
301 
food insecurity and use of measured height and weight to reduce the likelihood of responder 
302 
bias. 
303 
Rationale: The measurement error issues related to the use of self-reported weight have been 
304 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
9 
 
well documented in the literature. In order to conduct rigorous studies in this area that can be 
305 
compared and evaluated as to the causal nature of the role of food insecurity on body weight, 
306 
standard methodology is warranted both in the measurement of the exposure as well as the 
307 
outcome. 
308 
 
309 
Acculturation  
310 
16. Conduct prospective longitudinal studies, including those that start in early childhood to 
311 
track dietary intake, sedentary behaviors, body weight, and chronic disease outcomes across 
312 
the life course. Include the diversity of ethnic/racial groups in the United States, including 
313 
individuals and families of diverse national origins. Include comparison groups in countries 
314 
of origin to rule out, among other things, the potential confounding by internal migration 
315 
from rural to urban area within the country of origin.  
316 
Rationale: Acculturation is a time-dependent life course process that requires longitudinal 
317 
studies to be properly understood. Because the impact of acculturation on dietary, weight and 
318 
health outcomes can be expected to be modified by the life course stage of life when 
319 
individuals migrate to the United States, prospective acculturation studies need to start 
320 
following individuals from very early childhood.  
321 
 
322 
17. Develop a standard tool to measure acculturation or validation of multidimensional 
323 
acculturation scales in different immigrant groups and in different languages. 
324 
Rationale: Acculturation is a complex construct that is seldom measured with 
325 
multidimensional scales that can capture the different paths that migrant scan take with 
326 
regards to the acculturation process, including assimilation, integration, segregation, and 
327 
marginalization. Although research in acculturation measurement has been conducted among 
328 
Hispanic/Latinos, it has been predominantly based on Mexican American populations and 
329 
little acculturation measurement research has been conducted among other groups, including 
330 
individuals from Asia, Africa, Europe, and the Middle East. 
331 
 
332 
Sleep Patterns   
333 
18. Conduct prospective studies that start in childhood (including transition to adulthood), to 
334 
investigate the longitudinal effect of sleep patterns on diet and body weight outcomes while 
335 
accounting for confounders, mediators, and moderators including: physical activity, 
336 
socioeconomic variables (such as education, employment, household income), sex, alcohol 
337 
intake, smoking status (including new smoker, new non-smoker), media use/screen time, and 
338 
depression. 
339 
Rationale: While research associates short sleep duration and disordered sleep patterns with 
340 
adverse differences and changes in food and beverage consumption, body weight, and indices 
341 
of metabolic and cardiovascular health, less is known about the impact of potential modifying 
342 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
10 
 
lifestyle factors. This research will help delineate the role of sleep patterns, duration and 
343 
quality, i.e., mediator or moderator, on diet and weigh-related outcomes. Research in children 
344 
shows that sleep deprivation and weight are related but this relationship is not apparent in adult 
345 
studies. This may be due to the fact that energy intake increases during transition to short sleep 
346 
duration, but levels off when short sleep duration becomes consistent. 
347 
 
348 
19. Conduct studies to assess the effects of diet on sleep quality to examine the mechanism by 
349 
which dietary intake, energy intake, and energy expenditure may impact sleep.  
350 
Rationale: Most research has focused on sleep quality and duration as modifying factors on 
351 
diet, body weight, and health. A paucity of research exists on the potential impact of diet on 
352 
sleep-related outcomes. This line of research would use diet as the means to improve indices of 
353 
sleep, which in turn may subsequently improve health-related outcomes. 
354 
 
355 
 
356 
CHAPTER 4: FOOD ENVIRONMENT AND SETTINGS 
357 
1. Develop more valid and reliable methods for measuring all aspects of the food environment, 
358 
including the total food environment of communities. These methods can then be used to 
359 
assess the impact of the food environment on community health as well as on economic 
360 
development and growth. 
361 
Rationale:  The food environment has become more complex, with more and more retail 
362 
outlets selling food and beverages. Having valid and reliable methodologies for a variety of 
363 
food environments and settings (tools and new analytical approaches) will allow more 
364 
meaningful inquiry into the contributions of various settings in supporting or hindering 
365 
nutritional health. 
366 
 
367 
2. Identify, implement, evaluate, and scale up best practices (including private-public 
368 
partnerships) for affordable and sustainable solutions to improving the food environment and 
369 
increasing food access, especially in those environments of greatest need. 
370 
Rationale: The environments in which people live, work, learn, and play greatly influence 
371 
their food intake. To best guide efforts to improve the food environment, research is needed 
372 
to identify and evaluate best practices to direct available resources to new programs and scale 
373 
up. 
374 
 
375 
3. Identify, implement, accelerate, evaluate, and scale up programs that improve access to 
376 
healthy food and that can be integrated seamlessly with Federal nutrition assistance 
377 
programs, such as SNAP, WIC and elder nutrition. 
378 
Rationale: Federal nutrition assistance programs reach individuals and populations with the 
379 
greatest health disparities. Identifying and evaluating initiatives that integrate improvements 
380 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
11 
 
in the food environment with Federal programs will help ensure that Federal nutrition 
381 
assistance programs have as great an impact as possible. 
382 
  
383 
4. Conduct additional obesity prevention intervention research in child care settings (e.g., child-
384 
care centers, family child-care homes) to: 1) Identify the most potent components of the 
385 
interventions and the optimal combinations for improving diet quality, physical activity, and 
386 
weight outcomes; 2) Assess implementation and translation costs and benefits of the 
387 
intervention, including impact, cost-effectiveness, generalizability and reach, sustainability 
388 
and feasibility; 3) Develop and evaluate culturally appropriate and tailored interventions for 
389 
preschool children in low-income and racial/ethnic communities, given the disproportionate 
390 
impact of obesity in these groups; 4) Explore intervention strategies on how to use child care 
391 
settings as access points to create linkages to parents, caretakers, and health care providers as 
392 
partners in health promotion; 5) Evaluate the impact of Federal, state, and local policies, 
393 
regulations, and support (e.g., provider training and technical assistance) for child care 
394 
programs on the eating and physical activity practices and behaviors, and weight status of 
395 
young children. 
396 
Rationale: Early care and education settings are an important venue for interventions 
397 
targeting young children. A strong evidence base is essential to identify and support 
398 
evidence-based practices and policies that can be implemented at Federal, state, and local 
399 
levels and to mobilize efforts to improve healthy eating and physical activity, leading to 
400 
healthy weight development in these settings. Interventions found to effectively reduce risk 
401 
of obesity in one setting need to be appropriately adapted for diverse groups and different 
402 
settings. 
403 
 
404 
5. Improve intervention research methods by the use of stronger study designs and the 
405 
development of standardized assessments of body composition, weight status. Develop 
406 
enhanced validated measures of diet quality, feeding and physical activity practices, and 
407 
physical activity and eating behaviors and policies. Create standardized measures to assess 
408 
the nutrition quality of meals and snacks in child care settings, as well as the food and 
409 
physical activity environments. Create standardized methods for assessing the relationship of 
410 
child care food, nutrition and physical activity-related measures to similar measures 
411 
representing non-child care time are needed to provide greater consistency in determining the 
412 
contributors to the development and progression of childhood overweight and obesity. 
413 
Rationale: Although many of the studies included in these evidence reviews were 
414 
methodologically strong and were controlled studies, some were limited by small sample 
415 
size, lack of adequate control for confounding factors, and different outcome measures and 
416 
different tools used to measure the outcome variables.  
417 
 
418 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
12 
 
6. Examine the effect of the recommended Child and Adult Care Food Program (CACFP) 
419 
through ongoing periodic evaluations and fill gaps in the knowledge regarding participation, 
420 
demand, food procurement and practices, nutrient intake, and food security. 
421 
Rationale: Improvements in school meals and the school food environment have been 
422 
fostered by national data from periodic studies such as the USDA/FNS School Nutrition 
423 
Dietary Assessment Studies (SNDA), the HHS/CDC School Health Policies and Practices 
424 
Studies (SHPPS) and the HHS/NIH C.L.A.S.S. In contrast, considerably fewer periodic 
425 
national studies are conducted of meals and dietary intake in child care settings and their 
426 
relation to the child care food and physical activity environment. 
427 
 
428 
7. Conduct new research to document the types and quantities of foods and beverages students 
429 
consume both at school and daily outside of school, before, during, and after school-based 
430 
healthy eating approaches and policies are implemented. 
431 
Rationale: Effective school-based approaches and policies to improve the availability, 
432 
accessibility, and consumption of healthy foods and beverages, and reduce competition from 
433 
unhealthy offerings, are central to improving the weight status and health of children and 
434 
adolescents. Accurate quantification of the types and quantities of foods and beverages the 
435 
students consume before, during, and after approaches and policies are implemented is 
436 
fundamental to assessing effectiveness. However, many of the studies included in the 
437 
systematic reviews and meta-analyses used by the DGAC to address this issue did not 
438 
comprehensively measure or report dietary information. Although the USDA/FNS-sponsored 
439 
School Nutrition Dietary Assessment (SNDA) series collects student dietary intake data 
440 
every 10 years, the DGAC recommends more frequent and consistent data collection, 
441 
especially before and periodically after implementation of school-based nutrition and 
442 
physical activity policy and program changes. 
443 
 
444 
8. Improve the quality of research studies designed to assess the effects of school-based 
445 
approaches and policies on dietary behaviors and body weight control to reduce the risk of 
446 
bias, with an emphasis on randomized controlled trials. 
447 
Rationale: Although the methodological quality of the systematic reviews and meta-analyses 
448 
used by the DGAC to evaluate school-based approaches and policies on dietary intake and 
449 
body weight outcomes was high, the authors of these reviews commented that the scientific 
450 
quality of individual studies was generally poor and the risk of bias high. Many of the studies 
451 
were done using quasi-experimental (with or without control), pre-post intervention, or cross-
452 
sectional designs. Future research should prioritize using prospective, repeated measures, 
453 
randomized controlled trial experimental designs, with randomization at the individual, 
454 
classroom, school, or school district level. Pilot feasibility studies also may be helpful to 
455 
quickly identify promising novel approaches to improve dietary intake and weight control 
456 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
13 
 
outcomes. 
457 
 
458 
9. Conduct post-program follow-up assessments lasting longer than 1 year to determine the 
459 
long-term retention of the changed nutrition behaviors as well as the usefulness of continuing 
460 
to offer the programs while children advance in school grade. Also, conduct research is 
461 
needed in adolescents (grades 9-12). 
462 
Rationale: Literature supports that eating and physical activity behaviors and body weight 
463 
status of children predict changes over time as they progress into adolescence and adulthood. 
464 
Ideally, improvements in dietary intake and weight status achieved due to a given school-
465 
based approach or policy would be sustained over time and progressive improvements would 
466 
occur long-term. The vast majority of published research focuses on children in grades K-8, 
467 
or ages 4-12 years, and new and improved data are needed on adolescents and the transition 
468 
from childhood to adolescence. 
469 
 
470 
10. Encourage a wider variety of school-based approaches and policies to develop and evaluate 
471 
innovative approaches focused on increasing vegetable intakes.  
472 
Rationale: Consumption of non-potato vegetables is below 2010 Dietary Guidelines for 
473 
Americans recommendations in both children and adolescents. Published research indicates 
474 
that school-based approaches and policies designed to increase vegetable and fruit intakes are 
475 
generally more effective at increasing fruit intake, except for –school gardens and economic 
476 
incentives, which increase vegetable intake among school-aged children. Some past public 
477 
policies (e.g. the Basic 4) treated fruit and vegetables and as a single food group, which props 
478 
the need for new research that uses prospective, repeated measures, and randomized 
479 
controlled trial experimental designs to specifically target increased consumption of healthy 
480 
vegetables. 
481 
 
482 
11. Conduct assessments of the effectiveness of worksite interventions that emphasize obesity 
483 
prevention and weight control among workers across racially/ethnically diverse populations, 
484 
blue and white collar employees, and at-risk populations. Scientifically rigorous studies 
485 
(especially randomized controlled trials) addressing the long-term health impact of worksite-
486 
based approaches and policies that improve employee diet, physical activity, and body 
487 
weight control would have public health relevance.  
488 
Rationale: In light of the high rates of obesity and overweight, worksite interventions 
489 
targeting obesity prevention and weight control through enhanced dietary behaviors and 
490 
increased physical activity among workers is important. The majority of the studies to date 
491 
have been conducted for relatively short periods of time, and the long-term impact of these 
492 
approaches and policies may prove beneficial.  
493 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
14 
 
 
494 
CHAPTER  5: FOOD SUSTAINABILITY AND SAFETY 
495 
Dietary Patterns and Sustainability 
496 
1. Conduct research to determine whether sustainable diets are affordable and accessible to all 
497 
sectors of the population and how this can be improved, including how policy strategies 
498 
could influence the supply chain (all steps from farm to plate) to affect this improvement. 
499 
Rationale:  Ensuring that sustainable diets are accessible and affordable to all sectors of the 
500 
population is important to promote food security. 
501 
 
502 
2. Develop, conduct, and evaluate in-depth analyses of U.S. domestic dietary patterns and 
503 
determine the degree to which sustainability practices, domestically and internationally, are 
504 
important to food choice and how to increase public awareness of the impact of food choices 
505 
on environmental outcomes. 
506 
Rationale: Understanding consumer choice across demographic groups and the degree to 
507 
which either health and/or sustainability is a significant decisional criterion as well as the 
508 
degree to which choice theory can be used to improve choices will be important to helping 
509 
drive change. 
510 
 
511 
3. Develop a robust understanding of how production practices, supply chain decisions, 
512 
consumer behaviors, and waste disposal affect the environmental sustainability of various 
513 
practices across the USDA food components of MyPlate. 
514 
Rationale:  Developing sustainable production and supply chain practices for all parts of 
515 
MyPlate, especially meat and dairy products will be important to reduce their environmental 
516 
impact.  
517 
 
518 
4. Determine the potential economic benefits and challenges to supply chain stakeholders in 
519 
relationship to findings in Research Recommendation 3. 
520 
Rationale: Experience demonstrates that many practices over the past few decades that 
521 
improve the environmental footprint of, for example, production practices, also have led to 
522 
improved profit (e.g., Integrated Pest Management to reduce pesticide use in many fruit and 
523 
vegetables). It is important to know how changes will affect profit to help enable future 
524 
policy in both the private and public spheres. 
525 
 
526 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
15 
 
Seafood Sustainability 
527 
5. Conduct research on methods to ensure the maintenance of nutrient profiles of high-trophic 
528 
level farmed seafood and improve nutrient profiles of low-trophic farmed seafood 
529 
concurrently with research to improve production efficacy. 
530 
Rationale:  The evidence supporting healthfulness of seafood consumption is based on 
531 
consumption of predominantly wild caught species. Many popular low-trophic level farmed 
532 
seafood have nutrient profiles that depend on feeds. Efficient production of seafood with 
533 
nutrient profiles that are known to be healthful should be emphasized. 
534 
 
535 
6. Conduct research to develop methods to ensure contaminant levels in all seafood remain at 
536 
levels similar to or lower than at present. Maintain monitoring of contaminant levels for 
537 
capture fisheries to ensure that levels caused by pollution do not rise appreciably. This 
538 
research should include developing effective rapid response approaches if the quality of 
539 
seafood supply is acutely affected. 
540 
Rationale: Current research findings support the contention that contaminant levels are 
541 
generally well below those that significantly alter the healthfulness of seafood. As industry 
542 
naturally improves efficiency, feeds and environmental conditions should be monitored to 
543 
maintain or reduce priority contaminants and insure significant new contaminants do not 
544 
enter the seafood supply. 
545 
 
546 
Usual Caffeine/Coffee Intake 
547 
7. Evaluate the effects of coffee on health outcomes in vulnerable populations, such as women 
548 
who are pregnant (premature birth, low birth weight, spontaneous abortion).  
549 
Rationale: Given the limited evidence of the effects of coffee/caffeine consumption on 
550 
pregnancy outcomes, future studies need to establish safe levels of coffee/caffeine 
551 
consumption during pregnancy. 
552 
 
553 
8. Examine the effects of coffee on sleep patterns, quality of life, and dependency and 
554 
addiction. 
555 
Rationale: Because coffee is a known stimulant, future research should examine the effect of 
556 
coffee/caffeine on sleep quality, dependency, addiction, and overall quality of life measures. 
557 
 
558 
9. Evaluate the prospective association between coffee/caffeine consumption and cancer at 
559 
different sites.  
560 
Rationale: Large well-conducted prospective cohort studies that adequately control for 
561 
smoking (status and dosage) and other potential confounders are needed to understand the 
562 
association of coffee (caffeinated and decaffeinated) with cancer at different sites. 
563 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
16 
 
 
564 
10. Examine prospectively the effects of coffee/caffeine on cognitive decline, neurodegenerative 
565 
diseases, and depression.  
566 
Rationale: Neurodegenerative diseases affect millions of people worldwide and more than 
567 
five million Americans are living with Alzheimer’s disease. Given the limited evidence of 
568 
coffee/caffeine on neurodegenerative diseases, well-designed prospective studies should 
569 
examine the association of coffee/caffeine consumption on cognitive decline, depression, and 
570 
Alzheimer’s disease. 
571 
 
572 
11. Understand the mechanisms underlying the protective effects of coffee on diabetes and CVD.  
573 
Rationale: Evidence for a biological plausibility for coffee on risk of type 2 diabetes and 
574 
CVD stems primarily from animal studies. Randomized controlled trials in humans should 
575 
evaluate the effect of coffee/caffeine on measures of glycemia, insulin sensitivity, endothelial 
576 
dysfunction, and inflammation.  
577 
 
578 
12. Understand the association between coffee and health outcomes in individuals with existing 
579 
CVD, diabetes, cancer, neurodegenerative diseases, or depressive symptoms.  
580 
Rationale: Strong evidence supports a protective effect of moderate coffee consumption on 
581 
chronic disease risk in healthy adults, but its association among those with existing diseases 
582 
has been less studied. Given that a substantial number of people suffer from these chronic 
583 
diseases, the role of coffee in preventing other health outcomes in such groups remains 
584 
understudied. 
585 
 
586 
High-dose Caffeine Intake 
587 
13. Define excessive caffeine intake and safe levels of consumption for children, adolescents, 
588 
and young adults. 
589 
Rationale: Current research on caffeine and health outcomes has focused primarily on 
590 
adults. Given the increasing prevalence of energy drink consumption among children, 
591 
adolescents, and young adults, research is needed to identify safe levels of consumption in 
592 
these groups. 
593 
 
594 
14. Determine the prevalence of excessive caffeine intake in children and adults beyond intake of 
595 
energy drinks.  
596 
Rationale: Data on the sources (other than energy drinks) and doses of caffeine intake in 
597 
children and adults are limited. Identifying the sources and safe levels of consumption will 
598 
help in formulating policy and framing recommendations. 
599 
 
600 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
17 
 
15. Examine the effect of excessive consumption of caffeine and energy drinks on health 
601 
outcomes in both children and adults. 
602 
Rationale: Prospective studies of associations of excessive caffeine and energy drink intake 
603 
with health outcomes in children and adults are necessary, as randomized controlled trials are 
604 
not be feasible given ethical constraints.  
605 
 
606 
16. Conduct observational studies to examine the health effects of alcohol mixed with energy 
607 
drinks.  
608 
Rationale: In recent years, consumption of alcohol energy drinks by adolescents has resulted 
609 
in emergency room admissions and deaths. No data exist on the prospective association 
610 
between consumption of alcohol energy drinks and health outcomes in both adolescents and 
611 
adults. 
612 
 
613 
Aspartame 
614 
17. Examine the risks of aspartame related to some cancers, especially hematopoietic ones, and 
615 
pregnancy outcomes.  
616 
Rationale: Limited and inconsistent evidence suggests a possible association between 
617 
aspartame and risk of hematopoietic cancers (non-Hodgkin lymphoma and multiple 
618 
myeloma) in men, indicating the need for long-term human studies. Additionally, limited and 
619 
inconsistent evidence indicates a potential for risk of preterm delivery, which warrants 
620 
further research. 
621 
 
622 
 
623 
CHAPTER 6: CROSS-CUTTING TOPICS OF PUBLIC HEALTH 
624 
IMPORTANCE 
625 
1. Design and conduct studies with sufficient power to define the impact of improving dietary 
626 
quality, including the lowering dietary sodium intake, on hypertension and relevant disease 
627 
outcomes, including cardiovascular disease, stroke, peripheral vascular disease, kidney 
628 
disease, and others. The interactions with patterns of therapeutic medication use (e.g., 
629 
diuretics, antihypertensives, and lipid-lowering) should be considered.  
630 
Rationale: The current literature is incomplete, limited in power and durations, and often 
631 
compromised by methodological challenges that must be addressed in well-designed studies 
632 
with relevant clinical outcomes. 
633 
 
634 
2. Assess the accuracy of 24-hour urine collections for sodium assessment in populations with 
635 
different health conditions (e.g., diabetes, chronic kidney disease, heart failure, 
636 
cardiovascular disease) and interactions with different patterns of medication use (e.g., 
637 
diuretics, antihypertensives).  
638 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
18 
 
Rationale: If there is systematic error in sodium assessment because individuals with various 
639 
co-morbidities who are taking medications systematically do not provide accurate urine 
640 
collections, paradoxical findings between sodium and health outcomes may be observed. 
641 
 
642 
3. Examine the effect of behavioral interventions, with novel approaches (e.g., flavorful recipes, 
643 
cooking techniques) on adherence to dietary sodium recommendations.  
644 
Rationale: For decades, the population has exceeded dietary sodium intake 
645 
recommendations. A public health approach that results in reformulation of commercially 
646 
processed foods to lower sodium content should be the primary strategy for decreasing 
647 
sodium intake in the U.S. population. However, individual support for public health policies 
648 
will be needed to further document demand for changes in the sodium food environment. To 
649 
this end, interventions that modify individual knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors around 
650 
sodium intake should be evaluated. 
651 
 
652 
4. Examine the effect of low sodium intake on taste preferences for sodium and healthy dietary 
653 
patterns.  
654 
Rationale: It has been argued that populations desire higher levels of sodium intake and will 
655 
inevitably revert to higher levels of sodium intakes after acute reductions in sodium intake. It 
656 
has also been argued that after six weeks of reduced sodium intake, taste preferences are 
657 
modified such that higher sodium is no longer desirable. Studies are needed to elucidate the 
658 
effects of lowering sodium intake on diet preferences. 
659 
 
660 
5. Document the relationship between portion size and sodium intake.  
661 
Rationale: These data are needed to inform whether dietary recommendations for sodium 
662 
should be adjusted for caloric intake. It is known that the absolute amount of sodium intake is 
663 
highly correlated with caloric intake. As a result, the absolute recommended amount of 
664 
sodium is harder to achieve for a larger, high energy consuming person than for a smaller, 
665 
low energy consuming person. The science to inform whether sodium density confers 
666 
different risk than absolute intake of sodium is limited because of methodologic limitations 
667 
in surveys where both calories and sodium intake can be calculated. Furthermore, the 
668 
existing correlation between sodium and calories may be an artifact of the current food 
669 
supply. 
670 
 
671 
6. Determine the effects of replacement of saturated fat with different types of carbohydrates 
672 
(e.g., refined vs. whole grains) on cardiovascular disease risk.  
673 
Rationale: Most randomized controlled trials and prospective cohort studies compared 
674 
saturated fat with total carbohydrates. It is important to distinguish different types of 
675 
carbohydrates (e.g. refined vs. whole grains) in future studies.  
676 
 
677 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
19 
 
7. Examine the effects replacement of saturated fat with polyunsaturated fat vs. 
678 
monounsaturated fat on cardiovascular disease risk.  
679 
Rationale: Most existing studies have examined the effects of substituting PUFA for 
680 
saturated fat on cardiovascular disease risk. Future studies should also examine the potential 
681 
benefits of substituting monounsaturated from plant sources such as olive oil and nuts/seeds 
682 
for saturated fat on cardiovascular disease risk.  
683 
 
684 
8. Examine lipid and metabolic effects of specific oils modified to have different fatty acid 
685 
profiles (e.g. commodity soy oil (high linoleic acid) vs. high oleic soy oil).  
686 
Rationale: As more modified vegetables oils become commercially available, it is important 
687 
to assess their long-term health effects. In addition, future studies should examine lipid and 
688 
metabolic effects of plant oils that contain a mix of n-9, n-6, and n-3 fatty acids, as a 
689 
replacement for animal fat, on cardiovascular disease risk factors.  
690 
 
691 
9. Examine the effects of saturated fat from different sources, including animal products (e.g. 
692 
butter, lard), plant (e.g., palm vs. coconut oils), and production systems (e.g. refined 
693 
deodorized bleached vs. virgin coconut oil) on blood lipids and cardiovascular disease risk.  
694 
Rationale: Different sources of saturated fat contain different fatty acid profiles and thus, 
695 
may result in different lipid and metabolic effects. In addition, virgin and refined coconut oils 
696 
have different effects in animal models, but human data are lacking.  
697 
 
698 
10. Conduct gene-nutrient interaction studies by measuring genetic variations in relevant genes 
699 
that will enable evaluation of effects of specific diets for individualized nutrition 
700 
recommendations.  
701 
Rationale: Individuals with different genetic background may respond to the same dietary 
702 
intervention differently in terms of blood lipids and other cardiovascular disease risk factors. 
703 
Future studies should explore the potential role of genetic factors in modulating the effects of 
704 
fat type modification on health outcomes. 
705 
 
706 
11. Identify sources and names of added sugars and low-calorie sweeteners used in the food 
707 
supply and quantify their consumption levels and trends in the U.S. diet.  
708 
Rationale: It is unclear whether all food and nutrient databases capture all added sugars 
709 
because: 1) added sugars have varied and inconsistent nomenclature and may not be 
710 
recognized as added sugars in nutrient analyses; and 2) many foods with added sugars have 
711 
formulations considered proprietary by the manufacturers and for this reason actual added 
712 
sugars content is difficult to obtain. Accurate assessment of added sugars in the U.S. diet is 
713 
needed to quantify the population level exposure and subsequent health risks from added 
714 
sugars. The lack of information on the various added sugars in the food supply hinders efforts 
715 
to make policy about consumption. 
716 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
20 
 
 
717 
12. Conduct prospective research with strong experimental designs and multiple measurements 
718 
of the consumption of added sugars and low-calorie sweeteners on health outcomes, such as 
719 
body weight, adiposity, and clinical markers of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.  
720 
Rationale: High heterogeneity exists among published research with regard to the types and 
721 
forms of added sugars and low-calorie sweeteners-containing foods/beverages used for 
722 
interventions, which precludes assessing the effects of specific added sugars and low-calorie 
723 
sweeteners on body weight, adiposity, and cardio-metabolic health in adults and children. 
724 
Many studies use single baseline measurements of diet to reflect usual patterns and quantities 
725 
of intake over time. New research should emphasize assessments within the context of usual 
726 
dietary intakes and patterns of food and beverage consumption in free-living populations, 
727 
along with specific added sugars and low-calorie sweeteners, especially those that are 
728 
currently understudied. Large prospective studies with repeated measurements of low-calorie 
729 
sweeteners are needed to monitor their long-term effects on cancer and other health 
730 
outcomes. 
731 
 
732 
13. Design studies that emphasize assessments of relationships between the intakes of added 
733 
sugars and low-calorie sweeteners and body weight, adiposity, and cardio-metabolic health in 
734 
diverse sub-populations who are at high risk of obesity and related morbidities. 
735 
Rationale: Insufficient evidence exists to assess the impact of added sugars and low-calorie 
736 
sweeteners contained in foods and beverages on individuals from diverse populations who 
737 
have high risk for adverse health outcomes. These include (but are not limited to) different 
738 
race/ethnicity groups; low income groups, especially those with food insecurity; groups who 
739 
live in specific geographic locations with high prevalence of obesity (e.g. inner city, rural, 
740 
and Southern regions of the United States); and age and sex groups (women, children, and 
741 
elderly adults). 
742 
 
743 
14. Assess and improve approaches and policies to reduce the amount of added sugars in the 
744 
food and beverage supply as well as in school and community settings. 
745 
Rationale: Results from this research would assist policy makers and the private sector in 
746 
establishing sustainable approaches and policies to limit the availability and consumption of 
747 
added sugars. These approaches and policies would also be important for multi-component 
748 
strategies to improve weight control and health among people living in the United States. 
749 
 
750 
15. Conduct consumer research to identify and test elements of a standardized, easily understood 
751 
front-of-package label. 
752 
Rationale: Research is needed to provide an evidence base to support the need and identify 
753 
critical elements of a front of package label. This is particularly important to support the 
754 
Food and Drug Administration in implementing a front-of-package labeling system. 
755 

 
Appendix E‐1: Needs for Future Research 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
21 
 
CHAPTER 7: PHYSICAL ACTIVITY  
756 
1. Evaluate best practices in programming at the community and national level and identify 
757 
which local and national policies in the public and private sector have demonstrated the 
758 
greatest effect on increasing physical activity participation across the lifespan, especially in 
759 
populations with the greatest health disparities.  
760 
Rationale:  Physical activity participation rates are exceptionally low across all age groups, 
761 
and are especially low in individuals with the greatest health disparities. Many different 
762 
initiatives are currently underway in the private and public sector to help increase physical 
763 
activity on a population level. Understanding which programs and policies are having the 
764 
greatest impact will help focus valuable resources and national recommendations for 
765 
maximum public health benefit. 
766 
 
767 
2. Identify the dose of physical activity needed to achieve health benefits, as well as appropriate 
768 
growth and development, for children younger than age 6 years.  
769 
Rationale: Until recently, very little effort has been focused on understanding the health 
770 
benefits of physical activity for young children. Given that this is a critical age of growth and 
771 
development, considerable research should be focused on this age group. 
772 
 
773 
3. Evaluate the effects of various modes and doses of physical activity on health outcomes in 
774 
older adults. 
775 
Rationale:  Older adults are the fastest growing segment of the population. They also have 
776 
the greatest burden of disease and functional (mental and physical) limitations. To reduce 
777 
burden of disease and related economic impacts, research regarding mode and dose of 
778 
physical activity should be focused on this age group. 
779 
 
780 
4. Further evaluate the importance of light activity, short bouts of physical activity (i.e., 10-
781 
minutes or less) and modes of activity on health outcomes across the lifespan.  
782 
Rationale:  The review of the evidence in the 2008 PAGAC Report focused primarily on 
783 
moderate- and vigorous-intensity activity. Emerging research highlights the positive effects 
784 
of light activity as well as shorter bouts of vigorous activity on health outcomes. 
785 
Understanding the health impact of the full range of mode, intensity, duration, frequency, and 
786 
setting will help to further refine the PAG to support maximum public health benefit. 
787 
 
788 
5. Further investigate the effects of sedentary behaviors on health outcomes, including duration, 
789 
frequency, and mode of sedentary activities. 
790 
Rationale: Increasing evidence demonstrates the negative health consequences of sedentary 
791 
behaviors. Clarity on the types and duration of sedentary behaviors that have the most 
792 
negative health impact would help to identify meaningful evidence-based public health 
793 
recommendations. 
794 

 
Appendix E-2: Supplementary Documentation 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Appendix E-2: Supplementary Documentation to the 2015 
1 
DGAC Report 
2 
The 2015 DGAC used a variety of scientifically rigorous approaches to address its science-based 
3 
questions. These approaches are described in Part C. Methodology. Slightly more than one-third 
4 
of the questions were answered using a state-of-the-art systematic review process, and these 
5 
reviews are publically available in the Nutrition Evidence Library (NEL) at www.NEL.gov.  
6 
 
7 
The remaining questions were answered using existing sources of evidence (including systematic 
8 
reviews, meta-analyses, or reports), data analyses, and food pattern modeling analyses. These 
9 
three approaches allowed the Committee to ask and answer its questions in a systematic, 
10 
transparent, and evidence-based way.  
11 
 
12 
Appendix E-2 provides a list of supplementary documentation related to the existing sources of 
13 
evidence and data analyses used by the Committee in evidence reviews (see Appendix E-3 for 
14 
USDA Food Patterns for Special Analyses). These sources are publically available online 
15 
through active links within this document at www.DietaryGuidelines.gov.  
16

Appendix E-3: USDA Food Patterns for Special Analyses 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Appendix E-3: USDA Food Patterns for Special Analyses 
1 
The 2015 DGAC identified specific questions that they felt could best be addressed through 
2 
a food pattern modeling approach, using the USDA Food Patterns and the modeling process 
3 
developed to address similar requests by the 2005 and 2010 DGACs. The approach used for the 
4 
2015 DGAC food pattern modeling questions is described in Part C: Methodology.  
5 
Seven modeling analyses requested by the Committee were completed by staff working 
6 
closely with Subcommittee 1 members, and provided as reports for the full Committee to 
7 
consider. The food pattern modeling analyses conducted for the 2015 DGAC are listed below. 
8 
Full reports for each analysis are available online through active links within this document at 
9 
www.DietaryGuidelines.gov.  
10 
 
11 
E-3.1 
Adequacy of USDA Food Patterns 
How well do updated USDA food intake patterns meet IOM Dietary Reference Intakes 
and 2010 Dietary Guidelines nutrient recommendations?  How do the recommended 
amounts of food groups compare to current distributions of usual intakes for the 
American population? 
E-3.2 
Food Group Contributions to Nutrients in USDA Food Patterns and Current 
Nutrient Intakes 
What is the contribution of whole grain foods and fruits and vegetables to (1) total fiber 
intake and (2) total nutrient intake in the USDA Food Patterns? What is the 
contribution of fruits and vegetables to current nutrient intake (focus on nutrients of 
concern, including fiber)? 
E-3.3 
Meeting Vitamin D Recommended Intakes in USDA Food Patterns 
Can vitamin D EARs and/or RDAs be met with careful food choices following 
recommended amounts from each food group in the USDA Food Patterns?  How 
restricted would food choices be, and how much of the vitamin D would need to come 
from fortified food products? 
E-3.4 
USDA Food Patterns—Adequacy for Young Children 
How well do the USDA Food Patterns meet the nutritional needs of children 2 to 5 
years of age and how do the recommended amounts compare to their current intakes?  
Given the relatively small empty calorie limit for this age group, how much flexibility 
is possible in food choices?  
 
 
12 

 
Appendix E-3: USDA Food Patterns for Special Analyses 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
E-3.5 
Reducing Saturated Fats in the USDA Food Patterns 
What would be the effect on food choices and overall nutrient adequacy of limiting 
saturated fatty acids to 6 percent of total calories by substituting mono- and 
polyunsaturated fatty acids? 
E-3.6 
Dairy Group and Alternatives 
What would be the impact on the adequacy of the patterns if (1) no Dairy foods were 
consumed, (2) if calcium was obtained from nondairy sources (including fortified 
foods), and (3) if the proportions of milk and yogurt to cheese were modified?   
What is the relationship between changes in types of beverages consumed (milk 
compared with sugar-sweetened beverages) and diet quality? 
E-3.7 
Developing Vegetarian and Mediterranean-style Food Patterns 
Using the Food Pattern Modeling process, can healthy eating patterns for vegetarians 
and for those who want to follow a Mediterranean-style diet be developed?  How do 
these patterns differ from the USDA Food Patterns previously updated for the 2015 
DGAs? 
 
13 
 
14 

 
Appendix E-4: NHANES Data Used in DGAC Data Analyses 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Appendix E-4: NHANES Data Used in DGAC Data Analyses 
1 
 
2 
Most of the DGAC data analyses used the National Health and Nutrition Examination 
3 
(NHANES) data and its dietary component, What We Eat in America (WWEIA), NHANES 
4 
(Zipf et al., 2013). These data were used to answer questions about food and nutrient intakes 
5 
because they provide national and group level estimates of dietary intakes of the U.S. population 
6 
on a given day as well as usual intake distributions. These data contributed substantially to 
7 
questions answered using data analyses.  This appendix describes the NHANES data in greater 
8 
detail. 
9 
 
10 
NHANES 
11 
NHANES consists of ongoing, comprehensive, cross-sectional, population-based surveys 
12 
designed to collect data on health, nutritional status, and health behaviors of the non-
13 
institutionalized civilian population living in households in the United States. It is conducted by 
14 
the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) of the Centers for Disease Control and 
15 
Prevention (CDC). NHANES has had a long history starting in the early 1960s (Zipf et al., 
16 
2013); it has been monitoring food and nutrient intake and nutritional status of the U.S. 
17 
population since 1971, starting with NHANES I. Since then, several cycles of NHANES have 
18 
been conducted as a series of cross-sectional surveys focusing on different population groups in 
19 
terms of age and race/ethnicity, or health topics. In 1999, NHANES became a continuous survey, 
20 
sampling U.S. residents of all ages, with a changing focus on a variety of health and nutrition 
21 
measurements to meet emerging needs. The goals of the continuous NHANES are to provide 
22 
prevalence data on selected diseases and risk factors for the U.S. population; to monitor trends in 
23 
selected diseases, behaviors, and environmental exposures; to explore emerging public health 
24 
needs; and to maintain a national probability sample of baseline information on health and 
25 
nutritional status of the U.S. household population (Zipf et al., 2013).  
26 
 
27 
NHANES has a complex, multi-stage, probability sampling design and examines a nationally 
28 
representative sample of about 5,000 persons each year. In NHANES, certain subgroups have 
29 
been periodically oversampled. These include low income, older Americans, infants and 
30 
children, pregnant women and certain race/ethnic groups (e.g., Hispanics, including Mexican 
31 
Americans, African Americans, and more recently, Asian Americans). The NHANES survey is 
32 
unique because it combines personal interviews with standardized physical examinations and 
33 
laboratory tests administered by a specially trained staff that travels with the Mobile 
34 
Examination Center (MEC) to survey sites selected to represent the U.S. population (Zipf et al., 
35 
2013).  
36 
 
37 

 
Appendix E-4: NHANES Data Used in DGAC Data Analyses 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
In the continuous NHANES, dietary intake is assessed through two 24-hr recalls, administered 
38 
by trained dietary interviewers using the USDA’s Automated Multiple Pass Method (AMPM) 
39 
(Blanton et al., 2006) through What We Eat in America (WWEIA). The first 24-hr recall (day 1) 
40 
is collected in-person at the MEC and a subsequent 24-hr recall (day 2) is obtained 7 to 10 days 
41 
later over the telephone. Information on dietary supplements consumed during the 24-hour recall 
42 
period is also collected. The strengths of the WWEIA, NHANES dietary data include that 
43 
because two 24-hour recalls are available in WWEIA, NHANES (from 2003 onwards), usual 
44 
intake distributions can be estimated based on statistical techniques that reduce the effect of 
45 
intra-individual variation in food and nutrient intakes in 24-hour recalls (Nusser et al. 1996; 
46 
Tooze et al. 2006; Dodd et al. 2006).   
47 
 
48 
The WWEIA, NHANES dietary data are one of the few sources that can provide national 
49 
estimates of total nutrient intake from diet and dietary supplements for the U.S. population. 
50 
Moreover, dietary intakes can be described by specific socio-demographic groups including 
51 
race/ethnic groups, income status, and participation in Federal nutrition assistance programs 
52 
(e.g., Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program). Dietary data from WWEIA, NHANES can 
53 
be linked to thorough anthropometric, laboratory, and clinical evaluation data as well health 
54 
outcomes to examine cross-sectional associations at the national and large subgroup levels.  It 
55 
must be recognized that WWEIA, NHANES dietary data are not designed for individual-level 
56 
assessment. These data can be useful to inform nutrition policy, but not sufficient by themselves 
57 
to form policy recommendations.   
58 
 
59 
No single perfect method for assessing dietary intake information is available in surveys (Willett 
60 
1998; Gibson 2005; Berdainer et al., 2008) and different methods may be indicated for specific 
61 
purposes (Willett 1998; Beaton et al., 1983; Berdainer et al., 2008). NCHS has been actively 
62 
involved in researching and reviewing its data collection methods, including dietary data, over 
63 
the years internally and in consultation with expert groups (Wright et al., 1994; Briefel & 
64 
Sempos, 1992). The methods used in NHANES are adapted in light of its large sample size and 
65 
complex design, cost and feasibility, and respondent burden to ensure a high response rate to 
66 
derive nationally representative estimates. Some examples of adaptations in methods include the 
67 
transition to USDA’s standardized automated multi-pass method for collection of dietary recalls 
68 
by trained interviewers that has been evaluated and associated with reduced measurement error 
69 
(Moshfegh et al., 2008). Other examples include collection of an additional 24-hour dietary 
70 
recall in NHANES since 2003 (for a total of two 24-hour recalls), coupled with targeted food 
71 
frequency questionnaires over various NHANES cycles.  
72 
 
73 
The strengths and shortcomings of these dietary assessment methods have been discussed over 
74 
time in various meetings (e.g., International Conference on Diet and Activity Methods and 
75 
American Society for Nutrition/Experimental Biology), workshops, and expert groups.  This has 
76 
also been discussed for several years in the scientific literature (Beaton 1994; Berdainer et al., 
77 

 
Appendix E-4: NHANES Data Used in DGAC Data Analyses 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
3 
 
2008) and in recent articles (Archer et al., 2013; Hébert et al., 2014; Webb, 2013). No 
78 
assessment method is perfect and the choice of dietary method is based on the purpose for which 
79 
it is intended. For NHANES, repeated 24-hour recalls remain the backbone of dietary assessment 
80 
and monitoring. These data are useful in providing national- and group-level estimates of dietary 
81 
intakes of the U.S. population, on a given day as well as in describing usual intake distributions 
82 
using appropriate statistical approaches, to inform nutrition policy. 
83 
 
84 
  
85
