Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms  
1 
 
2 
Aquaculture—The farming of aquatic organisms, including fish, mollusks, crustaceans, and 
3 
aquatic plants. Farming includes activities to enhance production, such as regular stocking, 
4 
feeding, and protection from predators.  
5 
Acculturation—The process by which immigrants adopt the attitudes, values, customs, beliefs, 
6 
and behaviors of a new culture. Acculturation is the gradual exchange between immigrants’ 
7 
original attitudes and behavior and those of the host culture. 
8 
Added sugars—Sugars that are either added during the processing of foods, or are packaged as 
9 
such. They include sugars (free, mono- and disaccharides), syrups, naturally occurring sugars 
10 
that are isolated from a whole food and concentrated so that sugar is the primary component 
11 
(e.g., fruit juice concentrates), and other caloric sweeteners. Names for added sugars include: 
12 
Brown sugar, corn sweetener, corn syrup, dextrose, fructose, fruit juice concentrates, glucose, 
13 
high-fructose corn syrup, honey, invert sugar, lactose, maltose, malt sugar, molasses, raw sugar, 
14 
turbinado sugar, trehalose, and sucrose. 
15 
Behavioral weight-management program—A structured, multi-component program that 
16 
encompasses a number of behavior changes, including diet and physical activity with the intent 
17 
to improve weight (lose weight or maintain weight loss). 
18 
Biodiversity—The variety and variability among living organisms and the ecosystems in which 
19 
they occur. Biodiversity includes the numbers of different items and their relative frequencies; 
20 
these items are organized at many levels, ranging from complete ecosystems to the biochemical 
21 
structures that are the molecular basis of heredity. Thus, biodiversity expresses the relative 
22 
abundance of different ecosystems, species, and genes. 
23 
Body mass index (BMI)—A measure defining weight in kilograms (kg) divided by  height in 
24 
meters (m) squared. BMI is an indicator of deficient or excess body tissue, both fat and muscle.  
25 
BMI status categories include underweight, normal weight, overweight, and obese. (Normal 
26 
weight is often referred to as “healthy” weight.) Overweight and obese describe ranges of weight 
27 
that are greater than what is considered healthy for a given height, while underweight describes a 
28 
weight that is lower than what is considered healthy. Because children and adolescents are 
29 
growing, their BMI is plotted on growth charts for sex and age. The percentile indicates the 
30 
relative position of the child's BMI among children of the same sex and age. This is generally 
31 
referred to as a BMI z-score. 
32 
 
 
33 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
Children and Adolescents (ages 2 to 19 years) Adults 
Underweight 
Less than the 5th percentile 
Less than 18.5 kg/m2 
Normal weight 
5th percentile to less than the 85th percentile 
18.5 to 24.9 kg/m2 
Overweight 
85th to less than the 95th percentile 
25.0 to 29.9 kg/m2 
Obese  
Equal to or greater than the 95th percentile  
Obese class I 
30.0 to 34.9 kg/m2 
Obese class II 
35.0 to 39.9 kg/m2 
Obese class III 
40.0 kg/m2 and greater 
 
34 
Built environment—The physical form of communities, including urban design (i.e., how a city 
35 
is designed; its physical appearance and arrangement), land use patterns (i.e., how land is used 
36 
for commercial, residential, and other activities), and the transportation system (i.e., the facilities 
37 
and services that link one location to another).  
38 
Calorie—A unit commonly used to measure energy content or energy use. It is used as a 
39 
convenient measure to relate the energy content of food to the energy needs of the body. A 
40 
calorie is equal to the amount of energy required to raise the temperature of one liter of water 1 
41 
degree centigrade. Energy, as measured in calories, is required to sustain the body’s various 
42 
functions, including metabolic processes and physical activity. Carbohydrate, fat, protein, and 
43 
alcohol provide all of the energy supplied by foods and beverages.  
44 
Carbohydrates—One of the three classes of macronutrients. Carbohydrates include sugars, 
45 
starches, and fibers: 
46 
• 
Sugars—A simple carbohydrate composed of one unit (a monosaccharide, such as 
47 
glucose and fructose) or two joined units (a disaccharide, such as lactose and sucrose). 
48 
Sugars include white and brown sugar, fruit sugar, corn syrup, molasses, and honey. (See 
49 
Added sugars) 
50 
• 
Starches—Many glucose units linked together. Examples of foods containing starch 
51 
include vegetables, dry beans and peas, and grains (e.g., brown rice, oats, wheat, barley, 
52 
corn). 
53 
• 
Fiber—Nondigestible carbohydrates and lignin that are intrinsic and intact in plants. 
54 
Fiber consists of dietary fiber, the fiber naturally occurring in foods, and functional fiber, 
55 
which are isolated, nondigestible carbohydrates that have beneficial physiological effects 
56 
in humans. 
57 
Child-care settings—Locations that include child-care centers and child-care provided in 
58 
homes. Early childhood education settings, such as preschool and Head Start programs, also are 
59 
included.  
60 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
3 
 
Competitive foods—Foods and beverages offered at schools that are sold or offered outside of 
61 
the Federally reimbursed school lunch and breakfast programs. Competitive foods include food 
62 
and beverage items sold through à la carte lines, snack bars, student stores, vending machines, 
63 
and school fundraisers.  
64 
Comprehensive lifestyle intervention—Interventions that are designed to address chronic 
65 
disease risk factors and improve health. They generally include three principal components—a  
66 
diet component, a  physical activity component, and a program of behavior change to facilitate 
67 
adherence to diet and physical activity recommendations.  
68 
Comprehensive lifestyle intervention team—A multidisciplinary team of highly trained 
69 
professionals, including registered dietitians and nutritionists, exercise specialists, and 
70 
behaviorists who work with individuals on weight loss or other lifestyle behavior change to 
71 
improve health and reduce chronic disease risk. (See Interventionist) 
72 
Cross-contamination—The spread of bacteria, viruses, or other harmful agents from one 
73 
surface to another. 
74 
Cup equivalent (cup eq)—The amount of a food product that is considered equal to 1 cup from 
75 
the vegetable, fruit, or milk food group. A cup eq for some foods may differ from a measured 
76 
cup in volume because (1)  the foods have been concentrated (such as raisins or tomato paste), 
77 
(2)  the foods  are airy in their raw form and do not compress well into a cup (such as salad 
78 
greens), or (3) the foods are measured in a different form (such as cheese). 
79 
Dietary pattern—The quantities, proportions, variety or combinations of different food and 
80 
beverages in diets, and the frequency with which they are habitually consumed. 
81 
Dietary Reference Intakes (DRIs)—A set of nutrient-based reference values that expand upon 
82 
and replace the former Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDAs) in the United States and the 
83 
Recommended Nutrient Intakes (RNIs) in Canada. They include the values shown in the graphic 
84 
(http://www.dsld.nlm.nih.gov/dsld/dri.jsp) and described here: 
85 
 
86 
 
87 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
4 
 
• 
Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Ranges (AMDR)—Range of intake for a 
88 
particular energy source that is associated with reduced risk of chronic disease while 
89 
providing intakes of essential nutrients. If an individual’s intake is outside of the AMDR, 
90 
there is a potential of increasing the risk of chronic diseases and/or insufficient intakes of 
91 
essential nutrients. 
92 
• 
Adequate Intakes (AI)—A recommended average daily nutrient intake level based on 
93 
observed or experimentally determined approximations or estimates of mean nutrient 
94 
intake by a group (or groups) of apparently healthy people. This is used when the 
95 
Recommended Dietary Allowance cannot be determined. 
96 
• 
Estimated Average Requirements (EAR)—The average daily nutrient intake level 
97 
estimated to meet the requirement of half the healthy individuals in a particular life stage 
98 
and sex group. 
99 
• 
Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA)—The average dietary intake level that is 
100 
sufficient to meet the nutrient requirement of nearly all (97 to 98 percent) healthy 
101 
individuals in a particular life stage and sex group. 
102 
• 
Tolerable Upper Intake Level (UL)—The highest average daily nutrient intake level 
103 
likely to pose no risk of adverse health effects for nearly all individuals in a particular life 
104 
stage and gender group. As intake increases above the UL, the potential risk of adverse 
105 
health effects increases. 
106 
Eating out—A behavior that includes meals eaten outside of the home at a variety of venues and 
107 
takeout or ready-to-eat meals purchased and consumed either away from or in the home. 
108 
Empty calories—The calories from components of a food or beverage that contribute few or no 
109 
nutrients. Major sources of empty calories are solid fats and added sugars. Other sources of 
110 
empty calories include refined starches (e.g., corn starch, potato starch) and alcohol. In some 
111 
foods, such as soda and many candies, all the calories are empty calories. However, empty 
112 
calories also can be found in foods that contain important nutrients. For example, whole milk 
113 
contains solid fats (butterfat) and sweetened applesauce contains added sugars, which means that 
114 
some of their calories are empty calories. 
115 
Energy drink—A beverage that contains caffeine as a major active ingredient, along with other 
116 
ingredients, such as taurine, herbal supplements, vitamins, and sugar. It is usually marketed as a 
117 
product that can improve perceived energy, stamina, athletic performance, or concentration.  
118 
Enrichment—The addition of specific nutrients (iron, thiamin, riboflavin, and niacin) to refined 
119 
grain products in order to replace losses of the nutrients that occur during processing. 
120 
Environmental sustainability—Long-term maintenance of ecosystem components and 
121 
functions for future generations. 
122 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
5 
 
Existing reports—Previously published reports or articles that were used as sources of evidence 
123 
to answer some questions posed by the 2015 DGAC. These sources included reports (e.g., the 
124 
2013 American College of Cardiology/ American Heart Association (ACC/AHA) Guidelines on 
125 
Lifestyle Management to Reduce Cardiovascular Risk), systematic reviews, and meta-analyses. 
126 
(See Meta-analysis) 
127 
Fast food—Foods designed for ready availability, use or consumption and sold at eating 
128 
establishments for quick availability or take-out. Fast food restaurants are also known as quick- 
129 
service restaurants. 
130 
Fats—One of the three classes of macronutrients. (See Solid Fats and Oils) 
131 
• 
Monounsaturated Fatty Acids—Monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFAs) have one 
132 
double bond. Plant sources that are rich in MUFAs include nuts and vegetable oils that 
133 
are liquid at room temperature (e.g., canola oil, olive oil, high oleic safflower and 
134 
sunflower oils). 
135 
• 
Polyunsaturated fatty acids—Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) have two or more 
136 
double bonds and may be of two types, based on the position of the first double bond.  
137 
o n-6 PUFAs—Linoleic acid, one of the n-6 fatty acids, is required because it cannot be 
138 
synthesized by humans and, therefore, is considered essential in the diet. Primary 
139 
sources are nuts and liquid vegetable oils, including soybean oil, corn oil, and 
140 
safflower oil. Also called omega-6 fatty acids. 
141 
o n-3 PUFAs—Alpha-linolenic acid is an n-3 fatty acid that is required because it 
142 
cannot be synthesized by humans and, therefore, is considered essential in the diet. 
143 
Primary sources include soybean oil, canola oil, walnuts, and flaxseed. 
144 
Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) are very long chain 
145 
n-3 fatty acids that are contained in fish and shellfish. Also called omega-3 fatty 
146 
acids. 
147 
• 
Saturated fatty acids—Saturated fatty acids have no double bonds. Major sources 
148 
include animal products such as meat and dairy products, and tropical oils such as 
149 
coconut or palm oils. In general, fats high in saturated fatty acids are solid at room 
150 
temperature. 
151 
• 
trans fatty acids—Trans fatty acids are unsaturated fatty acids that contain one or more 
152 
isolated (i.e., nonconjugated) double bonds in a trans configuration. Sources of trans 
153 
fatty acids include partially-hydrogenated vegetable oils that have been used to make 
154 
traditional shortening and some commercially prepared baked goods, snack foods, fried 
155 
foods, and traditional stick margarine. Trans fatty acids also are present in foods that 
156 
come from ruminant animals (e.g., cattle and sheep) and are called “natural” or rTFA. 
157 
Such foods include dairy products, beef, and lamb. 
158 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
6 
 
Fight Bac!®—A national public education campaign to promote food safety to consumers and 
159 
educate them on how to handle and prepare food safely. In this campaign, pathogens are 
160 
represented by a cartoonlike bacteria character named “BAC.”  For more information, visit: 
161 
http://www.fightbac.org.  
162 
Fishery—An activity leading to harvesting of fish. It may involve capture of wild fish or the 
163 
raising of fish through aquaculture. 
164 
Food access—Accessibility to sources of healthy food, as measured by distance to a store or the 
165 
number of stores in an area; individual-level resources such as family-income or vehicle 
166 
availability; and neighborhood-level indicators of resources, such as average income of the 
167 
neighborhood and the availability of public transportation. 
168 
Food categories—A method of grouping similar foods in their as-consumed forms, for 
169 
descriptive purposes. The USDA/ARS has created 150 mutually exclusive food categories to 
170 
account for each food or beverage item reported in What We Eat in America (WWEIA), the food 
171 
intake survey component of the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (for more 
172 
information, visit: http://seprl.ars.usda.gov/Services/docs.htm?docid=23429). Examples of 
173 
WWEIA Food Categories include soups, nachos, and yeast breads. In contrast to food groups, 
174 
items are not disaggregated into their component parts for assignment to food categories. For 
175 
example, all pizzas are put into the pizza category. 
176 
 
177 
Food environments—Factors and conditions that influence food choices and food availability. 
178 
These environments include settings such as home, child care (early care and education), school, 
179 
after-school programs, worksites, food retail stores and restaurants, and other outlets where 
180 
children and their families make eating and drinking decisions. The food environment also 
181 
includes macro-level factors and includes food marketing, food production and distribution 
182 
systems, agricultural policies, Federal nutrition assistance programs, and economic price 
183 
structures. 
184 
Food groups—A method of grouping similar foods for descriptive and guidance purposes. Food 
185 
groups in the USDA Food Pattern are defined as fruits, vegetables, grains, dairy, and protein 
186 
foods.  Some of these groups are divided into subgroups, such as dark-green vegetables or whole 
187 
grains, which may have intake goals or limits (for more information, see Appendix E3.1 Table 
188 
A1. USDA Healthy U.S.-Style Food Patterns—Intake Amounts). For assignment to food groups, 
189 
mixed dishes are disaggregated into their major component parts. For example, pizza may be 
190 
disaggregated into the grain (crust), dairy (cheese), vegetable (sauce and toppings), and protein 
191 
foods (toppings) food groups. 
192 
Food pattern modeling—The process of developing and adjusting daily intake amounts from 
193 
food categories or groups to meet specific criteria, such as meeting nutrient intake goals, limiting 
194 
nutrients or other food components, or varying proportions or amounts of specific food 
195 
categories or groups. 
196 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
7 
 
Food policies—Regulations, laws, policy-making actions or formal or informal rules established 
197 
by formal organizations or government units. Food and nutrition policies are those that influence 
198 
the food environment and eating behavior to improve eating and body weight.  
199 
Food security—A condition in which all people, now and in the future, have access to sufficient, 
200 
safe, and nutritious food to maintain a healthy and active life. (See Household food insecurity) 
201 
Fortification—The addition of one or more essential nutrients to a food whether or not it is 
202 
normally contained in the food for the purpose of preventing or correcting a demonstrated 
203 
deficiency of one or more nutrients in the population or specific population groups. 
204 
Greenhouse gases (GHG)—Any gas that absorbs infrared radiation in the atmosphere. 
205 
Greenhouse gases include carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, ozone, chlorofluorocarbons, 
206 
hydrochlorofluorocarbons, hydrofluorocarbons, perfluorocarbons, and sulfur hexafluoride. 
207 
Health—A state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely the absence 
208 
of disease or infirmity. 
209 
Household food insecurity—Circumstances in which the availability of nutritionally adequate 
210 
and safe food, or the ability to acquire acceptable foods in socially acceptable ways, is limited or 
211 
uncertain.  
212 
• 
Persistent household food insecurity—Occurs when people are unable to meet their 
213 
minimum food requirements over a sustained period of time. 
214 
• 
Progressing household food insecurity—A change in situation from food secure to food 
215 
insecure or from acute or temporary food insecurity to persistent food insecurity.  
216 
• 
Household food insufficiency—A similar measure to food insecurity considered more 
217 
severe than the concept of food security, although not as severe as hunger.  
218 
Interventionist—Trained health professionals (e.g., registered dietitians, psychologists, exercise 
219 
physiologists, health counselors, or professionals in training) who adhere to formal protocols in 
220 
providing healthy lifestyles counseling and treatment, such as for weight management. In a few 
221 
cases, lay persons are used as trained interventionists; they received instruction in protocols 
222 
(designed by health professionals) for programs that have been validated in high-quality trials 
223 
and published in peer-reviewed journals. 
224 
Isocaloric—Having the same caloric values. For example, two dietary patterns that vary in 
225 
macronutrient proportions but have the same calorie content are isocaloric. 
226 
Lean meat—Any meat with less than 10% fat by weight, or less than 10 grams of fat per 100 
227 
grams, based on USDA and FDA definitions for food label use. Examples include 95% lean 
228 
ground beef, cooked; broiled beef steak, lean only eaten; baked pork chop, lean only eaten; 
229 
roasted chicken breast or leg, no skin eaten; and smoked/cured ham, lean only eaten.  
230 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
8 
 
 
231 
Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)—A technique for assessing the biophysical environmental 
232 
aspects and potential impacts associated with a product, by: 
233 
• 
Compiling an inventory of relevant inputs and outputs of a product system; 
234 
• 
Evaluating the potential environmental impacts associated with those inputs and outputs; 
235 
• 
Interpreting the results of the inventory analysis and impace assessment phases in relation 
236 
to the objectives of the study. 
237 
LCA studies the environmental aspects and potential impacts throughout a product’s life (i.e., 
238 
cradle to grave), from raw material acquisition through production, use, and disposal. The 
239 
general categories of environmental impacts needing consideration include resource use, human 
240 
health, and ecological consequences. 
241 
Macronutrient—A dietary component that provides energy. Macronutrients include protein, 
242 
fats, and carbohydrates. Alcohol also provides energy but, for purposes of the DGAC report, it is 
243 
not considered when discussing macronutrients. 
244 
Meta-analysis—The statistical analysis of multiple individual studies for the purpose of 
245 
integrating the findings and deriving conclusions from the body of literature.    
246 
Mobile Health (mHealth)—The use of mobile and wireless technologies to support the 
247 
achievement of health objectives. 
248 
Moderate alcohol consumption—Average daily consumption of up to one drink per day for 
249 
women and up to two drinks per day for men, with no more than three drinks in any single day 
250 
for women and no more than four drinks in any single day for men. One drink is defined as 12 fl. 
251 
oz. of regular beer, 5 fl. oz. of wine, or 1.5 fl. oz. of distilled spirits. 
252 
Nutrient-dense foods—Foods that are naturally rich in vitamins, minerals, and other substances 
253 
that may have positive health effects, and are lean or low in solid fats and without added solid 
254 
fats, sugars, starches, or sodium and that retain naturally-occurring components such as fiber. All 
255 
vegetables, fruits, whole grains, fish, eggs, and nuts prepared without added solid fats or sugars 
256 
are considered nutrient-dense, as are lean or low-fat forms of fluid milk, meat, and poultry 
257 
prepared without added solid fats or sugars. Nutrient-dense foods provide substantial amounts of 
258 
vitamins and minerals (micronutrients) and relatively few calories compared to forms of the food 
259 
that have solid fat and/or added sugars. 
260 
Nutrition Evidence Library (NEL) systematic review—A process that uses state-of-the-art 
261 
methodology to search, evaluate, and synthesize food and nutrition-related research. This 
262 
rigorous, protocol-driven methodology is designed to minimize bias, maximize transparency, and 
263 
ensure relevant, timely, and high-quality systematic reviews to inform Federal nutrition-related 
264 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
9 
 
policies, programs, and recommendations. The NEL is a division of the USDA Center for 
265 
Nutrition Policy and Promotion. For more detailed information, visit: www.nel.gov. 
266 
Oils—Fats that are liquid at room temperature. Oils come from many different plants and some 
267 
fish. Some common oils include canola, corn, olive, peanut, safflower, soybean, and sunflower 
268 
oils. A number of foods are naturally high in oils, such as: nuts, olives, some fish, and avocados. 
269 
Foods that are mainly made up of oil include mayonnaise, certain salad dressings, and soft (tub 
270 
or squeeze) margarine with no trans fats. Oils are high in monounsaturated or polyunsaturated 
271 
fats, and lower in saturated fats than solid fats. A few plant oils, termed tropical oils, including 
272 
coconut oil, palm oil and palm kernel oil, are high in saturated fats and for nutritional purposes 
273 
should be considered as solid fats. Partially-hydrogenated oils that contain trans fats should also 
274 
be considered as solid fats for nutritional purposes. (See Fats) 
275 
Ounce equivalent (oz eq)—The amount of a food product that is considered equal to one ounce 
276 
from the grain or protein foods food group. An oz eq for some foods may be less than a 
277 
measured ounce in weight if the food is concentrated or low in water content (nuts, peanut butter, 
278 
dried meats, flour) or more than a measured ounce in weight if the food contains a large amount 
279 
of water (tofu, cooked beans, cooked rice or pasta). 
280 
Persistent organic pollutants (POPs)—Toxic chemicals that can adversely affect human health 
281 
and the biophysical environment. Because they can be transported by wind and water, most 
282 
POPs generated in one country may affect people and wildlife distant to where they are used and 
283 
released. They can persist for long periods of and can accumulate and pass from one species to 
284 
the next through the food chain. 
285 
Plant-based foods—Foods such as vegetables, fruits, whole grains, nuts and seeds. 
286 
Point-of-purchase—A place where sales are made. Various intervention strategies have been 
287 
proposed to affect individuals’ purchasing decisions at the point of purchase, such as board or 
288 
menu labeling with various amounts of nutrition information or shelf tags in grocery stores. 
289 
Portion size—The amount of a food served or consumed in one eating occasion. A portion is not 
290 
a standardized amount, and the amount considered to be a portion is subjective and varies. 
291 
Processed meat—Meat, poultry, or seafood products preserved by smoking, curing or salting, or 
292 
addition of chemical preservatives. Processed meat includes bacon, sausage, hot dogs, sandwich 
293 
meat, packaged ham, pepperoni, and salami. 
294 
Protein—One of the three macronutrients classes. Protein is the major functional and structural 
295 
component of every animal cell. Proteins are composed of amino acids, nine of which are 
296 
indispensable, meaning they cannot be synthesized by humans and therefore must be obtained 
297 
from the diet. The quality of dietary protein is determined by its amino acid profile relative to 
298 
human requirements as determined by the body's requirements for growth, maintenance, and 
299 
repair. Protein quality is determined by two factors: digestibility and amino acid composition. 
300 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
10 
 
• 
Animal protein—Protein from meat, poultry, seafood, eggs, and milk and milk products. 
301 
• 
Vegetable protein—Protein from plants such as dry beans, whole grains, fruit, nuts, and 
302 
seeds.  
303 
Refined grains—Grains and grain products missing the bran, germ, and/or endosperm; any grain 
304 
product that is not a whole grain. Many refined grains are low in fiber but enriched with thiamin, 
305 
riboflavin, niacin, and iron, and fortified with folic acid. 
306 
Screen time—Time in front of a computer, television, video or computer game system, or smart 
307 
phone or tablet or related device. 
308 
Seafood—Marine animals that live in the sea and in freshwater lakes and rivers. Seafood 
309 
includes fish, such as salmon, tuna, trout, and tilapia, and shellfish, such as shrimp, crab, and 
310 
oysters. 
311 
Sedentary behavior—Any waking activity predominantly done while in a sitting or reclining 
312 
posture. A behavior that expends energy at or minimally above a person’s resting level (between 
313 
1.0 and 1.5 metabolic equivalents), is considered sedentary behavior. 
314 
Self-monitoring—Self-monitoring refers to the process by which an individual observes and 
315 
records specific information about his or her behaviors. For example, in weight management 
316 
self-monitoring, observations and records would reflect dietary intake, physical activity, and/or 
317 
body weight. 
318 
Solid fats—Fats that are usually not liquid at room temperature. Solid fats are found in animal 
319 
foods except for seafood, and can be made from vegetable oils through hydrogenation. Some 
320 
tropical oil plants, such as coconut and palm, are considered as solid fats due to their fatty acid 
321 
composition. Solid fats contain more saturated fats and/or trans fats than liquid oils (e.g., 
322 
soybean, canola, and corn oils), with lower amounts of monounsaturated or polyunsaturated fatty 
323 
acids. Common fats considered to be solid fats include: butterfat, beef fat (tallow, suet), chicken 
324 
fat, pork fat (lard), stick margarine, shortening, coconut oil, palm oil and palm kernel oil. Foods 
325 
high in solid fats include: butter, full-fat cheeses, creams, whole milk, full fat ice creams, 
326 
marbled cuts of meats, regular ground beef, bacon, sausages, poultry skin, and many baked 
327 
goods made using these products (such as cookies, crackers, doughnuts, pastries, and 
328 
croissants).The fat component of milk and cream (butter) is solid at room temperature. (See 
329 
Fats) 
330 
Sugar-sweetened beverages—Liquids that are sweetened with various forms of added sugars 
331 
(see Added Sugars and Carbohydrates: Sugars). These beverages include, but are not limited 
332 
to, soda, fruitades, and sports drinks. Also called calorically-sweetened beverages. 
333 

 
Appendix E-5: Glossary of Terms 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
11 
 
Sustainable diets—A pattern of eating that promotes health and well-being and provides food 
334 
security for the present population while sustaining human and natural resources for future 
335 
generations.  
336 
Trophic level—A functional classification of species that is based on feeding relationships. 
337 
Generally, aquatic and terrestrial green plants comprise the first, or lowest, trophic level, 
338 
herbivores comprise the second, and primary carnivores comprise the third, or highest level. 
339 
Examples of high trophic fish species are salmon and trout. Low trophic fish species include 
340 
crayfish and catfish. 
341 
Whole grains—Grains and grain products made from the entire grain seed, usually called the 
342 
kernel, which consists of the bran, germ, and endosperm. If the kernel has been cracked, crushed, 
343 
or flaked, it must retain the same relative proportions of bran, germ, and endosperm as the 
344 
original grain in order to be called whole grain. Many, but not all, whole grains are also sources 
345 
of dietary fiber. 
346 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Appendix E-6:  History of Dietary Guidance Development in 
1 
the United States and the Dietary Guidelines for Americans 
2 
In early 1977, after years of discussion, scientific review, and debate, the U.S. Senate Select 
3 
Committee on Nutrition and Human Needs, led by Senator George McGovern, recommended 
4 
Dietary Goals for the American people (U.S. Senate Select Committee, 1977). The Goals 
5 
consisted of complementary nutrient-based and food-based recommendations. The first Goal 
6 
focused on energy balance and recommended that, to avoid overweight, Americans should 
7 
consume only as much energy as they expended. Overweight Americans should consume less 
8 
energy and expend more energy. For the nutrient-based Goals, the Senate Committee 
9 
recommended that Americans:  
10 
• Increase consumption of complex carbohydrates and “naturally occurring sugars;” and 
11 
• Reduce consumption of refined and processed sugars, total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, 
12 
and sodium.  
13 
• For the food-based Goals, the Senate Committee recommended that Americans: 
14 
• Increase consumption of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains;  
15 
• Decrease consumption of:  
16 
o refined and processed sugars and foods high in such sugars; 
17 
o foods high in total fat and animal fat, and partially replace saturated fats with 
18 
polyunsaturated fats;  
19 
o eggs, butterfat, and other high-cholesterol foods; 
20 
o salt and foods high in salt; and 
21 
• Choose low-fat and non-fat dairy products instead of high-fat dairy products (except for 
22 
young children).  
23 
The Dietary Goals was met with considerable debate and controversy, as industry groups and the 
24 
scientific community expressed doubt that the science available at the time supported the 
25 
specificity of the numbers provided in the Dietary Goals. To support the credibility of the science 
26 
used by the Senate Committee, the U.S. Department of Agriculture and U.S. Department of 
27 
Health and Human Services (then called the Department of Health, Education, and Welfare) 
28 
selected scientists from the two Departments and obtained additional expertise from the scientific 
29 
community throughout the country to address the public’s need for authoritative and consistent 
30 
guidance on diet and health.  
31 
 
32 
In February 1980, the two Departments collaboratively issued Nutrition and Your Health: 
33 
Dietary Guidelines for Americans, a brochure that, in describing seven principles for a healthful 
34 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
diet, provided assistance for healthy people in making daily food choices (USDA/HHS, 1980). 
35 
These Guidelines were based, in part, on the 1979 Surgeon General’s Report on Health 
36 
Promotion and Disease Prevention (DHEW/PHS, 1979) and reflected findings from a study on 
37 
the relationship between dietary practices and health outcomes (ASCN, 1979). Ideas for 
38 
incorporating a variety of foods to provide essential nutrients while maintaining recommended 
39 
body weight were a focus. The brochure also provided guidance on limiting dietary components 
40 
such as fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium, which were beginning to be considered risk 
41 
factors in certain chronic diseases. Both the Dietary Goals and the first Dietary Guidelines for 
42 
Americans were different from previous dietary guidance in that they reflected emerging 
43 
scientific evidence and changed the historical focus on nutrient adequacy to also identify the 
44 
impacts of diet on chronic disease. These documents discussed the concepts of moderation as 
45 
well as nutrient adequacy. 
46 
 
47 
Even though the recommendations of the 1980 Dietary Guidelines for Americans were presented 
48 
as innocuous and straightforward extrapolations from the science base, they, too, were met with 
49 
controversy from a variety of industry and scientific groups.  
50 
 
51 
The debate about the 1980 Dietary Guidelines for Americans led to Congressional report 
52 
language that directed the two Departments to convene an advisory committee that would ensure 
53 
that outside advice, both formal and informal, was captured in developing future editions of the 
54 
Dietary Guidelines. A Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee composed of scientific experts 
55 
outside the Federal sector was established shortly after that directive and was very helpful in the 
56 
development of the 1985 Nutrition and Your Health: Dietary Guidelines for Americans 
57 
(USDA/HHS, 1985). The Departments made relatively few changes from the first edition, but 
58 
this second edition was issued with much less debate from either industry or the scientific 
59 
community. The 1985 Dietary Guidelines were widely accepted and were used as the framework 
60 
for consumer nutrition education messages. They also were used as a guide for healthy diets by 
61 
scientific, consumer, and industry groups. 
62 
 
63 
In 1989, USDA and HHS established a second scientific advisory committee to review the 1985 
64 
Dietary Guidelines and make recommendations for revision. The basic tenets of earlier Dietary 
65 
Guidelines were reaffirmed, and the 1990 Nutrition and Your Health: Dietary Guidelines for 
66 
Americans (USDA/HHS, 1990) promoted enjoyable and healthful eating through variety and 
67 
moderation, rather than dietary restriction. For the first time, the Guidelines also suggested 
68 
quantitative goals for total fat and saturated fat, though they stressed that the goals were to be 
69 
met through dietary choices made over several days, not through choices about one meal or one 
70 
food. 
71 
 
72 
The 1980, 1985, and 1990 editions of the Dietary Guidelines were issued voluntarily by the two 
73 
Departments. With the passage of the 1990 National Nutrition Monitoring and Related Research 
74 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
3 
 
Act (Section 301 of Public Law 101-445, 7 USC 5341, Title III) (US Congress, 1990), the 1995 
75 
edition of Nutrition and Your Health: Dietary Guidelines for Americans became the first Dietary 
76 
Guidelines policy document mandated by statute. This Act directed the Secretaries of USDA and 
77 
HHS to jointly issue at least every 5 years a report entitled Dietary Guidelines for Americans.  
78 
 
79 
A Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee was established to prepare technical reports that 
80 
advised the Federal government on the status of the evidence on nutrition and health. These 
81 
technical reports were used  in developing the 1995, 2000, 2005, and 2010 versions of the 
82 
Dietary Guidelines for Americans (HHS/USDA, 1995a; HHS/USDA, 1995b; USDA/HHS, 
83 
2000a; USDA/HHS, 2000b; HHS/USDA, 2004; HHS/USDA, 2005a; USDA/HHS, 2010; 
84 
USDA/HHS, 2011). This report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee will serve a 
85 
similar purpose for HHS and USDA as the Departments develop the 2015 edition of Dietary 
86 
Guidelines for Americans. 
87 
 
88 
Since 1980, the Dietary Guidelines have been notably consistent in their recommendations on the 
89 
components of a healthful diet, but they also have changed in some significant ways to reflect 
90 
emerging science as well as public health concerns, such as the increasing prevalence of major 
91 
chronic diseases among the majority of the general population. In keeping with growing 
92 
emphasis on data quality in developing recommendations, the 2005 Committee used a modified 
93 
systematic approach for reviewing the scientific literature. This systematic review of the 
94 
evidence was further realized for the 2010 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee with the 
95 
establishment of the USDA’s Nutrition Evidence Library, a process that uses state-of-the-art 
96 
methodology to search, evaluate, and synthesize food and nutrition-related research. This 
97 
rigorous, protocol-driven methodology is designed to minimize bias, maximize transparency, and 
98 
ensure relevant, timely, and high-quality systematic reviews to inform Federal nutrition-related 
99 
policies, programs, and recommendations. (See PART C: Methodology for a brief description of 
100 
the systematic evidence review process used by the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory 
101 
Committee and www.NEL.gov for additional information about the Nutrition Evidence Library.)  
102 
 
103 
Over the past two decades, Nutrition and Your Health: Dietary Guidelines for Americans has 
104 
evolved to become a broadly accepted, science-based document that serves as the Federal 
105 
nutrition policy on which nutrition standards, nutrition programs, and nutrition education are 
106 
based. The Dietary Guidelines have proven to be a mechanism for addressing public health 
107 
concerns by providing focused guidance that can help to promote health and reduce chronic 
108 
disease risk. As such, while earlier editions of the Dietary Guidelines focused specifically on 
109 
healthy Americans ages 2 years and older, more recent editions also have included those who are 
110 
at increased risk of chronic disease. The Dietary Guidelines, however, are not directly intended 
111 
for disease treatment, but they can be used as a basis for developing clinical guidelines. 
112 
 
113 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
4 
 
Future editions of the Dietary Guidelines will continue to evolve to address public health 
114 
concerns and the nutrition needs of specific populations. For example, a Federal initiative has 
115 
been established to develop comprehensive guidance for infants and toddlers from birth to 24 
116 
months and women who are pregnant so that by 2020, the Dietary Guidelines will also include 
117 
these important populations comprehensively. For now, nutrition and health professionals 
118 
actively promote the Dietary Guidelines as a means of encouraging Americans to focus on eating 
119 
a healthful diet and being physically active throughout the entire lifespan.  
120 
 
121 
HISTORY OF DIETARY GUIDANCE DEVELOPMENT IN THE UNITED 
122 
STATES AND THE DIETARY GUIDELINES FOR AMERICANS – A 
123 
CHRONOLOGY 
124 
 
125 
1977 
Dietary Goals for the United States (the “McGovern Report”) was issued by the U.S. 
Senate Select Committee on Nutrition and Human Needs (U.S. Senate Select 
Committee, 1977). The Dietary Goals reflected a shift in focus from obtaining adequate 
nutrients to avoiding excessive intake of food components linked to chronic disease. 
These goals were controversial among some nutritionists and others concerned with 
food, nutrition, and health.  
1979 
The American Society for Clinical Nutrition formed a panel to study the relationship 
between dietary practices and health outcomes (ASCN, 1979). The findings, presented 
in 1979, were reflected in Healthy People: The Surgeon General's Report on Health 
Promotion and Disease Prevention (DHEW/PHS, 1979). 
1980 
Seven principles for a healthful diet were jointly issued by the then U.S. Department of 
Health, Education, and Welfare (now HHS) and the U.S. Department of Agriculture 
(USDA) in response to the public's desire for authoritative, consistent guidelines on diet 
and health. These principles became the first edition of Nutrition and Your Health: 
Dietary Guidelines for Americans (USDA/HHS, 1980). The 1980 Guidelines were 
based on the most up-to-date information available at the time and were directed to 
healthy Americans ages 2 and older. The Guidelines generated some concern among 
consumer, commodity, and food industry groups, as well as some nutrition scientists, 
who questioned the causal relationship between certain guidelines and health. 
1980 
A U.S. Senate Committee on Appropriations report directed that an external advisory 
committee be established to review scientific evidence and recommend revisions to the 
1980 Nutrition and Your Health: Dietary Guidelines for Americans (U.S. Senate, 1980).  
1983 
An external Federal advisory committee of nine nutrition scientists was convened to 
review and make recommendations in a report to the Secretaries of USDA and HHS 
about the first (1980) edition of the Dietary Guidelines (USDA/HHS, 1985a). 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
5 
 
1985 
USDA and HHS jointly issued the second edition of Nutrition and Your Health: Dietary 
Guidelines for Americans (USDA/HHS, 1985b). This edition was nearly identical to the 
first, retaining the seven guidelines from the 1980 edition. Some changes were made for 
clarity, while others reflected advances in scientific knowledge of the associations 
between diet and chronic diseases. The second edition received wide acceptance and 
was used as the basis for dietary guidance for the general public as well as a framework 
for developing consumer education messages. 
1987 
Language in the Conference Report of the House Committee on Appropriations 
indicated that USDA, in conjunction with HHS, “shall reestablish a Dietary Guidelines 
Advisory Group on a periodic basis. This Advisory Group will review the scientific data 
relevant to nutritional guidance and make recommendations on appropriate changes to 
the Secretaries of the Departments of Agriculture and Health and Human Services” 
(U.S. House of Representatives, 1987). 
1989 
USDA and HHS established a second Federal advisory committee of nine members, 
which considered whether revisions to the 1985 Dietary Guidelines were needed and 
made recommendations for revision in a report to the Secretaries (USDA/HHS, 1990a). 
The 1988 Surgeon General's Report on Nutrition and Health (HHS/PHS, 1988) and the 
1989 National Research Council’s report Diet and Health: Implications for Reducing 
Chronic Disease Risk were key resources used by the Committee (NAS/NRC, 1989). 
1990 
 
 
 
USDA and HHS jointly released the third edition of Nutrition and Your Health: Dietary 
Guidelines for Americans (USDA/HHS, 1990b). The basic tenets of the 1985 Dietary 
Guidelines were reaffirmed, with additional refinements made to reflect increased 
understanding of the science of nutrition and how best to communicate the science to 
consumers. The language of the new Dietary Guidelines was positive, was oriented 
toward the total diet, and provided specific information regarding food selection. For the 
first time, quantitative recommendations were made for intakes of dietary total fat and 
saturated fat. 
1990 
The 1990 National Nutrition Monitoring and Related Research Act (Section 301 of 
Public Law 101-445, 7 USC 5341, Title III) directed the Secretaries of the USDA and 
HHS to jointly issue at least every 5 years a report entitled Dietary Guidelines for 
Americans (U.S. Congress, 1990). This legislation also required USDA and HHS to 
review all Federal publications containing dietary advice for the general public. 
1993 
HHS initiated a charter establishing the 1995 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee. 
1994 
An 11-member Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee was appointed by the 
Secretaries of HHS and USDA to review the third edition of the Dietary Guidelines and 
determine whether changes were needed. If so, the Committee was to recommend 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
6 
 
suggestions and the rationale for any revisions.  
1995 
The report of the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee to the Secretaries of HHS and 
USDA was published (HHS/USDA, 1995a).  
1995 
Using the 1995 report of the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee as the foundation, 
HHS and USDA jointly developed and released the fourth edition of Nutrition and Your 
Health: Dietary Guidelines for Americans (HHS/USDA, 1995b). This edition continued 
to support the concepts from earlier editions. New information included the Food Guide 
Pyramid, Nutrition Facts label, boxes highlighting good food sources of key nutrients, 
and a chart illustrating three weight ranges in relation to height.  
1997 
USDA initiated the charter establishing the 2000 Dietary Guidelines Advisory 
Committee. 
1998 
An 11-member Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee was appointed by the 
Secretaries of USDA and HHS to review the fourth edition of the Dietary Guidelines to 
determine whether changes were needed and, if so, to recommend suggestions for 
revision.  
2000 
The Committee submitted its report to the Secretaries of USDA and HHS (USDA/HHS, 
2000a). This report contained the proposed text for the fifth edition of Nutrition and 
Your Health: Dietary Guidelines for Americans. 
2000 
The President of the United States spoke of the Dietary Guidelines in his radio address 
after USDA and HHS jointly issued the fifth edition of Nutrition and Your Health: 
Dietary Guidelines for Americans earlier in the day (USDA/HHS, 2000b). Earlier 
versions of the Guidelines included seven statements. This version included 10—created 
by breaking out physical activity from the weight guideline, splitting the grains and 
fruits/vegetables recommendations for greater emphasis, and adding a new guideline on 
safe food handling.  
2003 
HHS initiated the charter establishing the 2005 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee. 
2003 
A 13-member Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee was appointed by the Secretaries 
of HHS and USDA to review the fifth edition of the Dietary Guidelines to determine 
whether changes were needed and, if so, to recommend suggestions for revision.  
2003-
2004 
In keeping with renewed emphasis on data quality, the Committee used a modified 
“systematic approach” to review the scientific literature and develop its 
recommendations. Committee members initially posed approximately 40 specific 
research questions that were answered using an extensive search and review of the 
scientific literature. Issues relating diet and physical activity to health promotion and 
chronic disease prevention were included in the Committee’s evidence review. Other 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
7 
 
major sources of evidence used were the Dietary Reference Intake (DRI) reports 
prepared by expert committees convened by the Institute of Medicine (IOM) as well as 
various Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ) and World Health 
Organization (WHO) reports. In addition, USDA completed numerous food intake 
pattern modeling analyses and the Committee analyzed various national data sets and 
sought advice from invited experts. 
2004 
The Committee submitted its technical report to the Secretaries of HHS and USDA 
(HHS/USDA, 2004). This 364-page report contained a detailed analysis of the science 
and was accompanied by many pages of evidence-based tables that were made available 
electronically. After dropping some questions because of incomplete or inconclusive 
data, the Committee wrote conclusions and comprehensive rationales for 34 of the 40 
original questions.  
2005 
Using the Committee’s technical report as a basis, HHS and USDA jointly prepared and 
issued the sixth edition of Dietary Guidelines for Americans in January 2005 
(HHS/USDA, 2005a). This 80-page policy document was the first time the Departments 
prepared a policy document that was intended primarily for use by policy makers, 
healthcare professionals, nutritionists, and nutrition educators. The content of this 
document included nine major Dietary Guidelines messages that resulted in 41 Key 
Recommendations, of which 23 were for the U.S. population overall and 18 for specific 
population groups. The policy document highlighted the USDA Food Guide and the 
DASH Eating Plan as two examples of eating patterns that exemplify the Dietary 
Guidelines recommendations. A companion, 10-page brochure called Finding Your 
Way to a Healthier You (HHS/USDA, 2005b) was released concurrently with the 
Dietary Guidelines to provide advice to consumers about food choices that promote 
health and decrease the risk of chronic disease. Shortly thereafter, USDA released the 
MyPyramid Food Guidance System, an update of the Food Guide Pyramid, which 
included more detailed advice for consumers to help them follow the Dietary 
Guidelines. 
2008 
USDA initiated the charter establishing the 2010 Dietary Guidelines Advisory 
Committee. 
2008 
 
A 13-member Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee was appointed by the Secretaries 
of USDA and HHS to review the sixth edition of Dietary Guidelines for Americans to 
determine whether changes were needed and, if so, to recommend suggestions for 
revision.  
2008-
2009 
 
USDA’s Center for Nutrition Policy and Promotion established the Nutrition Evidence 
Library (NEL) to conduct systematic reviews to help inform Federal nutrition policy and 
programs. The NEL supported the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee in 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
8 
 
 
answering approximately 130 of the total 180 diet and health-related questions posed. 
This was the most rigorous and comprehensive approach used to date for reviewing the 
science in order to develop nutrition-related recommendations for the public. Other 
sources of evidence for answering scientific questions included modeling analyses of 
USDA’s Food Patterns, review of reports from various data analyses, as well as other 
available authoritative reports (e.g., 2005 DGAC Report and IOM reports). An elaborate 
web-based public comments database was developed and provided a successful 
mechanism for the public to provide comments and thereby participate in the 
Committee’s evidence review process. The database also allowed the public to read 
other comments that were submitted. This database eventually included more than 800 
public comments related to the DGAC process. 
2010 
 
The Committee submitted its technical report to the Secretaries of USDA and HHS 
(USDA/HHS 2010). This 445-page report contained a detailed analysis of the science 
and was accompanied by additional 230 pages of food pattern modeling appendices 
made available electronically at www.DietaryGuidelines.gov.  
2011 
Using the Committee’s technical report as the basis, HHS and USDA jointly prepared 
and published the seventh edition of Dietary Guidelines for Americans released 
publically in January 2011 (USDA/HHS, 2011). The 95-page policy document 
encompassed the overarching concepts of maintaining calorie balance over time to 
achieve and sustain a healthy weight, and consuming nutrient-dense foods and 
beverages. The policy document included 23 key recommendations for the general 
population and six additional key recommendations for specific populations. To assist 
individuals to build a healthy diet based on the Dietary Guidelines, the USDA Food 
Patterns were updated and new vegetarian adaptations were included. The DASH Eating 
Plan also was included as an example of a healthy dietary pattern. This publication will 
serve as the basis for Federal nutrition policy until the next policy document is released 
in 2015. In June, USDA released MyPlate, a new visual icon, and the 
ChooseMyPlate.gov website that provides tools to help consumers of all ages, 
educators, and health professionals learn about and follow the Dietary Guidelines. 
2013 
HHS initiated the charter establishing the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee. 
2013 
A 15-member Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee was appointed by the Secretaries 
of USDA and HHS to review the seventh edition of Dietary Guidelines for Americans 
and recommend suggestions for revision. One member resigned due to professional 
obligations within the first three months after appointment; 14 members served the 
remainder of the two-year charter. The Committee also added three consultant 
subcommittee members during its work to address specific issues; these members 
participated in discussions and decision at the subcommittee level but were not members 

 
Appendix E-6: History of the Dietary Guidelines 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
9 
 
of the full Committee. 
2015
 
 
The Committee submitted this technical report to the Secretaries of USDA and HHS in 
January 2015. This 580-page report contained a detailed analysis of the science and was 
accompanied by substantial documentation of the process made available electronically 
at www.DietaryGuidelines.gov and www.NEL.gov.

Appendix E-7: Public Comments 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
1 
 
Appendix E-7: Public Comments 
1 
As a government advisory committee, the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee (DGAC) is 
2 
required by the Federal Advisory Committee Act to function in an open process in which the 
3 
public may participate. This is accomplished through public submission of written comments and 
4 
oral testimony given to the DGAC. 
5 
 
6 
Federal Register notices alerted the public to DGAC meetings held in-person and/or by webcast. 
7 
In these notices the public was invited and reminded to submit their comments to an online 
8 
database at www.DietaryGuidelines.gov. The public comments process opened on May 29, 
9 
2013. Comments continued to be submitted throughout the time the DGAC operated. Following 
10 
the submission of the 2015 DGAC Report to the Secretaries of HHS and USDA, the Federal 
11 
government will alert the public of its availability through a Federal Register notice. This notice 
12 
also will announce a public comment period and the date of an in-person meeting where the 
13 
public can provide comments to the Federal government about the DGAC Report. 
14 
 
15 
A public comments online database was developed for the 2015 DGAC process based on the 
16 
structure and content used for the 2010 process, but with many enhancements that were intended 
17 
to streamline submission of comments by the public and processing by staff.  
18 
 
19 
When submitting comments, the public selected one or more topic areas into which they felt their 
20 
comments belonged. Initially, these topic areas were:  Food Groups, Eating Patterns-Diets, 
21 
Energy Balance, Carbohydrates, Protein, Fats, Micronutrients, Water and Nonalcoholic 
22 
Beverages, Alcoholic Beverages, Food Safety, Behavior and Food Environment, Lifespan 
23 
Needs, and Other. During their deliberations and at the DGAC’s request, the topic area 
24 
“Behavior and Food Environment” was split into two distinct topic areas, “Behavior” and “Food 
25 
Environment,” and a new topic area, “Sustainability,” also was added. Individual submissions 
26 
were allowed to include up to five attachments, such as journal articles, reports, and other 
27 
scientific material for the DGAC to consider. The submission page noted that submitters should 
28 
take care to not violate copyright laws when submitting attachments. 
29 
 
30 
For the first time, the 2015 DGAC requested public comments related to specific topic areas. 
31 
Subcommittee (SC) 2 requested comments on “steps the food industry is taking or has taken to 
32 
reduce the nutrients listed below in the food supply, including what nutrients have been 
33 
increased as a consequence of reductions where applicable:  sodium, added sugars, fats (i.e., total 
34 
fats, saturated fats, trans fats, and other individual fatty acids).”  SC 5 requested public 
35 
comments on “a targeted topic on food system sustainability, including comments from both the 
36 
private and public sectors and addressing local, regional, national, or international scales. 
37 
Specifically, it seeks approaches and current examples of sustainability in the food system. 
38 
Comments are encouraged that address:  (a) Elements of a whole food system; (b) Information 
39 

 
Appendix E-7: Public Comments 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
2 
 
on specific food groups or commodities; and (c) Sustainability metrics that have been 
40 
implemented or are in development.” 
41 
 
42 
In addition, for the first time, the Committee also provided specific guidance to the public on 
43 
“length and timing of public comments.” This guidance was shared through the Federal Register 
44 
and on www.DietaryGuidelines.gov. This guidance stated to “provide a brief summary (approx. 
45 
250 words) of the points or issues in the comment text box.” It asked that “if providing literature 
46 
or other resources, one of the following forms is preferred: complete citation, as in a 
47 
bibliographic entry; abstract; electronic link to full article or report.” The public was encouraged 
48 
to “provide comments as early as possible in the Committee’s process to increase the opportunity 
49 
for meaningful impact.” Lastly, as of April 2014, it stated that “a deadline for comment 
50 
submission prior to each public meeting will no longer be used.” 
51 
 
52 
For all public comments, submitters were required to provide the following information:  topic 
53 
area(s), the comment itself (5,000 character limit), any accompanying attachments, full name 
54 
(with option to make it public), affiliation, and organization. They also were required to provide 
55 
their email address, phone number, and zip code, but this information was not included when the 
56 
comment was posted on the www.DietaryGuidelines.gov public comments page. Submitters 
57 
were given the option, but not required, to also provide their business or academic credentials 
58 
and postal address, including country. This information was not posted on the public website. 
59 
After the comment was submitted, confirmation was provided to the submitter by e-mail. 
60 
 
61 
Staff reviewed each submitted comment. Only a few comments were not posted; reasons were:  
62 
(1) duplicate submission of another comment posted by the same submitter, (2) test submission, 
63 
(3) partial comment due to the 5,000 character limit, which was corrected by a shorter comment 
64 
being submitted, and (4) comments that did not pertain to the DGAC. 
65 
 
66 
At the request of the DGAC, staff generated reports and drafted summaries on each topic area for 
67 
comments submitted since the previous meeting or since the previous comment summary. On 
68 
occasion, various Committee members also chose to access the public comments database 
69 
themselves in order to read comments. 
70 
 
71 
A total of 972 comments were submitted from May 29, 2013 through the closing of the public 
72 
comments database on December 30, 2014. Of these, 918 were relevant to the DGAC’s work. 
73 
 
74 
The majority of comments submitted fell into these topic areas:   Food Groups; Eating Patterns-
75 
Diets; Sustainability; and Energy Balance. However, comments were received in all 18 topic 
76 
areas and covered a wide range of issues. Comments came from the United States, Australia, 
77 
India, Spain, Canada, Brazil, France, Belgium, Norway, Iraq, United Kingdom, Pakistan, 
78 
Indonesia, and Denmark.   
79 

 
Appendix E-7: Public Comments 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
3 
 
 
80 
In addition to written comments, oral comments from 53 individuals were presented at the 
81 
January 2014 public meeting. The list of presenters along with their affiliations is located on 
82 
www.DietaryGuidelines.gov under Meeting 2 (January 13-14, 2014). These 53 individuals each 
83 
provided 3 minutes or less of testimony before the Committee, and they submitted a brief outline 
84 
of their comments when registering to participate in the comment session.  
85 
 
86 
The oral and written comments provided by the public were valuable in that they helped  
87 
the Committee gather background information and understand public and professional 
88 
perceptions. Comments from the public brought new issues to light as well as new approaches to 
89 
current issues and emerging evidence. They also highlighted and ensured consideration of topics 
90 
deemed to be important by the submitters, who represented a variety of backgrounds and focus 
91 
areas. The public comments will remain archived at www.DietaryGuidelines.gov. 
92

Appendix E-8: Biographical Sketches of the 2015 DGAC 
1 
Chair: Barbara Millen, DrPH, RD: Professor, Department of Family Medicine, Boston 
2 
University School of Medicine, Boston, MA (through 2009). Dr. Millen is currently the Founder 
3 
and President of Millennium Prevention, Inc., a U.S.-based start-up company with a public 
4 
health mission, which develops web-based platforms and mobile applications to encourage 
5 
healthy preventive lifestyle behaviors for clinical settings and corporate, academic, and 
6 
community wellness initiatives. Dr. Millen is a nutrition epidemiologist whose academic 
7 
research career focused on dietary patterns and lifestyle determinants of health and chronic 
8 
disease risk as well as evidence-based clinical and public health strategies to promote optimal 
9 
nutrition and well-being in younger and older adults as well as low-income and minority 
10 
populations. During her 30-year tenure at Boston University, she was the Founding Chairman of 
11 
the Graduate Programs in Medical Nutrition Sciences, the Associate Dean for Research and 
12 
Faculty Development of the School of Public Health, the Chairman of the Faculty Council, and 
13 
Director of Nutrition Research for the internationally-renown Framingham Heart Study. She has 
14 
advised research groups nationally and globally, including the World Health Organization, and 
15 
served from 2008 to 2013 on the expert panels for the American Heart Association 
16 
(AHA)/American College of Cardiology (ACC)/The Obesity Society (TOS) Guideline for the 
17 
Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults and the AHA/ACC Guideline on Lifestyle 
18 
Management to Reduce Cardiovascular Risk.  
19 
 
20 
Vice Chair: Alice H. Lichtenstein, DSc: Stanley N. Gershoff Professor of Nutrition Science and 
21 
Policy, Friedman School of Nutrition Science and Policy, Tufts University, Boston, MA. Dr. 
22 
Lichtenstein is also Director and Senior Scientist, Cardiovascular Nutrition Laboratory, Jean 
23 
Mayer USDA Human Nutrition Research Center on Aging and Professor of Medicine at Tufts 
24 
University School of Medicine. Dr. Lichtenstein has broad expertise in nutrition and 
25 
cardiovascular disease risk reduction. She previously served as a member of the 2000 Dietary 
26 
Guidelines Advisory Committee and as a member of the Institute of Medicine (IOM) Dietary 
27 
Reference Intake Panel on Macronutrients. Dr. Lichtenstein recently served as the vice-chair of 
28 
the IOM Committee on Examination of Front-of-Package Nutrient Rating System and Symbols, 
29 
a member of the IOM Committee on the Consequences of Sodium Reduction in Populations, the 
30 
vice-chair of the ACC/AHA Guideline on the Treatment of Blood Cholesterol to Reduce 
31 
Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Risk in Adults expert panel, a member of the AHA/ACC 
32 
Guideline on Lifestyle Management to Reduce Cardiovascular Risk expert work group, and is  
33 
chair of the American Heart Association's Nutrition Committee. She is currently a member of the 
34 
IOM Food and Nutrition Board. 
35 
 
36 
Steven Abrams, MD: Professor of Pediatrics, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, TX. Dr. 
37 
Abrams also is an Adjunct Professor at the University of Texas School of Public Health and the 
38 
Medical Director for the Neonatal Nutrition Program at Baylor College of Medicine. He is an 
39

expert on mineral requirements in children, including calcium, zinc, iron, magnesium, and

Wayne Campbell, PhD: Professor, Department of Nutrition Science, Purdue University, West 
79 
Lafayette, IN. Dr. Campbell also is an Adjunct Faculty in the Department of Health and 
80

81

92

93

94

95

96

97

98

Health, and a Professor of Medicine at Harvard Medical School and Channing Division of 
107 
Network Medicine, Brigham and Women's Hospital. Dr. Hu is an epidemiologist and an expert 
108 
in the areas of dietary and lifestyle determinants of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular 
109 
disease. He is the principal investigator for the diabetes component of the Nurses' Health Study. 
110 
Dr. Hu has served as an academic leader in a variety of roles, including on the National Heart, 
111 
Lung, and Blood Institute Obesity Guidelines Expert Panel and the IOM Committee on 
112 
Preventing the Global Epidemic of Cardiovascular Disease. 
113 
 
114 
Miriam Nelson, PhD: Associate Dean, Jonathan M. Tisch College of Citizenship and Public 
115 
Service, Tufts University, Boston, MA. Dr. Nelson also is a Professor in the Friedman School of 
116 
Nutrition Science and Policy. Dr. Nelson is an expert on nutrition and physical activity, with 
117 
extensive research experience integrating the science of energy balance into national-scale 
118 

 
Appendix E-8: Bio Sketches of DGAC 
 
Scientific Report of the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee 
4 
 
approaches. Her work combines civic engagement, public policy, communications, and systems 
119 
thinking to create change. Dr. Nelson is Founder of the Strong Women Initiative and Co-Founder 
120 
of ChildObesity180 at Tufts University. Dr. Nelson served as Vice Chair of the Physical Activity 
121 
Guidelines Advisory Committee in 2008 and was a member of the 2010 Dietary Guidelines 
122 
Advisory Committee.  
123 
 
124 
Marian Neuhouser, PhD, RD: Full Member, Cancer Prevention Program, Division of Public 
125 
Health Sciences, Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center, Seattle, WA. Dr. Neuhouser also is 
126 
an Affiliate Professor in the Department of Epidemiology and Core Faculty in the Graduate 
127 
Program in Nutritional Sciences, School of Public Health, University of Washington. Dr. 
128 
Neuhouser is a nutritional epidemiologist with broad experience in large clinical trials, including 
129 
the Women's Health Initiative and the Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial, small-scale controlled 
130 
dietary interventions, and large observational cohorts. She has expertise in the role of numerous 
131 
dietary components in cancer risk, including carbohydrates, dietary fiber, and vitamin D. Her 
132 
research focuses on methods to improve diet and physical activity assessment, diet and physical 
133 
activity in relation to energy balance, diet-related health disparities, and dietary factors related to 
134 
breast and prostate cancer prevention and survivorship. 
135 
 
136 
Rafael Pérez-Escamilla, PhD: Professor of Epidemiology and Public Health, Yale School of 
137 
Public Health, New Haven, CT. Dr. Pérez-Escamilla also serves as Director, Office of Public 
138 
Health Practice and the Global Health Concentration at the Yale School of Public Health. He is 
139 
an internationally recognized scholar in the area of community nutrition. Dr. Pérez-Escamilla has 
140 
specialized experience with Hispanic and low-income Americans, as well as populations in low 
141 
and middle income countries. His research program seeks to understand how best to protect, 
142 
promote, and support breastfeeding, causes and consequences of food insecurity, and how to 
143 
improve diabetes self-management through community health workers. Dr. Pérez-Escamilla has 
144 
published numerous articles that have led to improvements in breastfeeding outcomes, iron 
145 
deficiency anemia among infants, household food security measurement, and community 
146 
nutrition education programs worldwide. He is past-chair of the Global Nutrition Council of the 
147 
American Society for Nutrition and is a member of the IOM Food and Nutrition Board. 
148 
Previously, Dr. Pérez-Escamilla served as a member of the IOM Committee to Re-examine IOM 
149 
Pregnancy Weight Guidelines and was a member of the 2010 Dietary Guidelines Advisory 
150 
Committee.  
151 
 
152 
Anna Maria Siega-Riz, PhD, RD: Associate Dean for Academic Affairs and Professor, 
153 
Departments of Epidemiology and Nutrition, Gillings School of Global Public Health, University 
154 
of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, NC. Dr. Siega-Riz serves as the Program Leader 
155 
for the Reproductive, Perinatal, and Pediatric Program in the Department of Epidemiology. Dr. 
156 
Siega-Riz has focused her research on maternal nutritional status, including maternal obesity and 
157 
gestational weight gain and their effect on birth outcomes as well as the determinants of early 
158

5 
 
childhood obesity. She studies dietary patterns among Hispanic adults and children, in general, 
159 
and served on the Scientific Advisory Panel for the Feeding Infants and Toddlers Study. Dr. 
160 
Siega-Riz has served on multiple committees for the IOM, examining topics from the WIC food 
161 
packages to standards for systematic reviews in health care and currently serves on the advisory 
162 
council of the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute.  
163 
 
164 
Mary Story, PhD, RD: Professor, Community and Family Medicine and Global Health, Duke 
165 
University, Durham, NC. Before coming to Duke in January 2014 she was Senior Associate 
166 
Dean for Academic and Student Affairs and Professor in the Division of Epidemiology and 
167 
Community Health in the School of Public Health, University of Minnesota. Dr. Story 
168 
concurrently serves as Director of the National Program Office for the Robert Wood Johnson 
169 
Foundation Healthy Eating Research Program that supports research on environmental and 
170 
policy strategies to promote healthy eating among children to prevent childhood obesity. She has 
171 
conducted numerous school and community-based environmental intervention and obesity 
172 
prevention studies for children, adolescents, and families. Dr. Story was elected to the IOM in 
173 
2010 and is currently a member of the IOM Food and Nutrition Board and vice co-chair of the 
174 
IOM Roundtable on Obesity Solutions. 
175

6 
 
Consultant Subcommittee Members to the 2015 DGAC 
177 
Timothy S. Griffin, PhD: Director, Agriculture and Environment Program, Friedman School of 
178 
Nutrition Science and Policy, Tufts University, Boston, MA. Dr. Griffin also is an Associate 
179 
Professor at Tufts University where he serves on the Water: Systems, Science and Society 
180 
faculty steering committee and is a Faculty Co-Director for the Tufts Institute for the 
181 
Environment. His research expertise and interests include the intersection of agriculture and the 
182 
environment, and the development and implementation of sustainable production systems. 
183 
Previously he worked as a Research Agronomist and Lead Scientist with USDA-ARS New 
184 
England Plant Soil and Water Lab, and as Extension Sustainable Agriculture Specialist at the 
185 
University of Maine.  
186 
 
187 
Michael W. Hamm, PhD: Director, Center for Regional Food Systems, Michigan State 
188 
University, East Lansing, MI. Dr. Hamm is also the C.S. Mott Professor of Sustainable 
189 
Agriculture in the Department of Community Sustainability in the College of Agriculture and 
190 
Natural Resources and has appointments in the Department of Food Science Human Nutrition 
191 
and the Department of Plant, Soil and Microbial Sciences at Michigan State University. His 
192 
research expertise and interests include regional and sustainable food systems and food security.  
193 
 
194 
Michael G. Perri, PhD, ABPP: Dean, College of Public Health and Health Professions 
195 
University of Florida; Gainesville, FL. Dr. Perri is also the Robert G. Frank Endowed Professor 
196 
of Clinical and Health Psychology. His research focuses on health promotion and disease 
197 
prevention through changes in diet and physical activity. His NIH-funded studies involve the 
198 
translation, dissemination, and implementation of effective programs for the management of 
199 
obesity in underserved rural communities. Dr. Perri has served as a member on NIH data and 
200 
safety monitoring boards, including serving as chair of the recent NIH/NHLBI Data and Safety 
201 
Monitoring Board for the “EARLY Weight Loss Trials.”
